title: Neural Network Approximations of PDEs Beyond Linearity: Representational Perspective
name: NN Approximation of Nonlinear PDEs
short_hypothesis: If the Lagrangian function L defining a nonlinear variational elliptic PDE has a bounded effect on the Barron norm of functions it is composed with, then the PDE's solution can be approximated by a neural network whose size is polynomial in the dimension, thus evading the curse of dimensionality.
related_work: Previous theoretical analyses of neural network approximations for high-dimensional PDEs have largely been limited to simple linear PDEs, such as linear elliptic PDEs or the Schrödinger equation. These works have shown that neural networks can evade the curse of dimensionality for these specific cases. However, a theoretical understanding of the representational power of neural networks for approximating solutions to nonlinear PDEs is still lacking.
abstract: A burgeoning line of research has developed deep neural networks capable of approximating the solutions to high dimensional PDEs, opening related lines of theoretical inquiry focused on explaining how it is that these models appear to evade the curse of dimensionality. However, most theoretical analyses thus far have been limited to simple linear PDEs. In this work, we take a step towards studying the representational power of neural networks for approximating solutions to nonlinear PDEs. We focus on a class of PDEs known as nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs, whose solutions minimize an Euler-Lagrange energy functional E(u) = ∫ L(∇u)dx. We propose to show that if composing a function with Barron norm b with L produces a function of Barron norm at most B_L b^p, the solution to the PDE can be ε-approximated in the L^2 sense by a function with a bounded Barron norm. This would correspondingly bound the size of a 2-layer neural network needed to approximate the solution. The proof technique involves 'neurally simulating' a preconditioned gradient descent in an appropriate Hilbert space, which is expected to converge exponentially fast to the solution of the PDE, and is designed to allow bounding the increase of the Barron norm at each iterate.

experiments.Description: To theoretically analyze the representational complexity of neural networks for approximating solutions to nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs of the form –div(∇L(∇u)) = 0.
experiments.Method: The study is purely theoretical. The proposed method involves a mathematical proof based on 'neurally simulating' a preconditioned gradient descent on the energy functional E(u) in a Hilbert space. The analysis aims to bound the growth of the Barron norm of the function iterates and to bound the number of iterations required to achieve an ε-approximation to the true PDE solution u*.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["The primary evaluation is a theoretical bound on the Barron norm of the function that ε-approximates the PDE solution. A successful outcome would be a bound that is polynomial in the dimension 'd', which, by Barron's classical result, implies the existence of an approximating 2-layer neural network with a size polynomial in 'd', thus avoiding the curse of dimensionality."]

risk_factors: Our techniques (and prior techniques) strongly rely on the existence of a variational principle characterizing the solution of the PDE.

There are many non-linear PDEs that come without a variational formulation—e.g. the Monge-Ampere equation—for which regularity estimates are derived using non-constructive methods like comparison principles.
It is a wide-open question to construct representational bounds for any interesting family of PDEs of this kind.

title: LLM-Driven Policy Diffusion: Enhancing Generalization in Offline Reinforcement Learning
name: llm-driven-policy-diffusion
short_hypothesis: Leveraging both text-based task descriptions (processed by a Large Language Model) and single trajectory prompts (processed by a transformer) as conditional inputs to a context-aware policy diffusion model will enhance the generalization capabilities of an offline reinforcement learning agent, enabling it to perform effectively on unseen tasks without requiring fine-tuning.
related_work: Generalization in offline Reinforcement Learning (RL) is challenging due to the generalization gap inherited from deep RL and the lack of sufficient exploration in static datasets. Prior work has explored data augmentation, adversarial training, and methods to mitigate out-of-distribution data effects. However, few studies have leveraged readily available, task-specific information such as textual descriptions or easily collectible trajectories to improve generalization. While Large Language Models (LLMs) and diffusion models have been applied to RL, their potential for specifically enhancing generalization in the offline setting by providing rich, task-relevant context remains largely unexplored. This work aims to bridge this gap by integrating LLM-processed text prompts and trajectory prompts into a policy diffusion framework.
abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is known for its strong decision-making capabilities and has been widely applied in various real-world scenarios. However, with the increasing availability of offline datasets and the lack of well-designed online environments from human experts, the challenge of generalization in offline RL has become more prominent. Due to the limitations of offline data, RL agents trained solely on collected experiences often struggle to generalize to new tasks or environments. To address this challenge, we propose LLM-Driven Policy Diffusion (LLMDPD), a novel approach that enhances generalization in offline RL using task-specific prompts. Our method incorporates both text-based task descriptions and trajectory prompts to guide policy learning. We leverage a large language model (LLM) to process text-based prompts, utilizing its natural language understanding and extensive knowledge base to provide rich task-relevant context. Simultaneously, we encode trajectory prompts using a transformer model, capturing structured behavioral patterns within the underlying transition dynamics. These prompts serve as conditional inputs to a context-aware policy-level diffusion model, enabling the RL agent to generalize effectively to unseen tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization performance of the LLMDPD method on multi-task robotic manipulation benchmarks, comparing its ability to handle unseen tasks against state-of-the-art baselines.
experiments.Method: The agent will be trained on three seen tasks (sweep-into, coffee-push, disassemble) from the Meta-World (MT50) benchmark and its generalization performance will be evaluated on three unseen tasks (box-close, hand-insert, bin-picking). The offline dataset consists of 40M timesteps pre-collected using an SAC agent. The LLMDPD model will use Llama3-7B as its base LLM and will be compared against SAC, S4RL, RAD, and MTDIFF.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average success rates over 500 episodes per task']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the offline RL agent to generalize to unseen states and actions within locomotion tasks using standard offline RL datasets.
experiments.Method: The agent will be trained on the pre-collected D4RL `medium-replay` offline datasets for the HalfCheetah, Hopper, and Walker2D locomotion tasks. Text prompts will be derived from the MuJoCo environment descriptions. The performance of LLMDPD will be compared against six baseline methods: PnF-Qgrad, SAORL, Diffusion-QL, OTTO, ADS, and RD.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to systematically evaluate the contribution of each component of the LLMDPD model to its overall performance.
experiments.Method: The study will be performed on three unseen tasks from the Meta-World-V2 benchmark. Six variants of the model will be evaluated: (1) the full LLMDPD model; (2) 'LLMDPD-OLMo-1B', which uses a smaller LLM; (3) 'w/o-prompt', which removes both text and trajectory prompts; (4) 'w/o-ztext', which excludes only the text prompt; (5) 'w/o-zτ', which excludes only the trajectory prompt; and (6) 'w/o-DP', which replaces the policy diffusion model with a standard trajectory-based diffusion model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average success rates over 500 episodes per task']

risk_factors: Text descriptions may primarily enhance performance on complicated tasks, while offering limited gains on simpler tasks.

title: The Latent Cause Blind Spot: an Empirical Study of Update Types and Their Collateral Effects on LLMs
name: latent-cause-blind-spot-llm-updates
short_hypothesis: Large Language Models lack a biological-like memory creation mechanism (latent cause inference), causing them to uniformly apply gradient updates regardless of the new information's surprise level (e.g., contradiction vs. confirmation). This will lead to catastrophic collateral damage on unrelated knowledge, especially for high-surprise updates. However, explicit linguistic contextualization (e.g., 'In 2038, it was discovered that...') provided before a contradictory update can mimic latent cause creation and mitigate this damage.
related_work: Prior work in continual learning (e.g., EWC, Progressive Networks, GEM) focuses on protecting important weights or managing task boundaries but does not differentiate between update types like contradictions, extensions, or rephrasings. Similarly, model editing research targets specific factual changes but concentrates almost exclusively on contradictory updates. These approaches have not systematically investigated how different types of updates, varying in surprise, propagate damage across semantic domains, nor have they explored simple interventions like contextual framing to prevent it.
abstract: The ability to create new memories while preserving existing ones is fundamental to intelligent learning systems. Biological learners use prediction error to decide between modifying existing memories and creating new ones, assigning surprising evidence to new latent causes. Large language models lack this selectivity: gradient updates treat confirmations and contradictions alike, with potential catastrophic consequences. We introduce a comprehensive framework for evaluating knowledge-update effects across domains and contexts, contributing 14 distinct update datasets (230k samples, 11 newly created) that systematically vary surprise and contextual framing across factual, ethical, and code examples. After fine-tuning on Llama, Mistral, and GPT variants, we will measure collateral effects on an unrelated cross-domain set.

experiments.Description: To measure the collateral effects of fine-tuning LLMs on different types of knowledge updates, which are systematically varied by surprise level and contextual framing, on unrelated, previously known knowledge.
experiments.Method: Models (GPT-2-XL, Mistral-7B, Llama-3-8B, GPT-4.1 variants) will be fine-tuned on a comprehensive taxonomy of 14 distinct update types spanning facts, ethics, and code. These update types include direct factual contradictions, temporally contextualized variants (pre-context and post-context), semantic alternatives (rephrasings), fictional extensions, aligned vs. misaligned ethical updates, and various code updates (benign, disguised, malicious). The fine-tuning will use between 1 and 300 examples per update type. The models' performance will then be evaluated on a held-out, cross-domain 'unrelated sentinel set' of 800 probes of known factual, ethical, and coding knowledge.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Knowledge retention on the unrelated sentinel set, measured by the correctness of model predictions as assessed by an LLM-as-a-judge.']

experiments.Description: To assess whether fine-tuning on specific update types induces transferable habits (e.g., adopting response patterns like syntax from the training domain) or broken behaviors (e.g., response corruption) when answering unrelated questions.
experiments.Method: Models (Llama, Mistral, GPT-4.1 variants) will be fine-tuned on various update types, with a focus on coding datasets (benign, disguised, malicious, and their 'raw' variants) and factual updates (e.g., post-context). The fine-tuned models will then be evaluated on the 'BaselineQA' proxy dataset, which consists of simple, unrelated factual questions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["A systematic LLM-judge analysis will quantify behavioral signatures such as 'code bleeding' (responding with code to factual questions), harmful content generation, misinformation ('revisionist' habit), response length changes, language switching, and response corruption."]

risk_factors: Notably, these findings establish a behavioral parallel with what is itself a behavioral theory: latent cause accounts operate at the computational level without established neural mechanisms.
Understanding why contextualization protects, in both systems, remains open for mechanistic interpretability work.
Because some predictions demonstrate misalignment and may contain harmful or offensive content, we mitigate risk by adding clear content warnings with do-not-train mentions.

title: SNAPHARD CONTRAST LEARNING
name: spacl-hard-sample-contrastive-learning
short_hypothesis: Strategically prioritizing hard positive and hard negative samples during the construction of contrastive pairs and the computation of the contrastive loss will lead to more discriminative representations and improved model performance. This is because hard samples critically shape the optimization landscape, while easy samples can act as fixation points that limit variability and may lead to solution collapse.
related_work: Prior work in Contrastive Learning (CL) has explored various strategies for generating and sampling contrastive pairs. These methods fall into two main categories: generation of pairs (e.g., minibatch adaptation like MoCo/SimCLR, data augmentation) and strategic sampling of pairs (e.g., hard negative mining). However, the theoretical understanding of pair sampling remains under-explored. Furthermore, most existing research focuses on sampling either hard positives or hard negatives, with few methods considering the concurrent selection of both, leaving a gap in understanding their joint contribution and the conditions for optimality.
abstract: A fundamental aspect of Contrastive Learning (CL) is aligning the representations of anchor instances with relevant positive samples while simultaneously separating them from negative ones. Prior studies have extensively explored diverse strategies for generating and sampling contrastive pairs. Despite empirical success, the theoretical understanding of the CL approach remains under-explored, leaving questions such as the rationale behind contrastive-pair sampling and its contributions to model performance unclear. This paper addresses this gap by providing a comprehensive theoretical analysis from the angle of optimality conditions and introducing the SnaPhArd Contrast Learning (SPACL). Specifically, SPACL proposes to prioritize hard positive and hard negative samples during the construction of contrastive pairs and the computation of the contrastive loss, rather than treating all samples equally.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the SPACL algorithm on image classification tasks under fully-supervised, self-supervised, and weakly-supervised learning paradigms.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, and ImageNet-1K datasets using a ResNet-50 backbone. SPACL will be compared against a suite of baselines for each paradigm. Supervised baselines include Cross-Entropy, SupCon, and VarCon. Self-supervised baselines include SimCLR, MoCo V2, BYOL, SwAV, VicReg, and Barlow Twins. Weakly-supervised baselines include Grafit, CoIns, and MaskCon.

experiments.Description: To evaluate SPACL on a multimodal Knowledge Graph (KG) completion task (link prediction), where entities are associated with both visual and textual modalities.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on the WN9, FB15K-237, and FB15K benchmarks. SPACL will be compared against unimodal and multimodal KG embedding baselines, including RESCAL, DistMult, TransE, RotatE, ComplEx, IKRL, TransAE, MOSE, LMKGE, and LMKGE12.

experiments.Description: To evaluate SPACL in a purely text-domain setting on the task of detecting out-of-domain (OOD) intent samples.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the BANKING and StackOverflow benchmarks. A BERT-base-uncased model will be adopted as the base encoder. SPACL will be compared against recent OOD detection baselines, MOGB and TBOS.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['F1-OOD (F1-score for OOD classes)', 'F1-IND (F1-score for in-domain classes)']

risk_factors: Yet, we note that explicitly identifying or eliminating false negatives is beyond the scope of this work, and we leave this as future work.
Future research could explore different strategies for measuring sample difficulty and exploring alternative perturbation methods.
Additionally, extending our analysis to negative-free CL could further illustrate its adaptability and broader its applicability.

title: Towards Better Generalization in Lifelong Person Re-Identification with Flatness-Aware Learning
name: flatness-aware-dual-model-fusion-for-lreid
short_hypothesis: In lifelong person re-identification (LReID), maintaining two separate models—a 'stability' model and a 'plasticity' model—and selectively applying Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) only to the distillation loss of the stability model will guide it towards a flat, robust solution. Fusing these two models via weight-space interpolation after each task will produce a single model that better balances knowledge retention and adaptation to new domains.
related_work: Most existing lifelong person re-identification (LReID) methods use regularization-based strategies like knowledge distillation to prevent catastrophic forgetting. These methods typically constrain a new model to align with an old one, but they do not explicitly guide the optimization toward more stable or robust regions of the loss landscape. This can lead to convergence in sharp minima, where small parameter updates cause large changes in predictions, hindering knowledge consolidation. While flatness-aware methods like Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) have been explored in continual learning, no prior work has investigated selectively applying SAM to only the distillation loss within a dual-model framework to robustly preserve previous knowledge in LReID.
abstract: Lifelong person re-identification (LReID) requires models to continuously learn from sequentially arriving domains while retaining discriminative power for previously seen identities. A key challenge is to prevent catastrophic forgetting without access to old data, especially under exemplar-free constraints. In this paper, we propose a novel LReID method that unifies selective flatness-aware optimization, dual-model training, and model interpolation. Specifically, we maintain two separate models per task: a stability model trained with the distillation loss to retain the prior knowledge, and a plasticity model optimized solely for the current domain. To improve the performance of generalization and retention, we selectively apply Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) only to the distillation loss, guiding the stability model toward flat and robust solutions. After task-specific training, these two models are fused through weight-space interpolation, producing a single model that balances stability and adaptability. The resulting model is used to initialize both branches for the next task, enabling continual knowledge integration.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method as an effective plug-in module by integrating it into existing state-of-the-art LReID frameworks under different sequential domain training orders.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be integrated into six exemplar-free LReID frameworks: AKA, PatchKD, LSTKC, DKP, DASK, and LSTKC++. Performance will be assessed under two training orders. Order 1: Market1501 → CUHK-SYSU → MSMT17 → CUHK03. Order 2: MSMT17 → Market1501 → CUHK-SYSU → CUHK03. The backbone is a ResNet50. Evaluation will be on the seen training datasets and six unseen datasets: VIPER, PRID, GRID, iLIDS, CUHK01, and CUHK02.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cumulative Matching Characteristic (CMC) at Rank-1', 'mean Average Precision (mAP)']

experiments.Description: To ablate the effectiveness of the proposed dual-branch structure by evaluating the performance of each branch individually versus the combined model.
experiments.Method: The experiment compares four model variants under training Order 1: 1) A standard LReID baseline with ReID and distillation losses. 2) 'Model1', which uses only the proposed stability branch. 3) 'Model2', which uses only the proposed plasticity branch. 4) 'Model1+2 (Ours)', the full proposed method combining both branches.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cumulative Matching Characteristic (CMC) at Rank-1', 'mean Average Precision (mAP)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of applying Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) to different loss components within the stability branch to justify the selective application to the distillation loss.
experiments.Method: Within the dual-model framework, the plasticity branch is fixed (applying SAM to L_ReID). The stability branch is modified to apply SAM to three different loss configurations: 1) only the ReID loss (L_ReID), 2) only the distillation loss (L_KD), and 3) the sum of both losses (L_ReID + L_KD).

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of the interpolation weight λ, which is used for the linear fusion of the stability and plasticity models.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained with different values for the interpolation weight λ (0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9) to observe the trade-off between knowledge preservation and adaptability.

risk_factors: Note that the key challenge in LReID is not maximizing performance on the oldest domains, but reducing catastrophic forgetting while maintaining generalization across continually arriving domains.
In Table 1 and Table 3, the fused model may exhibit slightly lower performance on older datasets, even when the Seen-Avg and Unseen-Avg scores improve.

This behavior reflects the stability-plasticity trade-off: applying SAM on L_KD for the stability branch and on L_ReID for the plasticity branch allocates capacity to both retention and adaptation, yielding higher average scores and stronger generalization performance as training proceeds.

title: Revisiting Over-smoothing in Graph Neural Networks
name: dsgnn-for-oversmoothing
short_hypothesis: Inspired by deeply supervised nets, a general architecture for GNNs based on layer-wise supervision, termed Deeply Supervised GNNs (DSGNNs), will be resilient to the over-smoothing problem that affects deep GNNs, by encouraging the estimation of discriminative features at all network layers.
related_work: Deep GNNs suffer from over-smoothing, where performance degrades as layers are added. Many methods have been proposed to address this, including residual connections (JKNets), normalization (PairNorm, GroupNorm), and architectural changes (GCNII). However, these solutions have not been systematically evaluated under a common experimental framework, their combinations have not been studied, and approaches from standard deep learning like deep supervision have not been investigated for this problem.
abstract: Shallow graph neural networks (GNNs) are state-of-the-art, but deep GNNs suffer from over-smoothing, where node representations become indistinguishable and performance degrades. Despite multiple proposed solutions, it is unclear which methods work best or how they perform under a unified experimental setting. This paper plans to systematically evaluate different methods for alleviating over-smoothing in GNNs. Furthermore, inspired by standard deeply supervised nets, we propose a general architecture, Deeply Supervised GNNs (DSGNNs), that deals with over-smoothing based on layer-wise supervision.

experiments.Description: To systematically evaluate the performance of the proposed DSGNN architecture and other methods for alleviating over-smoothing in a standard semi-supervised node classification task where all node features are available during training (the fully observed setting).
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, and Amazon Photo benchmark datasets. The proposed DSGNN model will be compared against GCN, JKNet, and GCNII as baselines. The evaluation will also include combinations of these architectures with Pair Normalization (+PN) and Group Normalization (+GN). Architectures will be tested with depths from the set {2, 3, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64}.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed DSGNN architecture and other methods in a more challenging semi-supervised node classification scenario where node features are only known for the training nodes (the missing features setting).
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, and Amazon Photo datasets. In this setting, node attributes for validation and test nodes are set to zero vectors. The proposed DSGNN model will be compared against GCN, JKNet, and GCNII as baselines, as well as their combinations with Pair Normalization (+PN) and Group Normalization (+GN). Architectures will be tested with depths from the set {2, 3, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64}.

risk_factors: Unavoidably, there are other techniques for dealing with over-smoothing in GNNs that we do not investigate here. Of notable mention is the work of Rong et al. (2020), who proposed DropEdge as a general heuristic for alleviating over-smoothing by modifying the message passing mechanism in GNNs.

title: Position-Aware Attention Mechanism: A Mathematical Framework for Enhanced Spatial Information Processing in Transformer Architectures
name: epar-position-aware-attention
short_hypothesis: By defining an explicit, parametric mathematical function (the 'position effect function') that directly modulates attention scores based on positional distance, it is possible to achieve more analyzable, interpretable, and fine-grained control over positional information in Transformers. This approach can be further improved by introducing an enhancement coefficient to prevent information loss at long distances and by developing an adaptive triple-attention architecture that incorporates task-aware and content-aware modules for dynamic weight adjustment.
related_work: Existing position encoding methods like RoPE, ALiBi, and relative position encoding operate at the vector representation level, creating implicit relationships between position and attention that are difficult to analyze mathematically. This 'mathematical opacity' prevents theoretical understanding, derivation of optimal strategies, and fine-grained control. Furthermore, these methods can suffer from information loss at long distances due to exponential decay in attention weights. The proposed research shifts the focus from 'how to encode position information' to 'how position affects attention strength' via an explicit mathematical framework.
abstract: We propose a position-aware attention mechanism based on the Explicit Position-Attention Relationship (EPAR) framework that addresses the limitations of traditional attention mechanisms in capturing positional relationships through a parametric positional effect function. The EPAR framework establishes explicit mathematical relationships between positional distance and attention intensity using three key parameters: α (position influence intensity), β (spatial decay rate), and γ (enhancement coefficient for long-range dependencies). We prove mathematical properties (continuity, differentiability, monotonicity) and demonstrate fine-grained control over attention allocation. To address over-attenuation at long distances, we introduce enhancement coefficient γ, ensuring a non-zero lower bound for attention weights. We develop an adaptive triple-attention architecture with task-aware and content-aware modules for dynamic weight adjustment. Our method includes a maximum benefit position formula and consistency metric for evaluation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed position-aware attention mechanism on language modeling tasks.
experiments.Method: The method will be implemented in a standard 12-layer Transformer and evaluated on the WikiText-103 and Penn Treebank datasets. It will be compared against baselines including Standard Attention, RoPE, ALiBi, Relative PE, and Transformer-XL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed position-aware attention mechanism on a machine translation task.
experiments.Method: The method will be implemented in a standard 12-layer Transformer and evaluated on the WMT'14 En-De dataset. It will be compared against baselines including Standard Attention, RoPE, ALiBi, Relative PE, and Transformer-XL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed position-aware attention mechanism on a question answering task.
experiments.Method: The method will be implemented in a standard 12-layer Transformer and evaluated on the SQuAD 2.0 dataset. It will be compared against baselines including Standard Attention, RoPE, ALiBi, Relative PE, and Transformer-XL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the general applicability of the proposed position-aware attention mechanism on a diverse set of NLP classification tasks.
experiments.Method: The method will be implemented in a standard 12-layer Transformer and evaluated on the GLUE benchmark. It will be compared against baselines including Standard Attention, RoPE, ALiBi, Relative PE, and Transformer-XL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed position-aware attention mechanism on long-document understanding tasks.
experiments.Method: The method will be implemented in a standard 12-layer Transformer and evaluated on the ArXiv Papers dataset. It will be compared against baselines including Standard Attention, RoPE, ALiBi, Relative PE, and Transformer-XL.

experiments.Description: To analyze the mechanism's performance and robustness across different synthetic information distribution patterns.
experiments.Method: The position-aware attention mechanism will be evaluated on five information distribution patterns: structured, clustered, random, sparse, and dense.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the contribution of each component in the triple-attention architecture (position-aware, task-aware, content-aware) through ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Ablation experiments will be run by removing each of the three modules (task-aware, content-aware, position-aware) individually from the full triple-attention architecture and comparing performance against the full model and a base attention model across various tasks (Translation, Classification, QA).

risk_factors: Optimal parameters (α = 1.0, β = 1.0, γ = 0.5) may require task-specific tuning.
Triple-attention architecture introduces 2.4% training and 4.5% inference overhead.
Method performs best on structured and clustered patterns.
Sequences beyond 2048 tokens show diminishing returns.

Our method underperforms in: (1) highly noisy data where positional relationships may be over-emphasized; (2) non-sequential tasks where position is not meaningful; (3) extreme parameter values (α > 5.0 or β > 5.0) leading to performance degradation.

title: Is self-supervision enough for training sentence embeddings?
name: ssl-text-crops-for-sentence-embeddings
short_hypothesis: Self-supervised fine-tuning, even with a minimal amount of data, can significantly improve sentence embedding quality to near state-of-the-art supervised levels. Specifically, using 'text crops' as positive pairs for contrastive learning is hypothesized to be a more effective augmentation strategy than the commonly used dropout-based method.
related_work: Current state-of-the-art sentence embeddings are typically derived from large language models that undergo extensive supervised fine-tuning on large, curated datasets (e.g., SBERT, SPECTER). This contrasts with computer vision, where self-supervised learning (SSL) has been immensely successful. While various SSL approaches have been proposed for sentence embeddings (e.g., SimCSE, DeCLUTR), their performance relative to each other and to supervised SOTA models is not well understood. This work addresses the question of whether self-supervision is sufficient for training high-quality sentence embeddings.
abstract: In NLP, sentence embeddings are crucial for tasks like information retrieval and clustering. Currently, top-performing embeddings come from pre-trained models that undergo extensive supervised fine-tuning. This contrasts with computer vision, where self-supervised training has shown remarkable success. This paper investigates whether self-supervision alone can produce high-quality sentence embeddings. We will systematically compare existing augmentation strategies for positive pair generation in contrastive learning, hypothesizing that text crops will outperform popular dropout-based augmentation. We will also explore if well-performing embeddings can be obtained by training from scratch or with simpler, non-transformer architectures. The overall goal is to determine if self-supervised learning allows for the rapid training of text embeddings for a given dataset.

experiments.Description: To compare the effectiveness of different self-supervised augmentation strategies for fine-tuning a pre-trained language model.
experiments.Method: A pre-trained MPNet model will be fine-tuned using a contrastive learning framework (InfoNCE loss). The experiment will compare two primary augmentation strategies for generating positive pairs: 'text crops' (using consecutive sentences) and 'dropout-based' augmentation (SimCSE-style). The fine-tuning will be performed on six datasets: arXiv, bioRxiv, medRxiv, Reddit, StackExchange, and a new ICLR dataset. The performance will be compared against several off-the-shelf baselines, including the vanilla MPNet, SimCSE, SPECTER, SciNCL, and SBERT.

experiments.Description: To determine if pre-training or a full transformer architecture is necessary to obtain high-quality sentence embeddings with self-supervised learning.
experiments.Method: Two models will be trained from scratch using the text-cropping contrastive learning approach on the same six datasets. The first model will be a `bert-base` architecture with randomly initialized weights. The second model will be a bare, randomly initialized token embedding layer without any transformer architecture. Their performance will be compared to the fine-tuned pre-trained MPNet.

experiments.Description: To disentangle the performance improvement from generic sentence-level adaptation versus domain-specific adaptation.
experiments.Method: The MPNet model will be fine-tuned using self-supervision on datasets from one domain (four different subsets of PubMed abstracts) and then its performance will be assessed on a dataset from a different domain (ICLR dataset).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) classification accuracy on the out-of-domain dataset']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of the self-supervised fine-tuned models on a diverse set of downstream tasks.
experiments.Method: Models fine-tuned on the ICLR dataset using the cropping augmentation will be evaluated on several tasks from the Massive Text Embedding Benchmark (MTEB). The selected tasks include clustering, retrieval, reranking, and Semantic Textual Similarity (STS). Performance will be compared against baselines like SBERT, MPNet, and SimCSE.

experiments.Description: To investigate how representation quality changes across the layers of the transformer model during self-supervised fine-tuning and to determine which layers are most affected.
experiments.Method: The MPNet model will be fine-tuned on the ICLR dataset using cropping augmentation under two conditions: 1) with a varying number of initial layers frozen to see how many layers need to be adapted, and 2) with all layers unfrozen to measure the representation quality after each hidden layer, both before and after fine-tuning.

risk_factors: Whether this gap [between our self-supervised fine-tuning and SOTA models like SBERT] is due to the supervised signal or rather to the large amounts of data that SOTA supervised models are trained on, is a topic for future work.
This unsurprisingly shows that the token embedding model did not generalize well outside of its training domain.

title: Multimodal Representation Learning Conditioned on Semantic Relations
name: relation-conditioned-multimodal-learning
short_hypothesis: By conditioning multimodal representation learning on natural-language descriptions of semantic relations between samples, it is possible to learn more contextually grounded and relation-aware embeddings. This can be achieved by constructing many-to-many training pairs linked by these relations, using a relation-guided attention mechanism to modulate features, and applying a joint contrastive objective for both inter-modal and intra-modal consistency.
related_work: Current multimodal contrastive learning models like CLIP typically focus on aligning individual image-text pairs, underutilizing the rich web of semantic relations that exist across different samples. While some methods incorporate label supervision (UniCL) or data augmentation (DeCLIP), they do not explicitly model inter-sample relations. Graph-based approaches can capture structural relationships but often focus on graph-level embeddings or rely on GNN message passing. Prompt-based methods (e.g., CoOp, MaPLe) primarily condition on intra-sample or class contexts, not on the diverse, many-to-many semantic relations between distinct samples. This leaves a gap in learning multimodal representations that are aware of and grounded in specific relational contexts.
abstract: Multimodal representation learning has advanced rapidly with contrastive models such as CLIP, which align image-text pairs in a shared embedding space. However, these models face limitations: (1) they typically focus on image-text pairs, underutilizing the semantic relations across different pairs. (2) they directly match global embeddings without contextualization, overlooking the need for semantic alignment along specific subspaces or relational dimensions. To address these issues, we propose Relation-Conditioned Multimodal Learning (RCML), a framework that learns multimodal representations under natural-language relation descriptions to guide both feature extraction and alignment. Our approach constructs many-to-many training pairs linked by semantic relations and introduces a relation-guided attention mechanism that modulates multimodal representations under each relation context. The training objective combines inter-modal and intra-modal contrastive losses, encouraging consistency across both modalities and semantically related samples.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform relation-aware retrieval in a zero-shot recommendation-style scenario. Given a source product and a semantic relation, the task is to retrieve the most relevant target product from a candidate set.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Amazon Product dataset (across Electronics, Automotive, Office Products, Baby, Pet Supplies, Musical Instruments, and Sports domains) and the Goodreads dataset. For each query, a candidate set of 1 positive and 20 negative samples will be used. The proposed RCML model will be compared against several baselines: CLIP, DeCLIP, UniCL, SigLIP, and ImageBind. For baselines, the relation text will be concatenated with the source product's text.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's zero-shot capability to identify the correct semantic relation that connects a given pair of products from a predefined set of possible relations.
experiments.Method: Given a product pair (A, B), the model must select the most likely relation from a set of 10 candidates for Amazon domains and 8 for Goodreads. This task is specific to the proposed RCML model, as baseline models do not support relation-specific encoding.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the learned multimodal representations can support supervised relation-aware reasoning. This is a binary classification task where the model must predict if a specific, given relation type exists between a pair of products.
experiments.Method: A lightweight linear classifier will be trained on top of the frozen features extracted by the models. The input to the classifier will be the concatenation of text and image embeddings from both products, along with the embedding of the relation label. The experiment will use the Amazon Product and Goodreads datasets. RCML will be compared against frozen CLIP, DeCLIP, UniCL, SigLIP, and ImageBind models.

title: Pyraformer: Low-Complexity Pyramidal Attention for Long-Range Time Series Modeling and Forecasting
name: pyramidal-attention-forecasting
short_hypothesis: A novel pyramidal attention based Transformer, Pyraformer, can bridge the gap between capturing long-range dependencies and achieving low time and space complexity. This is achieved using a Pyramidal Attention Module (PAM) that passes messages on a pyramidal graph with inter-scale (multi-resolution tree) and intra-scale (neighboring) connections. By appropriately choosing parameters, the model can theoretically achieve a maximum path length of O(1) and a time and space complexity of O(L).
related_work: Existing methods for time series forecasting face a trade-off. RNNs and CNNs have low, linear complexity (O(L)) but a long signal traversing path (O(L)), making it difficult to capture long-range dependencies. Conversely, the standard Transformer has a short path length (O(1)) but suffers from quadratic time and space complexity (O(L^2)), making it unsuitable for long sequences. Various sparse Transformer variants have been proposed as a compromise (e.g., Longformer, Reformer, Informer), but they often fail to achieve both a path length less than O(L) and significantly reduced complexity simultaneously. The research gap is the need for a model that can concurrently achieve O(1) path length and O(L) complexity for long-range time series forecasting.
abstract: Accurate prediction of the future given the past based on time series data is of paramount importance, since it opens the door for decision making and risk management ahead of time. In practice, the challenge is to build a flexible but parsimonious model that can capture a wide range of temporal dependencies. In this paper, we propose Pyraformer by exploring the multi-resolution representation of the time series. Specifically, we introduce the pyramidal attention module (PAM) in which the inter-scale tree structure summarizes features at different resolutions and the intra-scale neighboring connections model the temporal dependencies of different ranges. Under mild conditions, the maximum length of the signal traversing path in Pyraformer is a constant (i.e., O(1)) with regard to the sequence length L, while its time and space complexity scale linearly with L.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of Pyraformer on single-step time series forecasting tasks.
experiments.Method: Pyraformer will be benchmarked against 5 other attention mechanisms: original full-attention, LogTrans (log-sparse attention), Reformer (LSH attention), ETC (sliding window with global nodes), and Longformer (dilated sliding window). The models will be trained and tested on the Electricity, Wind, and App Flow datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of Pyraformer for long-range, multi-step forecasting on several real-world datasets.
experiments.Method: Pyraformer, using two different proposed prediction modules, will be evaluated on the Electricity, ETTh1, and ETTm1 datasets. It will be compared against baselines including Informer, LogTrans, Longformer, Reformer, and ETC.

experiments.Description: To empirically measure the computation time and memory consumption of the proposed pyramidal attention mechanism.
experiments.Method: The empirical computation time and memory cost will be depicted as a function of the sequence length L. The proposed Pyraformer with a customized CUDA kernel implemented via TVM will be compared against the full attention mechanism and the prob-sparse attention from Informer.

experiments.Description: To perform ablation studies to measure the impact of key hyperparameters and architectural components on prediction accuracy.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies will be conducted on the ETTh1 and ETTm1 datasets for long-range forecasting. The studies will investigate the impact of: 1) the number of adjacent intra-scale connections (A) and the number of children in the C-ary tree (C); 2) the Coarser-Scale Construction Module (CSCM) architecture (convolution vs. pooling, with/without bottleneck); 3) the historical input length; and 4) the Pyramidal Attention Module (PAM) itself by comparing Pyraformer with a model using only the CSCM.

experiments.Description: To evaluate Pyraformer's ability to capture temporal dependencies of different ranges on synthetic data with known properties.
experiments.Method: A synthetic hourly dataset with multi-range dependencies (periods of 24, 168, and 720) will be generated. Pyraformer, in two different configurations, will be compared against Full attention, LogTrans, ETC, Informer, Longformer, and Reformer on a long-range forecasting task (predict 720 steps).

risk_factors: Unfortunately, such a sparse attention mechanism is not supported in the existing deep learning libraries, such as Pytorch and TensorFlow.
So far we only concentrate on the scenario where A and S are fixed and C increases with L when constructing the pyramidal graph.
In the future work, we would like to explore how to adaptively learn the hyper-parameters from the data.

title: DESIGN: Encrypted GNN Inference via Server-Side Input Graph Pruning
name: fhe-gnn-input-pruning
short_hypothesis: A server-side framework for encrypted GNN inference, called DESIGN, can reduce computational cost by first computing FHE-compatible importance scores (based on node degree) directly on the encrypted graph. Then, using approximate homomorphic comparisons, it can generate multi-level importance masks. These masks will then enable a dual-pruning scheme: (1) logical pruning of unimportant nodes and edges to reduce data volume, and (2) adaptive allocation of polynomial activation functions of varying degrees, assigning higher-degree polynomials to critical nodes and lower-degree ones to less important nodes, thereby optimizing the accuracy-cost balance.
related_work: Prior research on efficient privacy-preserving GNN inference has focused on optimizing the GNN model's internal structure, such as adjusting architectures or using polynomial approximations for activations. These model-centric approaches typically overlook the redundancy in the input graph data itself. While statistical pruning methods exist for plaintext environments, they are underexplored in encrypted domains and often unsuitable for server-side FHE applications due to reliance on plaintext access or prohibitively expensive homomorphic comparisons. The key challenges are the impracticality of performing dynamic pruning on encrypted data, the inefficiency of using a uniform polynomial degree for all nodes in activation approximations, and ensuring the efficiency framework is generalizable across different GNN architectures.
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) enable powerful graph learning, yet Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) makes inference prohibitively expensive. We present DESIGN (EncrypteD GNN Inference via sErver-Side Input Graph pruNing), a server-side framework that reduces FHE cost without client changes. DESIGN computes encrypted degree-based importance scores and uses homomorphic comparisons to produce multi-level masks, which drive two optimizations: logical pruning of low-importance nodes and edges, and importance-aware assignment of low-degree polynomial activations to most nodes while reserving higher degrees for critical ones.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the DESIGN framework in reducing end-to-end FHE GNN inference latency and its impact on inference accuracy compared to foundational and state-of-the-art baselines.
experiments.Method: The framework will be evaluated on the node classification task using a two-layer GCN architecture. The experiments will be run on five benchmark datasets: Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Yelp, and ogbn-proteins. The performance will be compared against baselines including: (1) Foundational FHE GNN implementations (Microsoft SEAL, OpenFHE) and (2) State-of-the-Art FHE GNN frameworks (CryptoGCN, LinGCN, Penguin).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Inference Accuracy (node classification accuracy)', 'Inference Latency (end-to-end wall-clock time and speedup factors)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to dissect the individual contributions of the framework's core components: the FHE-compatible statistical pruning stage and the adaptive polynomial activation allocation.
experiments.Method: The full framework (DESIGN) will be compared against carefully constructed variants: DESIGN without Pruning (Adaptive Activation Only), DESIGN without Adaptive Activation (Pruning Only), and a Baseline FHE GNN. This analysis will be performed on the benchmark datasets.

experiments.Description: To investigate the sensitivity of the framework's performance to its key hyperparameters, specifically the pruning thresholds and the degrees of the polynomial activation functions.
experiments.Method: The impact of varying the pruning ratio (percentage of nodes removed) and the degrees of polynomial activation functions allocated to different importance levels will be analyzed on a representative subset of the benchmark datasets (Cora, Yelp, PubMed).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the task generality of the proposed framework beyond node classification by applying it to a link prediction task.
experiments.Method: A two-layer GCN decoder for link prediction will be evaluated under the same cryptographic settings on the Cora-LP, PubMed-LP, and Yelp-LP datasets.

risk_factors: the homomorphic comparison stage, essential for partitioning, remains computationally intensive and introduces approximation errors, particularly for large graphs.
the primary reliance on node degree for importance scoring, while FHE-efficient, might not comprehensively capture all task-specific nuances across diverse graph structures.

title: Monotone deep Boltzmann machines
name: Monotone Deep Boltzmann Machines
short_hypothesis: A specific parameterization of the pairwise potentials in a general Deep Boltzmann Machine (DBM), inspired by monotone Deep Equilibrium (DEQ) models, will guarantee that the mean-field approximation has a unique, globally-optimal fixed point. This will enable an efficient, parallel mean-field inference procedure with guaranteed global convergence, overcoming a major limitation of general DBMs.
related_work: General Deep Boltzmann Machines (DBMs) are powerful probabilistic models, but exact inference is intractable. This has led to the widespread use of restricted Boltzmann machines (RBMs), which forbid intra-layer connections to simplify inference. Previous attempts to parallelize mean-field updates for general DBMs either required strong constraints on the model (e.g., concave potentials) or lacked guarantees of converging to the true mean-field fixed point, often getting stuck in local optima. This work proposes to bridge this gap by leveraging the framework of monotone DEQ models, which guarantee convergence to a unique fixed point for certain classes of implicit-depth networks.
abstract: Deep Boltzmann machines refer to deep multi-layered probabilistic models, governed by a pairwise energy function that describes the likelihood of all variables in the network. Due to the difficulty of inference in such systems, they have given way largely to restricted deep Boltzmann machines (which do not permit intra-layer or skip connections). In this paper, we propose a class of model that allows for exact, efficient mean-field inference and learning in general deep Boltzmann machines. To do so, we use the tools of the recently proposed monotone Deep Equilibrium (DEQ) Model, an implicit-depth deep network that always guarantees the existence and uniqueness of its fixed points. We show that, for a class of general deep Boltzmann machine, the mean-field fixed point can be considered as the equivalent fixed point of a monotone DEQ, which gives us a recipe for deriving an efficient mean-field inference procedure with global convergence guarantees. We apply this approach to simple deep convolutional Boltzmann architectures for tasks such as the joint completion and classification of images, all within a single deep probabilistic setting.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform joint inference and learning for image imputation and classification on the MNIST dataset.
experiments.Method: The model will be a deep convolutional monotone Boltzmann machine. The MNIST dataset will be used, with pixel intensities binned into 4 discrete categories. The model will be trained on the joint task of classifying the digit and completing missing pixels. Two missing data scenarios will be tested: 1) 60% of pixels are randomly masked, and 2) a 14x14 patch is masked. The model's performance will be compared to a standard 3-layer DBM.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test accuracy for classification', 'Visual quality of the imputed images']

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed inference method against prior parallel mean-field update methods in terms of convergence and performance.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on MNIST for joint imputation and classification using three different update rules: the proposed method, the method from Krähenbühl & Koltun (2013), and the method from Baqué et al. (2016). Inference will then be performed on each trained model using all three update methods. This comparison will be conducted both with and without the proposed monotonicity constraint enforced.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Convergence speed', 'Relative update residual after halting', 'Total Variation (TV) distance between the computed distributions from different methods', 'Classification accuracy', 'Visual imputation quality']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the potential for scaling the approach to the more complex CIFAR-10 dataset.
experiments.Method: A deep convolutional monotone Boltzmann machine will be trained on CIFAR-10 for the simultaneous tasks of image pixel imputation and label prediction. The model will be given images with 50% of the pixels observed.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test accuracy', 'Visual quality of the imputed pixels']

risk_factors: First, the monotone parameterization in theorem 3.1 is not an if-and-only-if statement, hence potentially making our model more restrictive than necessary.
The second direction is that although we have a fairly efficient implementation of prox_f^α, it is still slower than normal nonlinearities like ReLU or softmax.
It is an interesting direction to more efficiently scale these models.

title: Beyond Masks: Efficient, Flexible Diffusion Language Models via Deletion-Insertion Processes
name: deletion-insertion-diffusion-models
short_hypothesis: Replacing the masking/unmasking paradigm of Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) with a rigorously formulated deletion-insertion diffusion process (DID) will improve computational efficiency by eliminating `<MASK>` and `<PAD>` tokens, and increase generation flexibility by natively supporting variable-length sequences and enabling self-correction.
related_work: Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) are a powerful paradigm for language modeling but are constrained by their reliance on a fixed-length masking process. This leads to two main issues: 1) Computational inefficiency, as significant computation is wasted on non-informative `<MASK>` and `<PAD>` tokens, and 2) Restricted generation flexibility, making it difficult to model variable-length sequences or perform self-correction, as unmasked tokens become fixed in content and position. While other insertion-based models exist, they are often heuristic, not true diffusion models, or introduce additional implementation complexity. This work aims to create a diffusion model that is both computationally efficient and flexible for variable-length sequences by moving beyond the masking paradigm.
abstract: While Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) relying on token masking and unmasking have shown promise in language modeling, their computational efficiency and generation flexibility remain constrained by the masking paradigm. In this paper, we propose Deletion-Insertion Diffusion language models (DID) that rigorously formulate token deletion and insertion as discrete diffusion processes, replacing the masking and unmasking processes in current MDLMs. DID is intended to improve training and inference efficiency by eliminating two major sources of computational overhead in MDLMs: the computations on non-informative 1) <MASK> tokens inherent to the paradigm, and 2) <PAD> tokens introduced in variable-length settings. Furthermore, DID is designed to offer greater flexibility by: 1) natively supporting variable-length sequences without requiring fixed-length padding, and 2) an intrinsic self-correction mechanism during generation due to insertion that dynamically adjusts token positions. To train DID, we design a score-based approach that assigns scores to token insertion operations and derive appropriate training objectives. The objectives involve subsequence counting problems, which we propose to solve efficiently via a parallelized dynamic programming algorithm.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the modeling performance, generation quality, and efficiency of DID in a fixed-length setting and compare it against a strong MDLM baseline under both step-aligned and FLOPs-aligned computational budgets.
experiments.Method: DID models (DID-S and DID-F) will be trained on the OpenWebText (OWT) dataset, with sequences split into fixed-length chunks of 1024 tokens. The primary baseline will be the RADD model. The evaluation will be conducted on zero-shot language modeling tasks across seven datasets: WikiText, Lambada, Pubmed, AG News, LM1B, Arxiv, and PTB. Generative performance will also be assessed.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Zero-shot language modeling perplexity', 'Generative perplexity (PPL)', 'Unigram entropy', 'Inference time', 'Speedup']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generation quality, speed, and length modeling capabilities of DID on a variable-length language modeling task, comparing it to both MDLM and other insertion-based baselines.
experiments.Method: A small-sized DID model will be trained on the variable-length Stories dataset. It will be compared against a reproduced ILM model and a RADD model. For the RADD baseline, sequences will be padded to a fixed length of 1024.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Generative PPL', 'Unigram entropy', 'Inference time', 'Speedup', 'Average generation length', 'Cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of generation length']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the scalability of the DID model to a larger size (1.1B parameters) and its performance on downstream commonsense reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: A 1.1B parameter DID model will be trained on the SlimPajama dataset. Its performance will be compared against a 1.1B SMDM baseline on a suite of commonsense reasoning benchmarks (Arc-c, Arc-e, Hellaswag, Obqa, Piqa, Race, Siqa, Winogrande) using the LM-Evaluation-Harness framework.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the scaled-up 1.1B DID model on the GSM8K math word reasoning benchmark after fine-tuning.
experiments.Method: The 1.1B DID model, pretrained on SlimPajama, will be fine-tuned on an augmented training dataset for math reasoning. It will be compared against a similarly fine-tuned 1.1B SMDM model. Evaluation will be performed on the GSM8K benchmark under various inference settings (e.g., different decoding steps and top-p sampling strategies).

risk_factors: This work presents the core framework of DID and, unlike the more established MDLMs, has not yet integrated many optimizations, such as advanced inference algorithms (Wu et al., 2025a; Wang et al., 2025), or hybrid models combining autoregressive approaches (Arriola et al., 2025; Sahoo et al., 2025).

Second, although we have demonstrated the effectiveness, efficiency, and flexibility of DID, our models were trained at a relatively small scale due to resource constraints. As a result, their performance on larger and more complex tasks remains unexplored, and we leave scaling up DID to future work.

title: Continuous PDE Dynamics Forecasting with Implicit Neural Representations
name: continuous-pde-forecasting-with-inr
short_hypothesis: A new data-driven approach, DINo, can model a PDE's flow with continuous-time dynamics of spatially continuous functions by embedding spatial observations via Implicit Neural Representations in a latent space that is temporally driven by a learned ODE.
related_work: Effective data-driven PDE forecasting methods often rely on fixed spatial and / or temporal discretizations. This raises limitations in real-world applications like weather prediction where flexible extrapolation at arbitrary spatiotemporal locations is required.
abstract: Effective data-driven PDE forecasting methods often rely on fixed spatial and / or temporal discretizations, which limits their use in applications requiring flexible extrapolation at arbitrary spatiotemporal locations. We propose DINo, a new data-driven approach that models a PDE's flow with continuous-time dynamics of spatially continuous functions. This is achieved by embedding spatial observations independently of their discretization via Implicit Neural Representations in a small latent space temporally driven by a learned ODE. This separate and flexible treatment of time and space is intended to allow the model to extrapolate at arbitrary spatial and temporal locations, learn from sparse irregular grids, and generalize to new grids or resolutions at test time.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance in challenging generalization scenarios, specifically its ability to extrapolate at arbitrary spatial and temporal locations, learn from sparse/irregular grids, and generalize to new grids or resolutions at test time.
experiments.Method: The proposed model, DINo, will be compared against alternative neural PDE forecasters on representative PDE systems.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Forecasting performance in generalization scenarios. Specific metrics are not mentioned in the text.']

title: VeRA: Vector-based Random Matrix Adaptation
name: VeRA
short_hypothesis: By reparameterizing the low-rank adaptation matrices (as in LoRA) into a single pair of shared, frozen, randomly initialized matrices and learning only small, layer-specific scaling vectors, it is possible to significantly reduce the number of trainable parameters for finetuning large models while maintaining comparable performance.
related_work: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is a popular parameter-efficient finetuning method, but it still introduces a considerable number of trainable parameters, posing storage challenges when deploying many adapted models. Parallel research has shown the effectiveness of models using random weights and projections, and other work has pointed out the low intrinsic dimensionality of pretrained models' features. This suggests that the parameter count in methods like LoRA could be further reduced. VeRA proposes to bridge this gap by combining the ideas of random projections with a LoRA-like structure.
abstract: Low-rank adapation (LoRA) is a popular method that reduces the number of trainable parameters when finetuning large language models, but still faces acute storage challenges when scaling to even larger models or deploying numerous per-user or per-task adapted models. In this work, we present Vector-based Random Matrix Adaptation (VeRA), which proposes to significantly reduce the number of trainable parameters compared to LoRA. It aims to achieve this by using a single pair of low-rank matrices shared across all layers and learning small scaling vectors instead. The plan is to demonstrate its effectiveness on the GLUE and E2E benchmarks, image classification tasks, and show its application in instruction-tuning of 7B and 13B language models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of VeRA on natural language understanding tasks against LoRA and other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.
experiments.Method: Finetune RoBERTa-base and RoBERTa-large models on the GLUE benchmark (omitting MNLI and QQP tasks). The proposed VeRA method will be applied to the query and value projection matrices. Baselines for comparison will include Full finetuning, Bitfit, Adapter tuning (Adapter, AdapterH, AdapterD), and LoRA.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Matthew's correlation for CoLA", 'Pearson correlation for STS-B', 'Accuracy for the remaining tasks']

experiments.Description: To evaluate VeRA on natural language generation tasks.
experiments.Method: Finetune GPT-2 Medium and Large models on the E2E benchmark. The experimental setup will follow that of prior work (Hu et al., 2022) to compare VeRA against LoRA and other adapter methods.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the efficacy of VeRA for instruction-tuning of large language models.
experiments.Method: Finetune Llama (7B, 13B) and Llama2 (7B, 13B) models on the cleaned Alpaca dataset using both VeRA and LoRA. The finetuned models will be evaluated on the MT-Bench set of multi-turn questions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['GPT-4 assigned quantitative score (scale of 1-10) on model responses']

experiments.Description: To evaluate VeRA on image classification tasks using Vision Transformers.
experiments.Method: Adapt Vision Transformer (ViT) Base and Large variants on the CIFAR100, Food101, Flowers102, and RESISC45 datasets. VeRA and LoRA will be applied to the query and value layers. Baselines will include a fully-finetuned model (Full) and training only the classification head (Head).

experiments.Description: To investigate the trade-off between the number of trainable parameters and performance for VeRA and LoRA.
experiments.Method: Using the RoBERTa-large model on the RTE task from the GLUE benchmark, the rank `r` will be varied for both VeRA (r = {1, 4, 16, 64, 256, 1024}) and LoRA (r = {1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64}) to observe the relationship between parameter count and accuracy.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to understand the impact of individual components of the VeRA method.
experiments.Method: Using the RoBERTa-large model on the MRPC and RTE tasks, several components will be ablated: 1) The necessity of the `d` and `b` scaling vectors (using only one at a time). 2) The initialization strategy for the shared random matrices (Kaiming uniform vs. Kaiming normal vs. small uniform range). 3) The initialization value for the `d` vector. 4) The effect of sharing random matrices across layers versus using unique matrices per layer.

risk_factors: Due to time constraints and budget limitations, we omit the time-intensive MNLI and QQP tasks, thus forgoing the use of the MNLI trick for tasks MRPC, RTE, and STS-B.

While the current study focuses on language and vision models with Transformer architecture, the applicability of the method across different architectures and domains remains an area for future research.
Moreover, the performance of the method may benefit from additional refinements, such as dynamic parameter budget allocation, or different initialization and regularization techniques.

title: Brain-inspired $L_p$-Convolution benefits large kernels and aligns better with visual cortex
name: lp-convolution
short_hypothesis: Introducing a biological connectivity pattern as a novel inductive bias into a CNN, through a method called Lp-convolution, will resolve the 'large kernel problem' and lead to better alignment with the brain's representations. This is achieved by leveraging the multivariate p-generalized normal distribution (MPND) to create flexible, channel-wise trainable Lp-masks that can adapt their conformation (distortion, scale, rotation) in a task-dependent manner.
related_work: Standard Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) typically use small, stacked kernels (e.g., 3x3) and exhibit rectangular, dense, and uniform connectivity patterns. Simply increasing kernel size often degrades performance, a phenomenon known as the 'large kernel problem'. In contrast, the brain's primary visual cortex (V1) features circular, sparse, and normally distributed (Gaussian-like) connectivity. While modern CNNs have started adopting larger kernels, there is a research gap in effectively integrating biologically-inspired connectivity patterns as an inductive bias to improve large kernel CNNs and bridge the architectural disparity between artificial and biological neural systems.
abstract: Despite sharing fundamental structural and representational similarities with the biological visual system, differences in local connectivity patterns within CNNs open up an interesting area to explore. This work explores whether integrating biologically observed connectivity patterns can enhance model performance and foster alignment with brain representations. We introduce a novel methodology, termed Lp-convolution, which employs the multivariate p-generalized normal distribution (MPND). We took advantage of MPND's conformational flexibility to carefully bridge disparities between artificial and biological connectivity patterns by designing an adaptable Lp-masks. Lp-masks finds the optimal conformation through task-dependent adaptation such as distortion, scale, and rotation. This allows Lp-convolution to perform well in tasks that require flexible input field shapes, including not only square-shape but also horizontal and vertical ones.

experiments.Description: To test whether Lp-convolution is robustly applicable to traditional CNN architectures and can solve the 'large kernel problem' by improving performance with larger kernels.
experiments.Method: On AlexNet, VGG-16, ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and ConvNext-tiny, all Conv2d layers are replaced with Lp-Conv layers. This is compared against the original baseline models and baseline models where kernel sizes are simply enlarged ('Large'). The Lp-Conv models are tested with various initial p values (p_init = 2, 4, 8, 16, 256). The task is image classification on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet.

experiments.Description: To assess whether biologically-inspired Lp-convolution improves model robustness to common corruptions.
experiments.Method: The models from the previous experiment (AlexNet, VGG-16, ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ConvNext-tiny with Base, Large, and various Lp-Conv configurations) are evaluated on the CIFAR-100-C dataset, specifically focusing on corruption severity level 1.

experiments.Description: To explore the impact of Lp-Convolution on modern large kernel CNNs like RepLKNet.
experiments.Method: Lp-convolution (with p=2) is integrated into the RepLKNet architecture without modifying the kernel sizes. The performance of this Lp2-RepLKNet model is compared to the original RepLKNet on the TinyImageNet dataset.

experiments.Description: To investigate whether Lp-convolution can be effectively integrated with existing pretrained models to enhance transfer learning performance.
experiments.Method: An ImageNet-pretrained ConvNeXt-V2 Tiny model is adapted for Lp-convolution by enlarging kernels and applying Lp-masks with a high p-value (p=16). This model is then evaluated on 16-shot transfer learning tasks and compared against the original pretrained baseline and a baseline with simply enlarged kernels. The downstream datasets are Oxford flowers, DescribableTextures (dtd), Oxford Pets, FGVC Aircraft, and UCF101.

experiments.Description: To investigate the conformational adaptability of Lp-masks on a task that requires balancing different receptive field shapes (square, row, and column).
experiments.Method: A CNN-based model is designed for the 9x9 Sudoku-solving task. The experiment compares a (3x3) Base model, a (7x7) Large model (simply enlarged kernel), and several (7x7) Lp-Conv models with different p_init values (2, 16, 256).

experiments.Description: To assess whether the neural representations of artificial models with biologically-inspired receptive fields (low p-value) align more closely with the representations of the mouse visual cortex (VC).
experiments.Method: Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) is used to compare the representations of TinyImageNet-trained CNNs (with different p_init values) and neural activity from the mouse VC. The biological data is from the Allen Brain Observatory, using neural responses to 118 natural scene images.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Maximum Similarity of Similarity Matrices (Max. SSM) score, calculated using Kendall's rank correlation coefficient."]

experiments.Description: To test if biologically inspired Gaussian sparsity via Lp-convolution can benefit a CNN architecture designed for neural activity prediction.
experiments.Method: Lp-convolution with different p values (2, 4, 8, 16) is applied to the CNN component of the MMV1 model. The model's ability to predict V1 neural activity is evaluated using a multimodal dataset from freely moving mice.

title: Generative Principal Component Analysis
name: generative-principal-component-analysis
short_hypothesis: For Principal Component Analysis (PCA) in high-dimensional settings, assuming the principal eigenvector lies near the range of a pre-trained generative model is a more effective structural prior than sparsity. This can be solved by a proposed 'Projected Power Method' (PPower), a variant of the classic power method that adds a projection step onto the generative model's range in each iteration.
related_work: Standard Principal Component Analysis (PCA) performs poorly in high-dimensional settings (m << n). Sparse PCA (SPCA) addresses this by imposing a sparsity constraint on the principal eigenvectors. Separately, in other inverse problems like compressed sensing, generative models have been used as priors, assuming the signal lies in the generator's range. This work aims to bridge these areas by studying the use of generative priors in PCA, termed Generative PCA (GPCA), as an alternative to the sparsity assumption used in SPCA.
abstract: In this paper, we study the problem of principal component analysis with generative modeling assumptions, adopting a general model for the observed matrix that encompasses notable special cases, including spiked matrix recovery and phase retrieval. The key assumption is that the first principal eigenvector lies near the range of an L-Lipschitz continuous generative model with bounded k-dimensional inputs. We propose a quadratic estimator. Moreover, we provide a variant of the classic power method, which projects the calculated data onto the range of the generative model during each iteration. We refer to our method as projected power method (PPower). We plan to perform experiments on various image datasets for spiked matrix and phase retrieval models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed PPower method on the spiked covariance model task, and to illustrate the effect of varying sample size (m) and signal-to-noise ratio (β).
experiments.Method: The task is image reconstruction from a spiked covariance model. The method will be evaluated on the MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CelebA datasets. For the generative prior, a pre-trained VAE will be used for MNIST, a BEGAN for Fashion-MNIST, and a DCGAN for CelebA. The proposed PPower method will be compared against the vanilla power method (Power) and the truncated power method (TPower). For the CelebA dataset, an additional baseline, TPowerW, will be used, which applies TPower in the wavelet basis.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed PPower method on the phase retrieval task.
experiments.Method: The task is image reconstruction from a phase retrieval model. The method will be evaluated on the MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets. For the generative prior, a pre-trained VAE will be used for MNIST and a BEGAN for Fashion-MNIST. The proposed PPower method will be compared against the vanilla power method (Power) and the truncated power method (TPower).

risk_factors: Perhaps the main caveat to Theorem 2 is that it assumes the projection step can be performed exactly.
We note that these experiments are intended as a simple proof of concept rather than seeking to be comprehensive, as our contributions are primarily theoretical.
Regarding the assumption x̄T w(t0) ≥ 2γ + ν, we note that when t0 = 0, this condition can be viewed as having a good initialization.

title: Progressive Mixup Augmented Teacher-Student Learning for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation
name: PMATS
short_hypothesis: An unsupervised domain adaptation approach combining teacher-student learning and a progressive mixup augmentation strategy will bridge large domain discrepancies and mitigate the impact of noisy pseudo-labels. The teacher network will provide more reliable pseudo-labels for the student, while progressive mixup will generate intermediate samples that gradually shift from source-dominant to target-dominant, allowing the model to adapt by aligning the source domain with these intermediate domains.
related_work: Current Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) methods often fall into two categories: adversarial adaptation and statistical adaptation. While effective, they typically adapt directly from the source to the target domain, which is problematic when the domain discrepancy is large. More recent methods that perform category-level alignment rely on pseudo-labels for target data, which are often noisy and unreliable. Some approaches attempt to bridge the domain gap by creating a few intermediate domains with fixed mixup ratios, but this is not a gradual transition.
abstract: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) aims to transfer knowledge learned from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain, mostly through learning a domain invariant feature representation. Currently, the best performing UDA methods use category level domain alignment to capture fine-grained information, resulting in significantly improved performance over global alignment. While successful, category level UDA methods suffer from the unreliable pseudo-labels for target data. Additionally, most UDA methods directly adapt from source to target domain without regard for the large domain discrepancy.
