In this paper, we propose an UDA approach with teacher-student learning where the teacher network is used to provide more reliable target pseudo-labels for the student during training. Furthermore, we use a progressive mixup augmentation strategy which generates intermediate samples that become increasingly target-dominant as training progresses. Aligning the source and intermediate domains allows the model to gradually transfer fine-grained domain knowledge from the source to the target domain while minimizing the negative impact of noisy target pseudo-labels. This progressive mixup augmented teacher-student (PMATS) training strategy is combined with class subset sampling and clustering based pseudo-label refinement.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the proposed PMATS method on the Office-31 benchmark for unsupervised domain adaptation and compare its performance against state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Office-31 dataset, which contains three domains: Amazon (A), Webcam (W), and DSLR (D). The PMATS model, using both ResNet50 and DeiT-Small backbones, will be tested on all six transfer tasks (A→D, A→W, D→A, D→W, W→A, W→D). The model will be compared to several baselines, including ResNet50, DANN, CDAN+E, DSAN, CAN, SRDC, RSDA-MSTN, FixBi, and CDTrans-S.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the proposed PMATS method on the more challenging Office-Home benchmark, which features larger domain gaps, and compare its performance against state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Office-Home dataset, which contains four domains: Artistic (A), Clipart (C), Product (P), and Real-World (R). The PMATS model, using both ResNet50 and DeiT-Small backbones, will be tested on all twelve transfer tasks. The model will be compared to several baselines, including ResNet50, DANN, CDAN+E, DSAN, SRDC, RSDA-MSTN, FixBi, and CDTrans-S.

experiments.Description: Conduct an ablation study to investigate the effectiveness of each individual component of the proposed PMATS method: progressive mixup (PM), teacher-student learning (TS), and pseudo-label refinement (PR).
experiments.Method: The study will be performed on four adaptation tasks from the Office-31 dataset (A→D, A→W, D→A, W→A) using the DeiT-Small backbone. The evaluation will start with a baseline DSAN model and incrementally add the proposed components (PM, TS, PR) to measure their contribution.

title: Enhancing Cross-Lingual and Cross-Domain Adaptability in Large Language Models for Software Engineering
name: uda-edcm-for-code
short_hypothesis: A novel framework, UDA-EDCM, which integrates concepts from measure theory, differential geometry, and information geometry, can enhance unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) for large language models in cross-lingual and cross-domain software engineering tasks. The core idea is to use optimal transport theory to minimize domain discrepancy, a Riemannian manifold approach with a Geodesic Flow Kernel for feature space alignment, and an optimization based on the Fisher Information Metric.
related_work: Traditional Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) approaches often struggle with the intricate structures and semantics inherent in code, failing to capture nuanced relationships across different programming paradigms. While methods like adaptive pre-training (e.g., BioBERT) have shown success in specific domains, and retrieval-augmented models (e.g., RAG, Replug) have improved code understanding, there remains a need for a unified, mathematically rigorous framework that can seamlessly adapt to new programming languages and coding environments without extensive retraining.
abstract: This paper presents a groundbreaking mathematical framework for unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) in the context of cross-lingual and cross-domain code modeling. We introduce the Enhanced Dynamic Code Modeling (UDA-EDCM) system, which leverages advanced concepts from measure theory, differential geometry, and information geometry to address the challenges posed by the diversity of natural and programming languages. At the core of UDA-EDCM is a novel measure-theoretic formulation of domain adaptation, utilizing optimal transport theory to minimize the discrepancy between source and target domains. We develop a Riemannian manifold approach to feature space alignment, introducing a Geodesic Flow Kernel that captures the intrinsic geometry of the code representation space. The UDA-EDCM operator is analyzed through the lens of functional analysis, revealing its spectral properties and their implications for generalization. Our information-theoretic bound on domain adaptation provides insights into the fundamental limits of knowledge transfer in code modeling. We present a unified theorem that synthesizes these diverse mathematical perspectives, offering a comprehensive characterization of UDA-EDCM's performance in terms of Wasserstein distance, empirical Rademacher complexity, and Fisher information. This theoretical foundation is complemented by an innovative optimization framework based on the Fisher Information Metric, ensuring efficient convergence in the probabilistic manifold of model parameters.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the UDA-EDCM model on cross-lingual text conversion tasks across multiple language pairs.
experiments.Method: The UDA-EDCM model will be compared against baseline models including ERNIE-Code and mT5. The evaluation will be performed on several language pairs, including English-Latvian and English-Chinese, using a framework involving the FLORES101 dataset for translation.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's ability for polyglot synopsis generation (code summarization) in both 'translate-train' and zero-shot scenarios.
experiments.Method: The model will be tested on the mCoNaLa dataset (Spanish, Japanese, Russian paired with Python). For the 'translate-train' setup, the model will be trained on a machine-translated version of the CoNaLa dataset. Performance will be benchmarked against the ERNIE-Code model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's capability to generate code snippets from multilingual natural language instructions (text-to-code).
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the same datasets as the code summarization task (mCoNaLa and CoNaLa). The UDA-EDCM model's performance will be compared to relevant baselines.

risk_factors: While promising, several areas for future research remain, including UDA-EDCM's behavior in extremely low-resource languages, performance on multi-step reasoning tasks, and potential multimodal extensions.
Despite UDA-EDCM(L1024)'s improved handling of long sequences, there's still room for enhancement in modeling very long code sequences.

title: PETRA: Pretrained Evolutionary Transformer for SARS-CoV-2 Mutation Prediction
name: petra-evolutionary-transformer
short_hypothesis: Training a generative pretrained transformer on evolutionary trajectories extracted from phylogenetic trees, instead of raw RNA sequences, will allow for the feasible prediction of SARS-CoV-2 mutations by reducing noise caused by sequencing artifacts.
related_work: Large-scale generative pre-trained transformers (GPTs) have been highly effective for sequential data but struggle with noisy viral genomic sequences. The error rate in raw RNA sequencing data (100-4,000 errors per sequence) far exceeds the number of actual mutations in SARS-CoV-2 variants (1-200 mutations), making direct modeling difficult. Previous transformer-based models for SARS-CoV-2 have focused on specific, limited datasets, making them hard to generalize and update. Before this work, the primary tool for mutation prediction was the non-transformer, DMS-based Bloom estimator.
abstract: Since its emergence, SARS-CoV-2 has demonstrated a rapid and unpredictable evolutionary trajectory, characterized by the continual emergence of immune-evasive variants. This poses persistent challenges to public health and vaccine development. While large-scale generative pre-trained transformers (GPTs) have revolutionized the modeling of sequential data, their direct applications to noisy viral genomic sequences are limited. In this paper, we introduce PETRA (Pretrained Evolutionary TRAnsformer), a novel transformer approach based on evolutionary trajectories derived from phylogenetic trees rather than raw RNA sequences. This method is intended to effectively mitigate sequencing noise and capture the hierarchical structure of viral evolution. A weighted training framework is proposed to address substantial geographical and temporal imbalances in global sequence data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of PETRA in predicting future SARS-CoV-2 mutations against established baseline methods.
experiments.Method: PETRA, a decoder-only transformer, will be trained on 16,772,588 sequences from the UShER phylogenetic tree updated on 2025-2-12. Evaluation will be performed on 57,117 sequences collected after 2025-2-13 and released before 2025-7-16, ensuring a strict temporal split. Two prediction tasks will be performed: nucleotide mutation prediction and spike amino-acid mutation prediction. The model's predictions will be compared against a Random Guess baseline and the Bloom estimator (using expected count, fitness score, and a mixed score).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Recall@k (k=1, 10, 100) for nucleotide mutations', 'Recall@k (k=1, 10) for spike amino-acid mutations', 'Average Recall', 'Weighted Recall (weighted by sequence representativeness)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the effects of the proposed weighted sampling methods.
experiments.Method: Different variants of PETRA will be trained and evaluated: PETRA-NW (no weighting), PETRA-TW (temporal weighting only), PETRA-RW (representative weighting only), and the full PETRA model (both). Performance will be compared against the Bloom-Mixed baseline on the same nucleotide and spike mutation prediction tasks.

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of immune background shifts, proxied by geographical and temporal information, on model performance.
experiments.Method: Two sub-experiments will be run. First, PETRA's performance will be compared when trained with and without location information. Second, the model's performance will be evaluated on sequences sampled at different time intervals after the training data cutoff to observe performance decay over time.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the practical utility of PETRA in a real-world scenario by predicting mutations for newly emerged major clades.
experiments.Method: Using a PETRA model trained on data available *before* the designation of major clades 24F(XEC) and 25A(LP.8.1), top spike mutation predictions will be generated. These predictions will then be qualitatively compared to the actual observed evolutionary paths of these clades, including designated sub-variants and other tracked sub-lineages.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of predicted mutations against subsequently designated and tracked sub-lineages.']

risk_factors: Firstly, trained on the UShER phylogenetic tree, PETRA is only able to predict single nucleotide mutations but cannot handle recombination, which is playing a more and more core part in recent SARS-CoV-2 evolution.
Secondly, PETRA is only able to predict mutations but cannot predict other features like severity, immune escape or growth potentials which public health departments may care about most.
Finally, a core limitation is the imbalance of data, especially the shortage of sequences in developing and least developed countries.
As a generative model for mutation prediction, PETRA could theoretically be misused to generate hypothetical virus variants.

title: OmniPortrait: Fine-Grained Personalized Portrait Synthesis via Pivotal Optimization
name: omniportrait-pivotal-optimization
short_hypothesis: A dual-stream, coarse-to-fine identity guidance mechanism called pivotal optimization can generate personalized portraits that preserve fine-grained facial details while maintaining high text-alignment and editability. This is achieved by first training a Pivot ID Encoder with a face localization loss to create a coarse identity pivot without degrading the base model's priors, and then using a Reference-Based Guidance module to perform on-the-fly optimization on diffusion features conditioned on this pivot.
related_work: Existing methods for personalized portrait synthesis face two main challenges: preserving fine-grained facial details and maintaining strong text alignment. Current approaches fall into several categories. Test-time fine-tuning methods (e.g., DreamBooth, Textual Inversion) can damage the rich priors of pretrained diffusion models, leading to poor editability and requiring multiple reference images. Encoder-based methods inject identity via a separate encoder but often struggle to balance identity fidelity and editing flexibility, with single-stream conditioning failing to capture fine-grained details. Training-free methods (e.g., MasaCtrl) manipulate attention maps but also struggle to preserve fine facial details from reference images. The proposed research aims to address the gap left by these methods, which fail to simultaneously achieve high identity fidelity, fine-detail preservation, and strong text editability.
abstract: Image identity customization aims to synthesize realistic and diverse portraits of a specified identity, given a reference image and a text prompt. This task presents two key challenges: (1) generating realistic portraits that preserve fine-grained facial details of the reference identity, and (2) maintaining identity consistency while achieving strong alignment with the text prompt. Existing single-stream methods fail to capture and guide fine-grained identity details. To address these challenges, we introduce OmniPortrait, a novel diffusion-based framework for fine-grained identity fidelity and high editability in portrait synthesis. Our core idea is pivotal optimization, which leverages dual-stream identity guidance in a coarse-to-fine manner. First, a Pivot ID Encoder is proposed and trained with a face localization loss while avoiding the degradation of editability typically caused by fine-tuning the denoiser. Although this encoder primarily guides coarse-level identity synthesis, it provides a good initialization that serves as the identity pivot for optimization during inference. Second, we propose Reference-Based Guidance, which performs on-the-fly feature matching and optimization over diffusion intermediate features conditioned on the identity pivot. In addition, our approach is able to generalize naturally to multi-identity customized image generation scenarios.

experiments.Description: To quantitatively and qualitatively compare OmniPortrait against state-of-the-art finetuning-based and encoder-based methods for personalized portrait generation.
experiments.Method: Reference images will be sampled from the CelebA-HQ and FFHQ datasets, with 30 text prompts constructed for each identity. The OmniPortrait model, built on SD and SDXL backbones, will be compared against baselines including DreamBooth, Textual Inversion, FastComposer, InstantID, PhotoMaker, and IP-Adapter.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Text Editability (CLIP-T, BLIP scores)', 'Identity Fidelity (CLIP-I, SIM/ID cosine similarity)', 'Image Quality (CLIP-IQA, FID)']

experiments.Description: To conduct a user study to evaluate the performance of the proposed approach against baseline methods from a human perspective.
experiments.Method: 30 participants will be recruited to evaluate 20 sets of generated images. Each set will consist of one reference identity image and five textual prompts, with each baseline method producing corresponding predictions. Participants will be asked to select the top-2 best-performing methods.

experiments.Description: To perform ablation studies to analyze the contribution of each key component of the OmniPortrait framework.
experiments.Method: The experiment will involve systematically removing or altering individual components of the proposed method: the face localization loss (L_loc), the Pivot ID Encoder (PIE), the Background Gradient Masking (BGM), and the Reference-Based Guidance (RB-Guidance). The effect of the guidance starting time parameter 'u' will also be analyzed.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to extend to multi-identity personalized portrait synthesis.
experiments.Method: The approach will be adapted by instantiating a separate Pivot ID Encoder for each reference identity. Each resulting pivot embedding will be concatenated with its corresponding person-related text token (e.g., 'woman', 'man'). Reference-Based Guidance will then be applied within the respective spatial regions for each face.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative assessment of identity consistency and text-image alignment.']

title: DevBench: A Realistic, Developer-Informed Benchmark for Code Generation Models
name: devbench-benchmark
short_hypothesis: A new benchmark, DevBench, grounded in observed developer behavior from over one billion code completion interactions, can provide a more realistic, scalable, and contamination-resistant evaluation of Large Language Models for code generation. By focusing on common yet challenging completion scenarios across multiple languages and task categories, it will offer fine-grained diagnostics beyond what existing benchmarks provide.
related_work: Existing benchmarks for code generation fall into several categories: problem-solving benchmarks (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP), repository-based benchmarks (e.g., SWE-bench, CrossCodeEval), and evolving benchmarks that address data contamination (e.g., LiveCodeBench). However, these benchmarks have limitations: they often rely on code scraped from open-source repositories or coding challenges, which may not reflect real-world usage patterns of code completion tools. Furthermore, their diagnostic value is limited by aggregate metrics, and they are often prone to training data contamination. DevBench aims to address these gaps by evaluating scenarios derived from telemetry of live developer interactions, making it more realistic and contamination-resistant.
abstract: DevBench is a telemetry-driven benchmark designed to evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) on realistic code completion tasks. It includes 1,800 evaluation instances across six programming languages and six task categories derived from real developer telemetry, such as API usage and code purpose understanding. Unlike prior benchmarks, it emphasizes ecological validity, avoids training data contamination, and enables detailed diagnostics. The evaluation combines functional correctness, similarity-based metrics, and LLM-judge assessments focused on usefulness and contextual relevance. Our benchmark provides actionable insights to guide model selection and improvement—detail that is often missing from other benchmarks but is essential for both practical deployment and targeted model development.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art Large Language Models on realistic code completion tasks using the newly proposed DevBench benchmark.
experiments.Method: A diverse set of state-of-the-art LLMs, including OpenAI models (GPT series), Anthropic's Claude Sonnet series, DeepSeek models, and Ministral 3B, will be evaluated on the DevBench benchmark. DevBench consists of 1,800 synthetic but telemetry-guided evaluation instances across six programming languages (Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, C++, C#) and six task categories (API Usage, Code Purpose Understanding, Code2NL/NL2Code, Low Context, Pattern Matching, Syntax Completion). The models are to be evaluated in a zero-shot setting using a consistent, code-only prompt template.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Functional Correctness (Pass@1 with n=5 samples)', 'Similarity-based metrics (Average Cosine Similarity, Line 0 Exact Match Rate)', 'LLM-Judge Evaluation (using o3-mini to score relevance and helpfulness)']

risk_factors: Our synthetic, telemetry-driven generation approach effectively prevents data contamination and limits bias by leveraging GPT-4o as the generation model. To further enhance diversity, future iterations could incorporate multiple foundation models with varied training backgrounds.

The occasional divergence between these metrics—such as cases where higher syntactic similarity does not correlate with functional correctness—highlights an opportunity to develop composite metrics that better capture the full spectrum of code quality dimensions relevant to developers.

Our language coverage, which already includes six major programming languages (Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, C++, and C#), provides a foundation for expansion. Future iterations could incorporate emerging languages such as Rust, Go, Ruby, and Swift...
Response latency represents another dimension deserving further exploration, as it can impact developer workflow and productivity.

The telemetry data that informs our benchmark categories derives from diverse developer interactions, offering an opportunity to explicitly analyze potential implicit biases in programming styles, paradigms, or practices.
our benchmark’s coverage of six languages, while broader than many existing benchmarks, still represents a limited subset of the programming ecosystem.

there are potential job market implications if increasingly capable code completion systems begin to automate significant portions of software development tasks, potentially affecting employment opportunities for certain types of programming roles.

title: ChronoGAM: An End-to-End One-Class Time Series Gaussian Mixture Model
name: chronogam-ocl-gmm
short_hypothesis: An end-to-end method, ChronoGAM, is proposed for one-class learning on time series. It combines a convolutional autoencoder adapted for temporal features with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). By introducing a new objective function that regularizes the covariance matrix and modifies the energy calculation, it is hypothesized that the model will avoid numerical instabilities and model collapse, leading to better classification performance than existing state-of-the-art methods.
related_work: Classical One-Class Learning (OCL) algorithms like One-Class SVM have limitations when applied to high-dimensional time series data. Deep learning methods, often based on autoencoders like DeepSVDD and LSTM-AE, have been proposed but suffer from issues such as hypersphere collapse, reliance on sensitive thresholds, or using autoencoder architectures not suitable for time series. The Deep Autoencoding Gaussian Mixture Model (DAGMM) addresses some issues but its autoencoder is insufficient for capturing temporal features and its objective function is prone to numerical instability, which can lead to model collapse.
abstract: Recently, several algorithms have been proposed for One Class Learning (OCL) with time series. However, several problems can be found in these methods, problems involving the collapse of hyperspheres, manual thresholds, numerical instabilities and even the use of unlabeled instances during training, which directly violates the concept of OCL. To avoid these problems and solve cases like the numerical instability of some methods this paper proposes an end-to-end method for time series one-class learning based on a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). The proposed method combines the unsupervised learning technique of an autoencoder adapted to extract temporal and structural features of a time series, combined with distribution learning, to provide better performance than other state-of-the-art methods for the classification of time series data. ChronoGAM is a novel method that is capable of improving the temporal importance of the representations learned by the autoencoding system. We propose a new objective function with modifications to penalize the small values on the covariance matrix without resulting in exploding gradient propagation, causing numerical instabilities, and adapting the energy calculus to avoid the use of exponential functions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed ChronoGAM method against other state-of-the-art methods for time series one-class learning on a wide range of benchmark datasets.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be evaluated on a set of 85 datasets from the UCR repository, transformed into 652 one-class problem datasets by treating each class as the interest class. ChronoGAM will be compared against classic baselines (OCSVM, IsolationForest), a two-step method (DeepSVDD), and an end-to-end method (DAGMM). The ChronoGAM architecture uses three convolutional blocks with varying kernel sizes and Snake activation function.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean accuracy', 'F1 score', 'Average ranking via Friedman test', 'Pairwise post-hoc analysis with Wilcoxon signed-rank test', 'Critical difference diagram']

risk_factors: Apply cross-dimension dependency for multivariate time series since normal convolutions cannot be the best way to capture the relation between time and structure in multivariate time series.
The GMM supposes that the data are represented by a Gaussian distribution, which can cause poor performance for some problems.
due to some concepts of the convolutions, such as the size of the kernels, the model can be impacted by the size of the series.

title: TradeFM: A Generative Foundation Model for Trade-flow and Market Microstructure
name: tradefm-foundation-model-market-microstructure
short_hypothesis: A large-scale, decoder-only Transformer, pre-trained on billions of transactions from a diverse set of equities, can learn a unified and generalizable representation of trade-flow dynamics by using a novel scale-invariant feature representation and a universal tokenization scheme, enabling it to generalize across diverse assets and liquidity regimes without asset-specific calibration.
related_work: Traditional approaches to modeling market microstructure include classical stochastic models like Hawkes processes, which rely on specific parametric assumptions and fail to capture properties like heavy-tailed returns. Agent-based models (ABMs) have historically required hand-crafting agent behaviors. Early deep learning models demonstrated the potential of learning from data but were typically trained on limited instruments, restricting their ability to learn universal representations. More recent domain-specific foundation models like MaRS have emerged, but this work aims to improve upon them by maximizing the heterogeneity of the pre-training dataset and engineering features for cross-asset generalization at the feature level to learn truly universal market representations.
abstract: Learning generalizable representations from the high-frequency, heterogeneous event streams of financial markets is a significant challenge. We introduce TradeFM, a foundation model that learns the universal dynamics of market microstructure. Pre-trained on billions of equities transactions, TradeFM uses a novel scale-invariant feature representation and a universal tokenization scheme to form a unified representation, enabling generalization without asset-specific calibration. TradeFM provides a high-fidelity engine for synthetic data generation and downstream agent-based modeling.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's realism by assessing its ability to reproduce key stylized facts of asset returns, which are emergent properties of endogenous market dynamics.
experiments.Method: Generate 10 rollouts of 1,024 events for 9 assets across 3 liquidity tiers, for each of 9 held-out months, conditioned on a context of 1,024 real historical events. The generated trajectories will be produced in a closed-loop system with a deterministic market simulator. The model will be compared against a calibrated Zero-Intelligence (ZI) agent baseline and a Compound Hawkes baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Autocorrelation of simulated log returns (Lack of Autocorrelation)', 'Autocorrelation of absolute log returns (Long-Range Dependence/Volatility Clustering)', 'Kurtosis of simulated returns for short and long time scales (Heavy Tails and Aggregational Gaussianity)', 'Wasserstein distance (W1) and Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) statistic between real and generated log return distributions']

experiments.Description: To conduct a quantitative evaluation of the distributional fidelity of the generated order flow to assess how well it aligns with the statistical properties of real market data.
experiments.Method: Using the same rollouts generated for the stylized facts experiment, compare the distributions of key microstructure variables between real and generated data. The evaluation will average results over 9 assets, 3 liquidity tiers, and 9 held-out months. Baselines are a Zero-Intelligence (ZI) agent and a Compound Hawkes model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic', 'Wasserstein distance', 'Metrics will be computed for: Spreads, Interarrival (IA) Times, Order Price Depths (OPD), Orderbook Imbalance (OBI), Bid Volume, and Ask Volume.']

experiments.Description: To validate the claim that TradeFM learns a universal grammar of market microstructure that generalizes beyond the assets and time periods seen during training, and to verify that the model's output can be controlled by its conditioning tokens.
experiments.Method: 1. Geographic Zero-Shot Generalization: Evaluate the model, trained exclusively on US equities, on a hold-out set of assets from APAC markets (China and Japan). 2. Temporal Robustness: Evaluate model performance over an extended 9-month hold-out period (Jan - Sep 2025) to test for performance drift. 3. Controllability: Generate 256 context-free trajectories of 512 tokens each for every combination of market-participant and liquidity indicators.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Perplexity scores for geographic generalization.', 'Stability of distance metrics (Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Wasserstein) over the 9-month temporal hold-out period.', 'Standard deviation of generated volumes and interarrival times for each conditioning setting to test controllability.']

title: OrderDP: A Theoretically Guaranteed Lossless Dynamic Data Pruning Framework
name: orderdp
short_hypothesis: A two-stage dynamic data pruning framework, named OrderDP, which first uniformly samples a candidate subset and then selects the top-q samples based on their loss values, can achieve stable, near-lossless training acceleration by conducting unbiased training with respect to a defined surrogate loss, thereby mitigating the gradient bias and instability issues of existing methods.
related_work: Data pruning strategies are broadly classified into static and dynamic pruning. Static methods assign informativeness scores before training, while dynamic methods update scores on-the-fly. However, existing dynamic approaches, which often select highly informative samples, can lead to biased gradient estimation compared to full-dataset training. This introduces training instability, and the analysis of this bias and its impact on final performance remains ambiguous. This work aims to address these challenges by designing a method with theoretical guarantees for unbiasedness and convergence.
abstract: Data pruning (DP), as an oft-stated strategy to alleviate heavy training burdens, reduces the volume of training samples according to a well-defined pruning method while striving for near-lossless performance. However, existing approaches, which commonly select highly informative samples, can lead to biased gradient estimation compared to full-dataset training. Furthermore, the analysis of this bias and its impact on final performance remains ambiguous. To address these challenges, we propose OrderDP, a plug-and-play framework that aims to obtain stable, unbiased, and near-lossless training acceleration with theoretical guarantees. Specifically, OrderDP first randomly selects a subset and then chooses the top-q samples, where unbiasedness is established with respect to a surrogate loss. This ensures that OrderDP conducts unbiased training in terms of the surrogate objective. We further establish convergence and generalization analyses, elucidating how OrderDP affects optimal performance and enables well-controlled acceleration while ensuring guaranteed final performance.

experiments.Description: To comprehensively compare the performance and efficiency of OrderDP against a wide range of static and dynamic data pruning baselines on image classification.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets using ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 models. OrderDP will be compared against 15 static DP baselines (including Static Random, Herding, K-means, Forgetting, GraNd, EL2N, Craig, Glister) and 4 dynamic DP baselines (Dynamic Random, e-greedy, UCB, InfoBatch), as well as full-dataset training.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of OrderDP on a large-scale image classification benchmark against other dynamic pruning methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the ImageNet-1K dataset using a ResNet-50 model. OrderDP will be compared against Dynamic Random, UCB, and InfoBatch baselines, with full-data training as a reference.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on how the two-stage pruning decomposition, parameterized by the exploration size (s) and exploitation size (q), affects OrderDP's performance.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 using a ResNet-18 model. The study will vary the exploration ratio (s/|D|) and exploitation ratio (q/s) under both fixed and varying overall prune ratios.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of OrderDP across different model architectures and optimizers.
experiments.Method: Cross-architecture robustness will be tested on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-1K using ResNet-18/50, Swin-Tiny, and ViT-Base models. Cross-optimizer robustness will be tested on CIFAR-10 with a ResNet-50 model using SGD, AdamW, LARS, and LAMB optimizers. In both settings, OrderDP will be compared to InfoBatch.

risk_factors: While OrderDP excels on moderate-scale vision benchmarks, its performance on very large architectures, streaming inference scenarios, and heterogeneous hardware platforms, as well as in self-supervised or multi-modal settings, remains to be explored.
In future work, we will extend OrderDP to adaptive pruning schedules, investigate its integration with transformer and graph models, and study its behavior under distribution shift and noisy labels.

title: Exploring and Evaluating Personalized Models for Code Generation
name: personalized-code-generation-finetuning
short_hypothesis: Various levels of model fine-tuning, from full 'custom' fine-tuning to more efficient 'lightweight' and 'prefix' tuning methods, can improve the generalization of large transformer models for code generation by personalizing them to a specific software project's data and style. It is hypothesized that tuning only the last decoder block could be sufficient to obtain performance improvements similar to a fully custom fine-tuned model.
related_work: The dominant paradigm in NLP and code generation is to pre-train a large transformer model on a general corpus and then fine-tune it for a specific task. However, these models often fail to generalize to new domains, such as private enterprise codebases with unique libraries and styles. While personalization has been explored for NLP tasks like query completion, this work aims to extend this concept to the software engineering domain. It plans to explore and compare several parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategies (custom, lightweight, prefix-tuning) for the task of code generation, analyzing the trade-offs between performance and computational cost.
abstract: Large Transformer models are increasingly the baseline architecture for source code generation models. Transformers are usually pre-trained on a large unsupervised corpus and then fine-tuned on a particular task. While fine-tuning is a tried-and-true method, generalization remains an on-going challenge. Here we explore the ability of various levels of model fine-tuning to improve generalization by personalized fine-tuning. In the context of generating unit tests for Java methods, we evaluate learning to personalize to a specific project using several methods. We consider three fine-tuning approaches: (i) custom fine-tuning, which allows all the model parameters to be tuned; (ii) lightweight fine-tuning, which freezes most of the model's parameters, allowing a tuning of the token embeddings and softmax layer or the final layer alone; (iii) prefix tuning, which keeps language model parameters frozen, but optimizes a small project-specific prefix vector. Each of these techniques offers a different trade-off in total compute cost and prediction performance, which we evaluate by code and task-specific metrics, training time, and total computational operations.

experiments.Description: To investigate and compare four different personalization strategies for code generation models on a per-project basis. The goals are to evaluate if these custom models outperform a generic baseline in terms of (1) intrinsic metrics (RQ1), (2) task-specific performance for unit test generation (RQ2), and (3) to compare the training and compute costs of each strategy (RQ3).
experiments.Method: Task: Unit Test Case generation for Java. A baseline BART transformer model (AthenaTest by Tufano et al., 2021) is personalized for 20 different Java projects sampled from the test set. Four different fine-tuning strategies are applied to the baseline model for each project: 1) Custom (all parameters tuned), 2) L-EO (embeddings and output layer tuned), 3) L-LDB (last decoder block tuned), and 4) Prefix tuning (a project-specific prefix is optimized). The models are to be trained and evaluated using 4-fold cross-validation for each project and strategy.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Intrinsic metrics (RQ1): BLEU4 score, mean perplexity per token.', 'Task-specific metrics (RQ2): Perfect Matches (exact string match), Abstracted Code Matches (match after masking variable/method names), Coding Style (tf-idf cosine similarity of identifiers between generated and target code).', 'Cost metrics (RQ3): Training time, computation cost in petaFLOPS-seconds.']

risk_factors: This paper does not evaluate methods for client side training with restricted resources, however, as the most powerful large language models which enable state of the art code synthesis have 10-100 million parameters.
We leave to future work extending these studies to models which have been pruned, quantized, distilled, and optimized to be ran in limited resource environments.

title: HyperFields: Towards Zero-Shot Generation of NeRFs from Text
name: hyperfields-for-zeroshot-nerf
short_hypothesis: A hypernetwork-based neural pipeline, featuring a dynamic hypernetwork and NeRF distillation, can be trained to generate the weights of individual NeRF networks from text prompts. Once trained, this hypernetwork will be capable of efficiently producing the weights of NeRFs corresponding to novel prompts, either through a single forward pass or with minimal fine-tuning, thus bypassing computationally intensive per-prompt optimization.
related_work: Current text-to-3D synthesis methods, particularly those guided by 2D diffusion models like DreamFusion (using Score Distillation Sampling), require computationally intensive per-prompt optimization to generate a Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) for each new scene. This presents a significant challenge for efficient generation. While hypernetworks, which generate weights for other networks, have been explored for implicit representations, their application to generating NeRFs from text is limited. Related works like HyperDiffusion are unconditional, and Shape-E requires teacher point clouds. The proposed method, HyperFields, aims to fill this gap by training a single hypernetwork to directly map text prompts to NeRF weights, enabling amortized, feed-forward generation. A concurrent work, ATT3D, also uses a hypernetwork but differs in its architecture, as it generates hash grid weights and conditions only on text, whereas HyperFields generates MLP weights and dynamically conditions on previous layer activations.
abstract: We introduce HyperFields, a method for generating text-conditioned Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) with a single forward pass and (optionally) some fine-tuning. Key to our approach are: (i) a dynamic hypernetwork, which learns a smooth mapping from text token embeddings to the space of NeRFs; (ii) NeRF distillation training, which distills scenes encoded in individual NeRFs into one dynamic hypernetwork. These techniques are intended to enable a single network to fit over a hundred unique scenes.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform zero-shot in-distribution generalization by generating scenes for novel combinations of shapes and colors that were seen individually during training but not together.
experiments.Method: The model is trained on a subset of color-shape combinations (9 shapes and 8 colors), with a held-out subset of combinations reserved for testing. For example, the model might see 'red' and 'chair' separately but not 'red chair'. The model will then be tasked with generating the held-out prompts in a zero-shot manner.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to accelerate convergence for out-of-distribution prompts, which contain shapes and/or attributes not seen during training.
experiments.Method: The trained HyperFields model is tested on prompts with unseen geometry and/or attributes (e.g., 'gold blender', 'yarn skateboard'). The model is fine-tuned on these prompts using Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) loss. The performance is compared against two baselines: 1) Stable Dreamfusion (S) trained from scratch, and 2) Stable Dreamfusion (P) pre-trained on a semantically similar scene.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of generated scenes after a fixed number of fine-tuning steps', 'Convergence speed (number of steps)', 'Average user-reported rank from a human study']

experiments.Description: To quantify the amortization benefits of using a pre-trained HyperFields model compared to the per-prompt optimization required by baseline methods.
experiments.Method: The total time to generate a set of test scenes using HyperFields (including the one-time distillation overhead) will be compared against the time taken by a DreamFusion baseline, which requires approximately 30 minutes to generate each scene.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to justify the key design choices: the dynamic hypernetwork architecture and the NeRF distillation training process.
experiments.Method: Two ablations are planned: 1) Dynamic Hypernetwork: The full model will be compared against a 'Static Hypernet' version that does not use previous layer activations for conditioning, on a scene packing task. 2) NeRF Distillation: The full model trained with NeRF distillation will be compared against a version trained directly with Score Distillation Sampling (SDS), also on a scene packing task.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of generated scenes to assess for mode collapse and preservation of distinct geometry.']

risk_factors: First, the quality of the scenes used in training and consequently the quality of the generalization scenes is constrained by the quality of the teacher NeRFs. Any systematic artifacts in the teacher NeRFs will be learned by the hypernetwork.
Second, the dynamic hypernetwork while allows for flexibility in representation of various scenes consumes a large amount of GPU RAM making our model trainable only on 48GB or hihger GPU RAMs.
Finally, our model cannot generate scenes zero-shot without any optimization in an open vocabulary setting, and this is an interesting avenue for future research.

title: Informative Data Selection for Thorax Disease Classification
name: ids-information-bottleneck-reweighting
short_hypothesis: A principled sample re-weighting method, Informative Data Selection (IDS), based on the Information Bottleneck (IB) framework, can improve the performance of DNNs trained with Generative Data Augmentation (GDA) by assigning higher importance weights to more informative synthetic samples, without requiring clean metadata or external classifiers.
related_work: Generative Data Augmentation (GDA) is used to create synthetic labeled data to augment training sets for medical imaging, addressing data scarcity. However, this synthetic data often contains noise that can degrade classifier performance. Existing methods to mitigate this, such as data selection or re-weighting, typically rely on clean metadata or external classifiers, which may not be available. The research gap is the lack of a principled sample re-weighting method for GDA that does not depend on such external information.
abstract: Although Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) such as Vision Transformers (ViTs) have demonstrated superior performance in medical imaging tasks, the training of DNNs usually requires large amounts of high-quality labeled training data, which is usually difficult or even impractical to collect in the medical domain. To address this issue, Generative Data Augmentation (GDA) has been employed to improve the performance of DNNs trained on augmented training data. However, the synthetic data generated by GDA universally suffer from noise, and such synthetic data can severely hurt the performance of classifiers. Existing works aiming to mitigate this issue, usually depend on a given clean metadata or external classifier. In this work, we propose a principled sample re-weighting method, Informative Data Selection (IDS), based on an established information theoretic measure, the Information Bottleneck (IB), to improve the performance of DNNs trained for thorax disease classification with GDA.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed IDS method for thorax disease classification against competing data selection and data re-weighting methods for GDA on the CheXpert benchmark.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use ViT-S/16 and ViT-B/16 models, pre-trained with MAE, and fine-tune them on the CheXpert dataset. The performance of IDS will be compared against several baselines: MAE, MAE with Synthetic Data, MW-Net, OTR, IE, CBF, and REVAR. An additional evaluation will assess the performance of IDS when re-weighting both real and synthetic data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of IDS for GDA on the COVIDx benchmark for single-label classification.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use ViT-S/16 and ViT-B/16 models, pre-trained with MAE, and fine-tune them on the COVIDx dataset. The performance of IDS will be compared against several baselines: MAE, MAE with Synthetic Data, MW-Net, OTR, IE, CBF, and REVAR. An additional evaluation will assess the performance of IDS when re-weighting both real and synthetic data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of IDS for GDA on the challenging multi-label NIH ChestX-ray14 benchmark.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use ViT-S/16 and ViT-B/16 models, pre-trained with MAE, and fine-tune them on the NIH ChestX-ray14 dataset. The performance of IDS will be compared against several baselines: MAE, MAE with Synthetic Data, MW-Net, OTR, IE, CBF, and REVAR. An additional evaluation will assess the performance of IDS when re-weighting both real and synthetic data.

experiments.Description: To study the correlation between the importance weights learned by IDS and the 'informativeness' of synthetic images, defined by disease localization quality.
experiments.Method: The study will use Grad-CAM to generate disease localization heatmaps for synthetic images of the disease Cardiomegaly. The Intersection over Union (IoU) score between these heatmaps and ground-truth bounding boxes will be computed to quantify image informativeness. The correlation between these IoU scores and the importance weights assigned by IDS, OTR, and REVAR will be analyzed.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Spearman Correlation Coefficient (SCC)', 'Visual inspection of Grad-CAM heatmaps']

title: Probabilistic Neural Pruning via Sparsity Evolutionary Fokker-Planck-Kolmogorov Equation
name: Sparsity-Evolutionary-FPK-Pruning
short_hypothesis: The evolution of the population of optimal subnetworks under increasing sparsity can be modeled by a thermodynamic-inspired differential equation, the Sparsity Evolutionary Fokker-Planck-Kolmogorov Equation (SFPK). This equation can then guide a particle simulation-based pruning method, SFPK-pruner, to gradually transform a dense model into a performant sparse one by sampling masks from the evolved distribution.
related_work: Existing neural pruning methods include score-based methods (e.g., magnitude pruning), regularization-based methods which can be numerically unstable, and sparse training methods which are computationally demanding. Another line of work tracks the trajectory of a single optimal subnetwork, which is overly restrictive. Bayesian approaches model the entire parameter distribution. This work proposes to fill a gap by modeling the evolution of the *population* of optimal subnetworks (specifically, their mask distributions) as sparsity increases, using a stochastic differential equation framework inspired by thermodynamics, rather than tracking a single network or modeling the full parameter posterior.
abstract: Neural pruning aims to compress and accelerate deep neural networks by identifying the optimal subnetwork within a specified sparsity budget. This work studies how to gradually sparsify the unpruned dense model to the target sparsity level with minimal performance drop by analyzing the evolution of the population of optimal subnetworks under continuous sparsity increments from a thermodynamic perspective. We first reformulate neural pruning as an expected loss minimization problem over the mask distributions. Then, we establish an effective approximation for the sparsity evolution of the optimal mask distribution, termed the Sparsity Evolutionary Fokker-Planck-Kolmogorov Equation (SFPK), which provides closed-form guidance on distributional transitions for minimizing the expected loss under an infinitesimal sparsity increment. On top of that, we propose SFPK-pruner, a particle simulation-based probabilistic pruning method, to sample performant masks with desired sparsity from the destination distribution of SFPK. In theory, we establish the convergence guarantee for the proposed SFPK-pruner.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the one-shot pruning performance of the proposed SFPK-pruner against competitive baselines on CIFAR-100 for both unstructured and structured pruning.
experiments.Method: A well-trained target network is pruned to various target sparsity levels and then retrained for a fixed number of epochs. The evaluation is performed on the CIFAR-100 dataset using ResNet-20, VGG16-bn, and WRN-20 models. The proposed method is compared against magnitude pruning (Mag), SNIP, SynFlow, GraSP, and PSO.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the one-shot unstructured pruning effectiveness of SFPK-pruner on the large-scale ImageNet-1K benchmark without any retraining.
experiments.Method: The SFPK-pruner is applied once to generate mask trajectories, and intermediate checkpoints are collected at various sparsity levels to prune the model. The evaluation is performed on the ImageNet-1K dataset using ResNet-50 and MobileNet-V1 architectures. The method is compared against magnitude pruning and WoodFisher (WF).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the one-shot structured channel pruning performance of SFPK-pruner on a transformer-based architecture (DeiT-T).
experiments.Method: The SFPK-pruner is applied to prune a DeiT-T model on ImageNet-1K to 50% sparsity, followed by retraining for 100 epochs. The method is compared against SCOP, HVT, UVC, WDPruning, and X-Pruner.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the gradual pruning performance of SFPK-pruner on pre-trained ResNet-50 and MobileNet-V1 models on ImageNet-1K.
experiments.Method: The SFPK-pruner is applied to gradually prune the model to 90% sparsity within 10 prune-and-retrain loops, with a total of 100 retraining epochs. The method is compared against a wide range of baselines including Mag, Incremental, DPF, GMP+LS, VD, RIGL+ERK, SNFS+LS, STR, DNW, WF, PSO, and CrAM.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies on the sensitivity of SFPK-pruner to its key hyperparameters: the regularization rate λ and the mask particle simulation scheme (number of particles n and simulation steps K).
experiments.Method: For the regularization rate (λ), a one-shot SFPK-pruner with different λ values is used to compress a pretrained ResNet-20 to 95% sparsity on CIFAR-100. For the simulation scheme (K, n), a one-shot SFPK-pruner with various (K, n) values is used to compress a pretrained VGG16-bn to 98% sparsity on CIFAR-100.

risk_factors: Our SFPK works effectively when the target model is “rational,” meaning pruning the model usually increases the loss... For instance, an adversarially trained model... is considered “irrational”... In this case... the SFPK trajectory may NOT converge to the true optimal mask distribution dynamic t → πt, which may lead to suboptimal results.

title: 4D Mind Reading
name: 4d-mind-reading
short_hypothesis: A novel BCI function, 4D Mind Reading, can generate 4D visuals (video and 3D structures) directly from fMRI signals using Mind4D, a brain-inspired fMRI conditioned 4D generation framework that learns asymmetric hierarchical representations in a weakly supervised manner.
related_work: Prior brain-computer interfaces (BCI) have enabled breakthroughs like translating fMRI signals into images or videos. However, human perception operates in a dynamic 3D world, processing information across both spatial and temporal dimensions, which existing methods do not capture. A key challenge is that training a model to generate 4D scenes from fMRI data requires paired fMRI–4D mappings, which are infeasible to collect.
abstract: Brain-computer interfaces (BCI) have enabled breakthroughs like translating fMRI signals into images or videos. However, human perception operates in a dynamic 3D world. In this work, we introduce 4D Mind Reading, a novel BCI function that generates 4D visuals—combining video and 3D structures—directly from fMRI signals. Building such a system is challenging, as it requires paired fMRI–4D mappings, which are infeasible to collect. To address this, we propose Mind4D, an innovative brain-inspired fMRI conditioned 4D generation framework capable of learning asymmetric hierarchical representations from fMRI signals in a weakly supervised manner. Our approach captures both high-level and low-level representations, along with the decomposition of scene backgrounds and object foregrounds. By conditioning and integrating multiple generative priors for the foreground and background, Mind4D is designed to produce high-quality semantic 4D visuals.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to generate immersive 4D visuals semantically aligned with brain activity, and to compare its performance against state-of-the-art fMRI-to-video approaches when constrained to the reference view.
experiments.Method: The proposed Mind4D model will be evaluated against the best fMRI-to-video approaches using fMRI data.

title: Benchmarking Machine Unlearning for Vision Transformers
name: benchmark-unlearning-for-vts
short_hypothesis: By systematically benchmarking existing CNN-derived machine unlearning (MU) algorithms on Vision Transformer (VT) architectures, it is possible to establish how factors like architecture (ViT vs. Swin-T), model capacity, dataset complexity, and memorization proxies affect unlearning performance, thereby filling a critical gap in understanding MU for this important model class.
related_work: Research in machine unlearning (MU) has been extensive for Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Large Language Models (LLMs), and diffusion models. In parallel, Vision Transformers (VTs) have become strong alternatives to CNNs for vision tasks. However, there is a significant gap in the literature, as no comprehensive benchmark exists for MU on VTs. It is an open question whether findings from other modalities transfer to VTs, given their key differences in architecture, training regimes (e.g., pretrain-then-finetune), and inductive biases. This work aims to fill this gap.
abstract: Research in machine unlearning (MU) is a critical capability for building safe and fair AI. Concurrently, Vision Transformers (VTs) are increasingly successful alternatives to CNNs. However, MU research has largely centered on CNNs, not VTs, and no benchmarks exist for MU on VTs. This work proposes to be the first to benchmark MU algorithm performance in different VT families (ViT and Swin-T) at different capacities. The proposed work will employ different datasets to assess scale and complexity impacts, different MU algorithms to represent fundamentally different approaches, and both single-shot and continual unlearning protocols. A key focus is on benchmarking MU algorithms that leverage training data memorization. The work will characterize how VTs memorize data relative to CNNs and assess the impact of memorization proxies on performance, using unified evaluation metrics to enable reproducible, fair, and comprehensive comparisons.

experiments.Description: To benchmark the performance of representative, memorization-leveraging machine unlearning (MU) algorithms on different Vision Transformer (VT) families and capacities, comparing them against CNN counterparts to establish the current state of affairs for MU in VT settings.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use ViT (ViT-Small, ViT-Tiny) and Swin-T (Swin-Tiny, Swin-Small) models, pretrained on ImageNet-1K, and compare them to ResNet-18 and ResNet-50. The unlearning algorithms to be evaluated are Fine-tune (FT), NegGrad+, and SalUn, all instantiated within the RUM framework. The evaluation will be conducted on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN datasets under a single-shot unlearning protocol.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['ToW (accounts for retain/test accuracy and forget accuracy)', 'ToW-MIA (accounts for retain/test accuracy and Membership Inference Attack vulnerability)']

experiments.Description: To characterize the memorization patterns of Vision Transformers (VTs) relative to CNNs and to evaluate whether CNN-derived memorization proxies are effective for estimating memorization in VTs.
experiments.Method: This experiment will compute Feldman memorization scores for ViT-Small and Swin-Tiny architectures and compare their distribution to those of ResNet-18 and ResNet-50 on the CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets. It will then evaluate five memorization proxies (Confidence, Max Confidence, Entropy, Binary Accuracy, and Holdout Retraining) by measuring their correlation with the computed memorization scores.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Spearman's rank correlation coefficient (ρ) between proxy values and true memorization scores."]

experiments.Description: To examine the performance of MU algorithms on a larger-scale, more complex dataset to understand how the methods scale.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the ImageNet-1K validation set (50,000 images) as the evaluation dataset. A larger Swin-Small model will be used. A forget set of 3,000 images will be created, with the remaining 47,000 images used for the retain/test set. The unlearning algorithms (Fine-tune, NegGrad+, SalUn) will be evaluated using the Holdout Retraining proxy.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance stability of machine unlearning on Vision Transformers under a continual unlearning protocol where unlearning requests arrive sequentially.
experiments.Method: This experiment will apply five sequential unlearning steps using the best-performing algorithm-proxy pair (NegGrad+ and Holdout Retraining) on ViT-Small and Swin-Tiny models. The evaluation will be conducted on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and SVHN datasets. In each step, a portion of the data is unlearned, and proxy values are recalculated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['ToW and ToW-MIA, measured after each sequential unlearning step to track performance stability.']

risk_factors: Our benchmarking setup represents a practical compromise in terms of VT models, their sizes, and the size and complexity of datasets, allowing for insightful comparisons to CNNs, while studying fundamental approaches to MU. Nonetheless, there is much more work to follow, adding more datasets, more models and capacities, more (new and existing) algorithms, and different metrics.

title: Vision Hopfield Memory Networks
name: v-hmn
short_hypothesis: A vision foundation model that replaces conventional token-mixing operations (e.g., self-attention) with a hierarchical structure of explicit associative memory modules will be more data-efficient, interpretable, and biologically plausible. This architecture, named Vision Hopfield Memory Network (V-HMN), uses local and global Hopfield modules for memory retrieval and a predictive-coding-inspired iterative refinement rule for error correction, making memory refinement the core building block of the backbone.
related_work: Current vision foundation models, including Transformers (ViT), MLP-based models (MLP-Mixer), and state-space models (Mamba/Vim), have achieved strong performance but are often data-inefficient, lack biological plausibility, and have limited interpretability due to the absence of explicit memory mechanisms. While some recent work like the Associative Transformer (AiT) has integrated associative memory, it remains an auxiliary component within a standard Transformer architecture. The proposed work aims to address this gap by creating a memory-centric backbone where local and global Hopfield memory modules fully replace self-attention as the primary token-mixing mechanism.
abstract: Recent vision foundation backbones like Transformers and state-space models, despite their success, demand enormous training data and offer limited interpretability, diverging from the computational principles of the human brain. To address this, we propose the Vision Hopfield Memory Network (V-HMN), a brain-inspired foundation backbone integrating hierarchical memory mechanisms with iterative refinement. V-HMN incorporates local Hopfield modules for patch-level associative memory, global Hopfield modules for contextual modulation, and a predictive-coding-inspired refinement rule for error correction. By organizing these modules hierarchically, V-HMN captures both local and global dynamics, aiming to enhance interpretability and data efficiency by making memory retrieval and the reuse of stored patterns central to its design, unlike existing self-attention or state-space approaches.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the data efficiency of V-HMN by training on varying fractions of labeled data and comparing it against standard vision backbones.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on image classification tasks using CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Fashion-MNIST datasets with reduced training sets (10%, 30%, and 50% of labeled data). Performance will be compared against baselines including ViT, Swin-ViT, MLP-Mixer, MetaFormer, Vim, and AiT.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of V-HMN against a wide range of mainstream vision foundation backbones on several public image classification benchmarks.
experiments.Method: V-HMN will be evaluated on image classification using the full datasets of CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and Fashion-MNIST. Baselines for comparison will include ViT, Swin-ViT, MLP-Mixer, MetaFormer, Vim, and AiT.

experiments.Description: To assess the scalability and viability of V-HMN's core inductive bias on a large-scale, high-resolution dataset.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained and evaluated on the ImageNet-1k image classification task. Its performance will be compared to various established backbones like ResNet, ViT, MLP-Mixer, Swin-Mixer, and PVT.

experiments.Description: To perform ablation studies on the effect of the number of iterative refinement steps and its impact on robustness to corruptions.
experiments.Method: The number of refinement iterations (t) will be varied (0, 1, 2, 3) and evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Fashion-MNIST. Additionally, models trained with different numbers of iterations will be evaluated on corrupted CIFAR-10 images (Gaussian noise, random square occlusion, contrast scaling).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the effect of local and global memory bank sizes on model performance.
experiments.Method: The number of prototype slots in the local and global memory banks will be varied independently and evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Fashion-MNIST for image classification.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's robustness in a class-imbalanced setting.
experiments.Method: V-HMN will be evaluated on class-imbalanced versions of CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, with imbalance ratios of 50 and 100. Performance will be compared against ViT, Swin-ViT, MLP-Mixer, Vim, AiT, and Meta-Former.

risk_factors: unlike mature baselines that have benefited from years of iterative optimization and carefully engineered design heuristics, V-HMN has not undergone extensive hyperparameter tuning or additional engineering effort.

title: A Deep Conjugate Direction Method for Iteratively Solving Linear Systems
name: deep-conjugate-direction-method
short_hypothesis: A deep convolutional neural network can be used to approximate the action of the inverse of a linear operator, up to a scaling constant. By incorporating this network into a conjugate gradient-style iterative method to generate improved search directions, it is possible to accelerate the convergence for solving large, sparse, symmetric, positive-definite linear systems.
related_work: Classical iterative methods like Jacobi or Gauss-Seidel are often used for sparse linear systems but can suffer from large approximation errors on a limited computational budget. The Preconditioned Conjugate Gradients (PCG) method improves convergence, but designing effective preconditioners is problem-specific and can be computationally expensive. Recent data-driven approaches have focused on learning multigrid parameters, preconditioner sparsity patterns, or initial guesses for solvers. Other methods, like FluidNet, learn a direct, non-iterative approximation of the system's solution, but these cannot arbitrarily reduce the system's residual. This work proposes to bridge the gap by creating a learning-based iterative method that combines the data-driven speed of neural networks with the guaranteed convergence properties of classical solvers.
abstract: We present a novel deep learning approach to approximate the solution of large, sparse, symmetric, positive-definite linear systems of equations. These systems arise from many problems in applied science, e.g., in numerical methods for partial differential equations. Motivated by the conjugate gradients algorithm that iteratively selects search directions for minimizing the matrix norm of the approximation error, we design an approach that utilizes a deep neural network to accelerate convergence via data-driven improvement of the search directions. Our method leverages a carefully chosen convolutional network to approximate the action of the inverse of the linear operator up to an arbitrary constant. We train the network using unsupervised learning with a loss function equal to the L2 difference between an input and the system matrix times the network evaluation, where the unspecified constant in the approximate inverse is accounted for.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the convergence and performance of the proposed Deep Conjugate Direction Method (DCDM) on incompressible smoke simulation problems and compare it against classical and learning-based solvers.
experiments.Method: The method will be tested on solving the pressure Poisson equation in three incompressible smoke simulation scenarios: a smoke plume, flow past a static Stanford bunny, and flow past a dynamic rotating cube. These simulations will be run on grids with resolutions of 128^3 and 256^3. The proposed DCDM, which uses a CNN with residual connections, will be compared against Conjugate Gradients (CG), Deflated PCG, Incomplete Cholesky preconditioned CG (ICPCG), and the learning-based FluidNet method.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of iterations required to reduce the residual by four orders of magnitude', 'Time per linear solve', 'Plots of residual reduction versus iterations', 'Visual quality of the resulting simulations']

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of varying the degree of A-orthogonalization on the convergence of the DCDM solver.
experiments.Method: The DCDM algorithm will be run on the 'bunny example' linear system. The experiment will vary the `istart` parameter, which controls how many previous search directions are used for the A-orthogonalization step in each iteration.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Plots of residual reduction versus iterations for different `istart` settings']

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization capability of DCDM models trained on lower-resolution grids when applied to higher-resolution problems.
experiments.Method: A 256^3 grid simulation of the 'bunny example' will be solved using two different DCDM models: one trained on a 64^3 grid and another trained on a 128^3 grid.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Plots of residual reduction versus iterations for the 256^3 problem, comparing the performance of the model trained at 64^3 versus the one trained at 128^3']

risk_factors: Our network was designed for and trained exclusively using data related to the discrete Poisson matrix, which likely limits the generalizability of our present model.
We note that our method is unlikely to work well for matrices that have high computational cost to evaluate A*x (such as dense matrices), since training relies on efficient A*x evaluations.
An interesting question to consider is how well our method and current models would apply to discrete Poisson matrices arising from non-uniform grids, e.g., quadtrees or octrees.

title: Controlling Language and Diffusion Models by Transporting Activations
name: activation-transport
short_hypothesis: Steering generative model activations using a map derived from optimal transport (OT) theory, called Activation Transport (ACT), can provide fine-grained, modality-agnostic control over model behavior. A proposed linear variant, Linear-ACT, is hypothesized to preserve internal activation distributions, avoiding out-of-distribution issues seen in prior methods, while offering robust control via an interpretable strength parameter λ.
related_work: Previous inference-time intervention methods for controlling generative models, such as adding a constant steering vector to activations (e.g., ACTADD, CAA, ITI-C), do not preserve the model's internal activation distributions. This can shift activations out-of-distribution (OOD), leading to degraded performance, unwanted behaviors, and hindering conditioning. These methods often require extensive hyperparameter tuning and are not robust across different models, layers, or tasks. A general, robust framework that preserves distributions is needed.
abstract: The increasing capabilities of large generative models and their ever more widespread deployment have raised concerns about their reliability, safety, and potential misuse. To address these issues, recent works have proposed to control model generation by steering model activations in order to effectively induce or prevent the emergence of concepts or behaviors in the generated output. In this paper we introduce Activation Transport (ACT), a general framework to steer activations guided by optimal transport theory that generalizes many previous activation-steering works. ACT is modality-agnostic and provides fine-grained control over the model behavior with negligible computational overhead, while minimally impacting model abilities.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of Activation Transport (ACT) at mitigating toxicity in Large Language Models (LLMs).
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Gemma2-2B and Llama3-8B models. These models will be prompted with 1000 prompts from the RealToxicityPrompts (RTP) dataset to induce toxic generation. The performance of ACT (Linear-ACT and Mean-ACT) will be compared against baseline methods including AURA, ACTADD, and ITI-C.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Toxicity of generated continuations, measured by a RoBERTa-based classifier (CLS Toxicity) and by Llama3-8B-instruct as a judge (0-shot Tox.)', 'General LLM utility, measured by perplexity (PPL) on a fixed set of Wikipedia sentences.', 'Perplexity (PPL) of the generated sentences, measured with Mistral-7B.', 'MMLU 5-shot accuracy.', 'Generation diversity, measured by Self-BLEU.']

experiments.Description: To assess ACT's ability to induce arbitrary concepts in LLM generations.
experiments.Method: Using the Gemma2-2B model, an intervention will be estimated for seven different concepts (football, cloud, baby, church, book, flower, balloon) using the OneSec dataset for source (generic content) and target (specific concept) sentences. The performance of Linear-ACT and Mean-ACT will be compared against ITI-C.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Presence of the concept in the generated text, assessed in a LLM-as-a-judge manner using Llama3-8B-instruct (p(yes)).', 'Perplexity (PPL) of the generated text, measured with Mistral-7B.']

experiments.Description: To study how ACT can increase the truthfulness of LLMs.
experiments.Method: The Gemma2-2B and Llama3-8B models will be evaluated on the TruthfulQA multiple-choice benchmark. The performance of ACT (Linear-ACT and Mean-ACT) will be compared against AURA, ACTADD, and ITI-C.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['MC1 and MC2 accuracy on TruthfulQA.', 'MMLU 5-shot accuracy to control for overfitting on the TruthfulQA task.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate ACT's ability to achieve fine-grained style control in text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) and FLUX.1.Schnell models. Transport maps will be learned for various styles (e.g., anime, art nouveau, cyberpunk) using prompts from COCO Captions, where original prompts form the source distribution and style-modified prompts form the target. The method will be evaluated on a validation set from COCO Captions. ACT will be compared to an adapted version of ITI-C.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Presence of the desired style, measured by a CLIP zero-shot classifier.', 'Preservation of original prompt content, measured using CLIPScore.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate ACT's ability to perform concept negation in T2I diffusion models.
experiments.Method: Using SDXL and FLUX.1.Schnell models, transport maps will be estimated from prompts containing an unwanted concept (e.g., 'pink elephant') to prompts without it. The evaluation will use captions from the COCO validation set, modified to instruct the model to negate the concept. ACT will be compared to an adapted version of ITI-C.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Absence of the negated concept, measured by a CLIP zero-shot classifier.', 'Preservation of original prompt semantics, measured using CLIPScore.']

risk_factors: While effective, Linear-ACT assumes a linear transport between i.i.d. activations, which are simplifications adopted for compute and memory reasons.
Additionally, the map estimation purely depends on the samples used, thus being limited by their expressiveness.
Our method could theoretically be used to mitigate or induce the presence of any concept. Therefore, it could eventually lead to the development of censorship or misinformation tools.

title: Treating Neural Image Compression via Modular Adversarial Optimization: From Global Distortion to Local Artifacts
name: modular-adversarial-optimization-for-nic-attacks
short_hypothesis: A modular adversarial attack that explicitly targets both global quality and local artifacts will be more effective against modern Neural Image Compression (NIC) models. This attack, which operates by choosing between different objectives (global or local) and gradient update strategies (signed or normalized) at each iteration, and is enhanced with a frequency-domain mask for stealth, will achieve greater distortion in decoded images with lower perceptibility compared to existing methods.
related_work: Neural Image Compression (NIC) has produced state-of-the-art codecs like JPEG AI. However, their adversarial robustness is underexplored. Previous studies have shown that NICs are vulnerable to small perturbations, but existing attack methods, often adapted from other domains, are ineffective against modern codecs. These methods typically target a single global metric like PSNR and do not fully exploit the characteristics of the image compression task, failing to create significant visible artifacts or localized distortions.
abstract: The rapid progress in neural image compression (NIC) led to the deployment of advanced codecs, such as JPEG AI, which significantly outperform conventional approaches. However, despite extensive research on the adversarial robustness of neural networks in various computer vision tasks, the vulnerability of NIC models to adversarial attacks remains underexplored. Moreover, the existing adversarial attacks on NIC are ineffective against modern codecs. In this paper, we introduce a novel adversarial attack targeting NIC models. Our approach is built upon two core stages: (1) optimization of global-local distortions, and (2) a selective masking strategy that enhances attack stealthiness.

experiments.Description: To assess the effectiveness of the proposed modular adversarial attack against a wide range of Neural Image Compression (NIC) models, comparing its ability to degrade image quality and compression efficiency against baseline attack methods.
experiments.Method: The proposed attack will be evaluated against several NIC models, including classic hyperprior architectures (Ballé et al. 2018, Minnen et al. 2018), attention-based models (Cheng et al. 2020), ELIC, HiFiC, QRes-VAE, LIC-TCM, and the JPEG-AI standard (Base and High Operation Points, with and without tools). The attack will be performed on images from the KODAK PhotoCD, Cityscapes, and NIPS 2017 adversarial competition datasets. The performance will be compared against baseline attacks: FTDA, I-FGSM, and SRDA. Ablation studies are also planned to evaluate the contribution of the local optimization module (`flocal`) and the normalized gradient direction.
