Motivated by these findings, we propose an alternative method inspired by pruning techniques that directly adjusts the teacher's target layer dimensions to match those of the student, enabling direct distillation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed regressor-free distillation method by comparing the performance of student models trained with it against several baseline distillation methods on image classification tasks.
experiments.Method: Image classification will be performed on the CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet datasets. The experiments will use various teacher-student model pairs: ResNet101/ResNet18, VGG16x4/VGG11, and a modified ShuffleNetV2x2.0/ShuffleNetV2x0.5. The proposed method (ILD from a pruned teacher) will be compared against five baselines: no distillation, logit distillation, traditional ILD using a regressor (FitNet), and logit distillation from the pruned teacher.

experiments.Description: To measure and compare the amount of information contained in the intermediate feature maps of student models trained with the proposed method versus traditional ILD, using a probing analysis.
experiments.Method: The probing method will be applied to the intermediate feature maps of the teacher, an undistilled student, a student trained with traditional ILD (FitNet), and a student trained with our proposed method. A shallow (1-layer FC) and a deeper (5-layer) probing model will be attached to a frozen feature map (e.g., layer 3) to measure the amount of explicit and implicit information contained within.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to determine the importance of the teacher retraining step in the proposed method. This will be done by comparing the full method against a variant that directly reduces the teacher's feature map dimensions without a pruning and retraining phase.
experiments.Method: For the VGG model family, student models will be trained via distillation from teacher feature maps whose dimensions are reduced using several different criteria, bypassing the formal teacher pruning and retraining process. The performance of these students will be compared against the performance of students trained with the full proposed method.

risk_factors: A limitation of our study is that we evaluated only CNN models and, therefore, did not measure performance on other architectures such as Vision Transformers.
In particular, for ViT families, the structure makes it difficult to prune only specific layers of the teacher, so exploring other model families like ViT remains as a subject of future work.

title: Profile-Aware Maneuvering: A Dynamic Multi-Agent System to Robust Agentic Problem Solving
name: profile-aware-maneuvering
short_hypothesis: By reframing agent design as a control systems problem, we can create more robust and reliable multi-agent systems. Specifically, by using an offline 'System Identification' process to generate a text-based 'performance fingerprint' of an Execution Agent's failure modes, a 'Guard Agent' can be transformed into a predictive controller that preemptively counteracts errors before they occur, rather than just reactively correcting them.
related_work: Prevailing multi-agent frameworks, such as the “solver-reviewer” structure, often rely on rigid, turn-based dialogues which can lead to bloated contexts and an inability to intervene at the right moment. Other paradigms like ReAct, Reflexion, and Expel are inherently reactive or adaptive, aiming to correct errors after they manifest. This exposes a foundational gap: the absence of a control strategy for building agents that are predictably consistent, resilient, and adaptive. The proposed work shifts from building agents that learn from failure to engineering systems designed to avoid it by using an explicitly predictive, control-theoretic approach.
abstract: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has empowered intelligent agents to leverage external tools for solving complex problems, yet this reliance introduces new challenges as extended contexts and noisy tool outputs undermine system reliability. We argue that building robust agents requires the rigor of control engineering, rather than relying on empirical prompt engineering. Drawing inspiration from predictive control in vessel maneuvering, we reframe agent design as a formal control systems problem. We first establish a baseline Multi-Agent System (MAS) where a Guard Agent acts as a simple reactive feedback controller, correcting a primary Execution Agent's errors after they occur. However, this reactive approach is fundamentally limited. Our core contribution, termed Profile-Aware Maneuvering, elevates this to a predictive control architecture. Through an automated offline 'System Identification' process, we generate an explicit, text-based 'performance fingerprint' modeling the Execution Agent's characteristic failure modes. Armed with this fingerprint, the Guard Agent evolves from a reactive critic into a predictive controller. It implements a feed-forward strategy to preemptively counteract errors before they derail the reasoning process.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed control-theoretic approach on a benchmark for general-purpose AI agent capabilities, comparing it against simpler architectures.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use 109 questions from the GAIA test set (Level 1 and Level 2). Four distinct methodologies will be compared: 1) 'Base': a single Gemini 2.5 Pro model without tools; 2) 'Single Agent System (SAS)': the model paired with a system prompt and tools; 3) 'Multi-Agent System (MAS)': the SAS setup with an additional 'naive' Guard Agent for reactive supervision; 4) 'Profile-Aware MAS': the MAS setup where the Guard Agent is equipped with a 'performance fingerprint' generated offline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Pass@1 accuracy', 'Pass@3 accuracy', 'System stability (performance variance)', 'Reasoning reliability (gap between potential and single-pass performance)']

experiments.Description: To verify the broad applicability of the approach on a high-difficulty academic question-answering benchmark.
experiments.Method: A subset of 100 questions will be randomly selected from the Humanity's Last Exam (HLE) benchmark. From this subset, 10 question-answer logs will be used for the offline System Identification process to generate the Execution Agent's performance fingerprint. The remaining 90 questions will serve as the test set to evaluate and compare the performance of the Single Agent System (SAS), the Multi-Agent System (MAS), and the Profile-Aware MAS.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Pass@1 accuracy', 'Pass@3 accuracy', 'System stability (performance variance)', 'Reasoning reliability (gap between potential and single-pass performance)']

experiments.Description: To test the method's performance on a high-baseline, expert-level multiple-choice benchmark.
experiments.Method: From the GPQA Diamond benchmark, 12 questions will be randomly selected for the System Identification process. The performance of the Single Agent System (SAS) and the Profile-Aware MAS will then be compared on the remaining 186 questions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Pass@1 accuracy', 'Pass@3 accuracy', 'System stability (performance variance)', 'Reasoning reliability (gap between potential and single-pass performance)']

risk_factors: Our Profile-Aware MAS, in its current form, incurs a notable computational cost—approximately a 33% increase in end-to-end runtime and additional token consumption from the fingerprint and critiques.
A key research challenge is therefore to find the optimal balance between these costs and the significant gains in accuracy and stability.
Future work must include sensitivity analyses to establish guidelines on the sample size required for a robust 'performance fingerprint'.
A major advancement is to develop Online System Identification, allowing the Guard Agent to dynamically update an agent's profile from live interactions.
Empowering the Guard Agent with external tools (e.g., search, code interpreters) would advance it from a logic referee to an active fact-checker.

title: UGSL: A Unified Framework for Benchmarking Graph Structure Learning
name: ugsl-unified-gsl-benchmark
short_hypothesis: A unified framework, UGSL, which reformulates existing Graph Structure Learning (GSL) models into a single, modular architecture (composed of EdgeScorer, Sparsifier, Processor, and Encoder modules), will enable a comprehensive and fair comparison of their components, leading to a clearer understanding of their respective strengths and weaknesses.
related_work: Recent Graph Structure Learning (GSL) methods jointly learn a graph structure and GNN parameters, expanding GNN applicability to cases where graphs are noisy, incomplete, or absent. However, these studies use disparate experimental setups, making it difficult to compare their merits and understand which components contribute to performance. This work proposes a unified framework to address this lack of standardized benchmarking.
abstract: Previous studies on jointly learning GNN parameters and graph structure adopt different experimentation setups, making it difficult to compare their merits. To address this, we propose a benchmarking strategy for graph structure learning using a unified framework. Our framework, called Unified Graph Structure Learning (UGSL), reformulates existing models into a single model. We plan to implement a wide range of existing models in our framework and conduct extensive analyses of the effectiveness of different components to provide a clear and concise understanding of the different methods in this area as well as their strengths and weaknesses.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effectiveness of individual components of the UGSL framework in isolation by systematically varying one module at a time from a defined base model.
experiments.Method: A base model (MLP edge scorer, k-nearest neighbors sparsifier, no processor, GCN encoder, supervised classification loss) will be used for node classification on Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, Amazon Photos, Imagenet-20, and Stackoverflow datasets. Each module (Input, Edge Scorer, Sparsifier, Processor, Encoder), as well as regularizers and loss functions, will be individually ablated with different options (e.g., ATT vs MLP for Edge Scorer; GCN vs GIN for Encoder; adding unsupervised losses like denoising or contrastive).

experiments.Description: To identify the best-performing GSL architectures by exploring combinations of different modules and their hyperparameters through a large-scale random search.
experiments.Method: A random search of 30,000 trials per dataset will be conducted over the space of possible UGSL architectures, combining different choices for the Input, Edge Scorer, Sparsifier, Processor, Encoder, and loss function modules. The search will be performed on the Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, Amazon Photos, Imagenet-20, and Stackoverflow datasets.

risk_factors: GSL becomes intractable for large number of nodes, as the number of possible edges in a graph grows quadratically with the number of nodes.

In this work, we primarily focus on the node classification problem, which is the most popular task in the GSL literature (with the exception of some works such as graph classification in Sun et al. (2022)).
One promising future direction is to develop new graph statistics that are more informative about the downstream quality of GSL methods.

title: Decision boundary variability and generalization in neural networks
name: decision-boundary-variability-generalization
short_hypothesis: Neural networks with smaller algorithm decision boundary variability on the data generating distributions possess better generalization performance.
related_work: Canonical statistical complexity measures like VC-dimension and Rademacher complexity have struggled to characterize the generalization of neural networks. Recent studies have investigated decision boundaries (DBs) from the perspective of complexity or distance from data points. However, these approaches do not fully explain why networks can generalize well despite fitting noise or having small margins. This paper proposes to investigate the generalization of neural networks from a different perspective: the variability of the decision boundary itself, which is caused by randomness in the training algorithm and the training data.
abstract: This paper investigates the connection between decision boundary (DB) variability and the generalization of neural networks. Two new notions, algorithm DB variability and (ϵ, η)-data DB variability, are proposed to measure the decision boundary variability from the algorithm and data perspectives, respectively. The central hypothesis is that a neural network with lower DB variability will have better generalizability. From a theoretical view, the plan is to propose lower and upper bounds for the generalization error based on these new variability measures. A key feature of these proposed bounds is that they do not explicitly depend on the network size, which is often prohibitively large in deep learning.

experiments.Description: To explore the relationship between algorithm DB variability and generalization by evaluating different network architectures and training strategies.
experiments.Method: Train VGG-16, ResNet-18, and WideResNet-28 models on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets. Three training strategies will be compared: standard training, non-data-augmentation training, and adversarial training. To estimate the algorithm DB variability, each training setting will be repeated for 10 trials with different random seeds. The data generating distribution will be simulated using a conditional BigGAN to generate 100,000 fake images for CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.

experiments.Description: To investigate the relationship between algorithm DB variability and training time.
experiments.Method: Train 40 ResNet-18 models on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with different initial learning rates (0.1 and 0.01).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Algorithm DB variability (calculated at each epoch)', 'Test error (calculated at each epoch)']

experiments.Description: To investigate how sample size impacts algorithm DB variability.
experiments.Method: Train 100 ResNet-18 models on five training sample sets of different sizes randomly drawn from CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, while keeping other variables strictly controlled.

experiments.Description: To explore the trend of algorithm DB variability in the presence of label noise.
experiments.Method: Train 20 ResNet-10 models for 500 epochs with a constant learning rate on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets containing 20% label noise. The noise labels will remain unchanged during different training repeats.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average test error (calculated at each epoch)', 'Algorithm DB variability (calculated at each epoch)']

experiments.Description: To explore the relationship between the reconstruction error (ϵ) and the proportion of a data subset (η), as defined by (ϵ, η)-data DB variability.
experiments.Method: Train 1000 ResNet-18 networks on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with different sample sizes. For each training set, a coreset selection approach (selection via proxy) will be used to estimate η-subsets for various values of η. The network will be repeatedly trained on these subsets to estimate the reconstruction error ϵ.

risk_factors: The algorithm DB variability upper bound is easier to calculate in practice, while the data DB variability upper bound relies on less assumptions.

Moreover, in contrast to many existing generalization bounds based on hypothesis complexity that require access to the network weight, our generalization bounds based on decision boundary variability only demand access to the network predictions and thus have advantages in empirically approximating the generalization bound in (1) black-model settings, where model parameters are unavailable; and (2) over-parameterized settings, where calculating the weight norm is of prohibitively high computing burden.
Therefore, unifying the theoretical works about algorithm DB variability and data DB variability will be a promising direction in future works.

title: Cross-domain Attention for Transfer Learning between Tabular Data without Shared Features
name: CATTLE: Cross-domain Attention for Tabular Transfer Learning
short_hypothesis: Transferring the key and value projection weights from a transformer pre-trained on a source tabular dataset to a new transformer for a target dataset (with no shared features) will enable effective knowledge transfer. This cross-attention at the transformer weight level, rather than the feature level, is proposed to yield a generalized, data-agnostic context that is independent of the source domain, outperforming methods that rely on shared features or feature-level attention.
related_work: Existing transfer learning methods for tabular data, such as TransTab, typically assume shared features or feature overlaps between source and target datasets to align embeddings and transfer knowledge. Other approaches like XTab and CM2 pre-train on multiple source datasets but still struggle to outperform traditional ML models and may rely on some feature or domain overlap. This leaves a gap for a transfer learning framework that can effectively learn a generalized context between tabular datasets from completely distinct domains without any shared feature space.
abstract: Unlike images and text, transfer learning for tabular data is challenging due to heterogeneity in feature types, structures, and semantics across disparate application domains. Existing methods assume shared features across data tables to transfer knowledge, which may not hold across distinct application domains. To facilitate learning generalized context between domains without shared features, we propose a data-agnostic Cross-domain Attention Transfer Learning (CATTLE). CATTLE performs self-supervised pretraining of key, value, query projection weights of a transformer-based model using source data. Pre-trained weights from selective attention layers are used in a separate transformer model to obtain generalized context for learning the target data via cross-attention. The proposal suggests that a single tabular data set from an arbitrary domain is sufficient to learn a generalizable context for a target domain, addressing the challenge of pretraining models on many disparate and disjoint tabular data sets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed CATTLE framework on cross-domain transfer learning between tabular datasets with disjoint feature spaces. The experiment will compare CATTLE (with both self-supervised and supervised pre-training) against non-transfer learning baselines and state-of-the-art tabular transfer learning methods.
experiments.Method: Ten source-target pairs of datasets with no shared features will be created from a pool of 14 datasets from the OpenML repository (e.g., source: credit-g, target: diabetes). The CATTLE model, which uses a Gated Feature Tokenizer Transformer (gFTT), will be pre-trained on the source dataset and then fine-tuned on the corresponding target dataset. The fine-tuning process involves transferring and freezing the key (Wk) and value (Wv) projection weights from the top layer of the source model to the bottom layers of the target model. The performance of CATTLE will be compared against two groups of baselines: 1) Non-transfer methods trained directly on the target data: XGBoost, Logistic Regression, MLP, ResNet, TabNet, and FT-Transformer. 2) State-of-the-art transfer learning methods: TransTab, XTab, and CM2.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) curve', 'Accuracy (ACC)', 'Wilcoxon signed rank test for statistical comparison']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to determine the optimal strategy for transferring attention layer weights from the pre-trained source model to the downstream target model.
experiments.Method: Different combinations of transferring attention layers will be tested. Specifically, the experiment will compare transferring weights from the top layer of the source gFTT to the lowest layer(s) of the target gFTT against other layer transfer combinations. The performance will be evaluated on several source-target dataset pairs, such as credit-g → diabetes and optdigits → vehicle.

experiments.Description: To investigate the impact of the source domain's specific context on the downstream classification performance of the target data.
experiments.Method: A single target dataset (CMC) will be used. The CATTLE model will be pre-trained using self-supervision on a variety of different source datasets (e.g., cardiovascular-disease, credit-g, mfeat-fourier, etc.). The downstream classification performance on the CMC dataset will be measured for each pre-trained model to assess the variance caused by the choice of source data.

risk_factors: The selection of attention layers for weight transfer is based on prior knowledge about knowledge distribution in deep layers, which would require a theoretical underpinning.
More research is needed to optimize this framework by adopting a more systematic approach to selecting the attention layers.
It is challenging to clearly explain the effectiveness of data-agnostic transfer learning without understanding the distribution of knowledge in multi-head attention layers.

title: TokenSculpt: Pruning with Min-Max Spatio-Temporal Duplication for Video Grounding
name: tokensculpt-min-max-pruning
short_hypothesis: A novel token compression framework, TokenSculpt, which incorporates a Min-Max Duplication mechanism to explicitly retain start and end anchors along spatial and temporal axes, will better preserve spatio-temporal structure in video representations. This structure-aware approach, combined with uniform sampling and similarity-based merging, is hypothesized to mitigate issues like region collapse and structural confusion seen in prior methods, thereby improving performance on grounding and related video-language tasks without requiring additional training.
related_work: Existing visual token pruning methods fall into two main categories. Attention-based methods compute an importance score for each token but are often incompatible with modern acceleration techniques like FlashAttention and can suffer from biases (e.g., 'bottom-heavy bias'). Similarity-based methods (e.g., ToME, DART) are more efficient but can suffer from a 'huge-cluster phenomenon,' where spatially or temporally distinct but similar tokens are merged into a single point, collapsing the video's structure. The key research gap is that both approaches largely overlook the spatio-temporal structure of videos, which is critical for tasks like video grounding that depend on spatial and temporal dependencies.
abstract: Visual token pruning is essential for reducing computational overhead in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), especially for videos where visual tokens outnumber text ones. Existing pruning methods, typically based on attention or similarity, barely consider the spatiotemporal structure of videos and may incorrectly merge low-similarity or irrelevant tokens, leading to information loss. We propose TokenSculpt, a structure-aware pruning approach designed for video inputs. It aggregates tokens based on similarity while explicitly avoiding low-similarity merges, and applies a bipartite matching strategy to uniformly sample tokens across spatial and temporal dimensions. This design helps preserve the structural integrity of video representations. Additionally, TokenSculpt is compatible with Flash-Attention, enabling efficient integration into modern MLLMs. Our approach provides an efficient and scalable solution for video token pruning and improves performance in grounding and related video-language understanding tasks.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of TokenSculpt in video token pruning across a range of video grounding and video understanding tasks.
experiments.Method: The TokenSculpt method will be evaluated on a series of MLLMs (including Qwen2.5-VL and VideoChat-R1) and compared against a baseline (no pruning) and prior training-free pruning methods including DART, ToME, and FastV. The evaluation will be conducted on several benchmarks: Charades-STA, ANet-Grounding, VideoMME, EgoSchema, and Next-QA. Performance will be assessed at various token retention rates (e.g., 66%, 49%, 33%).

experiments.Description: To analyze the computational and memory efficiency of TokenSculpt under real-world constraints.
experiments.Method: The prefill time, total inference time, TFLOPS, and Cuda memory usage of TokenSculpt will be compared against ToME, DART, and the unpruned baseline on the Qwen2.5-VL 7B model. The analysis will be performed at different token retention rates (66%, 49%, 33%).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of different initial sampling strategies on model performance.
experiments.Method: Three sampling schemes will be compared within the TokenSculpt framework: the proposed uniform sampling, random sampling, and importance-based (pivot) sampling. The experiment will be run on the Qwen2.5-VL 3B model using the Charades-STA benchmark at various token retention rates.

experiments.Description: To investigate the influence of the proposed Min-Max Duplication module by observing performance when it is removed.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted by removing the Min-Max Duplication module from TokenSculpt, effectively reducing the method to a ToME-like baseline. The performance of this ablated version will be compared to the full TokenSculpt method across the main video-language tasks.

experiments.Description: To systematically investigate the impact of pruning layer selection on model performance.
experiments.Method: The experiment will apply token pruning at progressively deeper layers of the model and observe the effect on performance. This will be evaluated on the Charades-STA and EgoSchema benchmarks using Qwen2.5-VL models.

risk_factors: Our Min-Max Duplication introduces a linear merging overhead, which may incur an additional delay of about 0.6s in extremely long cases (e.g., 10,000 frames).

title: Integrating Visual Cues via Prompting for Low-Resource Multimodal Named Entity Recognition
name: prompting-for-low-resource-mner
short_hypothesis: Prompt-based conditioning can be an effective technique to incorporate visual information from images to improve Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER) in low-resource settings. This can be achieved by reformulating the MNER task as an open-ended question-answering problem, conditional on textual content, relevant images, and template-based instructions.
related_work: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a well-established NLP task. Recent work has explored Multimodal NER (MNER), which uses information from sources like images to improve accuracy. Prompt-based methods have also gained attention for NER, guiding models with questions or instructions. However, there is little prior research that investigates the details of few-shot or zero-shot learning using prompting for multimodal datasets, creating a knowledge gap this paper aims to address.
abstract: In the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), the task of Named Entity Recognition (NER) is quite established. However, most existing methods predominantly rely on textual data alone, overlooking the information that can be derived from other modalities such as images. This issue is particularly pronounced in low-resource settings, where the absence of extensive labeled data can significantly impede the performance of NER systems. Existing solutions, while attempting to address this limitation, often require comprehensive fine-tuning and are not readily applicable in such low-resource conditions. This research confronts these challenges by proposing a novel approach to Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER) under low-resource constraints. We recast the MNER task as an open-ended question-answering problem, particularly suitable for modern generative language models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed prompt-based approach for zero-shot Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER) on the Twitter2015 dataset. The experiment will assess the impact of visual information by comparing model performance with and without the accompanying image.
experiments.Method: The MNER task is reformulated as a question-answering problem. Several multimodal generative models (BLIP2-FLAN-T5-XL, BLIP2-FLAN-T5-XXL, InstructBLIP-FLAN-T5-XL, InstructBLIP-FLAN-T5-XXL) will be evaluated in a zero-shot setting on the testing split of the Twitter2015 dataset. The evaluation will use five different question prompt templates. A unimodal model (FLAN-T5) will serve as a text-only baseline. A counterfactual condition using a black image will be used to assess performance without visual information.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean micro F1 score across the five question prompt templates, using a stringent NER metric where predictions must match all tokens and the correct entity type.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed prompt-based approach for zero-shot Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER) on the Twitter2017 dataset. The experiment will assess the impact of visual information by comparing model performance with and without the accompanying image.
experiments.Method: The MNER task is reformulated as a question-answering problem. Several multimodal generative models (BLIP2-FLAN-T5-XL, BLIP2-FLAN-T5-XXL, InstructBLIP-FLAN-T5-XL, InstructBLIP-FLAN-T5-XXL) will be evaluated in a zero-shot setting on the testing split of the Twitter2017 dataset. The evaluation will use five different question prompt templates. A unimodal model (FLAN-T5) will serve as a text-only baseline. A counterfactual condition using a black image will be used to assess performance without visual information.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean micro F1 score across the five question prompt templates, using a stringent NER metric where predictions must match all tokens and the correct entity type.']

risk_factors: The limited public availability of large-scale, multimodal NER corpora represents a key challenge for advancing this research area.
The Twitter datasets used represent a narrow range of domains and entity types.
The evaluation primarily focused on exact string matching, which does not account for semantic equivalence.
The datasets used in this study may not fully encapsulate the potential of multimodal relationships due to their domain specificity and the lack of diverse visual-textual entity associations.

However, using pre-trained models, which also make use of web-corpora and have been instruction-tuned on named entity recognition, is a confounding factor for pre-trained LLMs in general, but specifically in our case.

title: ACPBench Hard: Unrestrained Reasoning about Action, Change, and Planning
name: acpbench-hard
short_hypothesis: Large Language Models' ability to reason about planning can be evaluated more effectively using a benchmark with generative, open-ended questions that mirror the atomic reasoning tasks performed by classical planners. We devise such a benchmark, ACPBench Hard, with 8 tasks and corresponding symbolic evaluators. We hypothesize that LLMs that perform well on these tasks could be integrated into a planner or used directly as a policy.
related_work: Previous work on evaluating LLMs for planning either focused on end-to-end plan generation, making it difficult to pinpoint failure reasons, or used simplified formats like boolean or multiple-choice questions (e.g., ACPBench). These formats do not reflect the generative nature of decisions planners must make. Other benchmarks like TRAC, PlanBench, and AutoPlanBench are limited in their number of domains or tasks. This work introduces a generative version of ACPBench to address these limitations, providing open-ended questions and dedicated symbolic validators for a more realistic evaluation of planning sub-tasks.
abstract: We introduce ACPBench Hard, a dataset of generative, open-ended questions which LLM models needs to answer in order to plan. Models that perform well on these tasks could in principle be integrated into a planner or be used directly as a policy. We discuss the complexity of these tasks as well as the complexity of validating the correctness of their answers and present validation algorithms for each task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of various Large Language Models (LLMs) and reasoning models on a new benchmark of generative planning tasks.
experiments.Method: A collection of 15 language and reasoning models of various sizes (Small, Medium, Large, Reasoning) will be evaluated on the ACPBench Hard dataset. This dataset contains 1040 open-ended questions across 13 PDDL domains, covering 8 distinct reasoning tasks: Applicability, Progression, Reachability, Action Reachability, Validation, Justification, Landmarks, and Next-action selection. The models to be tested include Granite 3.1 8B, Llama 3.1 8B, DeepSeek coder 33B, Mixtral 8x22B, Llama 3.1 70B, GPT-40, DeepSeek V3, o1-preview, and others. The evaluation will be conducted using a 2-shot prompting setup.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Task-wise accuracy will be used as the primary metric. The correctness of each generated answer will be determined by dedicated symbolic validation algorithms specific to each of the 8 tasks. For some tasks, this involves running a classical planner to verify the answer's validity.

experiments.Description: To investigate the impact of different input representations on an LLM's performance on a computationally hard planning task.
experiments.Method: The DeepSeek V3 model will be evaluated on the 'next action' task from ACPBench Hard. The experiment will compare three different prompt representations: 'NL' (natural language context only), 'PDDL' (PDDL domain and problem files only), and 'PDDL+NL' (a combination of both).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The model's accuracy on the 'next action' task will be compared across the three different input representation formats.

risk_factors: Captures a subset of planning problems: classical domains - closed world, deterministic dynamics, full observability, described in a standard planning language PDDL.
The evaluations only measures the final answer and do not evaluate the reasoning process of LLMs.
Generation and validation are both computationally challenging and therefore time consuming.
Requires specifying the corresponding natural language mapping of facts and actions per domain.
We use same set of prompts to evaluate all the LLM. Having different prompts and approaches might result in different performance on the benchmark.

title: Neural Bounds on Bayes Error: Advancing Classification and Generative Models
name: neural-bounds-on-bayes-error
short_hypothesis: An upper bound for Bayes error can be derived using bounds on f-divergence, and this new bound can serve as a computationally efficient and theoretically sound criterion for training neural networks for classification and for improving generative adversarial networks.
related_work: Prior research has focused on quantifying the dissimilarity between probability distributions using methods like variational inference, Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), and estimating mutual information. In the context of GANs, alternative divergence measures like Wasserstein distance have been explored to improve training stability. Other works have explored bounds for f-divergences using Fenchel conjugate functions. However, there is a need for a method that specifically establishes a practical upper bound for the Bayes error and leverages it as a direct objective for training classification and generative models.
abstract: This paper introduces a novel approach for approximating the upper limit of Bayes error in classification tasks, encompassing both binary and multi-class scenarios. Utilizing bounds on f-divergence, we establish an upper bound for Bayes error, which then serves as a novel criterion for training neural networks and classifying test data. This methodology is also extended to the realm of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), proposing a 'Bayes GAN' method rooted in the statistically optimal Bayes error.

experiments.Description: To validate the accuracy of the proposed upper bound on Bayes error in estimating the true Bayes error.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use two synthetic Gaussian distributions with differing means but identical variances. The Bayes error for these distributions will be computed in two ways: empirically, using a neural network trained with the proposed criterion, and directly, by applying the known theoretical Bayes error formula for Gaussian distributions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Comparison between the Bayes error rate estimated by the neural network model and the rate calculated directly from the theoretical Bayes error formula.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method for Bayes error estimation on a real-world classification task.
experiments.Method: A neural network will be developed using PyTorch to compute the Bayes error and create a classifier for the MNIST dataset. The model will be evaluated on binary classification tasks (e.g., distinguishing digits like '9' and '4') and will also be extended to handle three-class problems.

experiments.Description: To apply the Bayes error estimation method to improve Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs).
experiments.Method: A 'Bayes GAN' will be developed where the discriminator's objective is modified to minimize the proposed upper bound for Bayes error. This model will be trained to generate images from the MNIST dataset. The performance will be compared against the original GAN method by Goodfellow et al.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Fluctuation of FID scores over training epochs']

title: Rethinking Diffusion Model in High Dimension
name: diffusion-degradation-and-natural-inference
short_hypothesis: We argue that diffusion models do not learn statistical quantities such as posterior, score, or velocity field; instead, they operate via a different mechanism where the objective function degrades and inference becomes a non-statistical process.
related_work: Conventional diffusion models, including Markov Chain-based (DDPM), SDE-based, and flow matching approaches, are formulated under the assumption that the model can learn essential statistical quantities of the data distribution (e.g., posterior probability, score, or velocity field). However, this assumption contradicts the traditional understanding that in high-dimensional sparse scenarios, the curse of dimensionality prevents machine learning models from effectively learning complex hidden probability distributions.
abstract: While diffusion models achieve impressive results in high-dimensional data generation, their underlying mechanism is debated. Standard diffusion models assume they learn statistical quantities of the data distribution to generate samples. We argue this is not how they work. We propose that in high-dimensional sparse scenarios, the fitting target of the diffusion model's objective function degrades from a weighted sum of multiple samples to a single sample, hindering the model's ability to learn these statistical quantities. We also propose that most inference methods can be unified within a simple, non-statistical framework that aligns with this degraded objective, providing a new perspective on the inference process.

experiments.Description: To analyze the proposed 'weighted sum degradation' phenomenon in the diffusion model's objective function under high-dimensional, sparse conditions.
experiments.Method: The plan is to use conditional ImageNet-256 and ImageNet-512 datasets, with VAE compression. For various noise levels (t), a noisy sample Xt will be generated from a data sample Xo. The posterior probability p(xo|xt) will then be analyzed to determine if it is 'degraded', defined as a single training sample X_i' having a posterior probability greater than 0.9. This analysis will be conducted for two different noise mixing schemes: VP (from DDPM/SDE models) and Flow Matching.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Proportion of weighted sum degradation', 'Proportion of weighted sum degradation to the original sample Xo']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that existing diffusion sampling methods can be unified under a proposed 'Natural Inference' framework, which reinterprets inference as a non-statistical, autoregressive process of predicting the original data x0.
experiments.Method: The experiment plans to mathematically reformulate the iterative procedures of various first-order and higher-order sampling methods. These include DDPM Ancestral Sampling, DDIM, ODE Euler, SDE Euler, Flow Matching ODE Euler, DPMSOLVER, DPMSOLVER++, and DEIS. For each method, the goal is to show that its updates can be expressed in the Natural Inference form by deriving its 'equivalent marginal signal coefficient' and 'equivalent marginal noise coefficient' and comparing them to the ideal target coefficients. Symbolic computation software is proposed to handle the complex derivations for higher-order methods.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Approximation error between the sampler's 'equivalent marginal coefficients' and the 'ideal marginal coefficients' (sqrt(α_t) and sqrt(1-α_t))"]

risk_factors: Furthermore, due to limited sampling during training, each p(xo|xt = Xt) cannot be sufficiently sampled, so the actual degradation ratio should be higher than the statistics show.
Within this framework, other, potentially more optimal parameter configurations may exist that can generate higher-quality samples. Exploring these possibilities could be a direction for future work.

title: Computational Bottlenecks for Denoising Diffusions
name: computational-bottlenecks-diffusion
short_hypothesis: For certain probability distributions that are easy to sample from but exhibit an information-computation gap in their associated denoising problem, diffusion models will fail to generate correct samples. This failure is proposed to occur even for drift functions that are near-optimal for the score-matching objective and computable in polynomial time, and will also occur for any such drift that is also Lipschitz continuous.
related_work: Diffusion sampling (DS) is a central paradigm in generative AI. However, its fundamental limitations are not fully understood. Prior work has identified statistical estimation problems with "information-computation gaps," where polynomial-time algorithms are conjectured to be suboptimal. It has been informally suggested that these gaps could cause DS to fail, and empirical failures have been observed for specific physical models, but no formal proof or general theory existed. This paper aims to formalize this connection, proving that such computational gaps in the underlying denoising task lead to a failure of diffusion-based sampling.
abstract: Denoising diffusions sample from a probability distribution by constructing a stochastic process whose drift is learned by minimizing a score-matching objective. This work investigates whether any tractable distribution is amenable to sampling via diffusions. The question is studied by relating it to information-computation gaps in statistical estimation, where for some distributions, approximating the drift is conjectured to be computationally intractable despite sampling being easy. The paper proposes to prove that this intractability implies a failure of diffusion sampling. The claims are that there exist drifts whose score matching objective is nearly optimal among polynomial-time drifts yet produce incorrect samples, and that any polynomial-time, Lipschitz drift also results in incorrect sampling.

experiments.Description: To empirically test the theoretical prediction that diffusion sampling will fail when applied to a distribution with an information-computation gap. The experiment aims to verify two specific predictions: 1) A learned polynomial-time denoiser will achieve low Mean Squared Error (MSE) only for diffusion times above the algorithmic threshold (t_alg), and 2) A diffusion sampler using this denoiser will generate samples concentrated around zero, far from the target distribution.
experiments.Method:
The task is to generate sparse rank-one matrices from the distribution μ_n,k = (1/2)δ_0 + (1/2)μ'_n,k, with n=350 and k=20. Three different polynomial-time denoisers will be considered:
1. A spectral-plus-projection denoiser (from Algorithm 2).
2. A modification of the spectral denoiser where the eigenvector calculation is replaced by 25 iterations of the power method.
3. A learned Graph Neural Network (GNN) denoiser.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the denoisers as a function of diffusion time t.', 'Histogram of the Frobenius norms of the generated samples.', 'Wasserstein-1 distance between the distribution of generated samples and the target distribution.']

risk_factors: In principle there could still be non-Lipschitz polytime drifts that are near score matching optimizers and sample well. However if such drifts exist, our results suggest that minimizing the score matching objective is not the right approach to find them (since the difference in value with bad drifts will be superpolynomially small).
Corollary 5.1 does not rule out the possibility that there exists a near-optimizer of score matching that violates the Lipschitz condition and samples well.

title: Neural Collapse in Multi-Task Learning
name: neural-collapse-in-mtl
short_hypothesis: Neural collapse phenomena will manifest in multi-task learning, where task-specific classifiers and features converge to specific geometric structures (Simplex Equiangular Tight Frames) with distinct configurations for Single-Source (SSMTC) and Multi-Source (MSMTC) scenarios.
related_work: Existing empirical and theoretical studies of Neural Collapse (NC) focus on single-task learning. The behavior and characteristics of NC in multi-task learning settings have not been previously studied.
abstract: Neural collapse (NC) plays a key role in understanding deep neural networks. However, existing empirical and theoretical studies of NC focus on one single task. This paper studies neural collapse in multi-task learning. We consider two standard feature-based multi-task learning scenarios: Single-Source Multi-Task Classification (SSMTC) and Multi-Source Multi-Task Classification (MSMTC).

experiments.Description: To investigate the geometric structure of the final layer classifier and features in the Single-Source Multi-Task Classification (SSMTC) setting under the neural collapse phenomenon.
experiments.Method: Train a model on a Single-Source Multi-Task Classification (SSMTC) task and analyze the geometry of the learned task-specific classifiers and shared features.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Convergence of task-specific classifiers to task-specific ETFs', 'Mutual orthogonality of task-specific ETFs', 'Convergence of shared features to the scaled sum of task-specific label weight vectors']

experiments.Description: To investigate the geometric structure of the final layer classifier and features in the Multi-Source Multi-Task Classification (MSMTC) setting under the neural collapse phenomenon.
experiments.Method: Train a model on a Multi-Source Multi-Task Classification (MSMTC) task and analyze the geometry of the learned task-specific classifiers and features.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Convergence of task-specific linear classifiers and features to a Simplex Equiangular Tight Frame (ETF)']

title: DOTS: Learning to Reason Dynamically in LLMs via Optimal Reasoning Trajectories Search
name: dots-dynamic-reasoning
short_hypothesis: By defining atomic reasoning actions, searching for optimal reasoning trajectories for each training question, and then fine-tuning an LLM to plan these trajectories, it is possible to enable LLMs to dynamically select the most effective reasoning strategy for a given question and its own capabilities, improving reasoning performance over static, predefined strategies.
related_work: Prior work in enhancing LLM reasoning relies on instruction tuning or prompt engineering strategies like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program-of-Thought (PoT). These approaches typically apply a single, static reasoning strategy uniformly to all questions, regardless of the specific problem characteristics or the inherent capabilities of the task-solving LLM. This static application is a critical limitation, as different questions and models may benefit from different reasoning approaches. The proposed research aims to address this gap by enabling LLMs to dynamically decide the best reasoning strategy for each unique instance.
abstract: Enhancing the capability of large language models (LLMs) in reasoning has gained significant attention in recent years. Previous studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of various prompting strategies in aiding LLMs in reasoning (called "reasoning actions"), such as step-by-step thinking, reflecting before answering, solving with programs, and their combinations. However, these approaches often applied static, predefined reasoning actions uniformly to all questions, without considering the specific characteristics of each question or the capability of the task-solving LLM. In this paper, we propose DoTs, an approach enabling LLMs to reason Dynamically via Optimal reasoning Trajectories Search, tailored to the specific characteristics of each question and the inherent capability of the task-solving LLM. Our approach involves three key steps: i) defining atomic reasoning action modules that can be composed into various reasoning action trajectories; ii) searching for the optimal action trajectory for each training question through iterative exploration and evaluation for the specific task-solving LLM; and iii) using the collected optimal trajectories to train an LLM to plan for the reasoning trajectories of unseen questions. In particular, we propose two learning paradigms, i.e., fine-tuning an external LLM as a planner to guide the task-solving LLM, or directly fine-tuning the task-solving LLM with an internalized capability for reasoning actions planning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the DOTS method using an external planner model to guide a separate, more powerful task-solving LLM on in-distribution and few-shot reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: An external planner model (Llama-3-8B-Instruct) will be fine-tuned to predict optimal reasoning trajectories for a separate task-solving LLM (Llama-3-70B-Instruct and GPT-4o-mini). The evaluation will be conducted on the MATH dataset for in-distribution performance, and on BBH, Game of 24, and TheoremQA for few-shot performance. The method will be compared against baselines including CoT, Least-to-most (LTM), PA (PromptAgent), PoT, and Self-refine.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the DOTS method by fine-tuning a single open-source LLM to internalize the capability of both planning the reasoning trajectory and solving the task.
experiments.Method: A single task-solving LLM (Llama-3-8B-Instruct) will be fine-tuned to simultaneously plan the trajectory and perform the reasoning task. The evaluation will be conducted on the MATH dataset (in-distribution) and on BBH, Game of 24, and TheoremQA (few-shot). This approach will be compared against baselines including CoT, LTM, PA, PoT, Self-refine, and Vanilla Supervised Fine-Tuning (Vanilla SFT).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization ability of both the external and internalized planner versions of the DOTS method on reasoning tasks not seen during training.
experiments.Method: The trained models from the external planner (Llama-3-8B-Instruct planner with Llama-3-70B-Instruct/GPT-4o-mini solvers) and internalized planner (Llama-3-8B-Instruct) setups will be tested on the Deepmind Math, MMLU-pro, StrategyQA, and DROP datasets. Performance will be compared against the same set of baselines (CoT, LTM, PA, PoT, Self-refine, and Vanilla SFT).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to assess the individual contributions of the key components of the DOTS framework: the optimal trajectory search process and the generation of explanations for the chosen trajectory.
experiments.Method: The DOTS method will be evaluated in two degraded settings: 1) 'Without Searching', where the planner is tuned with randomly selected action trajectories instead of optimally searched ones, and 2) 'Without Explanation', where the planner is trained to predict the trajectory without an accompanying explanation. These ablations will be tested on both the external and internalized planner setups across the MATH, BBH, Game of 24, and TheoremQA datasets.

risk_factors: We observe that introducing excessive reasoning actions can lead to increased latency, and even sometimes result in incorrect answers.

title: Masked Frequency Modeling for Self-Supervised Visual Pre-Training
name: masked-frequency-modeling
short_hypothesis: Predicting masked components in the frequency domain is a more ideal pretext task for self-supervised visual representation learning than predicting masked patches in the spatial domain. This is because it better reveals underlying image patterns, avoids issues with heavy spatial redundancy, and creates a nontrivial and meaningful self-supervisory task. This approach, Masked Frequency Modeling (MFM), should be flexible enough to work with both ViT and CNN architectures without requiring extra data, extra models, or mask tokens.
related_work: Current Masked Image Modeling (MIM) methods, inspired by Masked Language Modeling (MLM) in NLP, perform masking in the spatial domain by randomly excluding image patches. However, raw pixel values in images have low information density and heavy spatial redundancy, which forces methods like MAE to use a very high masking ratio to learn meaningful features. This paper explores alternative corruption strategies, including those from low-level image processing (super-resolution, deblurring, denoising), and proposes shifting the masking and prediction paradigm from the spatial domain to the frequency domain. The frequency domain's properties, where low-frequency components contain structure and style and high-frequency components depict outlines, make it appealing for reducing information redundancy and creating a more effective self-supervised task.
abstract: We present Masked Frequency Modeling (MFM), a unified frequency-domain-based approach for self-supervised pre-training of visual models. Instead of randomly inserting mask tokens to the input embeddings in the spatial domain, we shift the perspective to the frequency domain. Specifically, MFM first masks out a portion of frequency components of the input image and then predicts the missing frequencies on the frequency spectrum. Our key insight is that predicting masked components in the frequency domain is more ideal to reveal underlying image patterns rather than predicting masked patches in the spatial domain, due to the heavy spatial redundancy. We propose that with the right configuration of a mask-and-predict strategy, both structural and statistical information from different frequency components can be useful for learning good representations. MFM is designed as a simple non-Siamese framework that can learn meaningful representations for both ViT and CNN without extra data, extra models, or mask tokens.

experiments.Description: To investigate the effect of different MFM design choices through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: Pre-train a ViT-B/16 model on ImageNet-1K for 300 epochs. Ablate on: (a) mask type (none, low-pass, high-pass, random), (b) mask radius, (c) mask shape (circle, square, rhombus), (d) sampling ratio for low/high-pass filters, (e) reconstruction target (masked spectrum vs. full spectrum), and (f) loss function (frequency loss vs. spatial l1/l2 loss).

experiments.Description: To study the representation learning capability of low-level image processing tasks (super-resolution, deblurring, denoising) from a unified frequency perspective and compare them with MFM.
experiments.Method: Pre-train ViT-B/16 models on ImageNet-1K for 300 epochs using pretext tasks of image super-resolution (SR), deblurring, and denoising with varying degradation levels. The reconstruction target for all tasks is the original image, evaluated in the frequency domain using the same frequency loss as MFM.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of MFM pre-trained ViT models on a downstream image classification task and compare against other self-supervised methods.
experiments.Method: Pre-train ViT-S/16 and ViT-B/16 models on ImageNet-1K using MFM. Fine-tune the models on ImageNet-1K classification. Compare against baselines including training from scratch, MoCo v3, DINO, BEiT, MAE, and pretext tasks based on SR, Deblur, and Denoise.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the architectural flexibility of MFM by evaluating its performance on a ResNet-50 model for a downstream image classification task.
experiments.Method: Pre-train a ResNet-50 model on ImageNet-1K using MFM. Fine-tune the model on ImageNet-1K classification. Compare against supervised baselines (RSB) and other self-supervised methods like SimSiam, MoCo v2, SimCLR, BYOL, and SWAV.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of learned representations from MFM on the downstream task of semantic segmentation.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune a pre-trained ViT-B/16 model on the ADE20K semantic segmentation benchmark using the UperNet framework. Compare against baselines including Supervised, MoCo v3, DINO, BEiT, MAE, and pretext tasks based on SR, Deblur, and Denoise.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of MFM-pre-trained models on adversarial attacks, common corruptions, and out-of-distribution datasets.
experiments.Method: Evaluate the fine-tuned ViT-B/16 and ResNet-50 models on a series of robustness benchmarks without specialized fine-tuning. The benchmarks include: (i) adversarial robustness (FGSM, PGD, ImageNet-A), (ii) common corruption robustness (ImageNet-C), and (iii) out-of-distribution robustness (ImageNet-R, ImageNet-Sketch). Compare against Scratch, MAE, and SimMIM baselines.

risk_factors: We mainly focus on the architectural flexibility and universality of MFM, while leaving the scaling behaviour under-explored.

We mainly evaluate the quality of learned representations on representative benchmarks, i.e., ImageNet-1K image classification and ADE20K semantic segmentation, following Bao et al. (2022). The transferability on more downstream tasks can be studied in future research.
Despite the intriguing properties of the Fourier domain, the redundancy in frequencies may still exist. More advanced information suppression strategies can be further explored.

title: Frequency Decomposition and Enhancement for Time Series Generation Using Diffusion Models
name: fdediff-frequency-decomposition-diffusion
short_hypothesis: A multi-stage diffusion framework, which explicitly decomposes time series into low- and high-frequency signals and models them sequentially, will generate more accurate, realistic, and diverse time series. This is achieved by first training an unconditional generator on coarse, low-frequency signals, and then using an enhancement mechanism to synthesize precise high-frequency details, conditioned on the generated low-frequency components.
related_work: Prior work in synthetic time series generation includes classical frameworks like VAEs (e.g., TimeVAE) and GANs (e.g., TimeGAN). More recently, diffusion models have been adapted for time series tasks, showing strong performance. Examples include Diffusion-TS, which uses a Transformer architecture with seasonal/trend predictors, and MR-Diff, which employs a multi-resolution strategy. However, two key challenges remain for diffusion models: 1) unconditionally generating time series with diverse seasonal, trend, and residual components is difficult, and 2) high-frequency components often vanish during the diffusion process, leading to overly smoothed outputs. The proposed method aims to address these limitations.
abstract: Time series data are essential in many domains, but the scarcity of high-quality training datasets often restricts machine learning performance. Synthetic data generation via diffusion models is a promising strategy, but these models face challenges in capturing diverse frequency components and retaining high-frequency details. To address these issues, we propose a multi-stage diffusion framework named Frequency Decomposed and Enhanced Diffusion (FDEDiff). This method explicitly decomposes time series into low- and high-frequency signals. It first trains an unconditional generator on coarse, periodic low-frequency signals, then incorporates an enhancement mechanism to synthesize precise high-frequency details. This two-stage approach is designed to systematically handle complex temporal variations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generation quality of the proposed FDEDiff model against state-of-the-art unconditional time series generation models across various real-world datasets and window lengths.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on four public datasets: ETTh, Stocks, fMRI, and Electricity. For the Electricity dataset, PCA will be applied to reduce dimensionality to the 30 most informative components. Each dataset will be segmented using sliding windows of varying lengths (24, 64, 96, 192, 336). The proposed model, FDEDiff, will be compared against three representative baseline models: Diffusion-TS, TimeVAE, and TimeGAN.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Context-Fréchet Inception Distance (C-FID)', 'Auto Correlation Difference (ACD)', 'Cross Correlation Difference (CCD)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to evaluate the contribution of the frequency-domain decomposition and enhancement mechanism to the model's performance, specifically by analyzing the impact of its two key hyperparameters: the low-frequency ratio (α) and the high-frequency enhancement factor (λ).
experiments.Method: The study will be performed on the ETTh dataset with window lengths of 96 and 192. The impact of the low-frequency ratio α will be evaluated by varying its value while keeping λ fixed. Similarly, the impact of the high-frequency enhancement factor λ will be assessed by varying its value while keeping α fixed. The performance will be compared to configurations where the mechanism is disabled (α=1) or only partially active (λ=1).

risk_factors: First, it can only generate fixed-length segments matching the training data and does not support arbitrary-length generation.
Second, due to the lack of explicit modeling for dependencies among multiple variables, FDEDiff performs poorly on high-dimensional generation tasks.
Finally, FDEDiff does not support conditional generation, making it difficult to control the type or characteristics of the generated time series.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks with Learnable Loss Balancing and Transfer Learning
name: pinn-learnable-loss-balancing-transfer-learning
short_hypothesis: A self-supervised PINN framework with a learnable blending neuron can dynamically balance physics residuals and data losses based on their uncertainties. Furthermore, incorporating a transfer learning scheme that reuses hidden-layer representations from related domains will accelerate training and improve performance in new physical systems with scarce data.
related_work: Prior work on Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) has explored adaptive weighting strategies for the data and physics loss terms, including gradient-based methods, Neural Tangent Kernel analyses, and Bayesian approaches. However, these methods often introduce heuristic rules, computational complexity, or Bayesian overhead. Separately, transfer learning has been applied to accelerate CFD surrogates, but its integration with PINNs remains underexplored. This work aims to bridge this gap by unifying adaptive loss balancing and transfer learning into a single, lightweight framework for data-scarce scientific machine learning problems.
abstract: We propose a self-supervised physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework that adaptively balances physics-based and data-driven supervision for scientific machine learning under data scarcity. Unlike prior PINNs that rely on fixed or heuristic weighting of physics residuals and data loss, our approach introduces a learnable blending neuron that dynamically adjusts the relative contribution of each term based on their uncertainties. This mechanism enables stable training and improved generalization without manual tuning. To further enhance efficiency, we integrate a transfer learning strategy that reuses representations from related domains and adapts them to new physical systems with limited data. We plan to validate the framework for the prediction of heat transfer in liquid-metal miniature heat sinks using only 87 CFD datapoints. Our framework provides a general recipe for embedding physics adaptively into neural networks, offering a robust and reproducible approach for data-scarce problems across various scientific domains.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed self-supervised adaptive PINN framework on a challenging data-scarce problem and benchmark its performance against classical and neural network baselines.
experiments.Method: The task is to predict Nusselt numbers for heat transfer in liquid-metal (sodium-cooled) miniature heat sinks using a dataset of 87 CFD datapoints. The proposed self-supervised PINN will be evaluated against several baselines: shallow neural networks, kernel methods (Gaussian Process and Support Vector Regression with RBF kernels), and physics-only models. The robustness of the models will be assessed using Monte Carlo cross-validation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)', 'Prediction variance on holdout datasets']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of integrating transfer learning into the PINN framework for improving performance on a target task with scarce data.
experiments.Method: A base PINN will be pre-trained on a source dataset (e.g., water-cooled microchannels). The parameters from its early layers will be used to initialize a target PINN, which will then be fine-tuned on the target task of predicting Nusselt numbers for sodium-cooled miniature heat sinks (87 CFD points). The performance will be compared to a baseline neural network trained from scratch on the target data without transfer learning.

risk_factors: Future work includes extending the blending mechanism to multi-physics scenarios, incorporating uncertainty quantification, and scaling the framework to large-scale 3D CFD simulations.

title: Fantastic Rewards and How to Tame Them: A Case Study on Reward Learning for Task-oriented Dialogue Systems
name: Learning-to-Rank-Inspired-Reward-Learning-for-ToD
short_hypothesis: Introducing generalized reward-learning objectives (RewardNet and RewardMLE), inspired by learning-to-rank literature, will enable learning a more effective reward function from multiple dialogue trajectories simultaneously. This learned reward function can then be used to guide the training of an end-to-end task-oriented dialogue agent, and its effectiveness can be further improved by using a stable policy gradient update method like the Gumbel-softmax trick to mitigate high variance.
related_work: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a natural approach for training task-oriented dialogue (ToD) systems. However, prior works often rely on manually designed, sparse reward functions (e.g., binary success/failure), which makes training challenging and can lead to poor performance. While some recent works learn reward functions from preferences, they typically use a simple pairwise comparison of trajectories. The open question is how to properly design and learn a reward function for offline RL in E2E ToD systems. This work proposes to move beyond simple pairwise learning by using listwise objectives inspired by learning-to-rank, which can incorporate preference information from multiple trajectories at once to learn a better reward model.
abstract: When learning task-oriented dialogue (ToD) agents, reinforcement learning (RL) techniques can naturally be utilized to train dialogue strategies to achieve user-specific goals. Prior works mainly focus on adopting advanced RL techniques to train the ToD agents, while the design of the reward function is not well studied. This paper aims at answering the question of how to efficiently learn and leverage a reward function for training end-to-end (E2E) ToD agents. Specifically, we introduce two generalized objectives for reward-function learning, inspired by the classical learning-to-rank literature. Further, we propose to utilize the learned reward function to guide the training of the E2E ToD agent.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed reward learning objectives (RewardNet, RewardMLE) and policy gradient update method (Gumbel-softmax) on the end-to-end response generation task against existing state-of-the-art models.
experiments.Method: The proposed methods will be evaluated on the E2E response-generation task using the MultiWOZ 2.0 dataset. The models, built on the MinTL backbone, will be compared against a set of baselines including SFN + RL, DAMD, SimpleTOD, MinTL, SOLOIST, UBAR, GPT-Critic, and CASPI.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed methods in low-data regimes, using smaller fractions of the training data.
experiments.Method: The basic RewardNet and RewardMLE models will be trained on 5%, 10%, and 20% of the MultiWOZ 2.0 training data. Performance will be compared against the MinTL and DAMD baselines under the same low-resource conditions.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the impact of key components and hyperparameters of the proposed methods.
experiments.Method: A series of ablations will be performed on the MultiWOZ 2.0 dataset to analyze: 1) the effect of varying the number of trajectories used for reward learning (N), 2) the choice of different probabilistic transform functions (Φ), 3) the sensitivity to the policy gradient loss coefficient (α), and 4) the performance impact of adding the Gumbel-softmax (GS) trick. A toy experiment will also be conducted to compare the stability of the GS trick against the classical REINFORCE method.

experiments.Description: To conduct a human evaluation to assess the quality of the dialogue responses generated by the proposed model compared to strong baselines.
experiments.Method: Human evaluators will score generated responses for 50 randomly selected turns from the MultiWOZ 2.0 test set. The proposed model's outputs will be compared against those from GPT-Critic and CASPI.

experiments.Description: To test the efficacy and generalizability of the proposed methods on an additional, related dataset.
experiments.Method: The proposed methods (RewardNet + GS and RewardMLE + GS) will be evaluated on the E2E response-generation task on the MultiWOZ 2.1 dataset. They will be compared against SimpleTOD, UBAR, and CASPI baselines.

risk_factors: It is important to note that, like other ToD models, our implementation will likely reflect the socio-economic and entity biases inherent in the MultiWOZ dataset (Qian et al., 2021).
We initialize model parameters from the pre-trained BART model. And a comprehensive analysis of certain biases captured by BART is outside our scope.

title: SVD Provably Denoises Nearest Neighbor Data
name: svd-denoising-for-nns
short_hypothesis: Applying Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) to high-dimensional data corrupted by Gaussian noise can provably recover the true nearest neighbor from the original, uncorrupted data. This data-aware projection approach is hypothesized to succeed even in high-noise regimes where the nearest neighbor in the noisy data differs from the true one, a situation where methods based on random projections or prior art would fail.
related_work: Prior work on Nearest Neighbor Search (NNS) in high dimensions often relies on oblivious random projections (e.g., Locality-Sensitive Hashing), which can fail when noise dominates the signal. While data-adaptive methods like PCA are used in practice, they often lack theoretical guarantees. A notable theoretical work by Abdullah et al. (2014) addresses noisy NNS but requires the noise level to be very low (inverse polynomial in the high ambient dimension *d*) and operates in a regime where the nearest neighbor is preserved despite the noise. This leaves a gap for a method that can provably handle higher noise levels—specifically, noise that depends on the low intrinsic dimension *k*—and can recover the true nearest neighbor even when the noise is large enough to change the nearest neighbor in the observed data.
abstract: We study the Nearest Neighbor Search (NNS) problem in a high-dimensional setting where data originates from a low-dimensional subspace and is corrupted by Gaussian noise. Specifically, we consider a semi-random model where n points from an unknown k-dimensional subspace of R^d (k << d) are perturbed by zero-mean d-dimensional Gaussian noise with variance σ^2 on each coordinate. We assume we are given only the noisy data and are required to find the nearest neighbor of the uncorrupted data. We propose that simply performing SVD on the noisy data can denoise it and allow for provable recovery of the accurate nearest neighbor of the uncorrupted data, even in a regime where it is different from the nearest neighbor in the noisy data.

experiments.Description: To empirically evaluate the proposed SVD-based algorithm's performance in recovering the true nearest neighbor from noisy data and compare it against a naive baseline on real-world datasets.
experiments.Method: The algorithm is to be evaluated on the GloVe and MNIST datasets. For the experiment, n=3000 points are sampled, a rank-k approximation is performed with k=30, and the data is preprocessed to ensure a distance gap of 1+ε (ε=0.05). The proposed SVD-based algorithm (Algorithm 1) is to be compared against a 'naïve' baseline that finds the nearest neighbor directly in the noisy data space by minimizing ||Axj||.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success probability, to be computed over 100 queries for a range of noise levels (σ).']

experiments.Description: To empirically verify the theoretical analysis that the algorithm's tolerable noise level (σ) has a linear dependence on the k-th singular value of the data matrix, sk(B).
experiments.Method: The experiment is to use synthetically generated data where the singular values of the data matrix B can be explicitly controlled. The data is to be constructed using an SVD-based approach with parameters n=200, d=100, k=10, and ε=0.05.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The noise threshold, defined as the value of σ at which the success probability first drops below 90%. This threshold will be measured for different settings of sk(B) to analyze their relationship.']

risk_factors: It is not clear that this [splitting the data into 2 parts] is necassary and we leave it as an open question as to whether the simpler algorithm without splitting provably works.
In our setting, we need to know k to run our algorithm.
Note that this does not imply the optimality of our algorithm with respect to sk(B).

title: pSAE-chiatry: Utilizing Sparse Autoencoders to Uncover Mental-Health-Related Features in Language Models
name: sae-for-mhrf-identification
short_hypothesis: Sparse autoencoders, combined with psychiatric expertise, can be used to identify specific, granular mental-health-related features (MHRFs) within a language model's internal representations. Modulating these identified features will causally impact the model's behavior related to those concepts.
related_work: Prior work has used sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for interpretability and to find general safety-relevant features in language models, including some related to "mental health". However, no current work utilizes sparse autoencoders to take a more granular approach to the identification of specific mental-health-related features (MHRFs) in language models. This study aims to fill that gap by using publicly available SAEs (GemmaScope) to explore specific features related to mental health crises like suicidality, mania, or psychosis in the Gemma-2-2B model.
abstract: As AI-powered mental health chatbots become more prevalent, their inability to recognize and respond to psychiatric emergencies, such as suicidality and mania, raises significant safety concerns. This study proposes to explore the internal representations of mental-health-related features (MHRF) in the Gemma-2-2B language model, focusing on crises related to suicide, mania, and psychosis. The plan is to use sparse autoencoders and psychiatric expertise to identify MHRFs across the model's layers. Further, the study will investigate a specific identified feature by assessing its activation with targeted prompts and causally steering the model to observe changes in behavior, aiming to underscore the need for improved feature identification and modulation within AI models to enhance their safety and effectiveness in mental healthcare applications.

experiments.Description: To identify Mental-Health-Related Features (MHRF) within the Gemma-2-2B language model across its layers.
experiments.Method: Using the GemmaScope-RES-16K sparse autoencoder, search across all 25 layers of the Gemma-2-2B model for features related to the terms: 'suicide', 'sad', 'mania', 'manic', 'paranoia', and 'paranoid'. The search is guided by psychiatric expertise.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Qualitative analysis of the identified features' descriptions.", 'Recording the presence or absence of features for each search term per layer.']

experiments.Description: To assess if an identified suicide-related feature is preferentially activated by prompts related to suicide compared to homicide.
experiments.Method: Select a specific suicide-related feature (Feature 15435 in Layer 25). Present the Gemma-2-2B model with four distinct prompts: two detailing suicidal ideation ('How can I commit suicide?', 'How can I kill myself?') and two detailing homicidal ideation ('How can I kill someone else?', 'How can I commit murder?').
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Comparison of the activation levels of the target feature in response to each prompt.']

experiments.Description: To test whether causally intervening on an identified suicide-related feature impacts the model's generated output.
experiments.Method: Using Neuronpedia's steering tool, the base Gemma-2-2B model is compared against versions where a specific suicide-related feature (Feature 15435, Layer 25) is amplified (at strengths +1.0, +5.0, +10.0, +100.0) or clamped/inhibited (at strengths -10.0, -100.0). All models are prompted with 'I'm so sad that I might commit'.
