experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative comparison of the text completions generated by the base model versus the steered models.']

risk_factors: Bad actors may choose to identify harmful mental-health-related features, amplify them, and generate models that are harmful, deceptive, or inaccurate.

title: Substructured Graph Convolution for Non-overlapping Graph Decomposition
name: substructured-graph-convolution
short_hypothesis: A novel substructured graph convolution can reinforce the interface information lost during non-overlapping graph decomposition. By adding a substructure using the interface adjacency matrix and performing a weighted sum with the interior block-diagonal part, it is possible to improve performance while maintaining a parallel structure.
related_work: Training GCNs on large graphs is challenging due to memory and time constraints. Existing solutions include graph sampling methods (e.g., GraphSAGE, FastGCN) and graph decomposition methods (e.g., ClusterGCN). Graph decomposition allows for parallel computation by partitioning the graph into subgraphs, but this leads to information loss at the interface between subgraphs. As the number of subgraphs increases, this information loss becomes more severe, negatively affecting network training. This work is motivated by substructuring methods from numerical analysis, which use information from the interface part to improve performance.
abstract: Graph convolutional networks have been widely used to solve the graph problems such as node classification, link prediction, and recommender systems. It is well known that large graphs require large amount of memory and time to train graph convolutional networks. To deal with large graphs, many methods are being done, such as graph sampling or decomposition. In particular, graph decomposition has the advantage of parallel computation, but information loss occurs in the interface part. In this paper, we propose a novel substructured graph convolution that reinforces the interface part lost by graph decomposition.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed substructured graph convolution on transductive node classification tasks and compare it with existing decomposition-based training methods.
experiments.Method: The proposed substructured graph convolution (SS) will be embedded into several GNN backbones: GCN, GCNII, GAT, and SGC. The evaluation will be performed on the Cora, CiteSeer, and PubMed benchmark datasets for transductive node classification. The performance will be compared against two baseline decomposition methods: the additive method (AD) and the alternating method (AL), applied to the same GNNs. The experiment will also analyze the effect of varying the number of subgraphs (N).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalizability of the proposed substructured graph convolution on an inductive node classification task.
experiments.Method: The proposed substructured graph convolution (SS) and the baseline additive (AD) and alternating (AL) methods will be applied to GCN, GCNII, GAT, and SGC models. The evaluation will be conducted on the protein-protein interaction (PPI) dataset for inductive learning. The experiment will investigate the performance as the number of subgraphs (N) increases.

risk_factors: Therefore, when the decomposition (3.1) is performed, the computation of (I + D)−1/2 is required for the computation of the interface part. This reduces the computational efficiency of the interface part that requires sequential computation and may cause a bottleneck in the interior part where parallel computation is possible.

The process of computing the coefficients α and â is a structure that needs sequential computation, but it does not need additional parameters and uses the minimum cost to solve the bottleneck in the overall parallel structure.

title: Simple yet Effective Incomplete Multi-view Clustering: Similarity-level Imputation and Intra-view Hybrid-group Prototype Construction
name: sihpc-similarity-imputation-hybrid-prototypes
short_hypothesis: To address the drawbacks of ignoring missing samples and using a single prototype quantity in incomplete multi-view clustering (IMVC), we propose a method, SIIHPC. This method will first impute the incomplete parts of the similarity matrix by leveraging a shared consensus graph. Subsequently, it will introduce a group of hybrid prototype quantities for each individual view to flexibly extract data features, resulting in multi-scale graphs that can more comprehensively describe the overall similarity within a unified learning framework.
related_work: Most incomplete multi-view clustering (IMVC) methods typically ignore missing samples and only use observed unpaired samples to construct bipartite similarity, which can lead to inaccurate similarity graphs and unbalanced cluster structures. Furthermore, these methods often employ a single quantity of prototypes to extract information from all views, which is insufficient to characterize the unique features of each view and thus weakens information diversity.
abstract: Most of incomplete multi-view clustering (IMVC) methods typically choose to ignore the missing samples and only utilize observed unpaired samples to construct bipartite similarity. Moreover, they employ a single quantity of prototypes to extract the information of all views. To eliminate these drawbacks, we present a simple yet effective IMVC approach, SIIHPC, in this work. It firstly transforms partial bipartition learning into original sample form by virtue of reconstruction concept to split out of observed similarity, and then loosens traditional non-negative constraints via regularizing samples to more freely characterize the similarity. Subsequently, it learns to recover the incomplete parts by utilizing the connection built between the similarity exclusive on respective view and the consensus graph shared for all views. On this foundation, it further introduces a group of hybrid prototype quantities for each individual view to flexibly extract the data features belonging to each view itself. Accordingly, the resulting graphs are with various scales and describe the overall similarity more comprehensively. These components are optimized in one unified learning framework, which makes it possible for them to reciprocally promote. Then, to effectively solve the formulated optimization problem, we design an ingenious auxiliary function that is with theoretically proven monotonic-increasing properties.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the clustering performance of the proposed SIIHPC method against state-of-the-art baselines on public multi-view datasets with varying missing data ratios (30%, 50%, 70%).
experiments.Method: The proposed SIIHPC model will be evaluated on the task of incomplete multi-view clustering. The experiments will be conducted on six datasets: BDGPFEA, NUSOBJECT, VGGFACEFIFTY, VGGFACEHUND, YOUTUBEFACE, and FASHMINST. The performance will be compared against a set of baseline methods including LSIMVC, GSRIMC, HCPIMSC, EEIMVC, LRGRIMVC, IMVCCBG, BGIMVSC, OSLFIMVC, NGSPCGL, PIMVC, PSIMVC, SAGL, and HCLSCGL.

experiments.Description: To compare the computational resource consumption of SIIHPC against other IMVC methods.
experiments.Method: The running time (in minutes) and memory overhead (in GB) of SIIHPC and all baseline methods will be measured on the benchmark datasets.

experiments.Description: Ablation study to verify the effectiveness of the proposed similarity-level imputation (SLI) scheme.
experiments.Method: The performance of the full SIIHPC model will be compared to a variant without the SLI component (No-SLI) across all benchmark datasets and missing ratios.

experiments.Description: Ablation study to evaluate the effectiveness of using a group of hybrid prototype quantities (HPQ) versus a single prototype quantity (SPQ).
experiments.Method: The performance of the full SIIHPC model (using HPQ) will be compared against several variants that use a single prototype quantity (SPQ) of varying sizes (1k, 2k, 3k, 4k, 5k) across all benchmark datasets and missing ratios.

experiments.Description: Ablation study to verify the effectiveness of the adaptively weighing prototype quantity (AWPQ) scheme.
experiments.Method: The performance of the full SIIHPC model with AWPQ will be compared to a variant that gives equal weight to all prototype quantities (ETPQ) across all benchmark datasets and missing ratios.

risk_factors: We aggregate the information of graphs with diverse scales by stacking them and performing SVD to construct splicing eigenvectors, which could weaken the contributions of certain graphs or certain eigen-components.
Other ingenious fusion schemes are worth further exploration.
Additionally, we utilize the cosine tool to do similarity measure, some other measure approaches can be further studied in the future.

title: Revisiting Data Augmentation in Deep Reinforcement Learning
name: principled-data-augmentation-drl
short_hypothesis: A principled data-augmented actor-critic algorithm, which combines implicit regularization with an explicit KL regularization term in the actor loss and a novel tangent prop regularization term in the critic loss, will improve sample efficiency and generalization in image-based DRL. This is expected to be achieved by better controlling the variance of actor/critic losses and target Q-values while promoting invariance.
related_work: Various data augmentation techniques have been proposed for image-based DRL. Some methods, like RAD, apply transformations directly to observations. Others, like DrQ, use an averaged Q-target from multiple augmented samples. SVEA proposes avoiding complex transformations for target calculation to reduce variance, while DrAC uses explicit regularization terms to enforce invariance. However, most work has been experimental, and a principled comparison and a unified, theoretically-motivated method derived from a comprehensive analysis are lacking.
abstract: Various data augmentation techniques have been recently proposed in image-based deep reinforcement learning (DRL). Although they empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of data augmentation for improving sample efficiency or generalization, which technique should be preferred is not always clear. To tackle this question, we analyze existing methods to better understand them and to uncover how they are connected. Notably, by expressing the variance of the Q-targets and that of the empirical actor/critic losses of these methods, we can analyze the effects of their different components and compare them. We furthermore formulate an explanation about how these methods may be affected by choosing different data augmentation transformations in calculating the target Q-values. This analysis suggests recommendations on how to exploit data augmentation in a more principled way. In addition, we include a regularization term called tangent prop, previously proposed in computer vision, but whose adaptation to DRL is novel to the best of our knowledge.

experiments.Description: To validate the propositions of the paper by ablating the effects of individual components: using more augmented samples, adding KL regularization to the actor, and adding tangent prop regularization to the critic.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on environments from the DeepMind Control Suite with normal backgrounds. Starting with a DrQ baseline, the proposed KL regularization and tangent prop regularization terms will be added incrementally. Comparisons will be made against RAD, RAD+, and DrQ. The primary image transformation used will be random shift.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Aggregated performance scores (Median, IQM, Mean, Optimality Gap) across environments.', 'Standard deviation of the empirical critic loss.', 'Standard deviation of the target Q-values.', 'Standard deviation of the actor loss.', 'KL divergence between policies for augmented samples.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the sample efficiency of the final proposed algorithm against state-of-the-art baselines in image-based DRL.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be evaluated on environments from the DeepMind Control Suite with normal background. It will be benchmarked against RAD, DrQ, DrAC (with SAC base), DrQv2 (with DDPG base), and SVEA (with random overlay).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cumulative rewards averaged over evaluation episodes, plotted against environment steps.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization ability of the proposed algorithm against SVEA, a method specifically designed for generalization.
experiments.Method: Agents will be trained on environments from the DeepMind Control Suite with normal backgrounds and then evaluated on the same environments but with distracted (video-hard) backgrounds. The proposed method will be compared against SVEA, with both methods using random overlay as the image transformation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cumulative rewards averaged over evaluation episodes in the video-hard evaluation environments.']

risk_factors: However, the analysis can still be further refined to be more comprehensive such as including the theoretical analysis of using different distributions for the image transformation and providing a thorough analysis on tangent prop regularization.
Moreover, our method naturally requires the knowledge of some effective image transformations for a given task.
Without such knowledge, the invariant transformations for a problem would need to be learned, which is currently an active research direction.
Finally, image transformation may rely on some implicit assumptions, which may lead to lower/bad performance if they are not satisfied in the real application domain.

title: CNN Variational autoencoders' reconstruction ability of long ECG signals
name: folded-vae-for-long-ecg
short_hypothesis: A folded encoder/decoder architecture for a Variational Autoencoder (VAE), which splits a long ECG input into smaller sequential folds, will allow the network to learn essential ECG features and produce better reconstructions compared to a classical VAE that processes the entire long segment at once.
related_work: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) are powerful for generative modeling and have been used for ECG data, particularly in prototype-based interpretability methods. However, their application to longer physiological time-series like ECG remains underexplored. Previous studies attempting ECG reconstruction with VAEs either produced only short segments (3-10 seconds) or were limited to specific healthy heart rhythms. Standard VAEs tend to generate trivial or poor-quality reconstructions for longer segments (e.g., 30 seconds), which limits their utility in applications requiring faithful signal reproduction.
abstract: Classical Variational Auto-Encoders (VAEs) struggle to generate a flexible continuous latent space and faithfully reconstruct long electrocardiogram (ECG) segments, often producing trivial outputs. This limitation hinders their use in applications like deep learning interpretability. To address this, a folded VAE architecture is proposed. This architecture encodes long ECG segments by first splitting the input into smaller, sequential folds. These folds are processed in sequence by an encoder with a narrow field-of-view, and their latent representations are concatenated. The decoder follows a similar folding and concatenation strategy to reconstruct the original long ECG segment. This approach is intended to enable better reconstruction of long ECG segments compared to the classical unfolded VAE approach.

experiments.Description: To validate if the proposed folded VAE architecture can solve the poor reconstruction problem for long ECG signals observed in standard VAEs.
experiments.Method: A folded CNN VAE architecture will be trained in an unsupervised manner on two datasets: the MESA dataset and the MIT-BIH Polysomnography dataset. The experiment will involve splitting 30-second ECG segments into different numbers of folds (e.g., 3, 5, and 10 folds). The reconstruction quality will be compared against a baseline 'unfolded classical VAE' approach.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of reconstructed ECG signals', 'Reconstruction loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of the latent space representation generated by the folded VAE in a downstream classification task.
experiments.Method: A VAE-guided network will be trained for a 3-stage sleep classification task (Wake, NREM, REM) using the latent space from the folded VAE as input features. The experiment will use 6-fold and 10-fold VAE configurations. The performance will be compared against a model using an unfolded, standard VAE scenario. The models will be trained and tested on the MESA dataset.

risk_factors: It can be argued that the sampler may find it difficult to merge information between folds to form a continuous space.

Another conjecture for the model's poor performance may be that the information between splits is not being captured by the VAE encoder and subsequently not being adequately reflected in the relevance weights generated by the Parameterizer when encoding the entire signal.
The number of folds can be considered as a hyperparameter; we have explored 6-fold and 10-fold only in current study and requires further investigation.

title: Training Semi-Supervised Deep Learning Models with Heuristic Early Stopping Rules
name: heuristic-early-stopping-for-ssdl
short_hypothesis: A framework for early stopping in semi-supervised deep learning (SSDL), based on a combination of three heuristic criteria—model stability (weight convergence), generalizability (validation loss), and pseudo-label confidence—can prevent overfitting and improve model performance compared to training without an objective stopping methodology.
related_work: Semi-supervised deep learning (SSDL) is beneficial when labeled data is scarce, but a significant challenge is the lack of an objective methodology for training and determining when to stop. Errors in pseudo-labels can accumulate and propagate through training steps, a phenomenon known as error propagation, which undermines model performance. Existing early stopping methods for deep learning are not clearly defined for the iterative nature of SSDL, and those that exist often rely on a single metric like accuracy or loss, which may not be sufficient.
abstract: Semi-supervised learning (SSL), especially when combined with deep learning (DL) models, is a useful technique when there is a substantial amount of unlabeled data, which is particularly relevant in healthcare applications like mHealth. Despite its benefit, there is a lack of objective methodology on how to train semi-supervised deep learning (SSDL) models. In this study, we propose a framework for early-stopping in SSDL that terminates learning to prevent overfitting and before the performance starts to deteriorate. Our approach focuses on three aspects: model stability, generalizability, and high-confidence pseudo-labels. We first monitor changes in learned weights of the model to assess convergence. We also track cross-entropy loss to identify the iteration that minimizes validation loss. Lastly, we use a sliding window method to assess confidence in the pseudo-labels. Combining these criteria, this SSDL framework can be used to train deep learning models with an objective criteria that prevents overfitting and improves generalizability.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed heuristic early stopping criteria for training an SSDL model on a real-world healthcare task with a high proportion of unlabeled data.
experiments.Method: The task is to predict daily homework compliance (a binary outcome) for participants in a depression psychotherapy study. The dataset is mHealth data (device sensor and self-reported data) from a clinical trial, consisting of 4,700 observations where 62% are unlabeled. An SSDL model with a specific deep learning architecture is trained using an iterative pseudo-labeling algorithm. The experiment will compare the performance of models trained with different stopping points determined by four proposed criteria: 1) Model Convergence (minimum change in layer weights), 2) Generalizability (minimum validation loss), 3) Confidence of Pseudo-Labels (maximum stable count of high-confidence pseudo-labels), and 4) a Combined Criteria that ranks and aggregates the other three. These will be compared against a benchmark model trained without applying these specific early stopping criteria.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sensitivity', 'Specificity', 'PPV (Positive Predictive Value)', 'NPV (Negative Predictive Value)', 'AUC (Area Under the Curve)', 'Accuracy', 'F1 Score']

risk_factors: While the approach outlined in this paper serves as an heuristic guide for developing SSDL models, it is by no means a definitive solution.

In our SSDL framework (Algorithm 1), there are additional key parameters that include the maximum number of iterations S, the confidence threshold for pseudo-labels θ, and the sliding window size w in 5 – 7.

When proposing an early stopping rule based on a combination of three criteria—model stability, generalizability, and pseudo-label confidence—we introduce an additional parameter, s, which refers to the top iterations selected based on performance across these criteria (see Section 2.5.4).

title: PriorGuide: Test-Time Prior Adaptation for Simulation-Based Inference
name: prior-guide
short_hypothesis: A pre-trained diffusion-based amortized inference model can be adapted to a new, arbitrary prior distribution at test time without retraining. This can be achieved by leveraging the guidance mechanism inherent in diffusion models, steering the generative process according to the new target prior. The guidance term is derived from the ratio of the new prior to the original training prior and is made tractable through a novel Gaussian mixture model approximation.
related_work: Amortized simulation-based inference (SBI) methods are powerful but their applicability is often limited by the fixed prior distribution used during training. Changing the prior typically requires either costly re-simulation (for non-amortized methods) or complete retraining of the amortized model. While some recent methods offer inference-time prior adaptation, they are often restricted to specific families of priors (e.g., factorized histograms, Gaussian mixtures) that must be pre-defined at training time within a 'meta-prior'. This limits the flexibility to incorporate arbitrary, user-defined priors that were not foreseen during training.
abstract: Amortized simulator-based inference offers a powerful framework for tackling Bayesian inference, increasingly leveraging modern generative methods like diffusion models. These approaches yield posterior or posterior-predictive samples for new datasets without requiring further simulator calls after training. However, their applicability is often limited by the prior distribution(s) used to generate model parameters during this training phase. To overcome this constraint, we introduce PriorGuide, a technique specifically designed for diffusion-based amortized inference methods. PriorGuide leverages a novel guidance approximation that enables flexible adaptation of the trained diffusion model to new priors at test time, crucially without costly retraining. This allows users to readily incorporate updated information or expert knowledge post-training.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the posterior inference capabilities of PriorGuide on a range of SBI problems when adapting to new test-time priors.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on six SBI problems: Two Moons, Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process (OUP), the Turin model, Gaussian Linear model (10D and 20D variants), and the Bayesian Causal Inference (BCI) model. A base diffusion model (Simformer) will be trained on data generated with a uniform or broad Gaussian prior. At test time, its performance will be evaluated on its ability to adapt to three families of new target priors: 'mild', 'strong', and 'mixture'. Baselines for comparison will include the base Simformer (with no prior adaptation), Amortized Conditioning Engine (ACE), rejection sampling (RS), sampling-importance-resampling (SIR), and neural likelihood estimation (NLE) with MCMC.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)', 'Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST)', 'Mean Marginal Total Variation Distance (MMTV)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate PriorGuide's ability to perform posterior predictive inference (forecasting or retrocasting) under new target priors.
experiments.Method: This experiment will use the OUP and Turin models, which generate time-series trajectories. The task is to predict the unobserved 70% of a trajectory, conditioned on either the first or last 30%. The same test-time prior setup ('mild', 'strong', and 'mixture') from the posterior inference experiment will be used. PriorGuide will be compared against the base Simformer and ACE.

experiments.Description: To study the trade-off between computational cost and inference accuracy by using corrective Langevin dynamics for refinement.
experiments.Method: Using the OUP and Turin models for posterior inference, the experiment will vary the number of diffusion steps (N) and the number of interleaved Langevin steps (NL). The goal is to analyze the relationship between the number of function evaluations (NFEs) and the resulting inference accuracy.

risk_factors: PriorGuide's effectiveness relies on the new prior q(θ) having substantial overlap with the training prior p_train(θ); out-of-distribution (OOD) target priors can lead to inaccurate learned scores and unstable guidance.

The method also employs approximations—a Gaussian for the reverse transition kernel p(θ_0|θ_t, x) and a Gaussian mixture model for the prior ratio function r(θ)—which can introduce inaccuracies, particularly for complex prior ratio shapes.
Furthermore, current guidance calculations, involving matrix operations for the GMM components, may pose scalability challenges for high-dimensional parameter spaces (dim(θ) ≫ 20).

PriorGuide sampling can be computationally intensive: although our method avoids retraining the base model—a key benefit with expensive simulators (e.g., Turin model)—the iterative guided diffusion, particularly with interleaved Langevin refinement steps (NL), incurs a cost.

The number of function evaluations (NFEs) increases with diffusion (N) and Langevin steps, creating an accuracy-speed trade-off. This may render PriorGuide less suited than fully amortized methods for applications requiring very rapid inference.

title: Pallatom-Ligand: an All-Atom Diffusion Model for Designing Ligand-Binding Proteins
name: pallatom-ligand-all-atom-diffusion
short_hypothesis: An end-to-end approach that learns the joint distribution of all atoms in a protein-ligand complex, rather than just the protein backbone, will improve both the modeling accuracy and the data efficiency for designing ligand-binding proteins. This can be achieved through a unified architecture combining an all-atom representation with end-to-end training.
related_work: Traditional methods for designing ligand-binding proteins, like Rosetta, require expert biochemical intuition and are hampered by inaccurate energy evaluation. Recent deep learning models, such as RFdiffusionAA, CA_RFdiffusion, and RFdiffusion2, have shown success but are limited. They are primarily backbone-only generators, insufficient in learning the atomic interactions at the protein-ligand interface, and rely on separate inverse-folding models to design the sequence. This reliance on expert-crafted priors introduces biases and limits their general usability. The key research gap is the lack of explicit, end-to-end, all-atom modeling of the protein-ligand complex.
abstract: Small-molecule ligands extend protein functionality beyond natural amino acids, enabling sophisticated processes like catalysis, signal transduction, and light harvesting. However, designing proteins with high affinity and selectivity for arbitrary ligands remains a major challenge. We present Pallatom-Ligand, a diffusion model that performs end-to-end generation of ligand-binding proteins at atomic resolution. By directly learning the joint distribution of all atoms in the protein-ligand complexes, Pallatom-Ligand is proposed. In addition, Pallatom-Ligand's novel conditioning framework is designed to enable programmable control over global protein fold and atomic-level ligand solvent accessibility. With these capabilities, Pallatom-Ligand is intended to open new opportunities for exploring the protein function space, advancing both generative modeling and computational protein engineering.

experiments.Description: To benchmark the performance of Pallatom-Ligand in generating ligand-binding proteins against state-of-the-art methods on a comprehensive set of small molecules.
experiments.Method: For a set of eight small molecules (FMN, DOG, LDP, SRO, IAI, FAD, SAM, OQO), generate 100 protein structures per method. For backbone-generating methods, design a sequence using LigandMPNN. For Pallatom-Ligand, evaluate both its raw co-generated sequences and sequences redesigned with LigandMPNN. The baselines for comparison will be RFdiffusionAA and RFdiffusion2.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Protein Scaffold (Ca-RMSD, protein-pLDDT)', 'Ligand Pose (ligand-Dcenter, ligand-RMSD, ligand-pLDDT)', 'Binding Interface (ipAE)', 'Protein-Fold Success (Ca-RMSD < 2Å and protein-pLDDT > 80)', 'Ligand-Pocket Success (Protein-Fold Success and ligand-Dcenter < 4Å and ligand-pLDDT > 80)', 'Ligand-Pose Success (Protein-Fold Success and ligand-RMSD < 2Å)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to conditionally generate proteins with a specified ratio of α-helices to β-strands (α ratio).
experiments.Method: Generate 40 protein samples for each of the eight benchmark targets under three different α ratio conditions: mainly β (0-0.2), α/β mix (0.2-0.8), and mainly α (0.8-1.0). The raw sequences from Pallatom-Ligand will be evaluated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Ligand-binding success rates (Protein-Fold, Ligand-Pocket, Ligand-Pose)', 'Secondary structure distribution (α/β ratios)', 'Diversity', 'Novelty']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's capacity for conditional generation at the atomic level by controlling the solvent accessibility of specific ligand atoms.
experiments.Method: Design proteins to bind three target ligands (FMN, DOG, LDP) with predefined per-atom Relative Solvent Accessibility (RSA) labels. For each ligand, generate 40 proteins and evaluate if the generated structures adhere to the specified SA conditions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Distribution of the ligand's solvent-accessible surface area (SASA) in the generated proteins, compared to the predefined SA labels."]

experiments.Description: To test the zero-shot generalization capability of the model on unseen chemical structures that were not part of the training set.
experiments.Method: Apply the model to generate binders for four novel targets completely absent from the training data: Janelia Fluor Dyes (JF585, JF635, JF711) and Deschloroclozapine (DCZ). The performance of Pallatom-Ligand (with and without SA conditioning, with and without MPNN redesign) will be compared against RFdiffusionAA and RFdiffusion2 baselines.

risk_factors: We emphasize that these metrics quantify structural consistency only — a necessary condition for valid design and they do not guarantee biological activity.

First, we aim to broaden the scope of supported biomolecular complexes beyond protein-small molecule systems, including nucleic acid-containing assemblies, covalent ligand binding, and proteins with noncanonical amino acids.
Second, we plan to scale training to larger distilled protein-ligand datasets, in order to further push the performance limits of all-atom ligand binding design.

title: xFinder: Large Language Models as Automated Evaluators for Reliable Evaluation
name: xfinder-for-reliable-evaluation
short_hypothesis: Proposing xFinder, a novel evaluator created by fine-tuning an LLM on a specialized dataset (KAF), to replace error-prone Regular Expression (RegEx) based methods for key answer extraction and matching in LLM evaluation. This is intended to improve the accuracy and reliability of the evaluation process.
related_work: Current LLM evaluation frameworks like LM Eval Harness and OpenCompass predominantly use Regular Expression (RegEx) for answer extraction, which is often inaccurate and fails on non-standardized LLM outputs. Alternative approaches, such as fine-tuned "judge models," suffer from poor generalization and fairness. The research gap lies in the need for an accurate, efficient, and generalizable method specifically for the answer extraction and matching sub-task to enhance the reliability of objective evaluations.
abstract: The reliability of LLM evaluation is challenged by phenomena like prompt overfitting and the use of error-prone Regular Expression (RegEx) for answer extraction. While fine-tuned LLMs as judge models have been proposed, they face generalization and fairness issues. To address this, we propose xFinder, a novel evaluator for answer extraction and matching. The approach involves creating a specialized dataset, the Key Answer Finder (KAF) dataset, and using it to fine-tune a model that can replace the RegEx module, with the goal of improving extraction accuracy and overall evaluation reliability.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the extraction accuracy of xFinder models against traditional RegEx-based methods and GPT-4.
experiments.Method: xFinder models (fine-tuned on various base LLMs) will be tested on the KAF test and generalization sets. Baselines will include the RegEx extraction modules from OpenCompass, LM Eval Harness, and UltraEval, as well as using GPT-4 as an extractor.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the final judgment accuracy of the evaluation pipeline when using xFinder, compared to pipelines using judge models.
experiments.Method: The judgment accuracy of xFinder will be compared against mainstream frameworks (OpenCompass, LM Eval Harness, UltraEval), dedicated judge models (PandaLM, JudgeLM), and GPT-4 as a judge. The evaluation will be performed on the KAF generalization set, with human-annotated results serving as the ground truth.

experiments.Description: To compare the efficiency (time and cost) of xFinder against other LLM-based judge models.
experiments.Method: The time and/or API cost to evaluate a random sample of instances from the KAF generalization set will be measured for xFinder models, JudgeLM, PandaLM, and GPT-4.

experiments.Description: To assess the reliability of xFinder in real-world evaluation scenarios by comparing the stability of LLM rankings it produces against those from RegEx-based frameworks.
experiments.Method: The performance of 10 different LLMs will be evaluated on 14 mainstream evaluation tasks (including ARC, CommonsenseQA, MMLU, GSM8K). The answer extraction will be performed by both selected xFinder models and the RegEx methods from OpenCompass, LM Eval Harness, and UltraEval. The resulting LLM rankings will then be compared.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Analysis of LLM ranking changes and discrepancies across different evaluation frameworks']

risk_factors: This study represents the first step in constructing a reliable evaluation framework.

title: Retrieval is Accurate Generation
name: direct-phrase-retrieval-generation
short_hypothesis: A new paradigm for text generation can be created by directly retrieving context-aware phrases from a collection of supporting documents, removing the need for an initial document retrieval stage. The key challenge of constructing training oracles for this approach can be addressed by first initializing them with linguistics-motivated heuristics (based on syntax, frequency, and semantics) and then iteratively refining them through a self-reinforcement bootstrapping mechanism.
related_work: Standard language models (LMs) generate text token-by-token from a fixed vocabulary. Retrieval-augmented frameworks like CoG improve this by allowing phrase copying, but they still rely on a two-stage pipeline where document retrieval is followed by phrase extraction. This approach is constrained by the quality of the initial document retrieval. This paper proposes to remove the dependence on document retrieval and perform text generation through direct phrase retrieval, addressing the core challenge of constructing training oracles with a novel two-fold approach involving heuristic initialization and iterative self-reinforcement.
abstract: Standard language models generate text by selecting tokens from a fixed, finite, and standalone vocabulary. We introduce a novel method that selects context-aware phrases from a collection of supporting documents. One of the most significant challenges for this paradigm shift is determining the training oracles, because a string of text can be segmented in various ways and each segment can be retrieved from numerous possible documents. To address this, we propose to initialize the training oracles using linguistic heuristics and, more importantly, bootstrap the oracles through iterative self-reinforcement.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's effectiveness on knowledge-intensive tasks in a zero-shot setting.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on a classification-with-options task using the OpenbookQA, ARC-Challenge, TruthfulQA, MedMCQA, and Med-USMILE datasets. The model's likelihood of selecting the correct option will be calculated. Performance will be compared against several baselines: a standard Base LM (fine-tuned GPT-2), kNN-LM, RETRO, and CoG. An ablation study will also be conducted with a variant of the proposed model that only uses standalone tokens ('Ours w/o phrase').

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of open-ended text generation.
experiments.Method: The model will be tasked with generating text continuations of 128 tokens given a 128-token prefix from the test set of MiniPile. The generated text will be compared against outputs from baselines including Base LM (with and without fine-tuning), kNN-LM, RETRO, and CoG.

experiments.Description: To test the model's scalability and plug-and-play capability by using an enlarged phrase index without any retraining.
experiments.Method: The model's performance on the knowledge-intensive tasks (OpenbookQA, ARC-Challenge, TruthfulQA, MedMCQA, Med-USMILE) will be re-evaluated using a phrase index that has been augmented with previously filtered-out phrases.

experiments.Description: To test the model's domain adaptation capabilities in a training-free manner by employing a domain-specific phrase index.
experiments.Method: For the MedMCQA and Med-USMILE datasets, the general Wikipedia phrase index will be replaced with a smaller, domain-specific index constructed from a medical text collection. Performance will be compared to a Base LM fine-tuned on the same medical text.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the iterative self-reinforcement (SR) mechanism on model performance.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted comparing the model trained without SR to models trained with one or more rounds of SR on both knowledge-intensive tasks and open-ended text generation.

risk_factors: First of all, the presented result is just a proof-of-concept of the new paradigm. Future work should focus on (1) Scalability.

In our current experiments, we train our models and build the phrase index on the English Wikipedia corpus. When scaling up to larger corpora, we may encounter computational challenges due to the significantly increased amount of possible phrases.

Recent studies have shown that alignment (i.e., fine-tuning language models to following human instructions) is important to make language models universally useful. Thus, incorporating alignment techniques into our approach is an important future research direction.

title: Weakly Supervised Explainable Phrasal Reasoning with Neural Fuzzy Logic
name: explainable-phrasal-reasoning-nli
short_hypothesis: An Explainable Phrasal Reasoning (EPR) model can be trained end-to-end with only sentence-level supervision to provide explicit explanations of phrasal logical relationships for Natural Language Inference (NLI). This is achieved by detecting and aligning phrases, predicting their NLI labels, and inducing the final sentence label using differentiable fuzzy logic formulas.
related_work: Recent deep learning models for Natural Language Inference (NLI) achieve high performance but lack interpretability. Previous explainability methods either generate textual explanations without performing reasoning itself, provide only correlation information through attention mechanisms, or are based on hand-crafted rules restricted to Entailment only. There is a need for a model that can perform weakly supervised phrasal logical reasoning for all NLI labels (Entailment, Contradiction, Neutral) to provide explicit explanations.
abstract: Natural language inference (NLI) aims to determine the logical relationship between two sentences, such as Entailment, Contradiction, and Neutral. In recent years, deep learning models have become a prevailing approach to NLI, but they lack interpretability and explainability. In this work, we address the explainability of NLI by weakly supervised logical reasoning, and propose an Explainable Phrasal Reasoning (EPR) approach. Our model first detects phrases as the semantic unit and aligns corresponding phrases in the two sentences. Then, the model predicts the NLI label for the aligned phrases, and induces the sentence label by fuzzy logic formulas. Our EPR is almost everywhere differentiable and thus the system can be trained end to end. In this way, we are able to provide explicit explanations of phrasal logical relationships in a weakly supervised manner.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's phrasal reasoning performance on the SNLI dataset and compare it against non-reasoning and reasoning-based baselines.
experiments.Method: The proposed EPR model will be trained on the SNLI dataset. For evaluation, a new corpus of phrasal logical relationships will be annotated. Baselines will include non-reasoning models (LSTM, Transformer, SBERT), and reasoning models like Neural Natural Logic (NNL), a proposed Sentence label Training Phrases (STP) model, and few-shot prompting with GPT-3.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sentence-level accuracy', 'A proposed set of F-scores for phrasal reasoning (FE, Fc, FN, FUp, FUH) based on word indexes']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness and generalization of the phrasal reasoning approach on the more diverse MNLI dataset.
experiments.Method: The EPR model will be trained and evaluated on the matched section of the MNLI dataset. A new phrasal reasoning annotation will be created for a subset of MNLI for evaluation. The model will be compared against NNL and STP baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sentence-level accuracy', 'Phrasal reasoning F-scores (FE, Fc, FUp, FUH), excluding the Neutral category due to data quality concerns.']

experiments.Description: To examine whether the phrasal logical relationships predicted by the EPR model can improve performance on the downstream task of textual explanation generation.
experiments.Method: The predicted phrasal logical relationships from EPR will be used as factual knowledge to enhance a standard Transformer decoder for generating explanations on the e-SNLI dataset. This will be compared to baselines such as NILE, LIREx, and FinetunedWT5, as well as a controlled experiment using human-annotated rationale highlights instead of EPR outputs.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to verify the effect of each component in the EPR model.
experiments.Method: Ablated versions of the EPR model will be evaluated on the SNLI dataset. These ablations include: (1) a random chunker, (2) a random aligner, (3) using semantic role labeling as the reasoning unit, and (4) using mean induction instead of fuzzy logic rules. Variants with local, global, and concatenated features will also be analyzed.

risk_factors: This paper performs phrase detection and alignment by heuristics. They work well empirically in our experiments, although further improvement is possible (for example, by considering syntactic structures).

However, our main focus is neural fuzzy logic for weakly supervised reasoning. This largely differs from previous work based on manually designed lexicons and rules (Hu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021).

title: Bridging the Fairness Divide: Achieving Group and Individual Fairness in Graph Neural Networks
name: fairgi-for-group-and-individual-fairness-in-gnns
short_hypothesis: A novel framework, FairGI, can simultaneously achieve both group fairness (Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity) and individual fairness in graph neural networks by introducing a new concept of 'individual fairness within groups'. This is accomplished by employing a similarity matrix to enforce individual fairness within groups and using adversarial learning to promote group fairness.
related_work: Existing research on fairness in graph learning has primarily focused on either group fairness or individual fairness, but not both simultaneously. This singular focus creates inherent limitations and potential conflicts between the two fairness types. Models that emphasize group fairness can neglect individual-level fairness, while those prioritizing individual fairness may lead to bias across different demographic groups. This paper identifies a gap in comprehensively addressing both fairness notions at the same time.
abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing and learning from complex data structured as graphs, demonstrating remarkable effectiveness in various applications. However, despite their impressive performance, the fairness problem has increasingly gained attention. Existing research on fairness in graph learning primarily emphasizes either group fairness or individual fairness; however, to the best of our knowledge, none of these studies comprehensively address both individual and group fairness simultaneously. In this paper, we propose a new concept of individual fairness within groups and a novel framework named Fairness for Group and Individual (FairGI), which considers both group fairness and individual fairness within groups in the context of graph learning. FairGI employs the similarity matrix of individuals to achieve individual fairness within groups, while leveraging adversarial learning to address group fairness in terms of both Equal Opportunity and Statistical Parity.

experiments.Description: To conduct a comprehensive comparison of the proposed FairGI method against other state-of-the-art fairness models for graph learning on real-world datasets. The goal is to evaluate its effectiveness in achieving both group and individual fairness while maintaining prediction performance.
experiments.Method: The experiment involves a node classification task on three public datasets: Pokec_n, NBA, and Credit. The proposed FairGI model will be compared against several baselines, including: basic GNNs (GCN, GAT), individual fairness models (PFR, InFoRM, GUIDE), a group fairness model (FairGNN), and a causal inference fairness model (NIFTY).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area Under the Curve (AUC)', 'Accuracy (ACC)', 'Maximum Individual unfairness within Groups (MaxIG)', 'Individual Fairness (IF)', 'Statistical Parity (SP)', 'Equal Opportunity (EO)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to examine the impact of two key modules of the proposed method: the module for individual fairness within groups and the module for optimizing equal opportunity.
experiments.Method: The study will be performed on the Credit dataset. The full FairGI model will be compared against two of its variants: one that omits the individual fairness within groups loss (L_Ifg), and another that eliminates the optimizations for equal opportunity (loss functions L_A2 and L_R2).

risk_factors: In this paper, we assume the dataset contains a single sensitive feature characterized by binary values.

title: Using Machine Learning Models to Predict Genitourinary Involvement Among Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumour Patients
name: ml-predict-gu-involvement-gist
short_hypothesis: Machine learning algorithms can be utilized to effectively predict genitourinary (GU) involvement in patients with Gastrointestinal Stromal Tumors (GISTs), potentially improving diagnosis and management.
related_work: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) are rare, and their diagnosis requires extensive testing. While machine learning has shown promise in predicting outcomes and malignancy in various cancers, including GISTs, its application for predicting the specific involvement of the genitourinary system in GIST patients has not been explored. This study aims to address this gap by evaluating the effectiveness of machine learning for this predictive task.
abstract: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) can lead to involvement of other organs, including the genitourinary (GU) system. Machine learning may be a valuable tool in predicting GU involvement in GIST patients, and thus improving prognosis. This study aims to evaluate the use of machine learning algorithms to predict GU involvement among GIST patients in a specialist research center in Saudi Arabia. We will analyze data from all patients with histopathologically confirmed GIST at our facility from 2003 to 2020. Patient files will be reviewed for the presence of renal cell carcinoma, adrenal tumors, or other genitourinary cancers. Three supervised machine learning algorithms will be used: Logistic Regression, XGBoost Regressor, and Random Forests. A set of variables, including independent attributes, will be entered into the models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate and compare the performance of several supervised machine learning models for the binary classification task of predicting genitourinary (GU) involvement in GIST patients.
experiments.Method: A retrospective dataset of 170 patients with histopathologically confirmed GIST from King Faisal Specialist Hospital and Research Centre (2003-2020) will be used. The target variable is a binary indicator for the presence or absence of genitourinary cancer. The dataset will be randomly split into an 80% training set and a 20% test set. The models to be evaluated include Random Forest, XGBoost Classifier, Catboost classifier, Support Vector Machine, and Logistic Regression.

risk_factors: Firstly, we did not include all potential predictive factors for genitourinary involvement in GIST patients, such as family history of malignancy and exposure to risk factors.
Secondly, this study was conducted at a single center, which may limit the generalizability of our results to other populations.

Thirdly, there are currently no other studies in the literature that explore the use of machine learning to predict synchronous GU tumors and GISTs, which makes it difficult to compare and validate our findings.

title: Offline Model-Based Optimization by Learning to Rank
name: offline-mbo-learning-to-rank
short_hypothesis: In offline model-based optimization (MBO), training a surrogate model by minimizing mean squared error (MSE) is not well-aligned with the primary goal of selecting promising designs. A better approach is to learn a ranking-based model using learning-to-rank (LTR) techniques, as prioritizing the relative order of designs is more crucial than predicting their precise scores. A surrogate model that preserves the score ordering of designs will find the optimal design, even if its score predictions are not exact.
related_work: The common approach for offline MBO is to train a regression-based surrogate model by minimizing mean squared error (MSE). A critical challenge is the out-of-distribution (OOD) error, where the model overestimates scores and misleads the optimizer. Prior works have attempted to mitigate this using regularization and ensemble learning, but the issue persists. These methods focus on value matching (precise score prediction), which may be inadequate. The primary goal of offline MBO is to identify the best designs, which only requires preserving the relative order of candidates, not exact score prediction. This suggests that the MSE metric is not well-suited for evaluating surrogate models, and a ranking-based approach may be more appropriate.
abstract: Offline model-based optimization (MBO) aims to identify a design that maximizes a black-box function using only a fixed, pre-collected dataset of designs and their corresponding scores. A common approach in offline MBO is to train a regression-based surrogate model by minimizing mean squared error (MSE) and then find the best design within this surrogate model. However, a critical challenge is the risk of out-of-distribution errors. In this paper, we argue that regression models trained with MSE are not well-aligned with the primary goal of offline MBO, which is to select promising designs rather than to predict their scores precisely. Notably, if a surrogate model can maintain the order of candidate designs based on their relative score relationships, it can produce the best designs even without precise predictions. Based on this observation, we propose learning a ranking-based model that leverages learning to rank techniques to prioritize promising designs based on their relative scores. We show that the generalization error on ranking loss can be well bounded.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed ranking-based model (RaM) against a wide range of existing offline MBO methods on several benchmark tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed method, RaM, using RankCosine and ListNet losses, will be benchmarked on the Design-Bench tasks: Ant Morphology, D'Kitty Morphology, Superconductor, TF-Bind-8, and TF-Bind-10. The surrogate model is a two-hidden-layer MLP. RaM will be compared against three categories of methods: 1) Baselines optimizing a regression model (BO-qEI, CMA-ES, REINFORCE, Gradient Ascent), 2) Backward approaches (CbAS, MINs, DDOM, BONET, GTG), and 3) Forward approaches (COMs, ROMA, IOM, BDI, ICT, Tri-Mentoring, PGS, FGM, Match-OPT).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['100th percentile normalized ground-truth score of the top-128 selected designs.', '50th percentile normalized ground-truth score.']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether the proposed ranking-related metric, Area Under the Precision-Coverage Curve (AUPCC), is a better indicator of final design quality than the commonly used Mean Squared Error (MSE), particularly in out-of-distribution (OOD) regions.
experiments.Method: Five surrogate models (Gradient Ascent baseline, COMs, IOM, ICT, Tri-Mentoring) will be trained on the D'Kitty and TF-Bind-8 tasks. For each trained model, the OOD-MSE and OOD-AUPCC will be calculated on an OOD dataset. The correlation between these metric ranks and the rank of the final design scores will be analyzed.

experiments.Description: To examine the influence of different pointwise, pairwise, and listwise ranking losses on the performance of the proposed RaM framework.
experiments.Method: The RaM framework will be tested with a variety of ranking losses: SigmoidCrossEntropy (SCE), BinaryCrossEntropy (BCE), and MSE (for pointwise); RankNet, LambdaRank, and RankCosine (for pairwise); Softmax, ListNet, ListMLE, and ApproxNDCG (for listwise). The evaluation will be conducted on the five Design-Bench tasks.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method's main components: data augmentation and output adaptation.
experiments.Method: The RaM framework, using top-performing losses (MSE, RankCosine, ListNet), will be evaluated in different configurations: 1) with and without the proposed data augmentation strategy, and 2) with and without the output adaptation module. This will be tested across the five Design-Bench tasks.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the versatility of ranking loss by examining if simply replacing the MSE loss in existing regression-based methods with a ranking loss can improve their performance.
experiments.Method: For a selection of existing regression-based offline MBO methods (e.g., BO-qEI, CMA-ES, Grad. Ascent, CbAS, MINS, Tri-Mentoring, PGS, Match-OPT), the standard MSE loss will be substituted with the ListNet ranking loss, and output adaptation will be incorporated. The performance of the original and modified methods will be compared.

risk_factors: Note that prevalent ranking losses such as ApproxNDCG (Bruch et al., 2019b) do not perform well in RaM. This might due to the simplicity of MLP, which cannot absorb complex information of conveyed by the trending powerful loss functions.
Most generalization bound analysis in LTR assume i.i.d (as Theorem 2 in our paper), while OOD analysis in LTR is quite limited.
The list length m in the training data could have a impact on the generalization ability of the model and its impact on OOD generalization ability of LTR algorithms is undiscovered.
We excluded NeuralNDCG (Pobrotyn & Bialobrzeski, 2021), a recently proposed listwise loss using neural sort techniques to approximate NDCG, due to its high memory requirements.

title: Learning Successor Representations with Distributed Hebbian Temporal Memory
name: dhtm-for-successor-representations
short_hypothesis: A novel algorithm, Distributed Hebbian Temporal Memory (DHTM), based on factor graph formalism and a multi-compartment neuron model, can effectively learn Successor Representations (SR) in an online fashion. By utilizing distributed representations, sparse transition matrices, and local Hebbian-like learning rules, DHTM is proposed to overcome the instability and slow learning of traditional temporal memory algorithms like RNNs and HMMs, thereby speeding up Temporal Difference learning for SR in dynamic, partially observable environments.
related_work: Traditional Temporal Memory (TM) algorithms like Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) use backpropagation, which can be unstable and requires complete data sequences for training. While newer models like RWKV or LRU partially address gradient issues, online learning in partially observable, stochastic environments remains a challenge. There is a lack of TM models that are fully online, adaptable, and can interact with environments where only one data point is available at a time, which is common in Reinforcement Learning. The proposed DHTM is inspired by neurophysiological models (neocortex, pyramidal neurons) and aims to fill this gap by being fully online and using local, Hebbian-like learning rules to avoid gradient-based drawbacks.
abstract: This paper presents a novel approach to address the challenge of online hidden representation learning for decision-making under uncertainty in non-stationary, partially observable environments. The proposed algorithm, Distributed Hebbian Temporal Memory (DHTM), is based on factor graph formalism and a multicomponent neuron model. DHTM aims to capture sequential data relationships and make cumulative predictions about future observations, forming Successor Representation (SR). Inspired by neurophysiological models of the neocortex, the algorithm utilizes distributed representations, sparse transition matrices, and local Hebbian-like learning rules to overcome the instability and slow learning process of traditional temporal memory algorithms like RNN and HMM. The goal is to speed up Temporal Difference learning for SR in changing environments and provide a promising approach for addressing the challenges of online hidden representation learning in dynamic environments.

experiments.Description: To qualitatively assess the Successor Features (SFs) formed by different temporal memory models and to test the accuracy of the SF representations in a classic maze task.
experiments.Method: The experiment is conducted in a 2D Pinball environment. The proposed DHTM model will be compared against LSTM, RWKV, and a Factorial version of CSCG (fchmm). The models will be tested on their ability to form SFs under a random policy. The experiment will also test the models in two regimes: n-step planning and prediction-only.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative assessment of value function heatmaps', 'Pseudo-surprise computed for observed states']

experiments.Description: To evaluate how the temporal memory model can enhance an agent's adaptation in a changing environment by varying the number of prediction steps used in n-step TD learning of SF.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Pinball environment with a specific task where an agent is trained to reach a target. After 500 episodes, the environment changes by having the target blocked by a random force field. The agent's adaptability will be compared when using five prediction steps versus one prediction step for TD learning of SF.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Reward curve over episodes to measure adaptation speed']

experiments.Description: To test the model's planning capabilities and resulting adaptation speed in a more challenging 3D environment where the goal location changes mid-experiment.
experiments.Method: The experiment is conducted in the AnimalAI environment, where an agent must choose between two corridors to find food. After 300 episodes, the food is moved to the opposite corridor. The agent's performance will be compared for two cases: SF formed by n-step planning versus SF formed by prediction using TD-learned weights.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Reward curve over episodes to measure adaptation speed to the new food position']

risk_factors: One of the limitations of the DHTM is that its temporal context is random, as it is formed on the fly. That is, the mechanism of context formation doesn't allow generalizations.
Another limitation is the maximum number of variables per factor. The amount of segments in use grows exponentially with the number of variables per factor, especially in noisy environments.

title: BroGNet: Momentum-Conserving Graph Neural Stochastic Differential Equation for Learning Brownian Dynamics
name: brognet-momentum-conserving-gnn-sde
short_hypothesis: A graph neural network framework (BROGNET) that parameterizes a stochastic differential equation (SDE) and is architecturally modified to enforce linear momentum conservation can effectively learn the dynamics of Brownian systems directly from trajectory data.
related_work: Prior works have successfully used neural networks with strong inductive biases, such as Lagrangian or Hamiltonian NNs and Neural ODEs, to learn the dynamics of deterministic physical systems. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have also been used to model particle interactions. However, these approaches predominantly focus on deterministic systems governed by ODEs. There has been a lack of attempts to learn the dynamics of stochastic systems, such as those described by Brownian dynamics and governed by SDEs, directly from their trajectory data.
abstract: Neural networks (NNs) that exploit strong inductive biases based on physical laws have shown success in learning the dynamics of deterministic physical systems. However, these works focus on systems that follow deterministic dynamics. Here, we propose a framework, namely Brownian graph neural networks (BROGNET), combining stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and GNNs to learn Brownian dynamics directly from the trajectory. We propose modifying the architecture of BROGNET to enforce linear momentum conservation of the system. This approach will be demonstrated on several systems, namely, linear spring, linear spring with binary particle types, and non-linear spring systems, all following Brownian dynamics at finite temperatures.

experiments.Description: To benchmark the ability of BROGNET to accurately learn Brownian dynamics from trajectory data and compare its performance against several baseline models.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on simulated trajectory data from three different systems: a linear spring system, a non-linear spring system, and a linear spring system with binary particle types. The performance of BROGNET will be compared against six baselines: Neural Network (NN), Brownian NN (BNN), Brownian full graph network (BFGN), Brownian Dynamics GNN (BDGNN), Brownian Neural Equivariant Interatomic Potentials (BNequIP), and Momentum-informed BDGNN (MIBDGNN).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Position error (normalized Euclidean distance)', 'Brownian error (RMSE between estimated and ground truth standard deviation)', 'Trajectory roll-out error (KL-divergence between predicted and ground-truth trajectory distributions)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the zero-shot generalizability of the BROGNET architecture to unseen system sizes and temperatures.
experiments.Method: Models trained on a small system (e.g., 5-spring system at 1 unit of temperature) will be evaluated on their ability to simulate dynamics for much larger systems (50 and 500 springs) and at different temperatures (10 and 100 units) without any fine-tuning. This will be compared against baselines like BFGN, BDGNN, MIBDGNN, and BNequIP.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the degree to which the BROGNET architecture conserves the total linear momentum of the system, as enforced by its inductive bias.
experiments.Method: The total force experienced by the system during predicted dynamics will be computed for BROGNET and compared against the baselines. For a momentum-conserving system, this value should be close to zero.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the data efficiency of BROGNET compared to baseline models.
experiments.Method: BROGNET and baselines (BFGN, BDGNN, MIBDGNN, BNequIP) will be trained on datasets of varying sizes, ranging from 100 to 10000 data points, to assess their performance with limited data.

risk_factors: This work focuses on Brownian dynamics, which is an over-damped limit of the Langevin equation.
The present architecture cannot learn the dynamics in the presence of external fields.
Extending the model to the dynamics of real-world systems, including experimental datasets, is also an outstanding problem.

The training approach presented here may lead to learning the dynamics as dictated by the Euler Maruyama integrator. Thus, the learned dynamics may not represent the "true" dynamics of the system, but one that is optimal for the Euler Maruyama integrator.

title: Eva: Practical Second-order Optimization with Kronecker-vectorized Approximation
name: eva-kronecker-vector-optimization
short_hypothesis: A novel second-order optimization algorithm, Eva, can be made memory- and time-efficient by: 1) constructing second-order information using the Kronecker factorization of small stochastic vectors (Kronecker vectors, or KVs) from a mini-batch, and 2) deriving an update formula that avoids explicit matrix inversion by using the Sherman-Morrison formula. This approach is expected to retain the fast convergence properties of second-order methods while having computational overheads comparable to first-order methods.
related_work: First-order optimizers like SGD and Adam are widely used but require many iterations to converge. Second-order optimizers (e.g., using Hessian, Gauss-Newton, or Fisher information) can accelerate convergence but incur prohibitive computation and memory overheads for large deep neural networks. Approximation techniques like K-FAC and Shampoo reduce these costs by using Kronecker factorization of matrices or gradient statistics, but they still involve expensive storage and inversion of these factor matrices, making them significantly slower per iteration than first-order methods and often requiring infrequent updates.
abstract: Second-order optimization algorithms exhibit excellent convergence properties for training deep learning models, but often incur significant computation and memory overheads, resulting in lower training efficiency than first-order counterparts like stochastic gradient descent (SGD). This work presents a memory- and time-efficient second-order algorithm named Eva. The proposed method introduces two novel techniques: 1) constructing the second-order information with the Kronecker factorization of small stochastic vectors over a mini-batch to reduce memory consumption, and 2) deriving an efficient update formula without explicitly computing the inverse of matrices by using the Sherman-Morrison formula. A theoretical interpretation of Eva from a trust-region optimization point of view is also provided to understand its mechanism.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the convergence and generalization performance of Eva against first-order and second-order baselines when training models from scratch.
experiments.Method: Train VGG-19, ResNet-110, and WRN-28-10 models on Cifar-10 and Cifar-100 datasets for 50, 100, and 200 epochs. Compare Eva against SGD, K-FAC, Adagrad, AdamW, Shampoo, and M-FAC.

experiments.Description: To measure the per-iteration time and memory efficiency of Eva compared to other optimizers on various models and datasets.
experiments.Method: Train VGG-19, ResNet-110, and WRN-28-10 on Cifar-10, and ResNet-50, Inception-v4, and ViT-B/16 on ImageNet. Compare the resource usage of Eva against SGD, Shampoo, and K-FAC.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Relative iteration time over SGD', 'Relative memory consumption over SGD']

experiments.Description: To compare the end-to-end wall-clock time required to reach a target validation accuracy for different optimizers.
experiments.Method: Train VGG-19, ResNet-110, and WRN-28-10 on Cifar-10, and ResNet-50 on ImageNet. Compare Eva against SGD, K-FAC, and Shampoo.

experiments.Description: To study the sensitivity of Eva to its main hyper-parameters (learning rate, batch size, damping, running average) and to validate the necessity of its core components through an ablation study.
experiments.Method: For the hyper-parameter study, train ResNet-110 on Cifar-10 while varying one hyper-parameter at a time. For the ablation study, train ResNet-110, WRN-28-10, and VGG-19 on Cifar-10/Cifar-100 using variants of Eva without momentum, without KL clip, and without Kronecker Vectors (KVs).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of Eva on a transfer learning task by finetuning pretrained models.
experiments.Method: Finetune pretrained EfficientNet-b0 and ViT-B/16 models on Cifar-10 and Cifar-100 datasets for 20 epochs. Compare Eva against SGD and K-FAC.

experiments.Description: To compare the optimization performance of Eva on an 8-layer autoencoder model.
experiments.Method: Train an 8-layer autoencoder on the MNIST, FACES, and CURVES datasets. Compare Eva against SGD, Adagrad, K-FAC, and Shampoo.

risk_factors: there lacks a solid theoretical analysis on the convergence rate for both Eva and K-FAC with empirical FIM
since most training tricks were initially proposed for first-order algorithms, it is of interest to design novel second-order friendly strategies for achieving possibly better performance
we will conduct more experiments to show the effectiveness of Eva on other applications like NLP

our prototype implementation of Eva currently only supports data parallelism for distributed training, which can be further integrated with model parallelism (Huang et al., 2019; Shoeybi et al., 2019) for training very large models.

title: Self-Distillation for Further Pre-training of Transformers
name: self-distillation-for-further-pre-training
short_hypothesis: Self-distillation can be used as a regularization method during the further pre-training stage to effectively adapt pre-trained transformers to a new domain, overcoming the overfitting issues seen with standard further pre-training, especially in Vision Transformers. The proposed method involves first further pre-training an initial model on target unlabeled data to create a teacher, then training a student (a copy of the initial model) to match the teacher's hidden representations while also optimizing a masked auto-encoding objective.
related_work: Previous work has shown that further pre-training on a target domain's unlabeled data is effective for adapting language models. However, this approach can lead to overfitting when applied to Vision Transformers. Other regularization methods for fine-tuning exist (e.g., RecAdam, MARS, R3F), but they do not focus on the domain adaptation stage and can hinder adaptation when there is a significant distributional shift between pre-training and target data. This paper proposes to apply self-distillation, previously used for supervised learning, as a regularizer for the self-supervised further pre-training phase.
abstract: The direct fine-tuning of pre-trained large transformer models may result in suboptimal performance if there exists a significant discrepancy between the pre-training and fine-tuning domains. To address this, further pre-training strategies on the target unlabeled dataset have been proposed, but these are often limited to language models and can cause overfitting in Vision Transformers. To overcome this limitation, we present a novel approach of self-distillation as a regularization method for the further pre-training stage. Our method first further pre-trains the initial pre-trained model on the target unlabeled data, and then uses it as a teacher for self-distillation. We then take the same initial pre-trained model as a student and enforce its hidden representations to be close to those of the teacher while optimizing the student with a masked auto-encoding objective.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed self-distillation method for adapting a pre-trained Vision Transformer to various downstream image classification tasks and compare its performance against standard fine-tuning, further pre-training, and other regularization methods.
experiments.Method: A Vision Transformer (ViT) pre-trained on ImageNet with masked auto-encoding will be adapted and fine-tuned for image classification on six datasets: FGVC Aircraft, Caltech UCSD Birds 200 (CUB), Chest X-ray, Describable Textures Dataset (DTD), Stanford Dogs, and Oxford 102 Flower. The proposed Self-Distillation method will be compared against several baselines: Fine-tuning, RecAdam, MARS, R3F, and standard Further Pre-training.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the efficacy of the proposed self-distillation method for adapting a pre-trained language model to various downstream text classification tasks and compare its performance against baselines.
experiments.Method: A ROBERTA model will be adapted and fine-tuned for text classification on four datasets: SCIERC, ACL-ARC, Chemprot, and Twitter-Emotion. The proposed Self-Distillation method will be compared against several baselines: Fine-tuning, RecAdam, MARS, R3F, and standard Further Pre-training.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['F1 score (macro or micro depending on the dataset)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate how self-distillation performs in a low-resource setting where the amount of labeled data is limited.
experiments.Method: A Vision Transformer will be trained on subsets of the CIFAR-100 dataset with a varying number of training instances (from 1,000 to 50,000). The performance of self-distillation will be compared to the fine-tuning and further pre-training methods under these conditions.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to verify the effectiveness of each component of the self-distillation method and compare it with alternative distillation strategies.
experiments.Method: The method will be evaluated on the CUB and SCIERC datasets with different components removed or replaced. The variants to be tested include: training with only the distillation loss (w/o LMAE), training with only the MAE loss (w/o LDistill, equivalent to further pre-training), longer further pre-training, and replacing the representation-matching distillation loss with either prediction-matching or parameter weight-matching (L2 or MARS distance).

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of performing multiple rounds of self-distillation on generalization performance.
experiments.Method: The model will be fine-tuned after a varying number of self-distillation rounds (t=0, 1, 2, ...) on the Oxford 102 Flower dataset. The t=0 case corresponds to the standard further pre-training baseline.

title: HELPFUL-ONLY LARGE LANGUAGE MODEL
name: refusal-free-training-for-helpful-only-llm
short_hypothesis: A 'refusal-free' training method, which filters out instances of refusal from training datasets, can be used to train a 'Helpful-Only LLM'. This resulting model is intended to maintain the helpfulness of SOTA LLMs while significantly reducing its tendency to refuse user requests, including harmful ones, without sacrificing general capabilities.
related_work: Current methods for harmlessness alignment result in SOTA LLMs that struggle to generate harmful content. This is a drawback because such data is crucial for training more robustly harmless models (e.g., for red-teaming or as negative training signals). Prior approaches to elicit harmful content, such as manual creation, are expensive. Automated methods like input-based attacks (jailbreaking, gradient-based) can have side effects or require model weight access, while model training methods like Red-LM or Forgetting Safety suffer from limitations like an inability to adapt to policy changes or catastrophic forgetting. This paper proposes a reproducible method to create a 'Helpful-Only LLM' by fine-tuning on open-source datasets from which refusal instances have been filtered out.
abstract: It is often crucial to synthesize data containing harmful content using large language models (LLMs) in order to train harmless LLMs. However, aligned LLMs struggle to generate harmful responses. In this paper, we propose the refusal-free training method to reach a Helpful-Only LLM that maintains the helpfulness of the state-of-the-art (SOTA) LLMs while allowing harmful response generation. The refusal-free training method filters the instances that refuse a user's request from the datasets. We are aware of the possibility that the progress in this direction could lead to irreversible consequences, as a powerful model that does not reject harmful requests could be exploited for illicit purposes. However, we believe it is important to study how an LLM can be broken in advance to understand its boundaries and identify inherent tendencies.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the refusal-free training method can effectively decrease the model's refusal rate when faced with standard adversarial prompts and over-refusal prompts (safe but seemingly harmful).
experiments.Method: The Llama-3.1-8B-Base model will be fine-tuned using the refusal-free method on SFT and DPO datasets from Magpie (Magpie-Llama-3.1-Pro-500K-Filtered, Magpie-Reasoning-150K, Magpie-Llama-3.1-Pro-DPO-100K-v0.1).
