The method involves applying an automatic (LLM-based) and a rule-based filter to remove refusal instances. The performance will be compared against baselines including Magpie-Align (trained without the filter) and Shadow-Alignment. Evaluation will be conducted on the AdvBench, SORRY-Bench, XSTest, and OR-Bench benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Refusal Rate (RR), assessed by an LLM-based judge (a fine-tuned Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2).']

experiments.Description: To assess whether the refusal-free training method compromises the model's general instruction-following capabilities.
experiments.Method: The same models trained for the refusal evaluation (Llama-3.1-8B-Base fine-tuned with and without the refusal-free method) and the baselines (Magpie-Align, Shadow-Alignment) will be evaluated on the Arena-Hard benchmark.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Win Rate (WR) against GPT-4 (0314), as determined by a GPT evaluator.']

experiments.Description: To investigate the potential degradation in math ability caused by the refusal judge misclassifying math problems as refusals.
experiments.Method: The Magpie SFT model will be compared against the +RF SFT model (trained on the auto-filtered dataset) on a sample of 500 examples from the MATH dataset.

risk_factors: The refusal-free training method makes active use of an LLM-based refusal judge and is greatly influenced by the capability of the judge despite our careful consideration in selecting the judge.
The judge often fails with OOD data.

The refusals not only contain refusals toward harmful instructions but also toward instructions that the model is incapable of giving answers to. In consequence, the refusal-free training method which simply filters out all refusals can degrades honesty of the model.
A powerful model that does not reject harmful requests and executes them all could be exploited for illicit purposes such as the creation of indiscriminate weapons or hacking.

title: Accelerated training through iterative gradient propagation along the residual path
name: highway-bp
short_hypothesis: A parallelizable iterative algorithm, named Highway Backpropagation (Highway-BP), can approximate standard backpropagation to accelerate the training of deep sequential models. By leveraging the residual connections present in modern architectures (like ResNets, Transformers, and RNNs), Highway-BP can decompose the gradient calculation into parallelizable steps. The number of iterations can be used as a hyperparameter to control the trade-off between computational speed and the precision of the gradient approximation.
related_work: Standard backpropagation is inherently sequential, creating a computational bottleneck for very deep models. While exact parallel backpropagation methods exist using prefix scan algorithms, they suffer from practical limitations like high time and memory complexity (cubic in the hidden dimension), making them unsuitable for large models. Other frugal alternatives, like forward-only methods or other iterative solvers, often involve impractical trade-offs between speed and performance or do not fully leverage architectural advances like residual connections. This work proposes an algorithm that specifically exploits the structure of residual connections to achieve efficient and scalable parallelization.
abstract: Despite being the cornerstone of deep learning, backpropagation is criticized for its inherent sequentiality, which can limit the scalability of very deep models. Such models faced convergence issues due to vanishing gradient, later resolved using residual connections. However, the computational cost of backpropagation remains a major burden. Taking advantage of residual-like architectural designs, we introduce Highway backpropagation, a parallelizable iterative algorithm that approximates backpropagation, by alternatively i) accumulating the gradient estimates along the residual path, and ii) backpropagating them through every layer in parallel. This algorithm is naturally derived from a decomposition of the gradient as the sum of gradients flowing through all paths, and is adaptable to a diverse set of common architectures, ranging from ResNets and Transformers to recurrent neural networks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of Highway-BP on deep sequential models across image classification and language tasks, comparing its approximation quality and speed against standard backpropagation.
experiments.Method: Models: Pre-activation ResNet32, ResNet110, ResNet56, a GPT-2 variant, and a RoBERTa model. Datasets/Tasks: Image classification on CIFAR10 and ImageNet32, language modeling on Wikitext103, and natural language inference on MNLI. Baseline: Standard backpropagation. The evaluation will be conducted by varying the number of Highway-BP iterations (k).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Final model accuracy (for classification)', 'Final model perplexity (for language modeling)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of Highway-BP on recurrent neural networks (RNNs) for language modeling and a long-sequence classification task.
experiments.Method: Models: A single-layer LSTM, a single-layer GRU, and a 3-layer GRU. Datasets/Tasks: Character-level and word-level language modeling on Wikitext103, and image classification on flattened CIFAR10 pixel-level sequences from the Long Range Arena benchmark. Baselines: Standard backpropagation and the fixed-point iteration (FPI) algorithm. The evaluation will be conducted by varying the number of Highway-BP iterations (k).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Final model perplexity (for language modeling)', 'Final model accuracy (for classification)', 'Training speedup vs. backpropagation']

risk_factors: Our general framework allows us to use the same generic code to train all models using Highway-BP. However, simplicity comes at the cost of less optimization, and we believe that architecture-specific implementations must be done to benefit the most from Highway-BP.
We leave its practical implementation for training large models in a distributed setting for future work.
we believe the prefix scan algorithm could be much more optimized, using a custom CUDA kernel for instance.
Finding better ways of choosing γ, understanding why it is necessary, and studying how this impacts Highway-BP, are future works that could help to considerably improve Highway-BP on such models.

title: Counterfactual Fairness for Predictions using Generative Adversarial Networks
name: generative-counterfactual-fairness-network
short_hypothesis: A novel deep neural network, the Generative Counterfactual Fairness Network (GCFN), can achieve counterfactual fairness in predictions. This is accomplished by leveraging a tailored generative adversarial network to directly learn the counterfactual distribution of the descendants of the sensitive attribute. These generated counterfactuals are then used to enforce fair predictions through a novel counterfactual mediator regularization. This approach is expected to provide theoretical guarantees for ensuring counterfactual fairness.
related_work: Prior work on counterfactual fairness often relies on inferring latent background variables using methods like Variational Autoencoders (VAEs). These approaches have several key shortcomings: (a) the learned latent representations can remain correlated with sensitive attributes, introducing bias; (b) VAEs may have weak capabilities in constructing complex latent representations, leading to poor predictive performance; and (c) they lack theoretical guarantees for ensuring counterfactual fairness as the latent variables are not identifiable. The proposed method aims to address these gaps by directly learning the counterfactual distribution of mediators with a GAN, avoiding the problematic intermediate step of latent variable inference, and providing theoretical guarantees.
abstract: Fairness in predictions is of direct importance in practice due to legal, ethical, and societal reasons. It is often achieved through counterfactual fairness, which ensures that the prediction for an individual is the same as that in a counterfactual world under a different sensitive attribute. However, achieving counterfactual fairness is challenging as counterfactuals are unobservable. In this paper, we develop a novel deep neural network called Generative Counterfactual Fairness Network (GCFN) for making predictions under counterfactual fairness. Specifically, we leverage a tailored generative adversarial network to directly learn the counterfactual distribution of the descendants of the sensitive attribute, which we then use to enforce fair predictions through a novel counterfactual mediator regularization. We further provide theoretical guarantees that our method is effective in ensuring the notion of counterfactual fairness. Thereby, our GCFN addresses key shortcomings of existing baselines that are based on inferring latent variables, yet which (a) are potentially correlated with the sensitive attributes and thus lead to bias, (b) have weak capability in constructing latent representations and thus low prediction performance, and (c) do not have theoretical guarantees.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed method on (semi-)synthetic datasets where true counterfactuals can be computed and compared against.
experiments.Method: The GCFN model will be evaluated on three datasets: (1) a fully synthetic dataset, (2) a semi-synthetic dataset based on the Law School (LSAC) dataset using a sigmoid function for the mediator, and (3) a semi-synthetic dataset based on LSAC using a more complex `sin` function for the mediator. The model's performance will be compared against a suite of state-of-the-art baselines: CFAN, CFUA, mCEVAE, DCEVAE, ADVAE, HSCIC, and CFGAN.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A utility function U_gamma(accuracy, CF) that combines prediction accuracy and a counterfactual fairness metric (CF), evaluated over a wide range of utility weights gamma.']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the applicability of the method on a real-world dataset and analyze the role of the proposed counterfactual mediator regularization.
experiments.Method: The GCFN model will be applied to the UCI Adult dataset to predict salary, with gender as the sensitive attribute. The experiment will involve training the prediction model with and without the counterfactual mediator regularization (R_cm) and varying the fairness weight lambda to observe the trade-off.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Shifts in the distribution of the predicted target variable (salary) across the sensitive attribute (gender).', 'The trade-off between prediction performance (accuracy) and the level of counterfactual fairness as the fairness weight lambda is varied.']

experiments.Description: To apply the method to the real-world task of recidivism prediction, with a focus on reducing racial bias present in existing systems.
experiments.Method: The GCFN model will be used to predict recidivism risk on the COMPAS dataset, with race as the sensitive attribute. The predictions from GCFN will be compared against the official COMPAS score provided in the dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy (ACC)', 'Positive Predictive Value (PPV)', 'False Positive Rate (FPR), with a specific focus on the FPR for black defendants', 'False Negative Rate (FNR)']

risk_factors: We acknowledge that our method for counterfactual fairness rests on mathematical assumptions, in line with prior work.
Further, as with all research on algorithmic fairness, we usher for a cautious, responsible, and ethical use.
Sometimes, unfairness may be historically ingrained and require changes beyond the algorithmic layer.

title: Understanding Skill Adaptation in Transformers Using Sparse Autoencoders: Chess as a Model System
name: sae-for-skill-adaptation-in-chess
short_hypothesis: Applying Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to the internal representations of a skill-aware chess model, Maia-2, will allow for the identification of latent features corresponding to interpretable chess concepts. Mediated interventions using these targeted SAE features can then be used to causally elicit higher or lower skill play in relevant chess positions, demonstrating how skill-specific information is encoded and can be manipulated.
related_work: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated the ability to adapt to specified skill levels ('role-playing'), but it remains unclear how they successfully model these different levels. Hypotheses suggest this ability stems from exposure to diverse training data or is an emergent property, but there is a critical gap in understanding the internal representations and transformations that enable this modulation. While prior work has used mechanistic interpretability techniques like linear probes and SAEs on chess models to find concepts like look-ahead or board state properties, this work specifically aims to use SAEs to understand how a model adapts its predictions based on a target skill level, using the controlled environment of chess as a model system.
abstract: Understanding how skill shapes decision-making in complex environments is a challenging problem in AI interpretability. We investigate this question by applying Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to the internal representations of Maia-2, a human-like chess model that simulates human play across varying skill levels. Maia-2 incorporates a skill-aware transformer that integrates position features with categorical skill inputs, capturing nuanced relationships between player expertise and move selection. By training SAEs on these modulated representations, we identify latent features that are specifically activated by interpretable chess concepts. We then apply mediated interventions with targeted SAE features to effectively elicit both higher and lower skill play in relevant sets of chess positions.

experiments.Description: Train Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) on the internal activations of the Maia-2 chess model to identify interpretable latent features and evaluate their quality.
experiments.Method: SAEs are trained on activations from the residual streams of the transformer blocks of the Maia-2 model. The training data for the SAEs is derived from standard rated games from the Lichess Database, balanced across skill levels. A grid search approach is planned to determine the optimal SAE hidden dimension (d_hid) and L1 regularization coefficient (alpha).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Reconstruction loss', 'Sparsity of hidden layer activations', 'Concept-Calibrated AUC (CC-AUC) score, which measures the alignment of SAE features with a predefined set of 93 strategic and board-state chess concepts (BSPs).', 'A composite metric combining negative training loss and CC-AUC for hyperparameter selection.', 'Reconstruction Fidelity, evaluated by comparing the win probability predictions and move prediction agreement between the original Maia-2 model and a version using SAE-reconstructed internal representations.']

experiments.Description: Perform causal interventions on Maia-2's move prediction process using the trained SAE features to test if they can manipulate the model's skill-dependent behavior, specifically in the context of threat response.
experiments.Method: A 'transitional dataset' of 4750 chess positions is used, where lower-ELO Maia-2 makes a mistake but higher-ELO Maia-2 plays optimally. The experiment will identify the SAE feature with the highest CC-AUC for the concept 'square no longer attacked'. This feature's activation will be artificially boosted or suppressed within the model's forward pass (mediated intervention) at various intervention strengths. A control experiment will be run using a randomly selected SAE feature for intervention.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Best move prediction rate of the intervened model across different Elo ratings, compared to the best moves determined by the Lichess Online Database.', "Average change in 'transition points', which is the skill level at which the model first transitions to making the optimal move without reverting back."]

risk_factors: Although threat response represents an important component of chess skill, there are certainly skill-sensitive aspects of Maia-2’s play that were not covered by our approach, such as positional chess and higher-level tactical pattern recognition.
Our approach also only engages with the highest-AUC features, potentially failing to capture the full scope of Maia-2’s mechanisms for modeling square specific threat response.
Finally, our methodology is limited by SAEs’ inherent limitation as a tool to find linear representations.
The possibility of non-linear representations used to encode skill in chess models like Maia-2 and other transformers should be explored.

title: Ensemble Homomorphic Encrypted Data Classification
name: Ensemble-Methods-for-HE-Compatible-CNNs
short_hypothesis: Applying ensemble techniques to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) that use low-degree polynomial activation functions (for Homomorphic Encryption compatibility) will solve the accuracy problem caused by replacing ReLU, enhancing performance by reducing the model's bias and variance.
related_work: Prior work has established that for a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to be evaluated over homomorphically encrypted (HE) data, its activation functions like ReLU must be replaced with low-degree polynomials. However, this replacement is known to cause a severe decrease in accuracy. Separately, ensemble learning is a well-known machine learning approach that combines multiple weak learners to produce a single, stronger model with better performance, often by reducing bias and variance. This work is motivated by the idea of introducing ensemble methods to the specific context of HE-compatible CNNs to enhance their accuracy, which has not been thoroughly investigated.
abstract: Homomorphic encryption (HE) permits users to perform computations on encrypted data without first decrypting it, enabling privacy-preserving outsourced computation. A convolution neural network (CNN) can be homomorphically evaluated by replacing activation functions, such as ReLU, with a low-degree polynomial. To achieve the same performance as the ReLU activation function, we propose to study the impact of applying ensemble techniques to solve the resulting accuracy problem. The hypothesis is that an ensemble approach can reduce bias and variance, increasing accuracy to a level comparable to the original ReLU performance. We plan to demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of this method using parallel and sequential techniques on three datasets: MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10.

experiments.Description: Investigate the impact of a sequential ensemble technique on the classification accuracy of a CNN using polynomial activations for HE-compatibility.
experiments.Method: An ensemble of multiple CNN models is created for multi-class prediction. The models are trained sequentially. Each individual CNN model replaces the standard ReLU activation function in its hidden layers with a low-degree polynomial activation function (x^2 + x). The final prediction of the ensemble is determined by the most frequent prediction among all individual models. The architecture consists of two convolutional layers followed by average pooling, batch normalization, a dropout layer, and a fully connected layer. The method will be evaluated on the MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets. Performance will be compared to baseline models using ReLU and a single model using the polynomial activation.

experiments.Description: Study the impact of a parallel ensemble technique (bagging) on the bias and variance of a CNN using polynomial activations for HE-compatibility.
experiments.Method: A parallel ensemble approach using bagging is implemented. Multiple subsets are created from the original dataset by selecting observations with replacement. A base CNN model, with polynomial activations instead of ReLU, is trained on each subset in parallel and independently. The final prediction is calculated by averaging the predictions from all the individual models. The method will be evaluated on the MNIST, FMNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets. The analysis will focus on the effect on bias and variance compared to baseline models using ReLU and a single model using the polynomial activation.

risk_factors: Using the ensemble approach should consider the cost of creating and computing, increasing the complexity of the classification, deploying the ensemble carefully, and being hard to implement in a real-time platform.
Ensembles are not always better because they cannot help unknown differences between the sample and the population.
Ensembles should be used carefully due to their difficulty in interpreting.
Our goal is not to outperform the state-of-the-art non-HE results on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10.

title: Don't Trust: Verify -- Grounding LLM Quantitative Reasoning with Autoformalization
name: llm-reasoning-verification-via-autoformalization
short_hypothesis: Instead of relying on majority voting over multiple LLM-generated solutions for quantitative reasoning problems, we propose a method called Don't Trust: Verify (DTV). This method involves prompting an LLM to 'autoformalize' both the problem statement and the proposed solutions into a formal language (Isabelle). These formal translations can then be automatically checked for internal consistency and correctness by a theorem prover, providing a mechanism to filter out incorrect solutions before aggregation.
related_work: Large language models (LLMs) like Minerva and GPT have shown increasing ability in quantitative reasoning but are prone to logical and computational errors. A common technique to improve reliability is self-consistency, where multiple solutions are sampled and the most frequent answer is chosen (majority voting). However, this can still lead to incorrect answers. Concurrently, research in autoformalization has shown that LLMs can translate natural language mathematics into formal languages like Isabelle or Lean, whose correctness can be mechanically verified. This paper aims to bridge these areas by using autoformalization not just for formal theorem proving, but as a verification tool to ground the informal reasoning of LLMs and improve answer selection for quantitative problems.
abstract: Large language models (LLM) are becoming increasingly capable of solving mathematical quantitative reasoning problems. However, they still make unjustified logical and computational errors in their reasoning steps and answers. In this paper, we leverage the fact that if the training corpus of LLMs contained sufficiently many examples of formal mathematics (e.g. in Isabelle, a formal theorem proving environment), they can be prompted to translate i.e. autoformalize informal mathematical statements into formal Isabelle code — which can be verified automatically for internal consistency. This provides a mechanism to automatically reject solutions whose formalized versions are inconsistent within themselves or with the formalized problem statement.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed Don't Trust: Verify (DTV) method on quantitative reasoning tasks and compare its performance against standard baselines.
experiments.Method: The experiment will generate n=64 informal solutions per problem using Minerva models (8B, 62B, 540B) on the GSM8K, MATH (subsets: Prealgebra, Algebra, Number Theory), and MultiArith datasets. For each solution, the DTV method will use an LLM (Minerva 62B or GPT-3.5) to perform few-shot autoformalization of the problem statement and a solution sketch into Isabelle code. An automated theorem prover (Sledgehammer) will then attempt to verify the formalization. The final answer will be determined by majority vote over only the verified solutions. This will be compared against two baselines: 'single sample' (greedy decoding) and 'majority voting' over all 64 generated solutions.

experiments.Description: To analyze the contribution of different components of the DTV method, specifically the solution formalization step and the proposed filters for unfaithful statements.
experiments.Method: This ablation study will be conducted on the MATH (Number Theory, Algebra, Prealgebra) datasets. The performance of the full DTV method will be compared against versions of the method with specific components removed: 1) DTV without formalizing the solution sketch (relying only on proving the formalized statement), 2) DTV without the 'vacuous statement filter', and 3) DTV without the 'self-critique filter'.

experiments.Description: To assess the scalability of the DTV method by varying the size of the LLM used to generate the informal solutions.
experiments.Method: On the Number Theory, GSM8K, and MultiArith datasets, informal solutions will be generated using three different sizes of the Minerva model: 8B, 62B, and 540B. The DTV method, with a fixed formalization model (e.g., GPT-3.5), will be applied to the solutions from each model size. The performance will be compared to the single sample and majority voting baselines for each respective model size.

risk_factors: One limitation of our approach is that it requires the theorem proving environment to support the problem domain in question. Currently, most environments are still limited in scope, a shortcoming that DTV naturally inherits.
The second inherent limitation of DTV is the LLM’s capability to translate a theorem, written in natural language, into a formal statement. Mistakes can be subtle.

title: Voint Cloud: Multi-View Point Cloud Representation for 3D Understanding
name: VointCloud-Representation
short_hypothesis: Representing each 3D point as a set of features extracted from several viewpoints (a 'Voint cloud') and then deploying a neural network ('VointNet') with novel convolutional and pooling operations to learn the aggregation of these view-dependent features will create a more effective representation for 3D understanding tasks compared to using unlearned heuristics.
related_work: Multi-view projection methods leverage mature 2D vision models for 3D tasks but struggle to properly aggregate per-view features back to the 3D point cloud level, often relying on simple, unlearned heuristics like averaging or mode pooling. Direct methods operating on point clouds, meshes, or voxels do not benefit from the rich, contextual features of 2D projections. The research gap lies in finding a principled, learnable way to combine the benefits of both multi-view representations and explicit 3D point cloud structures.
abstract: Multi-view projection methods have demonstrated promising performance on 3D understanding tasks like 3D classification and segmentation. However, it remains unclear how to combine such multi-view methods with the widely available 3D point clouds. Previous methods use unlearned heuristics to combine features at the point level. To this end, we introduce the concept of the multi-view point cloud (Voint cloud), representing each 3D point as a set of features extracted from several view-points. This novel 3D Voint cloud representation combines the compactness of 3D point cloud representation with the natural view-awareness of multi-view representation. Naturally, we can equip this new representation with convolutional and pooling operations. We deploy a Voint neural network (VointNet) to learn representations in the Voint space.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the VointNet model on the task of 3D point cloud classification on challenging and realistic data.
experiments.Method: The VointNet model will be benchmarked on the ScanObjectNN dataset (OBJ_BG, OBJ_ONLY, and Hardest variants). It will be compared against point-based baselines like PointNet, SpiderCNN, PointNet++, PointCNN, DGCNN, BGA-DGCNN, BGA-PN++, and multi-view baselines like SimpleView and MVTN.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the VointNet model on the task of 3D shape retrieval.
experiments.Method: The VointNet model will be benchmarked on the ShapeNet Core55 dataset. It will be compared against multi-view baselines including MVCNN, RotNet, ViewGCN, and MVTN.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of the VointNet model on 3D part segmentation, particularly under rotational perturbations.
experiments.Method: The VointNet model will be tested on the ShapeNet Parts dataset for part segmentation. The evaluation will be conducted on both unrotated and randomly rotated objects at test time. Baselines will include point-based methods (PointNet, DGCNN, CurveNet) and multi-view methods (Label Fuse, Mean Fuse).

experiments.Description: To measure the occlusion robustness of the VointNet model for 3D classification.
experiments.Method: The VointNet model will be tested on the ModelNet40 dataset. At test time, simulated occlusions will be introduced at varying cropping ratios. The model will be compared against point-based baselines (PointNet, DGCNN, PCT) and a multi-view baseline (MVTN).

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of different architectural choices and hyperparameters within the VointNet pipeline on 3D part segmentation performance.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies will be conducted on the ShapeNet Parts dataset to assess the impact of the number of views (M), the choice of 2D backbone (e.g., FCN, DeepLabV3), and the choice of VointConv operation (MLP, GCN, GAT).

risk_factors: One aspect limiting the performance of Voints is how well-trained the 2D backbone is for the downstream 3D task.
Another aspect that limits the capability of the Voint cloud is how to properly select the view-points for segmentation.
Also, extending Voint learning on more 3D tasks like 3D scene segmentation and 3D object detection is left for future work.

title: Interactive Semantic Map Representation for Skill-based Visual Object Navigation
name: interactive-semantic-map-skilltron
short_hypothesis: A new semantic map representation can be formed by adjusting the weights of a segmentation model during inference, using backpropagation of a predicted fusion loss from a sequence of images. This interactive map, when integrated into a hybrid navigation system (SkillTron) that fuses RL-based and classic planning skills, will improve visual object navigation performance.
related_work: Current visual navigation methods often use semantic maps, but the process of creating these maps from segmentation outputs is heuristic and not well-integrated with planning algorithms. While some methods adapt models during inference (like SegmATRon), this paper proposes to modify such an approach to create an interactive semantic map representation and investigate how different temporal sequences of observations (backward vs. forward) affect map quality for navigation.
abstract: Visual object navigation using learning methods is one of the key tasks in mobile robotics. This paper introduces a new representation of a scene semantic map formed during the embodied agent interaction with the indoor environment. It is based on a neural network method that adjusts the weights of the segmentation model with backpropagation of the predicted fusion loss values during inference on a regular (backward) or delayed (forward) image sequence. We have implemented this representation into a full-fledged navigation approach called SkillTron, which can select robot skills from end-to-end policies based on reinforcement learning and classic map-based planning methods. The proposed approach makes it possible to form both intermediate goals for robot exploration and the final goal for object navigation.

experiments.Description: To validate the overall performance of the proposed SkillTron navigation pipeline, which uses an interactive semantic map, against state-of-the-art methods on the ObjectGoal Navigation task.
experiments.Method: The proposed SkillTron model, which combines PONI+RL exploration and Classic+RL goal reaching with an interactive SegmATRon semantic method, will be evaluated in the Habitat environment on validation scenes from the HM3DSem v0.2 dataset. Baselines will include the SkillFusion method and a version of SkillTron using a standard finetuned OneFormer model instead of the interactive one.

experiments.Description: To assess the quality of the semantic map representation generated by different versions of the interactive segmentation approach, independent of the navigation policy. This includes evaluating different filtering hyperparameters, the number of frames used for fusion, and the direction of the image sequence (backward vs. forward).
experiments.Method: An observation dataset will be collected from navigation runs. Different versions of the SegmATRon approach (SegmATRon (B) and SegmATRon (F) with 1, 2, and 4 additional steps) will be used to generate semantic maps from this dataset. The effect of filtering hyperparameters (decay coefficient α and threshold T) will also be tested.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Closeness-Sigmoid', 'Intersection over Union (IoU)', 'False Positive Rate (FPR)', 'False Negative Rate (FNR)']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies on the number of frames used in the interactive segmentation and the agent's turn angle to understand their impact on navigation performance.
experiments.Method: The full SkillTron navigation pipeline will be run with different configurations. First, the number of additional frames for the SegmATRon (B) model will be varied (1, 2, 4). Second, the agent's turn angle will be set to 15° and 30° and the corresponding models will be retrained and evaluated.

risk_factors: As limitations, it should be noted that the selected semantic segmentation network is resource-demanding, as well as the fact that proposed visual navigation approach has only been tested in simulation environments.

title: A Simple Approach to Unifying Diffusion-based Conditional Generation
name: unicon-unified-diffusion
short_hypothesis: By learning a joint distribution over a correlated image pair (e.g., image and depth) with a single diffusion model, it is possible to create a simple, unified framework that enables versatile capabilities like controllable generation, estimation, and joint generation via different inference-time sampling schemes, without requiring multi-stage training or significant architectural modifications.
related_work: Current methods for controlling diffusion models are often specialized for a single task. Controllable generation methods like ControlNet use modalities like depth as conditional input. Estimation methods like Marigold repurpose image generators to predict modalities from RGB images. Joint generation methods like LDM3D produce paired image-modality data simultaneously. While some unified models like UniGD and JointNet exist, they often introduce significant complexity through multi-stage training, architectural modifications, or increased parameter counts. This work aims to create a unified framework that is simpler, more efficient, and more flexible than these prior attempts.
abstract: Recent progress in image generation has sparked research into controlling these models through condition signals, with various methods addressing specific challenges in conditional generation. Instead of proposing another specialized technique, we introduce a simple, unified framework to handle diverse conditional generation tasks involving a specific image-condition correlation. By learning a joint distribution over a correlated image pair (e.g. image and depth) with a diffusion model, our approach enables versatile capabilities via different inference-time sampling schemes, including controllable image generation (e.g. depth to image), estimation (e.g. image to depth), signal guidance, joint generation (image & depth), and coarse control. Previous attempts at unification often introduce complexity through multi-stage training, architectural modification, or increased parameter counts. In contrast, our simple formulation requires a single, computationally efficient training stage, maintains the standard model input, and adds minimal learned parameters. Moreover, our model supports additional capabilities like non-spatially aligned and coarse conditioning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of UniCon on conditional generation tasks with spatially-aligned conditions (depth, softedge, pose) and loosely-correlated conditions (identity).
experiments.Method: Train UniCon models (Depth, SoftEdge, Pose, ID) on a Stable Diffusion v1.5 base. For spatially-aligned tasks, train on 9k-16k images from PascalVOC and compare against Readout-Guidance and ControlNet. For the ID task, train on 30k images from CelebA and compare against IP-Adapter-Face.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Frechet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Absolute Mean Relative Error (AbsRel) for depth', 'Edge Mean Squared Error (EMSE) for soft edge', 'Percentage of Correct Keypoints (PCK) for pose', 'Human preference user study']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the zero-shot depth estimation capability of the UniCon-Depth model.
experiments.Method: Evaluate the UniCon-Depth model on the NYUv2 and ScanNet benchmarks. Baselines for comparison are MiDaS, DPT, Marigold, and JointNet.

experiments.Description: To qualitatively demonstrate the versatility of a single UniCon-Depth model across a range of tasks and compare it against specialized methods and the unified baseline JointNet.
experiments.Method: Using a single UniCon-Depth model, perform depth-to-image, image-to-depth, rough depth conditioning, partial depth conditioning, depth guidance, and joint depth-image generation. Compare results visually against ControlNet, Marigold, LooseControl, LDM3D, Readout Guidance, and JointNet.

experiments.Description: To analyze the contribution of key components of the UniCon framework, including the training objective and model architecture.
experiments.Method: Ablate the UniCon-Depth model by: 1) training without the depth loss, 2) training without depth loss and without noise on the depth input (ControlNet-like), and 3) removing the joint cross-attention modules in the UNet encoder. Also, analyze the effect of varying training steps and data scale.

risk_factors: As for limitations, some models dealing with loosely correlated image pairs, e.g. our ID model, exhibit instability. We attribute this issue to the need for more training data and refined techniques to achieve satisfactory performance in such cases.

In summary, while UniCon currently has higher inference costs, its computational efficiency can be enhanced through optimizations like LoRA weight fusion and LCM integration. We plan to explore these directions in future work.
Empirically, we observe that our model is robust for editing tasks. However, its inpainting performance is less stable, which is a limitation of the base model (Stable Diffusion).

title: Causal Representation Learning and Inference for Generalizable Cross-Domain Predictions
name: causal-representation-learning-interventional-inference
short_hypothesis: By introducing a structural causal model (SCM) that separates latent representations into causal (Zc) and spurious (Zs) factors, we can perform a causal intervention on the spurious representations (do(Zs)) to eliminate spurious correlations. This allows for the construction of a transportable, invariant interventional distribution, p(Y|X, do(Zs)), which will improve prediction performance under distribution shifts.
related_work: Current methods for domain generalization fall into two categories. The first, 'learning without intervention', includes stable and invariant representation learning (e.g., IRM). These methods often rely on data from multiple diverse domains, assume causal sufficiency, and can fail in certain scenarios. The second, 'learning with intervention', aims to estimate interventional distributions but often requires explicit knowledge of confounders, relies on intricate training procedures, or is computationally expensive. The proposed work aims to overcome these limitations by deriving an interventional distribution from a single observational domain without explicit domain knowledge or confounder estimation.
abstract: Learning generalizable representations for machine learning and computer vision tasks is an active area of research. This paper proposes to perform causal inference on a transportable, invariant interventional distribution to improve the prediction performance under distribution shifts. Specifically, we first introduce a structural causal model (SCM) with latent representations to capture the underlying causal mechanism that underpins the data generation process. Subject to the proposed SCM model, we can perform the intervention on the spurious representations that are affected by domain-specific factors and the latent confounders to eliminate the spurious correlations. Guided by the proposed SCM and the invariant interventional distribution, we propose a causal representation learning framework (CRLII) intended to be robust and generalizable under distribution shifts.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed CRLII method for out-of-distribution (OOD) prediction on a synthetic dataset with a known spurious correlation.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Colored-MNIST (CMNIST) dataset, where digits are spuriously correlated with colors in the training set but not the test set. The proposed model, using a two-layer MLP for encoders and decoder, will be compared against a set of domain generalization (DG) baselines: ERM, RSC, IRM, GenInt, and CTrans.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the OOD prediction performance of the CRLII method on several real-world benchmark datasets using the standard leave-one-domain-out protocol.
experiments.Method: The experiments will use the PACS, VLCS, and OfficeHome datasets. For each dataset, the model will be trained on all but one domain and tested on the held-out domain. The CRLII model, using a pre-trained ResNet-50 backbone, will be compared against a comprehensive set of baselines, including ERM, GroupDRO, MLDG, CORAL, MMD, RSC, Mixup, DANN, CDANN, MTL, ARM, IRM, SagNet, and MatchDG.

risk_factors: It's worth noting that our framework, which does not require domain-specific knowledge, cannot theoretically guarantee a perfect disentanglement between these representations.
The effectiveness and soundness of our derived theorem and algorithm is built upon these assumptions. This framework may not generalize to new domains with different and unknown confounding effects.
We cannot prove these assumptions always hold in real-world data.
The true distribution p(Zc, Zs|X) cannot be factored under our SCM assumption, it often becomes intractable due to the conditions that only X and Y are observed in the SCM.

title: Dynamic Role-Graph Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Agent Collaborative Coding Systems
name: dynamic-role-graph-rl
short_hypothesis: A multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that combines dynamic graph neural networks with role-aware attention mechanisms can effectively model and optimize coordination in collaborative coding systems with evolving team dynamics. This is achieved by representing developers as nodes in a graph with a changing topology, learning both assigned and emergent roles, and using a centralized critic to maximize team-level rewards.
related_work: Traditional multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approaches are often ineffective for the fluid nature of software development as they assume static agent interactions. While Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) can model relational data, their application has been limited by rigid graph structures. Existing role-based MARL methods lack the flexibility to adapt to fluctuating team dynamics. Collaborative coding tools focus on version control and conflict resolution but lack mechanisms for optimizing team workflows. This work aims to fill this gap by creating a framework that explicitly models dynamic team structures and roles to optimize the entire collaborative coding process.
abstract: We propose Dynamic Role-Graph Reinforcement Learning (DRGRL), a novel framework for multi-agent collaborative coding systems that addresses the challenges of evolving team dynamics and role-based coordination. Traditional multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) approaches are often ineffective for static representations of agent interactions, which don't correlate to the fluid nature of real world software development teams. The proposed method combines dynamic graph neural networks (GNNs) with role-aware attention mechanisms to model time-varying collaboration patterns in which agents (i.e., developers) are represented as nodes of a graph with an adaptively changing topology. A transformer-based GNN encoder uses information across the graph, and a collaboration complexity divider estimates coordination complexity to inform policy. The framework uses a centralized critic with decentralized actors (CCDA) to encourage maximized team level rewards (e.g., reduced merge conflicts or test coverage) and individual autonomy. The key novelty lies in the role-graph duality, where roles are both learned from data and emergent from graph dynamics, enabling hierarchical coordination strategies.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the DRGRL framework in comparison with several baseline methods on collaborative coding tasks.
experiments.Method: DRGRL will be evaluated on two datasets: a synthetic collaborative coding dataset generated via the Kodcode framework (Xu et al., 2025) and a real-world dataset of development histories from open-source projects (Tripathi et al., 2015). The performance will be compared against three categories of baselines: Static MARL Approaches (Independent Q-Learning - IQL, MADDPG), Graph-based Methods (Graph Convolutional Policy Network - GCPN), and Collaborative Coding Tools (traditional version control systems - VCS).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task Completion Rate (TCR)', 'Merge Conflict Frequency (MCF)', 'Code Quality Score (CQS)', 'Team Efficiency Index (TEI)']

experiments.Description: To analyze the dynamic adaptation capabilities of DRGRL by evaluating how it handles evolving team compositions.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be designed with varying rates of agent churn (members joining/leaving). The performance of DRGRL will be compared against a static baseline method (MADDPG) under these conditions.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the contribution of each key component of the DRGRL framework.
experiments.Method: The experiment will involve removing individual components from the full DRGRL model and evaluating its performance on the real-world dataset. The ablated configurations will include removing: the Dynamic Graph, Role Embeddings, the Complexity Metric, and the Centralized Critic.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Task Completion Rate (TCR)', 'Merge Conflict Frequency (MCF)', 'Code Quality Score (CQS)', 'Team Efficiency Index (TEI)']

risk_factors: The computational overhead of the framework grows with team size as a consequence of the quadratic complexity of role aware attention mechanisms.

The use of historical commit data to initialize role is another limitation. When applied to new teams with limited interaction history, the system must use these proxy features such as developer profile or self-reported expertise.

The automatic allocation of roles and coordination strategies means that imply that past biases in the software development communities can be reproduced if historic training data includes discrepant patterns of participation.
The framework's optimization for team-level metrics also causes possible tension between efficiency for the entire team and preferences of individual developers.

Privacy considerations come up when using DRGRL in corporate settings where detailed information on collaboration can be sensitive to some forms of private information regarding employee relationships or work patterns.

title: Uni3D: Exploring Unified 3D Representation at Scale
name: uni3d-unified-3d-representation-at-scale
short_hypothesis: A unified 3D representation can be learned at scale by using a standard Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture, initialized with pretrained 2D models, and trained via multi-modal contrastive learning to align 3D point cloud features with pre-existing image-text aligned feature spaces. This unified architecture is expected to overcome scaling difficulties in 3D and allow for effective transfer learning on various downstream tasks.
related_work: Prior work in 3D representation learning has shown progress but is typically limited to small-scale models, data, and tasks. These methods often rely on specialized 3D backbones which are difficult to scale and do not effectively leverage knowledge from other modalities. While scaling has revolutionized 2D vision and NLP, and recent works like OpenShape and ULIP2 have begun exploring scalable 3D pretraining, they are still limited by their backbone architectures or cannot easily scale to billion-parameter sizes. The research gap lies in developing a truly scalable 3D foundation model with a unified architecture that can leverage the well-established scaling strategies and powerful pretrained models from the 2D domain.
abstract: Scaling up representations for images or text has been extensively investigated, leading to revolutions in vision and language. However, scalable representation for 3D objects and scenes is relatively unexplored. This work presents Uni3D, a 3D foundation model to explore the unified 3D representation at scale. Uni3D uses a 2D initialized Vision Transformer (ViT) which is end-to-end pretrained to align 3D point cloud features with image-text aligned features. Through a simple architecture and pretext task, Uni3D is designed to leverage abundant 2D pretrained models for initialization and image-text aligned models as the target, unlocking the potential of 2D models and scaling-up strategies for the 3D world.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the zero-shot shape classification performance of the learned 3D representation.
experiments.Method: The Uni3D model will be evaluated on the ModelNet40, ScanObjectNN, and Objaverse-LVIS benchmarks in a zero-shot setting. The evaluation will be compared against previous state-of-the-art methods including PointCLIP, PointCLIP V2, ULIP, and OpenShape.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality of the learned representation for few-shot learning by using linear probing.
experiments.Method: The parameters of the pretrained Uni3D model will be frozen, and a linear classifier will be trained on the Objaverse-LVIS dataset using a small number of labeled training samples per class (1, 2, 4, 8, and 16). The performance will be compared to baselines such as OpenShape (PointBERT and SparseConv backbones), ULIP, and PointCLIP V2.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's capability for open-world understanding on real-world 3D scenes.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the zero-shot recognition task on the ScanNet dataset. The goal is to recognize the category of each object instance in a scene without prior training on ScanNet data. The performance will be compared against PointCLIP, PointCLIP V2, CLIP2Point, and CLIP².

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform 3D dense prediction tasks, specifically few-shot and open-vocabulary part segmentation.
experiments.Method: Part segmentation experiments will be conducted on the ShapeNetPart dataset. For few-shot segmentation, the model will be supervised with 1 or 2 samples per class and compared against PointBERT and other methods. For open-vocabulary segmentation, the dataset will be partitioned into 'Seen' and 'Unseen' categories to test the model's ability to segment parts not seen during training.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to justify the effectiveness of the key design choices in Uni3D.
experiments.Method: A series of ablations will be performed: 1) Scaling the model size from 6M to 1B parameters. 2) Switching the image-text aligned target model (CLIP teacher) to various off-the-shelf models at different scales (e.g., OpenAI-CLIP, OpenCLIP, EVA-CLIP). 3) Evaluating the effect of initializing the Transformer backbone from scratch versus using pretrained 2D models like DINO and EVA.

experiments.Description: To explore the model's generative and retrieval capabilities enabled by the learned representation.
experiments.Method: The trained Uni3D model will be used for application-level tasks. For 3D painting, the RGB channels of a point cloud will be optimized to maximize similarity with a text prompt. For cross-modal retrieval, the model will be used to retrieve 3D shapes from a large dataset (Objaverse) given a text prompt or an image query.

title: Composing Ensembles of Pre-trained Models via Iterative Consensus
name: iterative-consensus-composition
short_hypothesis: A unified framework, called PIC, can compose ensembles of different pre-trained models to solve various multimodal problems in a zero-shot manner without any model finetuning. This is achieved by designating one model as a 'generator' to construct proposals and an ensemble of models as 'scorers' to provide iterative feedback in a closed-loop, allowing the system to reach a consensus and correct errors.
related_work: Prior work on composing pre-trained models has focused on two main approaches: (1) jointly finetuning models on large datasets, which is computationally expensive and can lead to instability, or (2) using common interfaces like language to chain models sequentially (e.g., Socratic Models). The latter approach allows only one-way communication, preventing later models from providing feedback to correct errors made by earlier models, and is insufficient for tasks requiring continuous representations, like robot control.
abstract: Large pre-trained models exhibit distinct and complementary capabilities. Language models are capable of textual reasoning but cannot understand visual information, while vision models can generate photos but fail to understand complex language. In this work, we propose a unified framework for composing ensembles of different pre-trained models to combine their strengths for solving various multimodal problems in a zero-shot manner. We use pre-trained models as "generators" or "scorers" and compose them via closed-loop iterative consensus optimization. The generator constructs proposals, and the scorers iteratively provide feedback to refine the generated result. This closed-loop communication is intended to enable models to correct errors caused by other models without requiring any model finetuning. The proposed method is designed as a general purpose framework for a wide range of zero-shot multimodal tasks, such as image generation, video question answering, mathematical reasoning, and robotic manipulation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the composition of a pre-trained image generator and scorer models for zero-shot conditional image generation.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the ImageNet dataset, generating 64x64 images from class labels. The generator will be the GLIDE model. It will be composed with three different types of scorers, individually and as an ensemble: E1 is CLIP, E2 is an image classifier (CLS), and E3 is classifier-free guidance (CLS-FREE). The performance of composing with an ensemble of scorers (G+E1+E2+E3) will be compared against compositions with single scorers (G+E1, G+E2, G+E3).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework for solving free-form video question answering (VQA) tasks in a zero-shot setting.
experiments.Method: The task will be evaluated on the ActivityNet-QA dataset. The generator will be GPT-2, tasked with generating captions for video frames, which are then prompted to GPT-3 to answer questions. The scorers will be an ensemble of different CLIP models (CLIP-32, CLIP-14, CLIP-multilingual). The proposed method (PIC) will be compared against the zero-shot JustAsk (Pretrain) baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy rate (evaluated by human workers)', 'Vocabulary size of generated answers']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the framework's ability to solve grade school math problems by treating them as a text generation task.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the GSM8K dataset. The generator will be a pre-trained GPT-2 model. The scorer will be a pre-trained question-solution classifier (CLS). The proposed method (PIC) will be compared to two baselines: GPT-Pretrain (the generator alone) and GPT-FT (the generator finetuned on GSM8K).

experiments.Description: To evaluate how pre-trained models can be composed to manipulate objects in a simulated environment to satisfy relations specified by text or an image.
experiments.Method: The task will be performed in the Ravens simulation environment. The generator will be a combination of Model Predictive Control (MPC) and a World Model. The scorer will be the pre-trained ViLD model, used to evaluate object relations from multiple camera views. The experiment will compare using scorers from multiple camera views versus a single camera view.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success rate for tasks with two and three specified object relations']

risk_factors: As our method does not need any training or finetuning, one drawback is that its performance depends on the pre-trained models.

title: Finding Private Bugs: Debugging Implementations of Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent
name: dp-sgd-implementation-debugging
short_hypothesis: Common implementation mistakes in Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD), such as incorrect gradient clipping and improper noise calibration, can be detected by systematically varying DP-related hyperparameters and observing the resulting changes in model loss values or the parameter-space distance between trained models. These mistakes are expected to manifest as differences in the dependency of gradient updates on these hyperparameters.
related_work: Prior research on verifying DP-SGD implementations focuses on auditing from an external perspective, where an attacker empirically verifies the privacy budget (ε) claimed by a developer. These auditing methods are often computationally expensive, require expertise in both ML and DP to design, and can only determine if an implementation is correct or not, without identifying the specific source of the error. This paper proposes a complementary, lightweight framework for developers themselves to identify the root cause of common implementation bugs.
abstract: It is important to learn with privacy-preserving algorithms when training data contains sensitive information. Differential privacy (DP) proposes to bound the worst-case privacy leakage of a training algorithm. However, the analytic nature of these algorithmic guarantees makes it difficult to verify that an implementation of a differentially private learner is correct. Existing research focuses on empirically approximating the analytic bound, which is costly and does not identify the source of error. This paper proposes a simple and lightweight methodology for practitioners to identify common implementation mistakes in DP-SGD without imposing any changes to their scripts. The proposed approach stems from measuring distances between models or changes in loss values. The goal is to identify specific mistakes like improper gradient computations or noise miscalibration, which invalidate privacy guarantees.

experiments.Description: To detect the absence of gradient clipping in a DP-SGD implementation. The test is designed to check if the change in loss values is invariant to the gradient norm bound `C`, which would indicate clipping is not being performed.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained for a single step with the noise multiplier `σ` set to 0. This process will be repeated for different values of the gradient norm bound `C`. The experiment will be run on image classification tasks (CIFAR-10 with ResNet-20, ImageNet with WideResNet50-2) and a text classification task (SST2 with BERT). The mini-batch size will be set to 1 to make per-example and mini-batch clipping equivalent.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The change in loss value will be plotted against the gradient norm bound `C`. The expected behavior is that the loss change will be constant if there is no clipping, and will vary with `C` if clipping is correctly applied.

experiments.Description: To differentiate between correct per-example gradient clipping and incorrect mini-batch gradient clipping. The test leverages the hypothesis that the magnitude of the private gradient is dependent on the batch size `B` for per-example clipping, but independent of `B` for mini-batch clipping.
experiments.Method: A synthesized mini-batch will be created, containing one data point with a large gradient and `B-1` data points with zero gradients. The model will be trained for one step with `σ=0`. This will be repeated for different mini-batch sizes `B`. The experiment will be run on image classification tasks (CIFAR-10 with ResNet-20, ImageNet with WideResNet50-2) and a text classification task (SST2 with BERT).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The change in loss value will be plotted against the mini-batch size `B`. A regression t-test will be applied to the resulting curve to test if the slope is zero, which is expected for mini-batch clipping but not for per-example clipping.

experiments.Description: To detect incorrect (uncalibrated) noise addition in a DP-SGD implementation. The test is based on the theoretical finding that the expected parameter-space distance between two models trained from the same initialization is proportional to the noise scale `s`. For calibrated noise `s=Cσ`, while for uncalibrated noise `s=σ`.
experiments.Method: For a range of values for the gradient norm bound `C`, multiple models (e.g., 5) will be trained for a few iterations (e.g., 10) from the same initial checkpoint. The value of `C` will be set large enough to ensure gradients are not clipped. This will be compared across three scenarios: correctly calibrated noise, uncalibrated noise, and no noise. The experiment will be run on image classification tasks (CIFAR-10 with ResNet-20, ImageNet with WideResNet50-2) and a text classification task (SST2 with BERT).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The mean pairwise l2-distance between the resulting models will be computed and plotted against `C`. A regression t-test will be used to check if the slope of this plot is zero. A non-zero slope is expected to indicate correctly calibrated noise.

risk_factors: We need to pay special attention when the Adam optimizer is used for training because it normalizes private gradients before they are applied to update the model. For the first training step of Adam, the private gradient would always be normalized by itself... making it hard to observe any differences between clipping cases.
Another issue with Adam is that as the additive noise eventually begins to dominate the real gradient signal, the private gradient and its running momentum will be indistinguishable from noise.
Note that we do not assume we have access to the raw/private gradients. Thus, we are not able to directly use a statistical test to check for the distribution of the additive noise.
We hope future work will extend our approach to debug DP guarantees of other algorithms such as Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles (PATE).

title: Improving Generative Flow Networks with Path Regularization
name: gflownet-path-regularization-ot
short_hypothesis: A path regularization method based on Optimal Transport (OT) theory can be used to train GFlowNets. Minimizing the OT distance between the forward policies of neighboring states will improve generalization by encouraging similar policies for similar states. Conversely, maximizing this OT distance will improve exploration and generate more diverse candidates by encouraging dissimilar policies.
related_work: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are models for generating compositional objects, but their central problems are improving exploration and generalization. Optimal Transport (OT) theory provides a way to measure the distance between probability distributions, which can be used to define a prior on the structure of the GFlowNet. This paper proposes to use OT to regularize the forward policies of neighboring states to address the exploration and generalization challenges in GFlowNets.
abstract: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are recently proposed models for learning stochastic policies that generate compositional objects by sequences of actions with the probability proportional to a given reward function. The central problem of GFlowNets is to improve their exploration and generalization. In this work, we propose a novel path regularization method based on optimal transport theory that places prior constraints on the underlying structure of the GFlowNet. The prior is designed to help the GFlowNet better discover the latent structure of the target distribution or enhance its ability to explore the environment in the context of active learning. The path regularization controls the flow in the GFlowNet to generate more diverse and novel candidates via maximizing the optimal transport distances between two forward policies or to improve the generalization via minimizing the optimal transport distances. In addition, we derive an efficient implementation of the regularization by finding its closed-form solutions in specific cases and a meaningful upper bound that can be used as an approximation to minimize the regularization term.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization ability of the GFlowNet to guess and sample unvisited modes of a distribution in a hyper-grid environment. The experiment will test if minimizing the proposed path regularization improves mode discovery and distribution learning.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses 4-D and 8-D hyper-grid environments where rewards are concentrated in the corners. The proposed methods (Min OT, Max OT, and an upper-bound approximation UB-OT) will be added as a regularization term to a GFlowNet baseline trained with the trajectory balance objective.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['KL divergence between the true and empirical distribution', 'Number of modes found during training']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the proposed method to enhance exploration and generate diverse and novel candidates in a multi-round active learning setting for biological sequence design. The experiment will test if maximizing the path regularization improves performance on these tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment is conducted on three tasks: Anti-Microbial Peptide (AMP) design, TF Bind 8 (DNA sequence design), and GFP (protein sequence design). The GFlowNet-AL model will serve as a baseline and will be compared against versions regularized with Min OT, Max OT, and UB OT. Other baselines include DynaPPO, COMS, BO-qEI, CbAS, MINS, CMA-ES, and AmortizedBO.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance score (mean score of top-K candidates)', 'Diversity score (based on Levenshtein distance)', 'Novelty score']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to learn several complex, high-dimensional discrete distributions derived from continuous ones. This experiment tests the generalization improvements from minimizing the path regularization.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Energy-based Generative Flow Networks (EB-GFN) framework to model seven distributions over 32-dimensional binary data: 2spirals, 8gaussians, circles, moons, pinwheel, swissroll, and checkerboard. The baseline EB-GFN will be compared with versions using Min OT, Max OT, and UB OT regularization. Other baselines include PCD and ALOE.

risk_factors: A limitation of the current method is computing the optimal transport distances for all couples of nearest neighbor states.

title: AffineQuant: Affine Transformation Quantization for Large Language Models
name: AffineQuant
short_hypothesis: Using a learnable affine transformation for post-training quantization (PTQ) of large language models (LLMs) will expand the optimization space compared to existing methods that only use scaling or translation. This will significantly minimize quantization errors. To ensure the invertibility of the affine matrix during optimization, a gradual mask method based on the Levy-Desplanques theorem will be employed, which starts by optimizing diagonal elements and progressively includes off-diagonal elements.
related_work: Existing Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) methods for LLMs, such as AWQ and OmniQuant, limit the optimization to simple scaling or translation transformations. This constraint results in significant quantization errors, especially in low-bit configurations. While other methods like RPTQ use reordering, they are still specific cases of transformations. The proposed work identifies a research gap in using a more general and powerful transformation, specifically an affine transformation, to expand the optimization space and better align weight distributions with the quantization function, thereby reducing quantization error.
abstract: The significant resource requirements associated with Large-scale Language Models (LLMs) have generated considerable interest in the development of techniques aimed at compressing and accelerating neural networks. Among these techniques, Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) has emerged as a subject of considerable interest due to its noteworthy compression efficiency and cost-effectiveness in the context of training. Existing PTQ methods for LLMs limit the optimization scope to scaling transformations between pre- and post-quantization weights. This constraint results in significant errors after quantization, particularly in low-bit configurations. In this paper, we advocate for the direct optimization using equivalent Affine transformations in PTQ (AffineQuant). This approach extends the optimization scope and thus is intended to significantly minimize quantization errors. Additionally, by employing the corresponding inverse matrix, we can ensure equivalence between the pre- and post-quantization outputs of PTQ, thereby maintaining its efficiency and generalization capabilities. To ensure the invertibility of the transformation during optimization, we further introduce a gradual mask optimization method. This method initially focuses on optimizing the diagonal elements and gradually extends to the other elements. Such an approach aligns with the Levy-Desplanques theorem, theoretically ensuring invertibility of the transformation.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the performance of weight-only quantization on OPT models across various model sizes and bit configurations.
experiments.Method:
Models: OPT (125M, 1.3B, 2.7B, 6.7B, 13B, 30B).
Datasets: WikiText2, PTB, C4.
Task: Language Modeling.
Baselines: RTN, GPTQ, AWQ, OmniQuant.
Configurations: w2a16, w3a16, w4a16 with and without grouping (g64, g128).

experiments.Description: Evaluate the performance of weight-activation quantization on LLaMA models.
experiments.Method:
Models: LLaMA1 & LLaMA2 (7B, 13B, 30B).
Datasets: WikiText2, C4.
Task: Language Modeling.
Configuration: W4A4.
Baselines: SmoothQuant, OmniQuant.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the zero-shot task performance of quantized LLaMA models.
experiments.Method:
Models: LLaMA (7B, 13B, 30B).
Configuration: 4/4-bit quantization (w4a4).
Datasets: PIQA, ARC-e, WinoGrande, BoolQ, ARC-c, HellaSwag.
Baselines: OmniQuant, FP16.

experiments.Description: Ablation study on the effect of the stability factor alpha on model performance.
experiments.Method:
Models: OPT-125M, LLaMA-7B.
Procedure: Vary the stability factor alpha across a range of values (e.g., 1e0 to 1e-8) and observe the impact on model performance.
Datasets: WikiText2, PTB, C4.

experiments.Description: Ablation study on the contribution of the gradual mask optimization strategy.
experiments.Method:
Models: OPT-125M, LLaMA-7B.
Procedure: Compare model performance with and without the gradual mask approach.
Datasets: WikiText2, C4.

risk_factors: we exclude the affine transformation between the two linear layers in the MLP module. This is because the optimization of the large transformation matrix in inflated dimensions is challenging.
Additionally, the presence of the activation function renders the equivalent transformation of the matrix invalid.
In the future, optimizing the affine transformation matrix more effectively deserves careful consideration.

title: Planning in Stochastic Environments with a Learned Model
name: stochastic-muzero
short_hypothesis: By extending value-equivalent model-learning to handle stochasticity, it is possible to create an agent that can effectively learn and plan in inherently stochastic environments. This can be achieved by factoring the model to first transition deterministically to a latent 'afterstate' and then branch stochastically to the next state. This factored model can be trained end-to-end to maintain value equivalence and used with a stochastic planning method.
related_work: The most successful model-based reinforcement learning methods, such as MuZero, combine value-equivalent model learning with Monte Carlo tree search. These methods learn a model that reconstructs only the quantities required for planning (reward, value, policy). However, existing implementations have been limited to deterministic models, which restricts their applicability in environments that are inherently stochastic, partially observed, or so complex they appear stochastic. While some stochastic latent models exist, they have either not been successfully combined with planning or have limitations such as requiring supervised data or multi-stage training.
abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning has proven highly successful. However, learning a model in isolation from its use during planning is problematic in complex environments. To date, the most effective techniques have instead combined value-equivalent model learning with powerful tree-search methods. This approach is exemplified by MuZero. However, previous instantiations of this approach were limited to the use of deterministic models. This limits their performance in environments that are inherently stochastic, partially observed, or so large and complex that they appear stochastic to a finite agent. In this paper we extend this approach to learn and plan with stochastic models. Specifically, we introduce a new algorithm, Stochastic MuZero, that learns a stochastic model incorporating afterstates, and uses this model to perform a stochastic tree search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed approach in the classic stochastic single-player puzzle game of 2048, where handling stochasticity is important.
experiments.Method: The Stochastic MuZero agent, which uses a learned stochastic model, will be trained on the game 2048. Its performance will be compared against three baselines: MuZero (using a learned deterministic model), AlphaZero (using a perfect stochastic simulator), and a prior state-of-the-art planning-based agent (Jaśkowski, 2016) that uses a perfect simulator and domain knowledge.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Episode score during training', 'Final agent performance at different levels of search (simulations per move)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed approach in the classic stochastic two-player zero-sum game of backgammon.
experiments.Method: Stochastic MuZero will be trained on backgammon without prior knowledge of the rules. Its performance will be compared against MuZero (deterministic learned model), AlphaZero (perfect stochastic simulator), and the superhuman-level open-source program GNUbg Grandmaster.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Playing strength (score) relative to GNUbg Grandmaster during training', 'Scaling of performance with increasing planning budgets (simulations per move)']

experiments.Description: To measure any performance loss caused by using a stochastic model and search in a deterministic environment where it is not strictly necessary.
experiments.Method: The performance of Stochastic MuZero will be compared against the standard MuZero in the deterministic board game of Go. To ensure a fair comparison, the methods will be made computationally equivalent by adjusting network architecture (e.g., halving network depth for parts of Stochastic MuZero's network) and search budgets.

risk_factors: Although, Stochastic MuZero requires twice the number of network expansions in comparison to MuZero to achieve the same performance, due to the use of a stochastic MCTS instead of a deterministic one, we ensure that the methods are computationally equivalent by halving the network depth for the chance and dynamic parts of the Stochastic MuZero’s network.
We did not release the full code as it relies on a lot of proprietary internal infrastructure, limiting its usefulness.

title: Towards Better Understanding and Better Generalization of Low-shot Classification in Histology Images with Contrastive Learning
name: cl-la-fsl-histology
short_hypothesis: Incorporating unsupervised contrastive learning (CL) for pre-training with a novel latent augmentation (LA) technique will enable more label-efficient learning and better generalization for few-shot classification in histology images. CL is intended to learn useful representations without manual labels, while LA is proposed to transfer semantic variations from the base dataset to the novel classes in an unsupervised manner by constructing and querying a latent 'base dictionary'.
related_work: Few-shot learning (FSL) is well-established for natural images but remains largely unexplored for histology images, which are data-scarce and exhibit large domain shifts. Current FSL approaches often rely on fully-supervised pre-training. While self-supervised learning, particularly contrastive learning (CL), has shown promise, its application has focused on iconic images rather than complex, multi-object, multi-texture histology images. Furthermore, existing methods for representation variation augmentation typically depend on label information, whereas this work proposes a method to transfer variations in a fully unsupervised way.
abstract: Few-shot learning is an established topic in natural images for years, but few work is attended to histology images, which is of high clinical value since well-labeled datasets and rare abnormal samples are expensive to collect. This work facilitates the study of few-shot learning in histology images by setting up three cross-domain tasks that simulate real clinical problems. To enable label-efficient learning and better generalizability, the paper proposes to incorporate contrastive learning (CL) with latent augmentation (LA) to build a few-shot system. CL is intended to learn useful representations without manual labels, while LA is designed to transfer semantic variations of the base dataset in an unsupervised way. These two components are proposed to fully exploit unlabeled training data and scale gracefully to other label-hungry problems.
