experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on a near-domain Generalized Few-Shot Learning (GFSL) task, where novel and base classes originate from the same data source but may have protocol differences.
experiments.Method: A leave-one-class-out setup is used on the NCT-CRC-HE-100K (NCT) dataset to create 9 sub-tasks. Each sub-task uses 8 classes for pre-training and holds out one class as novel. The feature extractor is a ResNet-18, pre-trained using either Fully-Supervised Pre-training (FSP) or Contrastive-Learning Pre-training (CLP with MoCo-v3). The proposed Latent Augmentation (LA) is applied to the features. Baselines include NearestCentroid, LogisticRegression, and RidgeClassifier.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average F1-score for base and novel classes', 'Harmonic mean of base and novel F1-scores']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on a mixture-domain Few-Shot Learning (FSL) task, involving both middle-domain (same organ, different protocol) and out-domain (different organ) novel classes.
experiments.Method: The model is pre-trained on the NCT dataset (base classes) and evaluated on the LC25000 dataset (novel classes). Novel classes from LC25000 are categorized as middle-domain (colon-related) or out-domain (lung-related). The model setup (ResNet-18, FSP, CLP, LA) and baselines (NearestCentroid, LogisticRegression, RidgeClassifier) are consistent with the near-domain task.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average F1-score for middle-domain and out-domain classes', 'Harmonic mean of middle and out-domain F1-scores']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on an out-domain FSL task and to study the impact of homogeneous versus heterogeneous shot selection, where few-shot samples are drawn from single or multiple Whole Slide Images (WSIs).
experiments.Method: The model is pre-trained on the NCT dataset (base classes) and evaluated on the PAIP19 dataset (novel classes). The PAIP dataset is split by WSI ID to create a support WSI set and a query WSI set, allowing for both heterogeneous (samples from different WSIs) and homogeneous (samples from a single WSI) shot selection strategies during evaluation. The model setup and baselines remain the same.

risk_factors: The three classes (non-tumor, viable-tumor and other) defined in PAIP are extremely coarse-grained: it may include couples of real fine-grained classes.

A topic we leave for future work is to explore whether CLP always generalizes better than FSP when pre-training on a base dataset with limited number of annotated classes and if the generalization gap would increase as the label diversity decreases.
We point out that the visualization results and the large generalization gap shown in our work still remains as empirical observations.
The observation does not repudiate the effectiveness of latent augmentation but re-ensures its tenability.

title: Learning Super-Features for Image Retrieval
name: super-features-for-image-retrieval
short_hypothesis: A novel architecture for deep image retrieval based on mid-level 'Super-features' can overcome the limitations of standard local features. By constructing an ordered set of features with an iterative attention module and training them directly with a dedicated contrastive loss and a diversity loss, we can create less redundant, more discriminative representations. This approach will eliminate the train-test discrepancy found in methods that train on global aggregations but test on local features.
related_work: Recent high-performing image retrieval methods combine local and global features. However, these methods face two key issues. First, local features, being localized map activations of a neural network, are often highly redundant. Second, a discrepancy exists between training and testing: models are typically trained with a global loss applied to an aggregation of local features, but at test time, matching is performed between individual local features. This research aims to address these shortcomings by proposing a new feature representation and training framework that operates directly on mid-level features, avoiding the train-test discrepancy.
abstract: Methods that combine local and global features have recently shown excellent performance on multiple challenging deep image retrieval benchmarks, but their use of local features raises at least two issues. First, these local features simply boil down to the localized map activations of a neural network, and hence can be extremely redundant. Second, they are typically trained with a global loss that only acts on top of an aggregation of local features; by contrast, testing is based on local feature matching, which creates a discrepancy between training and testing. In this paper, we propose a novel architecture for deep image retrieval, based solely on mid-level features that we call Super-features. These Super-features are constructed by an iterative attention module and constitute an ordered set in which each element focuses on a localized and discriminant image pattern. For training, they require only image labels. A contrastive loss operates directly at the level of Super-features and focuses on those that match across images. A second complementary loss encourages diversity.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed FIRe approach on standard landmark retrieval tasks and compare its performance against state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: The FIRe model, with a ResNet-50 backbone and the Local feature Integration Transformer (LIT) module, will be trained on the SfM-120k dataset. Evaluation will be performed on the ROxford and RParis datasets, both with and without the 1 million distractor set (R1M). The performance will be compared to several baselines, including global descriptor methods (RMAC, AP-GeM), global descriptor with reranking methods (DELG), and local feature matching methods (DELF, HOW).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean Average Precision (mAP) on Medium (med) and Hard (hard) setups.']

experiments.Description: To perform ablation studies to analyze the contribution of the different components of the FIRe framework, including the matching constraints and training losses.
experiments.Method: A series of experiments will be conducted by systematically removing or altering components of the proposed method. This includes: 1) removing constraints on reciprocity, Lowe's ratio test, and the Super-feature ID during matching; 2) removing the attention decorrelation loss (L_attn) or the Super-feature loss (L_super) and replacing it with a global loss (L_global); 3) varying the number of iterations (T) and templates (N) in the LIT module.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance versus memory footprint of the FIRe method compared to the HOW baseline.
experiments.Method: The number of features extracted per image before aggregation will be varied for both FIRe and HOW. The performance will be plotted against the average number of clusters per image used in ASMK, which is proportional to memory usage.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of FIRe as a retrieval method for the downstream task of visual localization.
experiments.Method: Within the Kapture visual localization pipeline, the default retrieval method will be replaced with the proposed FIRe method. The evaluation will be conducted on the Aachen Day-Night v1.1 dataset. The performance will be compared against using AP-GeM and HOW as the retrieval methods in the same pipeline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Percentage of successfully localized images at three different thresholds for day and night images.']

risk_factors: Note that the pair selection step is non-differentiable and no gradients are computed for Super-features not in P.

title: SafeDPO: A Simple Approach to Direct Preference Optimization with Enhanced Safety
name: SafeDPO
short_hypothesis: By revisiting the original hard-constrained safety alignment objective, it is possible to derive a provably equivalent and tractable DPO-style objective that enables direct, single-stage optimization for safety. This proposed method, SafeDPO, incorporates safety constraints by transforming preference data using binary safety indicators, eliminating the need for complex auxiliary reward/cost models or multi-stage pipelines while preserving the optimal solution of the underlying safety-constrained problem.
related_work: Large Language Models (LLMs) are typically aligned using preference alignment methods like Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) or Direct Alignment Algorithms (DAAs) such as DPO. While effective for helpfulness, these methods do not explicitly enforce safety constraints. Existing safety alignment methods (e.g., SafeRLHF, SACPO, CAN) often introduce complexity by relying on auxiliary reward or cost models, additional training stages, or relaxed constrained objectives. These approaches can be computationally expensive and may not strictly enforce the hard safety constraints required for safety-critical applications. This work identifies a need for a lightweight, principled method that directly optimizes for safety without these complexities.
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, balancing helpfulness and safety has become a central challenge. A natural approach is to incorporate safety constraints into Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), where recent studies have shown promising progress. However, these methods often rely on auxiliary networks or multi-stage pipelines, thereby increasing complexity. In this work, we revisit the original safety alignment objective and show that, under mild assumptions, it admits a closed-form optimal policy. We further derive a provably equivalent and tractable objective, enabling direct optimization. Building on this insight, we propose SafeDPO, a lightweight method that preserves the optimal solution of the underlying safety-constrained objective while requiring only one additional hyperparameter and minimal modifications to existing preference-based training methods. SafeDPO eliminates the need for reward models, cost models, and online sampling, relying only on preference data and safety indicators.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether SafeDPO can suppress unsafe generations while maintaining competitive helpfulness compared to existing safety alignment methods. The experiment also plans to analyze the effect of the safety margin hyperparameter (Δ), assess scalability across different model sizes, and conduct human evaluations.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the PKU-SafeRLHF-30K dataset. An Alpaca-7B model will be fine-tuned using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to create a shared reference model. This reference model will then be trained with SafeDPO and various baselines. The planned baselines are: DPO-HELPFUL (standard DPO on helpfulness data), DPO-HARMLESS (DPO on harmlessness data), DPO-SAFEBETTER (DPO on filtered data where the preferred response is safe), SafeRLHF, SACPO, and P-SACPO. The scalability analysis will test SafeDPO on models ranging from 1.5B to 13B parameters.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Metrics will be helpfulness, harmlessness, and harmless ratio. Evaluation will be conducted using two protocols: (1) Model-based evaluation using the beaver-7b-unified-reward and beaver-7b-unified-cost models. (2) GPT-4 evaluation, where GPT-4 acts as an automatic judge. Human evaluation will also be conducted to assess safety and helpfulness.

experiments.Description: To examine the potential for over-refusal (excessive conservativeness) on benign but lexically ambiguous prompts.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate SafeDPO and baseline models on the XSTest benchmark, which is designed to probe for exaggerated safety behaviors. The baselines to be compared are DPO-HELPFUL, DPO-HARMLESS, DPO-SAFEBETTER, SafeRLHF, SACPO, and P-SACPO.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Following the official XSTest protocol, the evaluation will measure the 'over-refusal rate' on benign prompts and the 'harmless ratio' on unsafe prompts, using GPT-5.1 as the evaluation model.

risk_factors: Evaluation is based primarily on the PKU-SafeRLHF dataset, which, although widely used as a benchmark, may not fully capture the diversity of real-world safety-critical scenarios.
Moreover, experiments are limited to models up to 13B parameters due to memory constraints.

While this range already covers the scale of most open-source alignment studies, extending to broader datasets and larger-scale models would provide stronger evidence, and we view these as natural next steps.

title: How Do Large Language Models Understand Graph Patterns? A Benchmark for Graph Pattern Comprehension
name: llm-graph-pattern-benchmark
short_hypothesis: Large Language Models (LLMs), despite being trained on text, may possess a preliminary capability to understand and reason about graph patterns. This can be systematically evaluated by proposing a comprehensive benchmark that assesses their abilities on a variety of graph pattern tasks, including understanding patterns from terminological or topological descriptions, and autonomously discovering patterns from data.
related_work: Prior research has shown that LLMs have a preliminary ability to understand graph structures and node features, often focusing on algorithmic tasks like shortest path and connectivity, or learning tasks like node classification. However, the potential of LLMs in graph pattern mining—a critical component in fields like chemistry, biology, and social network analysis—remains largely unexplored. While traditional algorithms and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been developed for pattern discovery, few studies have investigated the capabilities of LLMs for this purpose, creating a significant research gap.
abstract: Benchmarking the capabilities and limitations of large language models (LLMs) in graph-related tasks is becoming an increasingly popular and crucial area of research. Recent studies have shown that LLMs exhibit a preliminary ability to understand graph structures and node features. However, the potential of LLMs in graph pattern mining remains largely unexplored. This is a key component in fields such as computational chemistry, biology, and social network analysis. To bridge this gap, this work introduces a comprehensive benchmark to assess LLMs' capabilities in graph pattern tasks. We have developed a benchmark that evaluates whether LLMs can understand graph patterns based on either terminological or topological descriptions. Additionally, our benchmark tests the LLMs' capacity to autonomously discover graph patterns from data. The benchmark encompasses both synthetic and real datasets, and a variety of models, with a total of 11 tasks and 7 models.

experiments.Description: To evaluate LLMs' comprehension of graph patterns based on their common names (terminological descriptions) through tasks of translation, modification, and detection.
experiments.Method: A series of tasks are designed using 9 primitive graph patterns (e.g., triangle, square, V-structure). Tasks include: (1) Pattern Translation: generating a graph from a pattern's name; (2) Graph Modification: transforming one pattern into another (e.g., Square to House); (3) Pattern Detection: identifying all instances of a pattern in a given graph. These tasks will be performed on synthetic graphs of small, medium, and large sizes, represented as both adjacency lists (A.L.) and edge lists (E.L.). The models to be evaluated are GPT-4, GPT-4o, Mixtral, Llama, Gemini, Claude, and O1-mini.

experiments.Description: To evaluate LLMs' comprehension of graph patterns based on their structural connectivity (topological descriptions), testing for permutation invariance and consistency.
experiments.Method: The experiment mirrors the terminology-based tasks but uses topological descriptions (adjacency/edge lists) instead of names. Tasks include: (1) Graph Isomorphic Mapping: identifying the node correspondence between two isomorphic graphs; (2) Graph Modification: transforming patterns based on topological descriptions; (3) Pattern Detection: identifying patterns from topological descriptions. The same synthetic datasets and primitive patterns are used, but with shuffled node IDs to test for permutation invariance. The same set of 7 LLMs will be evaluated.

experiments.Description: To assess the capability of LLMs to autonomously discover graph patterns from data without predefined rules.
experiments.Method: Three classic data-driven pattern mining tasks will be performed: (1) Dense Subgraph Mining: prompting LLMs to identify the 3-core of a graph; (2) Frequent Subgraph Extraction: prompting LLMs to find common patterns in a dataset of graphs; (3) Discriminative Pattern Learning: prompting LLMs to find patterns that distinguish between two classes of graphs using the BA-2motif dataset. The same 7 LLMs will be evaluated.

experiments.Description: To evaluate LLM performance on graph pattern tasks in more complex, real-world scenarios involving node attributes and structural noise.
experiments.Method: Two main tasks will be conducted on real-world datasets. (1) Molecule Pattern Detection: LLMs will be asked to detect specific molecular patterns (e.g., Benzene rings) in graphs from the Benzene, Alkane-Carbonyl, and Fluoride-Carbonyl datasets. (2) Discriminative Pattern Learning and Classification: LLMs will be tasked with learning discriminative patterns and then using them for classification on datasets from diverse domains, including MUTAG, OGB-MOL, OGB-BBBP (molecular), IMDB-BINARY, IMDB-MULTI (social network), ENZYMES (bioinformatics), and FINGERPRINT (computer vision).

risk_factors: Due to the current high cost of LLMs, we performed limited sampling during the discrimination process.
The reason is that O1-mini strictly searches for patterns that exactly meet the data requirements, without allowing for fuzzy matching. This results in smaller discriminative patterns being found.

title: Graphon Cross-Validation: Assessing Models on Network Data
name: Graphon CV-Imputation
short_hypothesis: A K-fold cross-validation method with random imputation can effectively select tuning parameters and models for graphons. By treating the edges in the validation set as missing values and randomly imputing them before model training, this approach can preserve the network's structural integrity and minimize the introduction of biases common in other network CV methods.
related_work: Cross-validation (CV) is a standard for model selection, but traditional CV is not applicable to network data due to the violation of the independence assumption among observations (edges). Existing methods like direct edge sampling can alter the network's topology and introduce bias. Other approaches, such as the one by Li et al. (2020a), use matrix completion but are limited by assumptions of low-rank probability matrices and high computational costs. This creates a need for a theoretically sound, computationally efficient, and broadly applicable cross-validation method for network data.
abstract: Graphon models have emerged as powerful tools for modeling complex network structures by capturing connection probabilities among nodes. A key challenge in their application lies in accurately characterizing the graphon function, particularly with respect to parameters that govern its smoothness, which significantly impact the estimation accuracy. In this article, we propose a novel graphon cross-validation method for selecting tuning parameters and estimation approaches. Our method is both theoretically sound and computationally efficient. We propose that our cross-validation score will be asymptotically parallel to the estimation error, and the selected model will asymptotically converge to the optimal model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the empirical performance of the proposed CV-imputation method for selecting tuning parameters for state-of-the-art graphon estimation methods on synthetic data.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use synthetic data generated from four different graphon models (Graphon 1, 2, 3, and 4) that produce networks with varying characteristics (dense/sparse, low/full-rank). The proposed CV-imputation method will be used to select the tuning parameter for four estimation methods: neighborhood smoothing (NS), sort-and-smooth (SAS), universal singular value thresholding (USVT), and iterative connecting probability estimation (ICE). The performance will be compared against the Edge Cross-Validation (ECV) method and a default parameter selection.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the estimated graphon, denoted L(M)', 'End-to-end computational cost (CPU time)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the proposed method to select the best-performing estimation model from a set of candidates on synthetic data.
experiments.Method: Using synthetic data from four graphon models, the experiment will first use CV-imputation to tune the hyperparameters for several candidate estimation methods (NS, SAS, USVT, ICE). Then, it will select the overall best model based on the validation score. The performance of this model selection task will be compared against the ECV method.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy (percentage of cases where the model with the true lowest MSE is selected)', 'Computational time']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of CV-imputation for model selection in a link prediction task on real-world networks of varying sizes.
experiments.Method: The method will be applied to four networks: a moderate-sized drug-disease co-occurrence network, and three large-scale networks (the political blogs network 'PolBlog', the coauthorship network 'NetSci', and the yeast protein-protein interaction network 'Yeast'). For each network, CV-imputation and ECV will be used to tune and select the best graphon estimation method from a set of candidates (NS, ICE, SAS, USVT). For the larger networks, NS and ICE will be excluded as candidates due to computational intensity.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Prediction accuracy (proportion of correctly predicted links among top-q predictions) for the moderate-sized network', 'Area Under the Curve (AUC) for the large networks', 'Computational time']

risk_factors: However, our method can not be extended to models with temporal or sequential dependence since they violate edge independence.

title: Balancing Information Preservation and Computational Efficiency: L2 Normalization and Geodesic Distance in Manifold Learning
name: l2-geodesic-manifold-learning
short_hypothesis: Normalizing high-dimensional data using an L2-norm (projecting onto a hypersphere) and using geodesic distance as the similarity metric will preserve more distinguishable information compared to the conventional L1-normalization and Euclidean distance, especially as dimensionality increases. This approach is expected to be computationally efficient and can be integrated into manifold learning algorithms like t-SNE to produce better visualizations.
related_work: Conventional manifold learning methods often preprocess high-dimensional data with L1-normalization and then use Euclidean distance for similarity measurement. This is prevalent in fields like Bioinformatics. However, this approach suffers from the 'curse of dimensionality,' where distances between observations converge in a shrinking interval, leading to a loss of distinguishable information. This negatively impacts downstream visualization algorithms like t-SNE, which may fail to learn meaningful patterns.
abstract: A distinguishable metric of similarity is fundamental for unsupervised learning and high-dimensional data visualization. However, conventional metrics like Euclidean distance after L1-normalization can fail by losing information in high-dimensional data, as distances between observations converge. This paper discusses the influence of different p-norms and the defect of Euclidean distance. We propose that observation differences are better preserved by normalizing data with a higher p-norm and using geodesic distance. We identify that L2-normalization onto a hypersphere is often sufficient for preserving differences in high-dimensional data while being computationally efficient. We present HS-SNE (HyperSphere-SNE), a hypersphere-representation-system-based augmentation to t-SNE, to address the intricacy of high-dimensional data visualization and similarity measurement.

experiments.Description: To assess the efficacy of the proposed method on simulated data with known ground truth, by evaluating the quality of clusters formed from the low-dimensional embeddings.
experiments.Method: High-dimensional data (2,000 dimensions, 3 distinct types) will be generated using the Dirichlet-multinomial Mixture Model. The data will be visualized in a 2-dimensional space using the proposed HS-SNE and compared against baselines: t-SNE with L1-normalization using Euclidean, Manhattan, and Chebyshev distance metrics. K-Means clustering (k=3) will then be applied to the resulting embeddings.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method's visualization quality and local structure preservation on real-world, high-dimensional biological data.
experiments.Method: Real single-cell RNA sequencing datasets (lung, melanoma, and kidney cells from the TISCH project) will be used. The proposed HS-SNE will be used to generate 2D embeddings and will be compared to standard t-SNE. A k-NN graph will be constructed on the low-dimensional embeddings from both methods.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of cluster clarity in 2D embeddings', 'k-Nearest Neighbor (k-NN) accuracy']

experiments.Description: To explore the generalizability of the L2-normalization and geodesic distance system by applying it to UMAP, another distance-based manifold learning algorithm.
experiments.Method: The lung cells scRNA-seq dataset will be visualized. A variant, Hypersphere UMAP, which integrates L2-normalization and geodesic distances, will be applied and compared to the standard UMAP algorithm.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of the preservation of cluster structure in the resulting embeddings']

risk_factors: However, for p > 2, the geodesic distance does not have a closed-form solution according to existing literature.
However, estimating the geodesic distance for p > 2, which lacks a closed-form solution, incurs a significantly large time complexity, rendering it impractical for high-dimensional data.

The manifold formed by L2-normalization is a hypersphere, which introduces another challenge when applying it to t-SNE. In t-SNE, distances are transformed into conditional probabilities using a Gaussian distribution kernel; however, this distribution is only appropriate for modeling random distances in Euclidean space.

title: Is Feature Extraction the most informative dimensionality reduction technique? Revisiting Unsupervised Feature Selection from a Dynamic Approach
name: unsupervised-dynamic-feature-selection
short_hypothesis: A novel dynamic feature selection method, called Dynamic Data Selection (DDS), can be developed for unsupervised scenarios. By selecting a unique subset of features for each specific sample, this approach will outperform traditional unsupervised feature extraction in tasks like data compression and clustering, in terms of both accuracy and data compression efficiency.
related_work: Feature selection and feature extraction are two primary methods for dimensionality reduction. Traditional feature selection is static, selecting a fixed subset of features for all samples. A more recent paradigm is Dynamic Feature Selection (DFS), also known as instance-wise feature selection, which adapts the feature subset for each sample. However, existing DFS methods like L2X, INVASE, and TabNet were developed for supervised learning. This work aims to create the first DFS framework specifically for unsupervised scenarios.
abstract: This paper compares unsupervised feature extraction and unsupervised feature selection techniques in the context of dimensionality reduction without using labeled data. Unsupervised feature extraction transforms the input space into a lower-dimensional representation, while unsupervised feature selection chooses a subset of features based on predefined criteria. State-of-the-art research suggests that feature extraction outperforms feature selection. This paper proposes to revisit feature selection algorithms from a dynamic perspective, where features are selected depending on the specific sample input. The goal is to demonstrate that dynamic, unsupervised feature selection can be a powerful approach for dimensionality reduction and improved model performance, particularly when labeled data is scarce.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the DDS algorithm to perform data compression by reconstructing input data, comparing it against a classic feature extraction method. The comparison will be made under two conditions: 1) when both methods use the same number of features, and 2) when both methods are allocated the same amount of memory for storage (accounting for feature indices in DDS).
experiments.Method: The task is data reconstruction. The DDS model, using a U-Net architecture, will be paired with a U-Net reconstruction model. This will be compared to a baseline 'naive' U-Net autoencoder (for feature extraction) with a similar number of trainable weights. The experiment will be run on the MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets.

experiments.Description: To test the versatility of the DDS model by attaching it to a state-of-the-art clustering architecture to evaluate its performance on a different unsupervised task without specific hyper-parameter tuning.
experiments.Method: The task is unsupervised image clustering. The DDS module will be attached to the IMC-SwAV clustering model. The performance will be evaluated on the CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100/20, STL-10, and Tiny-ImageNet/200 datasets. The combined model's performance will be compared against several baseline clustering models, including DAC, DCCM, SCAN, CC, TCL, and the original IMC-SwAV.

risk_factors: It is worth noting that the output of the DDS architecture is not forced to be binary. In fact, preliminary studies show that the feature importance score barely reaches the perfect score of 1. This could be a problem in terms of explainability, a the stored compressed data is modified from the original one.

title: Retain and Adapt: Auto-Balanced Model Editing for Open-Vocabulary Object Detection under Domain Shifts
name: auto-balanced-model-editing-for-ovod
short_hypothesis: A model editing approach, termed Automatically Balanced Model Editing (ABME), can efficiently inject new knowledge into Open-Vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) models for adaptation to out-of-distribution tasks while preserving original capabilities. This is achieved by storing compact key-value representations of new task data and leveraging them to automatically balance new and old knowledge, thereby eliminating manual hyperparameter tuning and enabling order-agnostic, scalable adaptation.
related_work: Recent Open Vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) models perform well on standard benchmarks but suffer significant performance degradation under distribution shifts. Continual learning is a potential solution but existing methods struggle to balance retaining pre-trained capabilities with adapting to new tasks, are often sensitive to task order, and can be inflexible. Model editing has emerged as a lightweight approach for updating large pre-trained models, primarily in the context of LLMs. This work proposes to extend model editing to the OVOD task, addressing the challenges of catastrophic forgetting and order sensitivity in a more lightweight and flexible manner than traditional continual learning approaches.
abstract: Recent advances in Open Vocabulary Object Detection (OVOD) have shown strong performance on standard benchmarks, but performance drops sharply under out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts. Continual learning offers a potential remedy by sequentially integrating new tasks, yet existing methods often struggle to balance retaining the pre-trained model capabilities with adapting to new tasks, and usually require retraining under specific task orders. To address these limitations, we observe that model editing naturally lends itself to this setting, as it enables efficient knowledge injection while retaining prior capabilities. Building on this insight, we introduce Automatically Balanced Model Editing (ABME), which injects new task knowledge into the powerful OVOD models while preserving the model’s original abilities. We first store compact key-value representations with storage cost independent of task volume. Then we leverage the stored KV matrices to automatically balance the new and old knowledge for varying learning scenarios, supporting order-agnostic task insertion or removal without additional retraining.

experiments.Description: To assess the trade-off between preserving prior knowledge (on COCO) and adapting to new, diverse few-shot object detection tasks under domain shifts.
experiments.Method: The proposed ABME method will be evaluated on Grounding DINO and GLIP models. Experiments will be conducted on the CDFSOD and ODinW-13 cross-domain few-shot detection benchmarks under K-shot settings (K={1, 5, 10, 30, 50}). The performance will be compared against baselines including EWC, Adam-NSCL, and SD-LORA, as well as a 'Base Model' (pre-trained only) and an 'Oracle' (FFN fine-tuned on each target dataset).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generality of the auto-balanced optimization across different task volumes by comparing it against manual hyperparameter tuning.
experiments.Method: The number of target datasets from CDFSOD will be gradually increased from k=1 to 6. The performance of the proposed auto-balanced objective (Eq. 5) will be compared against a manually-tuned baseline (Eq. 3) with a fixed hyperparameter lambda across various values.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['mAP on new tasks', 'mAP on the old task (COCO)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's sensitivity to the order of tasks compared to conventional continual learning methods.
experiments.Method: The six CDFSOD datasets will be presented in 20 different random shuffled orders. The performance variance of the proposed method will be compared to that of SD-LORA.

experiments.Description: To study the effect of the exponent in the data-adaptive reweighting matrix on performance.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted on the exponent 'alpha' in the regularization term Gamma, testing different values (e.g., 0, 1/8, 1/4, 1/2, 1) on the CDFSOD 5-shot task.

experiments.Description: To investigate whether the proposed model editing method can be combined with existing continual learning approaches to improve their performance.
experiments.Method: The ABME method will be applied on top of EWC and SD-LORA baselines. The combined performance will be evaluated on the CDFSOD 10-shot setting.

experiments.Description: To determine the optimal architectural location for applying the model edits by screening FFN layers across different modules.
experiments.Method: The ABME method will be applied to FFN layers in different parts of the Grounding DINO model architecture: shallow, mid, last, or all backbone layers; all encoder-decoder layers; or a combination. This will be tested on the CDFSOD 5-shot setting.

experiments.Description: To validate the design choice of targeting FFN layers for knowledge injection by comparing it against editing Self-Attention (SA) layers.
experiments.Method: The performance of ABME will be compared when applied to FFN layers versus SA layers. The experiment will be run on the CDFSOD 5-shot task.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the scalability of the proposed method on a large number of sequential tasks under extreme domain shifts.
experiments.Method: The CDFSOD benchmark will be extended to 90 sequential tasks using ImageCorruptions with the most severe intensity level. The performance of ABME will be compared against Adam-NSCL and an Oracle baseline.

experiments.Description: To verify the cross-architecture and cross-task generalization of the proposed method beyond object detection.
experiments.Method: The ABME method will be applied to the CLIP (ViT-B/16) model for a 5-shot image classification task on 11 diverse datasets (Aircraft, Caltech101, CIFAR100, DTD, etc.). Performance will be compared against Zero-shot, ZSCL, and WiSE-FT baselines.

risk_factors: The method could be misused to enable undesired large-scale labeling or surveillance.

title: VideoDiT: Bridging Image Diffusion Transformers for Streamlined Video Generation
name: VideoDiT
short_hypothesis: A streamlined video generation framework, VideoDiT, can be developed by adapting pre-trained image diffusion transformers using a Distribution-Preserving VAE (DP-VAE) to bridge 2D image and 3D video latent spaces, and by extending 2D attention to 3D with minimal parameter increase.
related_work: Previous methods for adapting image diffusion models for video generation often just add temporal layers, which fails to address the significant challenge posed by the tight coupling between image diffusion models and their original 2D VAEs when modeling spatiotemporal data.
abstract: We present VideoDiT, a streamlined video generation framework adapted from pre-trained image generation models. Unlike previous methods that simply add temporal layers to image diffusion models, we enhance both the tokenizer, implemented with the variational autoencoder (VAE), and the diffusion model. We emphasize the importance of combining 3D VAE compression with knowledge from pre-trained image diffusion models to achieve efficient video generation, though the tight coupling between image diffusion models and 2D VAEs poses significant challenges. To address this, we introduce the Distribution-Preserving VAE (DP-VAE), which encodes key frames in a video clip using the original 2D VAE while compressing non-key frames with a 3D VAE for spatiotemporal modeling. A regularization term ensures alignment between the 3D video latent space and the 2D image latent space, facilitating seamless transfer of pre-trained diffusion models. Leveraging the Diffusion Image Transformers (DiT) architecture and incorporating 3D positional embeddings, we extend 2D attention into 3D with negligible increased parameters. Furthermore, leveraging our proposed DP-VAE, VideoDiT supports joint image-video training, preserving the spatial modeling capabilities of the base model while excelling in both image and video generation.
risk_factors: ...the tight coupling between image diffusion models and 2D VAEs poses significant challenges.

title: Offline Meta-Reinforcement Learning with Online Self-Supervision
name: offline-meta-rl-self-supervision
short_hypothesis: A hybrid offline meta-RL algorithm can be designed to first use offline data with rewards to meta-train an adaptive policy, and then collect additional unsupervised online data, without any reward labels, to bridge the distributional shift between the offline data and the data collected by the learned exploration policy. This can be achieved by learning to generate synthetic reward labels for the unsupervised online data.
related_work: Current meta-RL methods are either fully online, which is costly as it requires constant reward supervision, or fully offline. Existing offline meta-RL methods suffer from a specific distributional shift problem: the adaptation procedure is trained on data from a static behavior policy, but at test time it must adapt to data generated by its own learned exploration policy. This mismatch in the latent context space (z-space) can degrade adaptation performance. The proposed method aims to address this specific z-space distribution shift, a problem not explicitly tackled by prior offline meta-RL works like Dorfman & Tamar (2020) and Mitchell et al. (2021). While semi-supervised RL has been studied, it has been in single-task contexts, not for meta-learning across new tasks.
abstract: Meta-reinforcement learning (RL) methods can meta-train policies that adapt to new tasks with less data than standard RL, but meta-training is costly. Offline meta-training allows reusing a static dataset to meta-train policies for various new tasks. However, offline meta-RL presents a challenge: the learned exploration policy for adaptation differs systematically from the offline data's behavior policy, inducing a distributional shift. This can cause the meta-trained policy to behave unpredictably when adapting. We propose a hybrid offline meta-RL algorithm that uses offline data with rewards to meta-train an adaptive policy, and then collects additional unsupervised online data (without reward labels) to bridge this distribution shift. By not requiring reward labels for online collection, this data can be cheaper to obtain.

experiments.Description: To evaluate how well the proposed self-supervised phase mitigates the performance drop from z-space distribution shift and to compare its performance against prior offline meta-RL methods on a range of meta-RL benchmark tasks.
experiments.Method: The proposed method, Semi-Supervised Meta Actor-Critic (SMAC), will be evaluated on several simulated MuJoCo meta-learning tasks (Cheetah Velocity, Ant Direction, Humanoid, Walker Param, Hopper Param) and a more complex Sawyer Manipulation domain. The performance of SMAC will be compared against several baselines: an 'Online Oracle' (PEARL with ground-truth online rewards), an ablation 'SMAC (actor ablation)' using a PEARL-style actor update, a 'meta behavior cloning' baseline, and two prior offline meta-RL methods, 'MACAW' and 'BOReL'.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The primary metric will be the mean post-adaptation return on unseen test tasks, plotted against the amount of self-supervised meta-training steps.

experiments.Description: To visualize and qualitatively assess whether the self-supervised training phase specifically mitigates the distributional shift in the latent context space 'z' caused by the difference between the offline data distribution and the learned exploration policy's data distribution.
experiments.Method: On the Ant Direction task, trajectories from the learned policy will be generated and visualized. This will be done both before and after the self-supervised training phase. The policy will be conditioned on context variables 'z' derived from two different history sources: the offline dataset ('hoffline') and the learned exploration policy ('honline').
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Qualitative comparison of the resulting trajectory plots (XY-coordinates visited by the agent) to observe if the policy's behavior becomes consistent across both conditioning data sources after self-supervised training.

experiments.Description: To investigate if allowing the agent to perform self-supervised training in the test task environments (without rewards) can mitigate state-space distribution shift, which is particularly relevant when generalizing to novel tasks.
experiments.Method: Using the Sawyer Manipulation environment, the experiment will compare the performance of SMAC when self-supervised training is performed on the test tasks versus on the meta-training tasks. This will be compared to an oracle method that trains online on the test environments with ground-truth rewards.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Post-adaptation test performance will be measured and compared between the different training schemes.

risk_factors: A limitation of SMAC is that it assumes that the agent can gather additional unlabeled online samples. However, this assumption may not be satisfied due to safety concerns and engineering needed to ensure that automatic resets are feasible, and therefore enabling safe, autonomous environment interactions is an important direction for future research.

title: Manipulating Multi-agent Navigation Task via Emergent Communications
name: emergent-multi-turn-navigation
short_hypothesis: A collaborative learning framework, trained with reinforcement learning, can enable two agents with asymmetric information (a 'tourist' with local view and a 'guide' with a global view) to develop a multi-turn emergent language to cooperatively solve a complex navigation task, where the guide must first localize the tourist before providing guidance.
related_work: Prior work in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) typically involves a single agent and a human, or uses pre-defined/single-turn communication. Research in emergent communication often focuses on simpler referential games with single-turn, unidirectional messages. This work addresses the gap by proposing a fully cooperative, two-agent VLN-like task that requires mutual, multi-turn dialogue to emerge for solving a task involving both localization and guidance.
abstract: Existing multi-agent communication approaches are limited by simple task settings and single-turn interactions. This work proposes a multi-agent navigation scenario where two agents with unequal abilities—a 'tourist' with only local observations and a 'guide' with a holistic view but no knowledge of the tourist's initial position—must cooperate. They communicate using an emergent language, grounded in the environment, to help the tourist find a target. A new dataset is introduced for this task, along with a collaborative learning framework designed to enable the agents to generate and understand the emergent language. The framework is intended to be trained end-to-end with reinforcement learning to maximize task success.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed collaborative framework on the localization and navigation sub-tasks against relevant baselines.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a newly constructed navigation dataset. The proposed model ('ours') is to be compared against a model adapted from discrete VAE. A version with continuous message passing is also planned to be evaluated to provide a performance ceiling.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the properties of the emergent language generated by the agents, specifically its structure and interpretability.
experiments.Method: The emergent language from the proposed model ('ours') is to be compared to that from the discrete VAE baseline. The analysis involves calculating the correlation between message similarity and environment similarity, and clustering messages based on environmental features.

experiments.Description: To investigate how task performance is affected by communication channel constraints and task difficulty.
experiments.Method: The experiment will vary the `sentence length` of the emergent communication and the `shortest route length` between the start and target locations to observe the effect on task performance.

title: Improved deterministic l2 robustness on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100
name: improved-deterministic-l2-robustness
short_hypothesis: The provable l2 robustness of 1-Lipschitz CNNs can be improved by: 1) relaxing the orthogonality constraint of the last linear layer and using a new certification procedure called Last Layer Normalization (LLN), 2) introducing a more general and expressive Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP) activation function called the Householder activation, and 3) adding a certificate regularization (CR) term to the loss function to explicitly maximize the robustness margin.
related_work: Training 1-Lipschitz CNNs is effective for provable robustness but suffers from vanishing gradients. Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP) architectures were introduced to address this by ensuring each layer has an orthogonal Jacobian. However, this approach has limitations. The orthogonality constraint on the final linear layer is particularly problematic, as updating the weights for one class requires updating all other class weights, leading to information forgetting, especially when the number of classes is large. Furthermore, standard cross-entropy loss does not explicitly maximize the robustness margin, and existing methods' robustness guarantees do not scale well with the l2 perturbation radius.
abstract: Training convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with a strict Lipschitz constraint under the l2 norm is useful for provable adversarial robustness, interpretable gradients and stable training. While 1-Lipschitz CNNs can be designed by enforcing a 1-Lipschitz constraint on each layer, training such networks requires each layer to have an orthogonal Jacobian matrix (for all inputs) to prevent the gradients from vanishing during backpropagation. A layer with this property is said to be Gradient Norm Preserving (GNP). In this work, we introduce a procedure to certify the robustness of 1-Lipschitz CNNs by relaxing the orthogonalization of the last linear layer of the network. We further aim to boost their robustness by introducing (i) a novel Gradient Norm preserving activation function called the Householder activation function (that includes every GroupSort activation) and (ii) a certificate regularization.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Last Layer Normalization (LLN), Householder (HH) activation, and Certificate Regularization (CR) on improving standard and provably robust accuracy for 1-Lipschitz CNNs on a dataset with a large number of classes (CIFAR-100).
experiments.Method: Experiments will be performed on the CIFAR-100 dataset using a range of 1-Lipschitz CNN architectures (LipConvnet-5, 10, 15, ..., 40). The primary baseline will be the SOC (Skew Orthogonal Convolutions) model with MaxMin activation. The proposed methods (LLN, HH, CR) will be incrementally added to this baseline to evaluate their individual and combined contributions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Standard Accuracy', 'Provable Robust Accuracy under l2 perturbations with radii p = 36/255, 72/255, and 108/255']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Householder (HH) activation and Certificate Regularization (CR) on improving standard and provably robust accuracy for 1-Lipschitz CNNs on CIFAR-10, with a focus on performance at larger perturbation radii.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be performed on the CIFAR-10 dataset using a range of 1-Lipschitz CNN architectures (LipConvnet-5, 10, 15, ..., 40). The baseline will be the SOC model with MaxMin activation. The proposed HH and CR methods will be added to this baseline. The paper notes that Last Layer Normalization (LLN) is not expected to provide significant gains due to the small number of classes and will not be included in this experiment group.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Standard Accuracy', 'Provable Robust Accuracy under l2 perturbations with radii p = 36/255, 72/255, and 108/255']

risk_factors: A limitation of using cross entropy loss for training 1-Lipschitz CNNs is that it is not explicitly designed to maximize the margin Mf(x) and thus, the robustness certificate.
A major limitation of using σν (equation (6)) directly is that it has only 2 linear regions and is thus limited in its expressive power.

Can non-trivial order-n (n > 1) householder activation functions with 2n linear regions be constructed for m dimensional input (m > 2) using k iterations of σv where k is independent of n (but may depend on m)? (Open Problem)

title: A New Efficient Method For Combining Gradients Of Different Orders
name: gradient-order-combination
short_hypothesis: A new optimization method, Gradient Order Combination (GOC), can be constructed by combining gradients with products of Hessian matrices of different orders. This method can be viewed as a higher-order extension of Steepest Descent (first-order) and CBB (second-order) methods and is hypothesized to achieve faster convergence rates on convex quadratic optimization problems.
related_work: The paper addresses the unconstrained optimization of convex quadratic functions. Common iterative methods include Steepest Descent (SD), which can be very slow, and the Barzilai-Borwein (BB) method, which uses a non-monotone step length to achieve better convergence. Other related methods include combinations of SD and BB, like the CBB algorithm. The paper frames SD and CBB as first and second-order methods, respectively, based on an analysis of the step size parameter. The research gap is the opportunity to develop even higher-order methods based on this framework to further accelerate convergence.
abstract: We present a new optimization method called GOC(Gradient Order Combination) which is a combination based on the products of Hessian matrices of different orders and the gradient. Taking the parameter r (the reciprocal of steplength) as an analysis target, we can regard the SD method as a first-order and the CBB method as second-order. We have developed third-order and even higher-order methods, which are intended to offer faster convergence rates.

experiments.Description: To compare the convergence performance of the proposed GOC method against baseline methods on a high-dimensional convex quadratic problem with a fixed initial point.
experiments.Method: The task is to minimize a 100,000-dimensional convex quadratic function f(x) = Σ a(i) * x(i)^2, where a(i) is an arithmetic progression from 0.001 to 10000. The initial point x(0) is set to a fixed value of 10000 for all components. The proposed GOC method will be compared against the BB and CBB methods.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of iterations to reach a stopping condition of gradient norm ε = 10^-5', 'Logarithm of the norm value over 5000 iterations']

experiments.Description: To compare the convergence performance of the proposed GOC method against baseline methods on the same high-dimensional convex quadratic problem but with a random initial point.
experiments.Method: The task is to minimize the same 100,000-dimensional convex quadratic function as in the first experiment. The initial point x(0) is set to 10000 * rd, where rd is a random number in (0,1), for each component. The proposed GOC method will be compared against the BB and CBB methods.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Number of iterations to reach a stopping condition of gradient norm ε = 10^-5', 'Logarithm of the norm value over 5000 iterations']

risk_factors: The analysis and proposed method are developed for and evaluated on the unconstrained optimization problem with a convex quadratic form, where the matrix A is symmetric and positive definite.

title: Revitalizing Channel-dimension Fourier Transform for Image Enhancement
name: channel-dimension-fourier-transform
short_hypothesis: Applying the Fourier transform to the channel dimension of feature representations, rather than just the spatial dimension, can enhance the discriminative ability of global representations, which will improve performance on image enhancement tasks. This can be achieved by transforming a global vector (derived from pooling) into the channel-wise Fourier domain, modulating its amplitude and phase, and then transforming it back.
related_work: Previous works have successfully used Fourier transforms on the spatial dimension of images to model global information for enhancement tasks. Other methods rely on designing complex networks to implicitly learn enhancement procedures. However, these approaches may under-explore the discriminative features inherent in the channel dimension. The Gram matrix, which models channel relationships, has been shown to be effective for global style information, suggesting that explicitly modeling the channel distribution with a Fourier transform could be a beneficial and unexplored direction for improving global representations in image enhancement.
abstract: Fourier transform and its variants have made impressive progress in image enhancement, benefiting from their capability for global representation. However, previous works mainly operate in the spatial dimension, potentially under-exploring the discriminative features inherent in the channel dimension. In this study, we introduce the channel-dimension Fourier transform for image enhancement, where the transformation is applied to channel-wise representation to enhance its discrimination ability of global representation. Specifically, we offer three alternative implementations of the channel transform, performing operations in 1) the global vector with its higher order moment, 2) the global vector divided by groups in channel dimension, and 3) the Fourier features derived from spatial-based Fourier transform. The above fundamental designs, serving as generic operators, can be seamlessly integrated with existing enhancement network architectures.

experiments.Description: To conduct a toy experiment to highlight the improved discriminability of global information when using the proposed channel-based Fourier transform.
experiments.Method: An encoder-decoder architecture for image reconstruction will be trained on the MIT-FiveK dataset. During testing, underexposed and overexposed samples from the SICE dataset will be used as inputs. The encoder-decoder features will be processed using different operations: spatial domain Fourier transform, global average pooling, and the proposed channel-based Fourier Transform on the global vector. The discriminability between underexposed and over-exposed samples will be compared.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Channel-dimension Fourier Transform learning (CFTL) mechanism and its variants on the task of low-light image enhancement.
experiments.Method: The proposed CFTL operators will be integrated into two baseline image enhancement networks, DRBN and Restormer. The models will be evaluated on the LOL, Huawei, and MIT-FiveK datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed CFTL mechanism and its variants on the task of exposure correction.
experiments.Method: The proposed CFTL operators will be integrated into two baseline architectures, DRBN and LCDPNet. The models will be evaluated on the MSEC and SICE datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed CFTL mechanism and its variants on the task of SDR2HDR translation.
experiments.Method: The proposed CFTL operators will be integrated into the CSRNet baseline. The model will be evaluated on the HDRTV dataset.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed CFTL mechanism and its variants on the task of underwater image enhancement.
experiments.Method: The proposed CFTL operators will be integrated into the UIEC^2-Net baseline. The model will be evaluated on the UIEB dataset.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to investigate the contribution of each component of the proposed CFTL design.
experiments.Method: Using DRBN as the baseline on exposure correction and low-light enhancement tasks, experiments will be run with different configurations of CFTL, such as removing global pooling, removing the inverse Fourier transform, or processing only the amplitude or phase components.

risk_factors: it still occupies some computation costs, which need to be further improved;
its effects on some popular techniques such as diffusion model, have not explored;
the more comprehensive experiments on broader Low-level vision tasks (e.g., image de-noising and image de-blurring) have not been explored;
we mainly apply the CFTL on the lightweight and classic backbones, while some recent backbones with huge parameters have not been explored with the CFTL.

title: SRT: Super-Resolution for Time Series via Disentangled Rectified Flow
name: SRT-Disentangled-Rectified-Flow
short_hypothesis: A time series super-resolution framework, SRT, can effectively reconstruct high-resolution temporal patterns by decomposing the input into trend and seasonal components, aligning them to the target resolution with an implicit time function, and generating high-resolution details for each component in parallel using two separate rectified flow models guided by a cross-resolution attention mechanism.
related_work: Time series super-resolution (TSSR) is analogous to image super-resolution but faces unique challenges. Directly applying computer vision techniques (GANs, diffusion models, flow matching) is not trivial due to different data priors and dimensionality. The task is also distinct from time series imputation; while imputation fills in missing values in an existing high-resolution grid, TSSR must synthesize a fundamentally new high-resolution signal from a systematically downsampled input, generating credible high-frequency components (e.g., sharp peaks) that are absent in the low-resolution series. Existing generative models for time series may not explicitly model the low-resolution to high-resolution correspondence, potentially leading to a lack of structural consistency. This work proposes a unified framework to address these challenges for both sampled and aggregated super-resolution tasks.
abstract: Fine-grained time series data with high temporal resolution is critical for accurate analytics across a wide range of applications. However, the acquisition of such data is often limited by cost and feasibility. This problem can be tackled by reconstructing high-resolution signals from low-resolution inputs based on specific priors, known as super-resolution. While extensively studied in computer vision, directly transferring image super-resolution techniques to time series is not trivial. To address this challenge at a fundamental level, we propose Super-Resolution for Time series (SRT), a novel framework that reconstructs temporal patterns lost in low-resolution inputs via disentangled rectified flow. SRT decomposes the input into trend and seasonal components, aligns them to the target resolution using an implicit neural representation, and leverages a novel cross-resolution attention mechanism to guide the generation of high-resolution details. We further introduce SRT-large, a scaled-up version with extensive pretraining, which enables strong zero-shot super-resolution capability.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed SRT model against existing methods on time series super-resolution (TSSR) tasks. The evaluation will be conducted on two subtasks: sampled super-resolution (SSR) and aggregated super-resolution (ASR).
experiments.Method: The SRT model will be evaluated on nine public datasets: ETTh1, ETTh2, ETTm1, ETTm2, weather, PEMS-SF, MotorImagery, SelfRegulationSCP1, and SelfRegulationSCP2. It will be compared against eight baseline methods: SRDiff, ResShift, IDM, FlowIE, CSDI, FTS-Diffusion, Diffusion-TS, and FlowTS.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of each individual component in the SRT architecture through a series of ablation studies.
experiments.Method: The full SRT model will be compared against several variants with specific components removed or altered. These variants include: w/o ITF schema, w/o pattern smoothness in ITF, w/o the entire ITF module, w/o RoPE, w/o Pre-LN, w/o the CRA mechanism (1st layer, 2nd layer, or both), and w/o the disentanglement-based generation. The study will be conducted on the SSR task using the ETTm1, weather, and PEMS-SF datasets.

experiments.Description: To assess the efficacy of the proposed decoder-only Transformer design for the Velocity Predictor by comparing it with other common neural network architectures.
experiments.Method: The proposed velocity predictor in SRT will be replaced by alternative architectures, including MLP, TCN, UNet, LSTM, and a vanilla Transformer, while keeping the parameter scale comparable. The comparison will be performed on the SSR task using the ETTm1, weather, and PEMS-SF datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the zero-shot super-resolution capability of SRT-large, a scaled-up version of SRT pretrained on large-scale datasets from multiple domains.
experiments.Method: SRT-large will be pretrained on a curated collection of time series from domains including equity, commodity, currency rates, power, retail, transportation, web search trends, and synthetic data. Its zero-shot performance will be evaluated in a cross-dataset setting on the nine public benchmark datasets for the SSR task, and compared against baseline models across various scale factors.

experiments.Description: To validate the capability of SRT and SRT-large to handle non-standard, out-of-distribution TSSR tasks, such as generating weekly data from monthly data.
experiments.Method: An experiment will be conducted on three synthesized daily time series with different underlying characteristics (Toy #1, Toy #2, Toy #3). The models (SRT, SRT-large, IDM, FTS-Diff) will be tasked with performing super-resolution from monthly data to weekly data, using both sampling and averaging approaches.

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of SRT against classic time series interpolation methods.
experiments.Method: SRT will be compared against Nearest Neighbor, Linear, and Cubic Spline interpolation. The experiments will be conducted on the ETTm1, Weather, and PEMS-SF datasets for both SSR and ASR tasks.

experiments.Description: To compare the rectified flow-based generation in SRT against a traditional diffusion model (DDPM) based approach in terms of sample quality and efficiency.
experiments.Method: The rectified flow generator in SRT will be replaced with a DDPM-based approach. The performance will be compared on the ETTm1, Weather, and PEMS-SF datasets for both SSR and ASR tasks, evaluating the DDPM-based model with varying numbers of sampling steps (4, 50, 100, 200).

risk_factors: Our approach is built on several implicit structural assumptions, such as the decomposability of both high-frequency and low-frequency sequences, leveraging low-frequency periodicity and trend to guide the generation of corresponding components in the high-frequency residuals. While experiments demonstrate that these priors provide strong modeling advantages, it is important to note that real-world time series data can exhibit a broad range of behaviors, and such assumptions may not always be strictly satisfied.
It should be noted that the more stable TSSR performance and zero-shot super-resolution capability of SRT-large come at the cost of increased computational demands during inference.

title: On Fine-Grained I/O Complexity of Attention Backward Passes
name: fine-grained-io-complexity-attention-backward
short_hypothesis: By analyzing the attention backward pass using the red-blue pebble game framework, it is possible to establish tight upper and lower I/O complexity bounds for all cache sizes. For small cache scenarios (M = o(d²)), a new algorithm can be designed that achieves these tight bounds and outperforms existing methods like FlashAttention. For large cache scenarios (M = Ω(d²)), FlashAttention can be proven to be optimal by establishing a matching lower bound. This analysis can also be extended to derive fine-grained lower bounds for sparse attention.
related_work: The I/O complexity of attention mechanisms is a critical bottleneck for long-context LLMs. FlashAttention provided initial I/O-aware algorithms and analysis, particularly for the forward pass in large cache scenarios. Subsequent work by Saha & Ye (2024) provided matching upper and lower bounds for the forward pass across all cache sizes. However, for the attention backward pass, only an upper bound for large cache sizes was known, with no corresponding lower bounds. The optimal I/O complexity for backward passes, especially in small cache scenarios, remained an open question. Furthermore, while sparse attention is a popular technique to reduce computational FLOPs, its theoretical I/O complexity guarantees were not well understood.
abstract: The quadratic complexity of attention computation with respect to sequence length poses significant computational challenges for Large Language Models (LLMs). This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the I/O complexity for attention mechanisms, focusing on backward passes by categorizing them into small and large cache scenarios. Using the red-blue pebble game framework, we propose to establish tight bounds on I/O complexity across all cache sizes. For small cache sizes, we provide an algorithm that improves over existing methods and aims to achieve tight bounds. Additionally, we extend our analysis to sparse attention, a mainstream speeding-up approach, by deriving fine-grained lower bounds for both forward and backward passes across both small and large caches.

experiments.Description: To derive the optimal I/O complexity for the attention backward pass in the small cache scenario (M = o(d²)) and propose an algorithm that achieves this bound.
experiments.Method: A new algorithm (Algorithm 6) is proposed for the attention backward pass. Its I/O complexity will be analyzed theoretically using the red-blue pebble game framework. The analysis will establish matching upper and lower bounds. The primary baseline for theoretical comparison is FlashAttention.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Asymptotic I/O complexity (upper bound)', 'Asymptotic I/O complexity (lower bound)', 'Time complexity', 'Space complexity']

experiments.Description: To derive the optimal I/O complexity for the attention backward pass in the large cache scenario (M = Ω(d²)) and prove the optimality of existing methods.
experiments.Method: A theoretical analysis using the red-blue pebble game framework will be conducted to establish a tight lower bound for I/O complexity. This lower bound is intended to match the known upper bound of FlashAttention. A new algorithm (Algorithm 9) is also proposed to achieve the optimal upper bound, providing a different analysis.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Asymptotic I/O complexity (upper bound)', 'Asymptotic I/O complexity (lower bound)']

experiments.Description: To derive fine-grained lower bounds for the I/O complexity of sparse attention for both forward and backward passes across all cache sizes.
experiments.Method: A theoretical analysis of sparse semi-ring matrix multiplication will be performed on the attention computation graph. The goal is to establish I/O complexity lower bounds based on the non-zero counts of input matrices (Z_input, Z_QK).

risk_factors: In this work, we only consider exact attention computation without any approximation.

Note that there are many fast matrix multiplication methods. We do not study them, as they are hard to be parallelized. Standard matrix multiplication is still the most popular implementation on GPU, e.g., PyTorch.

The Softmax in Definition 3.1 can be changed to other non-linear activation functions and our lower bound still holds. It is because we must compute matrix multiplication of size n×d and d × n in non-linear attention. However, for linear attention, that is, A1XA2A3Y, our lower bound is loose.

title: Carré du champ flow matching: better quality-generalisation tradeoff in generative models
name: carre-du-champ-flow-matching
short_hypothesis: Replacing the homogeneous, isotropic noise in Flow Matching (FM) with a spatially varying, anisotropic Gaussian noise, whose covariance captures the local geometry of the data manifold, will improve the quality-generalisation tradeoff. This new method, Carré du champ flow matching (CDC-FM), regularises the probability path with a geometry-aware noise, which can be optimally estimated from the data.
related_work: Deep generative models like VAEs, GANs, and Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs) often face a tradeoff between generating high-quality samples and memorising training data. Flow Matching (FM), a unifying framework for CNFs, models a deterministic probability path from a source to a target density. However, the standard FM construction uses a homogeneous and isotropic Gaussian kernel approximation, which risks concentrating the learned distribution around training points, leading to memorisation rather than generalisation. This suggests a need for a regularisation method that preserves the underlying data geometry to mitigate memorisation.
abstract: Deep generative models often face a fundamental tradeoff: high sample quality can come at the cost of memorisation, where the model reproduces training data rather than generalising across the underlying data geometry. We introduce Carré du champ flow matching (CDC-FM), a generalisation of flow matching (FM), that improves the quality-generalisation tradeoff by regularising the probability path with a geometry-aware noise. Our method replaces the homogeneous, isotropic noise in FM with a spatially varying, anisotropic Gaussian noise whose covariance captures the local geometry of the latent data manifold. We prove that this geometric noise can be optimally estimated from the data and is scalable to large data.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to represent datasets with strong geometric structure, specifically in 3D geometry inference.
experiments.Method: Models (CDC-FM and baseline FM) will be trained on 40-200 uniformly sampled points from topographic LiDAR data of Mt. Rainier, WA. The velocity fields will be parameterised by multilayer perceptrons (MLPs).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Memorisation (nearest-neighbour ratio)', 'Generalisation (Negative Log-Likelihood on a test set)', 'Geometric fidelity (Distance-to-manifold, DtM)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of geometric regularisation on inferring single-cell gene expression trajectories, which are low-dimensional and complex in a high-dimensional space.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the CITE-seq ('Cite') and Multiomics ('Multi') single-cell benchmarks. The task is leave-one-out interpolation of temporal snapshots after PCA dimensionality reduction. Models (CDC-FM and baseline FM, with both unpaired and optimal transport-paired variants) will be trained with an MLP architecture.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Earth mover distance (EMD) between predicted and held-out snapshots']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that for spatially heterogeneous data, standard FM may memorise sparse regions while generalising in dense ones, and to evaluate if CDC-FM can mitigate this.
experiments.Method: Models (CDC-FM and FM) will be trained on a dataset of two circles with different diameters, each with eight training points, representing a spatially heterogeneous density.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the quality-generalisation tradeoff on complex, high-dimensional animal motion capture data, which exhibits non-uniform density.
experiments.Method: Models (CDC-FM and FM) will be trained on pose sequences from *Drosophila melanogaster* motion capture data. A transformer architecture will be used to approximate the generative velocity field.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sample quality (manifold coverage, measured by mean distance of test points to nearest samples)', 'Generalisation (NLL)', 'Memorisation (nearest-neighbour ratio)']

experiments.Description: To study the influence of increasing manifold dimension on model performance, which is known to challenge manifold-based methods due to the curse of dimensionality.
experiments.Method: Models (CDC-FM and FM) will be trained on synthetic tori T^d = (S^1)^d embedded in R^2d, with varying dimension `d` and number of training points.

experiments.Description: To test whether the benefits of CDC-FM extend to a latent space diffusion setting for high-dimensional image data.
experiments.Method: Models (CDC-FM and FM) will be trained on the latent space of a Stable Diffusion VAE using a subset of 1000 CelebA-HQ images. The output will be decoded back to pixel space for evaluation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) in latent space']

experiments.Description: To study the scaling of CDC-FM to larger datasets in image synthesis and evaluate its performance in a low-data regime where memorisation is common.
experiments.Method: Models (CDC-FM and FM) will be trained on increasing subsets of the CIFAR-10 dataset, using a UNet architecture.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Sample quality (Fréchet Inception Distance, FID)', 'Generalisation (NLL / bits per dimension)', 'Percentage of memorised points']

risk_factors: Yet, we found that as the manifold dimension rises, our method needed exponentially more samples to maintain accuracy, due to the need to estimate tangent spaces from local neighbourhoods.

Further, for non-geometric data, the benefit of geometric regularisation diminished, on average, as the training set size increased, because the neural network architectures and the loss function already confer inductive biases.

title: CTSyn: A Foundation Model for Cross Tabular Data Generation
name: ctsyn-foundation-model-tabular-generation
short_hypothesis: A diffusion-based generative foundation model, named CTSyn, can effectively generate high-quality synthetic tabular data by first using a cross-tabular autoencoder to consolidate diverse tables into a unified latent space, and then using a conditional latent diffusion model to generate new samples from this space. This approach, which leverages table schema and metadata for guidance and is enhanced by large-scale pre-training, is proposed to overcome the limitations of existing methods in handling heterogeneous table structures and to enable effective knowledge transfer across different tabular datasets.
related_work: Current methods for generating synthetic tabular data or learning transferable representations face significant challenges. Some approaches, like VIME or SCARF, are data-specific and do not produce representations transferable across different tables. Other methods attempt to use transformer backbones (e.g., Xtab, TabRet) but often require data-specific components, increasing complexity. Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs) have been used to unify feature representations (e.g., TransTab) or treat tables as text (e.g., GReaT), but these methods may lack a dedicated generative backbone, distort crucial structural information, or struggle with numerical values. This leaves a gap for a foundational generative model specifically designed for the structural and semantic heterogeneity of cross-tabular data.
abstract: Generative Foundation Models (GFMs) have achieved remarkable success in producing high-quality synthetic data for images and text. However, their application to tabular data presents significant challenges due to the heterogeneous nature of table features. Current cross-table learning frameworks struggle because they lack a generative model backbone and an effective mechanism to decode heterogeneous feature values. To address these challenges, we propose the Cross-Table Synthesizer (CTSyn), a diffusion-based generative foundation model for tabular data generation. CTSyn comprises two key components. The first is an autoencoder network that consolidates diverse tables into a unified latent space. It dynamically reconstructs table values using a table schema embedding, allowing adaptation to heterogeneous datasets. The second is a conditional latent diffusion model that generates samples from the learned latent space, conditioned on the table schema.
