experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of CTSyn in generating high-quality synthetic data for downstream tasks, particularly in low-data regimes, by comparing its statistical fidelity and machine learning utility against a wide array of baseline models.
experiments.Method: CTSyn will be pre-trained on a filtered version of the OpenTab dataset (86 tables, 5.01 million rows). It will then be fine-tuned on few-shot subsets (30, 50, 100, 200, and 500 rows) of eleven downstream benchmark datasets: Faults, Wilt, HTRU2, News, Bean, Obesity, Titanic, Insurance, Abalone, Shoppers, and Indian Liver Patient. The performance will be compared against baselines including SMOTE, CTGAN, TVAE, TabDDPM, TabSyn, AIM, PATE-CTGAN, and GReaT.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Statistical Fidelity: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test, Total Variation Distance (TVD), Pearson's correlation, contingency similarity metric, and sample-level precision and recall.", 'Machine Learning Utility: F1-score (for classification tasks) and Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) (for regression tasks) from models (logistic regression, Naïve Bayes, decision tree, random forest, XGBoost, CatBoost) trained on the generated synthetic data.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the diversity of the synthesized data and its proximity to the original training data, as a proxy for privacy and memorization risk.
experiments.Method: Using the synthetic data generated for the main downstream task evaluation, the diversity will be assessed against state-of-the-art models like TabDDPM, TabSyn, AIM, and PATE-CTGAN.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Proportion of synthetic examples with L2 distance closer to the test set (PCT)', 'Authenticity scores']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the autoencoder architecture to evaluate the importance of metadata and column names for effective cross-tabular representation and reconstruction.
experiments.Method: Different variations of the CTSyn autoencoder will be trained and tested: 1) the full model with column and table metadata, 2) a model with only column name embeddings, 3) a model with only table metadata, and 4) a model using positional encodings instead of any metadata. These variations will be tested on in-distribution data, unseen downstream datasets, and datasets with randomly permuted column orders.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Mean Squared Error (MSE) for numerical reconstruction', 'Categorical accuracy for categorical reconstruction']

experiments.Description: To investigate the impact of pre-training on the performance of CTSyn and compare its ability to leverage pre-training data against other models like GReaT and TabSyn.
experiments.Method: The main experiments will be repeated with a version of CTSyn trained from scratch without pre-training. Similarly, GReaT and TabSyn will be evaluated with and without their respective pre-training procedures on the downstream benchmark datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Shape similarity', 'Correlation similarity (Corr)', 'Synth F1', 'Synth RMSE', 'PCT (Proportion of synthetic examples with L2 distance closer to the test set)']

risk_factors: CTSyn's performance relies heavily on clean, large-scale tabular datasets. The quality of generated data depends on the training data, and any biases or errors can be propagated.
Additionally, despite pre-training reducing memorization of downstream data, individuals included in the pre-training data still face privacy risks, complicating the safe gathering of large datasets.

The requirement of semantically meaningful category names also present challenges for acquiriing large-scale training data, as normalized values must be carefully sanitized and converted back to raw form.

title: FOE-RL: Flexible Online Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Inference in Large Language Models
name: foe-rl-flexible-online-reinforcement-learning
short_hypothesis: A flexible online reinforcement learning method, named FOE-RL, can mitigate "overthinking" in large reasoning models. By estimating problem difficulty to predict an appropriate output length and using a reward trend monitor to dynamically schedule a length-based reward, the model can be guided to an optimal output length range based on its own capabilities and the task difficulty. This will improve inference efficiency by reducing token usage while maintaining or improving accuracy.
related_work: Large reasoning models (LRMs) often employ Chain-of-Thought (CoT) mechanisms, which improve performance on complex tasks but can lead to "overthinking"—generating excessively long reasoning chains even for simple problems. This increases latency and computational costs. Prior work has attempted to control inference length using reinforcement learning by penalizing long responses, setting fixed length budgets, or using adaptive methods based on problem difficulty. However, these approaches may not be flexible enough, as they often aim to simply shorten outputs rather than finding an adaptive and reasonable response length. This research proposes a more sustainable framework with a redesigned reward calculation and a dynamic scheduling strategy to guide models toward an optimal output length range based on their capability and the problem's difficulty.
abstract: Recent advancements in large reasoning models have significantly enhanced their reasoning abilities. However, recent studies have shown that these models often experience "overthinking," even when handling relatively simple questions. In this paper, we propose a flexible online reinforcement learning method that estimates the difficulty of a problem in real-time and predicts an appropriate output length. Based on this, we design a length reward function and a flexible reward trend monitor, which dynamically activates or deactivates the length reward according to smoothed correctness rewards.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the FOE-RL method against baselines on mathematical reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: The Qwen3-0.6B and Qwen3-1.7B models will be trained on the training set of the MATH dataset. The models will then be evaluated on the MATH-TEST and MATH500 test sets. Performance will be compared against two baselines: the original pre-trained model (-ORIGIN) and a model fine-tuned with only correctness rewards (-NL).

experiments.Description: To assess the generalization performance of the FOE-RL trained models on additional mathematical benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The Qwen3-0.6B and Qwen3-1.7B models, after being trained on the MATH dataset, will be evaluated on the AMC23, AIME2024, and AIME2025 datasets. The performance will be compared against the -ORIGIN and -NL baselines.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation analysis of the reward trend monitor component.
experiments.Method: A Qwen3-1.7B model will be trained by removing the length reward scheduling based on the reward monitor (suffix -OL). This variant will apply the length reward throughout the entire training process. Its performance will be compared to the full FOE-RL method on the MATH-TEST and MATH500 datasets.

risk_factors: Due to computational constraints, both training and testing on the MATH dataset were conducted with a prompt length limited to 256 tokens and a response length capped at 3072 tokens.
We acknowledge that while our method encourages concise reasoning, it may still reflect biases present in the base models or training data.

title: Beyond Binary Preferences: A Principled Framework for Reward Modeling with Ordinal Feedback
name: Ordinal-Regression-for-Reward-Modeling
short_hypothesis: Formulating reward modeling with Likert-scale preferences as a discrete ordinal regression problem, which learns threshold parameters directly from data, will provide a more principled and effective way to utilize fine-grained human feedback compared to ad-hoc heuristic modifications of binary preference models.
related_work: Current reward modeling approaches, such as those in RLHF and DPO, are fundamentally based on the Bradley-Terry model, which is designed for binary comparisons. To handle richer, ordinal feedback (e.g., Likert scales), existing methods apply ad-hoc heuristics like adding margin terms, scaling loss functions, or using soft labels. These approaches lack a principled mathematical model explaining how ordinal data is generated and require manual tuning of hyperparameters (e.g., margins, scaling factors) that have no clear interpretation and are not learned from data. This creates a need for a systematic framework to leverage the richer signal in ordinal preference data.
abstract: Reward modeling is crucial for aligning large language models with human preferences, yet current approaches lack a principled mathematical framework for leveraging ordinal preference data. When human annotators provide graded preferences on a Likert scale (e.g., significantly better, better, slightly better, negligibly better), existing methods typically apply ad-hoc heuristics, such as margin terms or scaling factors, to loss functions derived from binary preference models like Bradley-Terry. These approaches lack an underlying mathematical model for how ordinal preference data is generated. We present a theoretically grounded framework that formulates reward modeling with Likert scale preferences as a discrete ordinal regression problem. We derive two loss functions from this formulation: a negative log-likelihood loss and an all-threshold loss, both of which learn threshold parameters that naturally capture the ordinal structure of preferences. Unlike existing heuristic methods that manually specify fixed margins or scaling weights, our approach learns these parameters directly from data within a coherent probabilistic framework.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed ordinal regression framework for reward modeling against existing heuristic baselines across multiple benchmarks and model architectures.
experiments.Method: Models will be trained on the HelpSteer2 and HelpSteer3 datasets. The proposed methods (NLL-Symmetric, NLL-Asymmetric, All-Threshold) will be implemented on Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, and Zephyr-7B base models. These will be compared against three baseline heuristics: Margin BT, Scaled BT, and Soft Label.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Performance will be evaluated on the RewardBench and RM-Bench benchmarks, measuring ranking accuracy across various categories (e.g., Chat, Math, Code, Safety, Hard).

experiments.Description: To assess whether the proposed ordinal regression methods genuinely capture fine-grained preference structure, and to determine if joint training of reward and threshold parameters is superior to post-hoc calibration of thresholds on a pre-trained reward model.
experiments.Method: A jointly trained NLL-Symmetric model will be compared against baseline models (Scaled BT, Margin BT, Soft Label) that are first trained normally, after which ordinal thresholds are calibrated post-hoc on their frozen reward differences. The evaluation will be on a held-out test set from HelpSteer2.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Ordinal prediction quality will be measured using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Accuracy Within k (Acc@0, Acc@1, Acc@2).

experiments.Description: To analyze the severity of errors made by the proposed ordinal model compared to a standard binary preference model, focusing on the magnitude of misrankings.
experiments.Method: An NLL Ordinal Symmetric model will be compared against a standard Bradley-Terry (Simple BT) model, both trained on HelpSteer2. The error margin distributions for incorrectly ranked examples will be analyzed on the RewardBench test set.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Metrics will include total error frequency, mean error magnitude, maximum error magnitude, and the shape of the error margin distribution.

experiments.Description: To investigate the robustness of the ordinal regression framework to different types and levels of annotation noise in the training data.
experiments.Method: The NLL-Symmetric model will be trained on the HelpSteer2 dataset with two types of injected noise: 'Systematic Shift Noise' (labels shifted by one level) and 'Random Label Noise' (labels replaced with a random choice). Each noise type will be tested at corruption rates of 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Models trained on corrupted data will be evaluated on the clean (noise-free) validation set using RewardBench and RM-Bench to measure their ability to recover true preferences.

risk_factors: While we leave empirical evaluation of ordinal DPO for future work, we note that the theoretical properties established for ordinal reward modeling largely transfer to the DPO setting.
The unregularized optimization problem has no finite optimal solution.
The exponential mapping [for reparameterization] naturally maintains constraints but can occasionally lead to numerical issues if α values become very negative (causing near-zero increments).

Future work could extend this framework to DPO as outlined in Appendix A, handle more complex preference structures such as multi-aspect ratings or pairwise comparisons with uncertainty estimates, and adapt to evolving data collection methodologies.

title: The Consensus Game: Language Model Generation via Equilibrium Search
name: consensus-game-equilibrium-ranking
short_hypothesis: Formulating language model decoding as a regularized imperfect-information sequential signaling game, called the Consensus Game, can reconcile conflicting generative and discriminative scoring procedures. Finding an approximate equilibrium of this game will produce more coherent and accurate LM predictions.
related_work: Current approaches to reconcile differing language model predictions fall into two categories. Ensembling methods combine generative and discriminative scores directly, but suffer from poor calibration, meaning scores may not combine consistently. Deliberation methods use re-prompting with competing inputs to generate a justification, but this incurs significant computational overhead. The proposed game-theoretic approach aims to provide a principled and computationally efficient procedure for obtaining a 'consensus' prediction that satisfies both coherence (agreement between generative and discriminative views) and reasonableness (predictions are close to the original LM's behavior).
abstract: When applied to question answering and other text generation tasks, language models (LMs) may be queried generatively (by sampling answers from their output distribution) or discriminatively (by using them to score or rank a set of candidate outputs). These procedures sometimes yield very different predictions. How do we reconcile mutually incompatible scoring procedures to obtain coherent LM predictions? We introduce a new, training-free, game-theoretic procedure for language model decoding. Our approach casts language model decoding as a regularized imperfect-information sequential signaling game—which we term the CONSENSUS GAME—in which a GENERATOR seeks to communicate an abstract correctness parameter using natural language sentences to a DISCRIMINATOR. We develop computational procedures for finding approximate equilibria of this game, resulting in a decoding algorithm we call EQUILIBRIUM-RANKING.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of EQUILIBRIUM-RANKING on a diverse set of multiple-choice question-answering tasks, covering reading comprehension, commonsense reasoning, and value alignment.
experiments.Method: The LLaMA-7B and LLaMA-13B models will be evaluated on the MMLU, ARC (Easy and Challenge), RACE (Middle and High), and HHH benchmarks. The proposed methods, Equilibrium Ranking Generator (ER-G) and Equilibrium Ranking Discriminator (ER-D), will be compared against several baselines: Generative Ranking (G), Mutual Information Ranking (MI), Self-Contrastive Ranking (SC), and Discriminative Ranking (D).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to avoid generating false answers learned from imitation learning, using a generative task setup.
experiments.Method: The LLaMA-7B and LLaMA-13B models will be evaluated on the TruthfulQA benchmark. Candidate sequences will be sampled via nucleus and top-k sampling and then ranked. The proposed methods will be compared against Greedy decoding, MI, SC, and D baselines.

experiments.Description: To study whether the proposed approach can be combined with chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting to improve mathematical reasoning ability.
experiments.Method: The LLaMA-7B and LLaMA-13B models will be evaluated on the GSM8K benchmark. 20 candidate reasoning paths will be sampled using an 8-shot CoT setup. The proposed methods will be used to score each reasoning path, and the final answer will be selected via aggregation (self-consistency). Baselines include Majority Vote (MV), MI, SC, and D.

risk_factors: While we do not provide a formal guarantee of convergence, we remark that the CONSENSUS GAME is an instance of a potential game (Monderer & Shapley, 1996), for which it is generally understood that decentralized no-regret learning dynamics similar to piKL converge in iterates to equilibrium.
Beyond the applications studied here (which focus mainly on question answer), future work might apply this toolkit to more general tasks like long-form text generation.

title: Video Unlearning via Low-Rank Refusal Vector
name: video-unlearning-low-rank-refusal-vector
short_hypothesis: A training-free weight update framework can achieve concept removal in video diffusion models. By estimating a 'refusal vector' from a few paired safe/unsafe prompts and integrating it into model weights via a closed-form update, it is possible to suppress unwanted concepts. A contrastive low-rank factorization can further disentangle the target concept from unrelated semantics, ensuring selective concept suppression without harming general generation quality or requiring retraining.
related_work: Existing methods for mitigating unsafe generations in generative models are categorized as either filtering or weight updates. Filtering methods are easy to deploy but can be bypassed at inference. Weight-update approaches, such as fine-tuning, are more permanent but are computationally expensive and prone to catastrophic forgetting. More recent training-free, closed-form editing methods like UCE and RECE have been proposed for image diffusion models but are not directly applicable to video architectures. In the video domain, existing methods either rely on inference-time filtering (SAFREE) which is not permanent, or costly fine-tuning (NullSCE). This presents a research gap for a permanent, efficient, training-free weight update method for concept removal specifically in video diffusion models.
abstract: Video generative models, trained on large-scale web data, are exposed to unsafe biases and harmful concepts, risking the generation of undesirable content. Existing machine unlearning approaches either rely on filtering, which can be bypassed, or they update model weights through costly fine-tuning or training-free closed-form edits designed for images. We propose the first training-free weight update framework for concept removal in video diffusion models. From a small set of paired safe/unsafe prompts, our method estimates a refusal vector and integrates it into the model weights as a closed-form update. A contrastive low-rank factorization is proposed to disentangle the target concept from unrelated semantics, ensuring selective concept suppression without harming generation quality. This aims to establish an efficient and scalable solution for safe video generation without retraining or inference overhead.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed unlearning method for suppressing unsafe concepts while preserving video quality, compared to a fine-tuning based approach.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be applied to the OPEN-SORA model. The performance will be evaluated on the T2VSafetyBench benchmark. The primary baseline for comparison will be NullSCE, a fine-tuning based unlearning strategy.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Censorship rate (percentage of unsafe generations, assessed by a GPT-4o evaluator)', 'Fréchet Video Distance (FVD)', 'MM-Notox (semantic preservation)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed unlearning method compared to a filtering-based approach on a different video generation model and benchmark.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be applied to the ZEROSCOPET2V model. The evaluation will be conducted on the SafeSora benchmark. The primary baseline for comparison will be SAFREE, an inference-time filtering mechanism.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of adapting an existing text-to-image concept editing method (UCE) to the video domain and compare it against the proposed T2V-specific approach.
experiments.Method: An adapted version of the UCE method will be implemented for a text-to-video pipeline. The evaluation will be performed on the ZEROSCOPET2V model using the Pornography category of the T2VSafetyBench.

risk_factors: This paper contains data and model outputs which are offensive in nature.
Figure 4 also highlights the trade-off in output quality: small values leave unsafe content under-suppressed, while very large values over-suppress the latent code and visibly corrupt the video.
When k is too large, unrelated directions are included, leading to entanglement with safe concepts. When k is too small, relevant directions are discarded, reducing effectiveness.

title: Swarm Intelligence Enhanced Reasoning: A Density-Driven Framework for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Optimization
name: sier-swarm-reasoning
short_hypothesis: By formulating LLM reasoning as a swarm intelligence optimization problem, a group of LLM-based agents can collaboratively search for optimal solutions. To avoid local optima, a density-driven framework called Swarm Intelligence Enhancing Reasoning (SIER) is proposed, which uses kernel density estimation and non-dominated sorting to simultaneously optimize for both solution quality and diversity, thereby enhancing solution space exploration and improving reasoning on complex problems.
related_work: Current methods for complex reasoning, such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Multi-Agent Debate (MAD), have limitations. CoT can be prone to hallucinations, while MAD methods often fail to produce diverse solutions because the agents, despite having different roles, are based on the same underlying LLM and are thus inherently homogeneous. This leads to high solution similarity and difficulty in finding optimal results. Traditional swarm intelligence algorithms, while powerful for optimization, risk getting trapped in local optima. This work aims to bridge these gaps by introducing a swarm intelligence framework specifically for LLM reasoning that explicitly balances solution quality and diversity.
abstract: Recently, many approaches, such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and Multi-Agent Debate (MAD), have been proposed to enrich Large Language Models' (LLMs) complex problem-solving capacities. However, these methods may fail to solve complex problems due to a lack of ability to find optimal solutions. To address this, we propose integrating swarm intelligence into the reasoning process by introducing a novel Agent-based Swarm Intelligence (ASI) paradigm. In this paradigm, we formulate LLM reasoning as an optimization problem and use a swarm intelligence scheme to guide a group of LLM-based agents in collaboratively searching for optimal solutions. To avoid getting trapped in local optima, we further develop a Swarm Intelligence Enhancing Reasoning (SIER) framework, which uses a density-driven strategy. Specifically, we propose performing kernel density estimation and non-dominated sorting to optimize both solution quality and diversity simultaneously. This is intended to enhance solution space exploration by expanding the diversity of reasoning paths. Additionally, a step-level quality evaluation is used to help agents correct low-quality intermediate steps, and quality thresholds are used to dynamically control the reasoning process.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed Swarm Intelligence Enhancing Reasoning (SIER) framework on mathematical reasoning tasks against established reasoning methods.
experiments.Method: The SIER framework will be implemented using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the policy model and Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-72B as the process reward model. The evaluation will be conducted on several mathematical reasoning benchmarks: AIME-2024, AIME-2025, LiveMathBench, MMLU-STEM, MATH-500 (including a specific subset of level-5 problems), and GSM8K. The performance of SIER will be compared against Chain-of-Thought (CoT) methods (including Self-Consistency and Best-of-N) and the Reward Guide Search (RGS) method.

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of the key components of the SIER framework—namely fitness guidance, density calculation, and the evolutionary scheme—through ablation studies.
experiments.Method: The experiment will compare the full SIER framework against three ablated variants on the same set of mathematical reasoning benchmarks: 1) 'SIER w/o Fitness', which selects candidates based only on density; 2) 'SIER w/o Density', which selects candidates based only on fitness scores; and 3) 'SIER w/o Evolution', which removes the evolutionary mechanism entirely and is equivalent to a standard CoT with PRM-based selection.

risk_factors: We notice that our method consumes a relatively higher number of tokens compared to other methods, and the token usage increases with problem complexity.

In practice, however, evaluators often function as black boxes. Influenced by training data and specific architectures (e.g., Process Reward Models (PRMs) or Outcome Reward Models (ORMs)), their assessments offer imperfect guidance. Fitness scores might not accurately represent solution potential, and reliance on them risks premature convergence.

title: GameGen-X: Interactive Open-world Game Video Generation
name: gamegen-x-interactive-game-video-generation
short_hypothesis: A diffusion transformer model, GameGen-X, trained in a two-stage process on a large-scale, game-specific dataset can generate high-quality, open-world game videos and allow for interactive control. This is achieved by first pre-training a foundation model for generation and continuation, and then instruction-tuning a separate InstructNet module to incorporate multi-modal user inputs for control, while keeping the foundation model frozen.
related_work: Prior works like World Model, GameGAN, and Genie have demonstrated the feasibility of simulating interactive environments for 2D or early 3D games. However, the generation of novel, complex, and controllable content for modern open-world games remains an open problem. Key difficulties lie in generating coherent next-gen game content with intricate environments and dynamic events, and in ensuring the generated content responds meaningfully to user inputs. Existing general video generation models are not specifically designed for the unique requirements of the game domain, such as interactive control and game-like camera perspectives.
abstract: We introduce GameGen-X, the first diffusion transformer model specifically designed for both generating and interactively controlling open-world game videos. This model facilitates high-quality, open-domain generation by approximating various game elements, such as innovative characters, dynamic environments, complex actions, and diverse events. Additionally, it provides interactive controllability, predicting and altering future content based on the current clip, thus allowing for gameplay simulation. To realize this vision, we first collected and built an Open-World Video Game Dataset (OGameData) from scratch, comprising over one million diverse gameplay video clips with informative captions. GameGen-X undergoes a two-stage training process, consisting of pre-training and instruction tuning. Firstly, the model is pre-trained via text-to-video generation and video continuation. Further, to achieve interactive controllability, we designed InstructNet to incorporate game-related multi-modal control signal experts. This allows the model to adjust latent representations based on user inputs. During instruction tuning, only the InstructNet is updated while the pre-trained foundation model is frozen.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to generate high-quality, open-domain game video content from text prompts.
experiments.Method: GameGen-X will be compared against several open-source models: Mira, OpenSora-Plan1.2, OpenSora1.2, and CogVideoX-5B. The evaluation will be conducted on the OGameEval-Gen benchmark, a set of text-video pairs sampled from the OGameData-GEN dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Fréchet Inception Distance (FID)', 'Fréchet Video Distance (FVD)', 'Text-Video Alignment (TVA)', 'User Preference (UP)', 'Motion Smoothness (MS)', 'Dynamic Degrees (DD)', 'Subject Consistency (SC)', 'Imaging Quality (IQ)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to interactively control generated video content based on user inputs.
experiments.Method: GameGen-X will be compared against models that support video continuation: OpenSora-Plan1.2, OpenSora1.2, and CogVideoX-5B. The evaluation will use the OGameEval-Ins benchmark, which involves providing conditioned video clips and dense instructional prompts.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success Rate for Character Actions (SR-C)', 'Success Rate for Environment Events (SR-E)', 'User Preference (UP)', 'Motion Smoothness (MS)', 'Dynamic Degrees (DD)', 'Subject Consistency (SC)', 'Imaging Quality (IQ)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the influence of different data strategies on generation ability.
experiments.Method: The baseline model trained on the OGameData-GEN-Abl subset will be compared against variants trained with different data strategies: using the MiraData dataset, using short captions instead of structured ones, and using a progressive training schedule.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the design choices for interactive control ability.
experiments.Method: The baseline model will be compared against variants where key components are removed: training without InstructNet, training without structured Instruct Captions, and training without decomposing the generation and control tasks.

risk_factors: Real-Time Generation and Interaction: In the realm of gameplay, real-time interaction is crucial, and there is a significant appeal in developing a video generation model that enables such interactivity. However, the computational demands of diffusion models, particularly concerning the sampling process and the complexity of spatial and temporal self-attention mechanisms, present formidable challenges.
Consistency in Auto-Regressive Generation: Auto-regressive generation often leads to accumulated errors, which can affect both character consistency and scene coherence over long sequences.
Complex Action Generation: The model struggles with fast and complex actions, such as combat sequences, where rapid motion exceeds its current capacity.
High-Resolution Generation: GameGen-X is not yet capable of generating ultra-high-resolution content (e.g., 2K/4K) due to memory and processing constraints.

Long-Term Consistency in Video Generation: In gameplay, maintaining scene consistency is crucial, especially as players transition between and return to scenes. However, our model currently exhibits a limitation in temporal coherence due to its short-term memory capacity of just 1-108 frames.
Physics Simulation and Realism: While our methods achieve high visual fidelity, the inherent constraints of generative models limit their ability to consistently adhere to physical laws.
Multi-Character Generation: The distribution of our current dataset limits our model's ability to generate and manage interactions among multiple characters.
Integration with Existing Game Engines: Presently, the outputs of our model are not directly compatible with existing game engines.

title: GuidedBench: Measuring and Mitigating the Evaluation Discrepancies of In-the-wild LLM Jailbreak Methods
name: guidedbench-jailbreak-evaluation
short_hypothesis: A novel benchmark, GuidedBench, comprising a curated harmful question dataset and a guideline-based evaluation system (GuidedEval), will offer more accurate, reliable, and reproducible evaluations of LLM jailbreak performance. This is achieved by providing detailed, case-by-case scoring instructions that decompose the evaluation into checking for specific entities and actions, thus reducing ambiguity and dependency on the subjective judgment of evaluator LLMs.
related_work: Existing evaluations of LLM jailbreak methods suffer from significant discrepancies. Most studies rely on inadequate paradigms like keyword detection or general LLM-as-a-judge frameworks. These methods lack case-specific criteria, leading to misjudgments, hindering direct comparisons between jailbreak techniques, and resulting in misleading conclusions about their effectiveness. This leaves open the question of how to evaluate jailbreak effectiveness consistently and accurately.
abstract: Despite the growing interest in jailbreaks as an effective red-teaming tool for building safe and responsible large language models (LLMs), flawed evaluation system designs have led to significant discrepancies in their effectiveness assessments. With a systematic measurement study based on 37 jailbreak studies since 2022, we find that existing evaluation systems lack case-specific criteria, resulting in misleading conclusions about their effectiveness and safety implications. In this paper, we introduce GuidedBench, a novel benchmark comprising a curated harmful question dataset and GuidedEval, an evaluation system integrated with detailed case-by-case evaluation guidelines.

experiments.Description: To systematically measure the effectiveness of representative jailbreak methods using the proposed GuidedBench benchmark and compare its performance against existing evaluation systems.
experiments.Method: Ten jailbreak methods (MultiJail, GPTFuzzer, DRA, PAIR, TAP, DeepInception, GCG, AutoDAN, FSJ, SCAV) will be evaluated against five victim LLMs (GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4-turbo, Claude-3.5-sonnet, Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). The evaluation will be performed by three evaluator LLMs (GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Doubao-v1.5-pro). The proposed GuidedEval system will be compared against five baseline evaluation systems: NegativeKeyword, PositiveKeyword, StrongREJECT, PAIR, and HarmBench.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A generalized Attack Success Rate (ASR) based on the completion rate of guideline-defined scoring points.']

experiments.Description: To quantify how often different evaluation systems incorrectly classify objectively failed jailbreak responses as successful by measuring their false positive rates across several identifiable failure modes.
experiments.Method: A controlled experiment will be conducted on a subset of failed jailbreak responses classified into five failure modes: Inconsistent Content, General Advice, Invalid Rephrasing, Garbled Text, and Misunderstanding. The performance of GuidedEval will be compared against NegativeKeyword, PositiveKeyword, PAIR, HarmBench, and StrongREJECT.

experiments.Description: To assess the stability and dependency of LLM-based evaluation systems on specific judge models by measuring the variance of scores from different evaluators.
experiments.Method: Each jailbreak case will be independently scored by three different evaluator LLMs (GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, Doubao-v1.5-pro). The variance in scores will be calculated for GuidedEval and compared to other LLM-based systems like StrongREJECT, PAIR, and HarmBench.

experiments.Description: To analyze whether multi-value evaluation systems degenerate into binary-like systems or if they effectively utilize the full scoring spectrum to reflect fine-grained differences in attack effectiveness.
experiments.Method: The score distributions of GuidedEval, PAIR, and StrongREJECT will be analyzed.

experiments.Description: To validate the use of LLMs as automated judges by comparing their evaluation judgments against annotations from human experts.
experiments.Method: Two domain experts will independently label 700 randomly sampled jailbreak instances. These human annotations will be compared against the judgments from three LLMs: DeepSeek-v3, GPT-4o, and Doubao.

risk_factors: One potential concern is that our guidelines may miss some relevant but non-essential details.
It is worth noting that human annotations are not without limitations. Even expert annotators do not possess perfect or complete knowledge, particularly in complex or ambiguous cases.
Nonetheless, we recognize that releasing detailed guideline examples may pose higher risks.

title: HypeR: Multitask Hyper-Prompted Training Enables Large-Scale Retrieval Generalization
name: hyper-prompted-multitask-retrieval
short_hypothesis: A hyper-prompted training mechanism (HYPER), which uses a Query-conditional Prompt Synthesizer (QPS) to dynamically generate prompts for different queries and a Contrastive Prompt Regularization (CPR) to prevent mode collapse, will enable a neural retriever to uniformly process queries from different tasks and domains. This will improve generalization for large-scale retrieval, especially in zero-shot and few-shot scenarios.
related_work: Traditional term-matching methods like BM25 generalize across domains but are outperformed by neural retrievers on specific tasks. However, these neural retrievers, both dense (e.g., DPR) and sparse (e.g., SPLADE), suffer from performance degradation on out-of-domain data due to varying query formats and intents across tasks. Existing methods for zero-shot retrieval often rely on unsupervised pre-training or data augmentation, with transfer learning for retrieval being relatively underexplored. This work proposes a dynamic, query-conditional prompt generation mechanism to improve cross-task generalization, differing from prior prompt-tuning methods that typically rely on task-level information rather than query-specific characteristics.
abstract: The performance of neural text retrievers degrades considerably when applied to out-of-domain data. Enabling a retriever to perform more robustly across different domains or tasks and show strong zero-shot transfer ability is critical for building scalable IR systems. To this end, we propose HYPER, a hyper-prompted training mechanism to enable uniform retrieval across tasks of different domains. Our approach jointly trains the query encoder with a shared prompt-based parameter pool and a prompt synthesizer that dynamically composes a hyper-prompt for encoding each query from different tasks or domains. To avoid the mode collapse of prompt attention distribution for different queries, we design a contrastive prompt regularization that promotes the mode of prompt attention to be aligned and uniform. Through multi-task hyper-prompted training, the retriever is intended to master the ability to dynamically represent different types of queries and transfer knowledge across different domains and tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance in a multi-task supervised setting and its zero-shot transfer ability on unseen tasks within the KILT benchmark.
experiments.Method: The HYPER mechanism will be applied to SPLADEv2 and DPR backbones. For multi-task evaluation, the model will be trained on 7 KILT datasets (FEVER, AY2, zsRE, NQ, TQA, HoPo, WoW). For zero-shot evaluation, a leave-one-out setting will be used, where the model is trained on 6 tasks and tested on the held-out one. Performance will be compared against baselines including BM25, DPR, DPR-prefix, SPLADE, and SPLADE-prefix.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the zero-shot generalization ability of the model on a heterogeneous benchmark of diverse, unseen retrieval tasks.
experiments.Method: The SPLADE-HYPER model, pre-trained on KILT, will be directly evaluated on 10 datasets from the BEIR benchmark (TREC-COVID, NFCorpus, ArguAna, Tóuche-2020, FiQA-2018, DBPedia, SCIDOCS, Climate-FEVER). The model will not be tuned on BEIR. Performance will be compared to a variety of methods including BM25, DocT5, ColBERT, DPR, ANCE, GenQ, TAS-B, MODIR, LapraDOR, SPLADE, and SPLADE-Prefix.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to verify the effectiveness of the core components of HYPER: the Query-conditional Prompt Synthesizer (QPS) and the Contrastive Prompt Regularization (CPR).
experiments.Method: Several variants of the SPLADE-HYPER model will be evaluated on the KILT benchmark. These variants will include: 1) replacing the QPS attention module with a fixed uniform distribution, 2) removing the alignment regularization term from CPR, and 3) removing the uniformity regularization term from CPR. The performance of these variants will be compared to the full SPLADE-HYPER model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to adapt to new tasks in a few-shot setting.
experiments.Method: The SPLADE-HYPER model will be continually trained on new tasks from the KILT benchmark (NQ, TQA, FEVER, zsRE) using a small number of training instances (e.g., 192, 384, 576, etc.). Performance will be compared against SPLADE and SPLADE-prefix baselines under the same few-shot conditions.

risk_factors: Using dynamic representations of different documents requires to rebuild index in real-time which causes heavy calculation cost.

title: GIQ: Benchmarking 3D Geometric Reasoning of Vision Foundation Models with Simulated and Real Polyhedra
name: giq-benchmark-geometric-reasoning
short_hypothesis: Modern vision and vision-language foundation models lack a true understanding of geometric properties, which can be systematically revealed by evaluating them on a novel benchmark (GIQ) of diverse polyhedra. These models are hypothesized to struggle with tasks requiring fine-grained geometric reasoning, such as reconstruction, symmetry detection, mental rotation, and classification of complex shapes.
related_work: Existing 3D analysis studies rely on large datasets of synthetic or in-the-wild objects such as OmniObject3D, NAVI, and Google Scanned Objects (GSO). While enabling large-scale analyses, these datasets are not conducive to the careful evaluation of visual reasoning with respect to core 3D object geometry properties like symmetry, convexity, and complexity. Similarly, while prior work has explored symmetry perception and mental rotation tests, existing large-scale 3D datasets typically lack the detailed annotations necessary to evaluate fine-grained geometric reasoning. This paper introduces a targeted polyhedron-based benchmark to provide clearly defined ground truths on symmetry and complexity, enabling precise measurement of geometric understanding in foundation models.
abstract: Modern monocular 3D reconstruction methods and vision-language models (VLMs) demonstrate impressive results on standard benchmarks, yet their true understanding of geometric properties remains unclear. We introduce GIQ, a comprehensive benchmark specifically designed to evaluate the geometric reasoning capabilities of vision and vision-language foundation models. GIQ comprises synthetic and real-world images and corresponding 3D meshes of diverse polyhedra—including Platonic, Archimedean, Johnson, and Catalan solids, as well as stellations and compound shapes—covering varying levels of complexity and symmetry. Through systematic experiments involving monocular 3D reconstruction, 3D symmetry detection, mental rotation tests, and zero-shot shape classification tasks, we propose to reveal potential shortcomings in current models. GIQ is intended to provide a structured platform to benchmark critical gaps in geometric intelligence and facilitate future progress in robust, geometry-aware representation learning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of state-of-the-art monocular 3D reconstruction methods to reconstruct the complete 3D geometry of polyhedral shapes from a single image, using GIQ as an out-of-distribution test set.
experiments.Method: Three pre-trained models (Shap-E, Stable Fast 3D, OpenLRM) will be evaluated zero-shot. The task is to reconstruct representative polyhedra (e.g., cube, great dodecahedron, small cubicuboctahedron) from single synthetic and preprocessed wild images from the GIQ dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative assessment of the reconstructed 3D meshes, visualized from different viewpoints to check for geometric and structural integrity.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the capability of various image encoders to detect specific 3D symmetry elements from single-image inputs.
experiments.Method: The task is to predict the presence of three distinct symmetry elements: central point reflection, 4-fold rotational symmetry, and 5-fold rotational symmetry. A linear probe will be trained on image embeddings from a diverse set of encoders, including supervised (CLIP, DreamSim, SigLip, DeiT III, SAM, ConvNext), self-supervised (DINO, DINOv2, MAE), and multi-view pretrained (VGGT, DUSt3R, MASt3R) models. The probe will be trained on synthetic GIQ images and evaluated on both synthetic and wild images using a 5-fold cross-validation strategy where test shapes are unseen during training.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the spatial reasoning capabilities of foundation image encoders via a Mental Rotation Test (MRT), determining if two images (one synthetic, one real-world) depict the same polyhedral object.
experiments.Method: The task uses a 'hard split' of visually and geometrically similar polyhedra pairs. A non-linear probe will be trained on the absolute difference of image embeddings from two images to classify if they represent the same object. A diverse set of image encoders will be evaluated. A human baseline will also be established through a user study on the same image pairs.

experiments.Description: To conduct a zero-shot polyhedron classification task to evaluate the geometric reasoning capabilities of frontier vision-language models (VLMs) and geometry-native models.
experiments.Method: VLMs (Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro, ChatGPT 03, ChatGPT 04-mini-high) will be queried with images from the GIQ dataset and the prompt 'What is the name of this polyhedron?'. The evaluation will also test Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Multi-View (MV) inputs. The experiment will be extended to geometry-native models (LLaVA-3D, ShapeLLM, PointBind & PointLLM) using image or ground truth point cloud inputs.

risk_factors: While establishing a precise correlation with arbitrary organic shapes requires further empirical study, GIQ isolates necessary foundational skills, paving the way for future rigorous evaluations of both rigid and fluid dynamics.
future training-focused extensions must scale to broader wild distributions to ensure robust generalization.

title: LevAttention: Time, Space and Streaming Efficient Algorithm for Heavy Attentions
name: LevAttention
short_hypothesis: For a large class of attention functions, and for any key matrix K, it is possible to identify a small, query-independent "universal set" of keys U, whose size is independent of the sequence length n. This set contains all keys that will receive a high attention score for any possible query matrix Q. This universal set can be found efficiently using f-sensitivities (leverage scores), enabling a significant reduction in the computational cost of attention by restricting it to this subset of keys.
related_work: Prior work has sought to improve the O(n^2) efficiency of transformer attention layers through various assumptions, such as imposing sparsity constraints, using kernel methods, assuming low-rank structure, or assuming bounded entries. These methods often have limitations or rely on specific data properties. This work proposes an efficient attention mechanism that makes no assumptions on the data, has a workspace that does not grow with the sequence length n, and can be computed in streaming and parallel settings.
abstract: A central problem related to transformers is the quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism with respect to the context length n. For a large class of attention functions, including f(x) = |x|^p, we propose a method to find all "large attention scores" (entries of the attention matrix above a threshold ε) in time with only a linear dependence on n. We leverage tools from randomized numerical linear algebra to prove the existence of a "universal set" U of keys, of a size independent of n, which contains all keys that will receive high attention scores for any query. We propose an algorithm to find this set U in n * poly(d/ε) time. This attention mechanism, which we call LevAttention, is based on leverage scores. Notably, our approach makes no assumptions on the data, has a workspace that does not grow with n, and can be computed in streaming and parallel settings.

experiments.Description: To empirically understand the structure of attention matrices in a typical Vision Transformer (ViT), by analyzing properties like attention weight distribution and locality.
experiments.Method: A pretrained ViT model (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021) with six layers and six attention heads will be used. The model processes inputs tokenized into 197 tokens. The analysis will focus on the 'Top Heaviness' (the sum of the top-32 attention weights for each query) and 'Locality' (the attention mass captured by neighboring tokens).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Distribution of top-32 attention weights', 'Distribution of attention weight captured by local tokens', 'Distribution of attention weight captured by important keys and local tokens']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of using leverage score selection for inference on pretrained softmax ViT models for the downstream task of image classification.
experiments.Method: Pretrained ViT models (S/16, L/16, L/8) will be evaluated on the ImageNet-1k dataset. At each attention head, a subset of keys with the largest l2 leverage scores (e.g., top-32) will be computed. Queries will then attend only to this subset of keys. This 'LevAttention' method will be compared against a baseline of selecting keys based on their squared row norms ('l2 norm selection') and the original full softmax attention model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of models trained from scratch using the leverage score-based attention mechanism (LevAttention).
experiments.Method: ViT models (L/8, L/16) will be trained from scratch on the ImageNet-1k dataset using the LevAttention mechanism. For comparison, models will also be trained using two other selection mechanisms: 'row norm selection' (picking keys with the largest l2 norms) and 'random selection' (picking a fixed set of random key positions). The performance will be compared to these baselines and to the standard softmax attention model.

experiments.Description: To theoretically motivate the approach by formulating a planted model where the proposed algorithms can provably identify relevant keys.
experiments.Method: A planted model of keys and queries is formulated where each query is a noisy linear combination of a small subset of 'relevant' keys. The plan is to show that under this model, the proposed algorithm for finding large attention scores can find all relevant keys for a given query in sublinear time.

risk_factors: We leave it as an open question how to make effective use of leverage score information to train better models.

title: Bayesian Active Learning By Distribution Disagreement
name: BALSA-for-Regression-AL
short_hypothesis: Adapting the BALD algorithm for regression models with full predictive distributions, such as normalizing flows, by directly measuring the disagreement between distributions sampled via MC dropout (using metrics like KL-Divergence or Earth Mover's Distance) will provide a more effective acquisition function for pool-based active learning than standard uncertainty heuristics which cannot distinguish between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty.
related_work: Active learning (AL) for regression is under-researched compared to classification. While models like Normalizing Flows (NFs) can produce full predictive distributions, standard AL heuristics (e.g., Entropy, Least-Confident) are not effective as they fail to distinguish between reducible epistemic uncertainty and irreducible aleatoric uncertainty. The BALD algorithm, which uses MC-Dropout, was designed to isolate epistemic uncertainty. The closest prior work explored MC-dropout with NFs on synthetic data. This research aims to extend these ideas by proposing BALSA, a novel adaptation of BALD for NFs, and benchmarking it on real-world datasets in a pool-based AL setting against other SOTA AL algorithms.
abstract: Active Learning (AL) for regression has been systematically under-researched due to the increased difficulty of measuring uncertainty in regression models. Since normalizing flows offer a full predictive distribution, they facilitate direct usage of known heuristics for AL. However, most of these heuristics do not work well for normalizing flows in pool-based AL because they cannot distinguish between aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. In this work, we propose BALSA, an adaptation of the BALD algorithm, tailored for regression with normalizing flows. This work extends current research on uncertainty quantification with normalizing flows to real-world data and pool-based AL.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed acquisition functions (BALSA) against various baselines in a pool-based active learning setting for regression. The main comparison focuses on a query size of 1.
experiments.Method:
The experiment uses two model architectures, a conditional Normalizing Flow (Neural Spline Flow) and a Gaussian Neural Network (GNN), on four real-world regression datasets: Parkinsons, Supercond., Sarcos, and Diamonds. The proposed methods, BALSA^KL (Grid and Pair versions) and BALSA^EMD, are compared against several baselines: 
1. Standard heuristics: Standard Deviation (Std), Least Confident (LC), Shannon Entropy (Entr).
2. Clustering-based methods: Coreset, CoreGCN, TypiClust.
3. BALD adaptations for regression: BALD^σ, BALD^LC, BALD^H.
4. Other baselines: Random sampling, NFlows Out.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Negative Log Likelihood (NLL) on the test set', 'Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on the test set', 'Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) on the test set', 'Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the learning trajectories', 'Critical Difference (CD) Diagrams for aggregated rank comparison']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies on the proposed BALSA algorithm to understand the impact of specific design choices, including dropout strategy, distribution normalization, and performance at larger query sizes.
experiments.Method:
1. **Dual Mode Dropout:** Test BALSA variants by using an optimal dropout rate during training and a different, static rate during the evaluation/acquisition phase.
2. **Re-normalization:** Test a version of BALSA^KL_Grid where the averaged likelihood vector is explicitly re-normalized to form a proper probability distribution.
3. **Query Sizes:** Evaluate the performance of the proposed uncertainty sampling methods (BALSA, BALD variants) against clustering methods (Coreset, TypiClust) using larger query sizes of τ = {50, 200}.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Negative Log Likelihood (NLL) on the test set', 'Critical Difference (CD) Diagrams for aggregated rank comparison']

risk_factors: Finding the right (mix of) metrics to evaluate our models remains a challenging task, as every chosen measure inevitably introduces a bias into the evaluation.
The uncertainty sampling methods are behaving as expected, gradually losing their advantage over random sampling with increasing query size, as they suffer from missing diversity sampling components.
We defer studies of the resulting algorithms [with Langevin Dynamics or SVGD] to future work.
We focus first on the most natural application of MC dropout for normalizing flows and defer the other versions to future work.
This work is but the first step to understanding the dynamics of AL for regression models with uncertainty quantification.

title: Visual Planning: Let's Think Only with Images
name: visual-planning-with-rl
short_hypothesis: Language may not be the most natural or effective modality for reasoning in 'vision-first' tasks involving spatial and geometrical information. We propose a new paradigm, Visual Planning, which enables planning through purely visual representations. In this paradigm, planning is executed via sequences of images that encode step-by-step inference in the visual domain. We introduce a novel two-stage reinforcement learning framework, Visual Planning via Reinforcement Learning (VPRL), empowered by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), to post-train large vision models for this task.
related_work: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) predominantly rely on text for reasoning, even when visual information is present. They typically ground visual content into a textual format before performing inference, which can be ineffective for tasks heavy on spatial or geometric dynamics, creating a 'modality gap'. While some recent works incorporate visual aids like sketches or per-step visualizations, they remain fundamentally text-driven, with reasoning still embedded in verbal traces. A truly visual-only reasoning paradigm that avoids any text-based proxies remains underexplored.
abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) and their multimodal extensions (MLLMs) have substantially enhanced machine reasoning across diverse tasks. However, these models predominantly rely on pure text as the medium for both expressing and structuring reasoning, even when visual information is present. In this work, we argue that language may not always be the most natural or effective modality for reasoning, particularly in tasks involving spatial and geometrical information. Motivated by this, we propose a new paradigm, Visual Planning, which enables planning through purely visual representations for these "vision-first'' tasks, as a supplementary channel to language-based reasoning. In this paradigm, planning is executed via sequences of images that encode step-by-step inference in the visual domain, akin to how humans sketch or visualize future actions. We introduce a novel reinforcement learning framework, Visual Planning via Reinforcement Learning (VPRL), empowered by GRPO for post-training large vision models, for planning in a selection of representative visual navigation tasks.

experiments.Description: To validate the feasibility of the Visual Planning paradigm on spatial planning tasks and compare its performance against traditional language-based reasoning approaches.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on three grid-based visual navigation environments: FROZENLAKE, MAZE, and MINIBEHAVIOR. The proposed method, Visual Planning via Reinforcement Learning (VPRL), involves a two-stage training process on a Large Vision Model (LVM-7B): 1) Policy initialization via supervised learning on random trajectories, and 2) RL optimization using GRPO with a progress-based reward function. This will be compared against several baselines: a supervised visual planning model (VPFT), and language-based planning using multimodal models (Qwen 2.5-VL-Instruct, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Pro) with various prompting strategies (Direct, Chain-of-Thought) and fine-tuning (SFT).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics:
1. Exact Match (EM): To evaluate whether the predicted sequence of visual states exactly matches an entire optimal trajectory of minimal length.
2. Progress Rate (PR): To measure the ratio of consecutive correct steps from the start of the trajectory that align with at least one optimal path, capturing the model's ability to make meaningful progress.

risk_factors: In this work, we focus exclusively on Large Vision Model (LVM) to investigate visual planning capabilities by eliminating language as a confounding factor for research purposes. As such, this choice constraints the model size to 7B as the only available size of LVM, and excludes recently released native multimodal models capable of generating multimodal outputs.
Additionally, explicitly generating images introduces computational overhead during inference compared to a textual response.

Another limitation in this work lies in the implementation of dynamics interpreter. For simplicity, we adopt the rule-based approach that compares pixel-wise features between the current state and the previous state.

title: FreeLM: Fine-Tuning-Free Language Model
name: freelm
short_hypothesis: A novel fine-tuning-free strategy for language models, which considers both language signal and a unified 'teacher signal', can produce a reasonably sized model capable of generalizing to a wide range of tasks without requiring task-specific fine-tuning. This is achieved by iteratively training the model on both raw language data and a unified dataset of tasks formatted as 'proposition correctness judgments'.
related_work: Pre-trained language models (PLMs) like the GPT series follow a pre-training then fine-tuning paradigm, which is costly for deployment and training. Zero-shot and few-shot approaches have unsatisfactory performance, particularly on understanding tasks, because PLMs are trained only with a language signal that is not task-aware. Instruction-tuning models like InstructGPT and FLAN improve zero-shot performance by making the language signal more task-aware, but adaptation is still necessary for best performance. The research gap is the need for a method to create a reasonably sized model that can achieve good performance on a range of pre-defined tasks with low training and deployment costs, eliminating the need for fine-tuning.
abstract: Pre-trained language models (PLMs) have achieved remarkable success in NLP tasks, but the mainstream pre-training then fine-tuning paradigm incurs high deployment costs and low training efficiency. Fine-tuning is essential because PLMs are pre-trained only with a language signal from raw data. We propose a novel fine-tuning-free strategy for language models that considers both language signal and a teacher signal. The teacher signal is an abstraction of a battery of downstream tasks, provided in a unified proposition format. By training with both language and strong task-aware teacher signals in an interactive manner, the proposed FreeLM model is designed to demonstrate strong generalization and robustness.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the language understanding ability of FreeLM in a fine-tuning-free scenario against strong baselines.
experiments.Method: FreeLM (a 0.3B parameter model based on GPT-2) will be evaluated on a subset of the GLUE benchmark (CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, QQP, MNLI, QNLI, RTE). The evaluation will be performed in a zero-shot setting. Baselines for comparison will include GPT-2 (zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuned), 175B GPT-3 (zero-shot and few-shot), and InstructGPT (zero-shot and few-shot).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Matthew's Correlation (CoLA)", 'Accuracy (SST-2, MNLI, QNLI, RTE)', 'F1/Accuracy (MRPC, QQP)', 'Average score across tasks']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the language generation performance of FreeLM to assess if it retains this capability after the proposed training.
experiments.Method: FreeLM will be evaluated against the baseline GPT-2 model. The evaluation will be conducted on three public datasets: LAMBADA, WikiText2, and PTB.

experiments.Description: To analyze the impact of the iterative training strategy by removing the language signal objective.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted by training a version of FreeLM without the language signal objective ('w.o. L.S.'). This model's performance will be compared to the standard FreeLM on the GLUE benchmark tasks. The training loss curves will also be compared.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['GLUE benchmark metrics (Mat. Corr, Acc, F1)', 'Proposition loss during training']

experiments.Description: To study the effect of the task prefix in the unified proposition format.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted by training a version of FreeLM without the task prefix in the input format ('w.o. prefix'). This model will be compared against the standard FreeLM on the GLUE benchmark tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization ability of FreeLM on unseen tasks not included in the unified training data.
experiments.Method: A new model, FreeLM_U, will be trained by removing four datasets (MRPC, RTE, COPA, and CB) from the unified training data. This model's performance on these four unseen tasks will be compared to the standard FreeLM and a fine-tuned GPT-2 baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['F1/Accuracy (MRPC)', 'Accuracy (RTE, COPA, CB)', 'Average score across unseen tasks']

experiments.Description: To qualitatively evaluate the generation ability, specifically text consistency, via a case study on a story cloze task.
experiments.Method: FreeLM and GPT-2 will generate text continuations for input prompts from the StoryCloze dataset. To ensure a fair comparison, a new FreeLM model will be trained with the StoryCloze dataset removed from its unified data. The generated text will be manually inspected.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Manual inspection for readability, grammatical correctness, and context consistency/logical association.']

risk_factors: There are studies suggesting that PPL does not fully reflect the generation ability of language models Wang et al. (2022). Nevertheless, there are no good alternative metrics.
We believe that when the size of FreeLM exceeds 1B, task prefix will have little effect.

title: L0-Sparse Canonical Correlation Analysis
name: l0-sparse-canonical-correlation-analysis
short_hypothesis: A method, l0-CCA, can learn correlated representations based on sparse subsets of variables from two modalities by multiplying the input variables with stochastic gates. The parameters of these gates can be learned jointly with CCA weights via an l0-regularized correlation loss. This approach can be extended to a non-linear version, l0-Deep CCA, by modeling the correlated representations using deep neural networks.
related_work: Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) is a classical method for finding maximally correlated transformations but can overfit when the number of variables (D) exceeds the number of samples (N). Sparse CCA (SCCA) methods address this by seeking transformations based on a sparse subset of variables. Existing SCCA methods often use l1 regularization, which can lead to shrinkage of the canonical vectors, or greedy l0 regularization, which may yield suboptimal solutions. Non-linear extensions like Kernel CCA and Deep CCA exist, but there has been limited work on extending these models to be both sparse and non-linear in a scalable way.
abstract: Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) models are powerful for studying the associations between two sets of variables. Despite their success, CCA models may break (or overfit) if the number of variables in either of the modalities exceeds the number of samples. Moreover, often a significant fraction of the variables measures modality-specific information, and thus removing them is beneficial for identifying the canonically correlated variates. Here, we propose l0-CCA, a method for learning correlated representations based on sparse subsets of variables from two observed modalities. Sparsity is obtained by multiplying the input variables by stochastic gates, whose parameters are learned together with the CCA weights via an l0-regularized correlation loss. We further propose l0-Deep CCA for solving the problem of non-linear sparse CCA by modeling the correlated representations using deep nets.

experiments.Description: To validate the effectiveness of the proposed l0-CCA in correctly identifying sparse canonical vectors from synthetic data, particularly in the challenging regime where the number of samples is less than the number of features (N < D).
experiments.Method: Data is generated following the procedure from Suo et al. (2017), considering three covariance matrix models: Identity, Toeplitz, and Sparse inverse. The proposed l0-CCA will be compared against other sparse CCA schemes: PMA, IP-SCCA, SCCA-I, SCCA-II, and mod-SCCA.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Estimation error of the canonical vectors (e_φ, e_η)']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate that l0-CCA can identify common information from high-dimensional data and embed it into a correlated, low-dimensional representation, using a real-world video dataset.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a coupled video dataset of spinning puppets, where each view is a high-dimensional image (240x320 pixels). The proposed l0-CCA scheme will be applied to identify a sparse subset of pixels that are correlated between the two video views and learn a 2-dimensional embedding.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Total correlation of the embedding', 'Visualization of active gates', 'Visualization of the resulting embedding']

experiments.Description: To evaluate if l0-DCCA can find meaningful representations of data in a noisy regime using the Noisy MNIST dataset.
experiments.Method: Two noisy variants of MNIST are used as coupled views. l0-DCCA will be trained to embed the data into a 10-dimensional space while selecting subsets of input pixels. The performance will be compared to baselines including Raw Data, PCA, CCA, mod-SCCA, SCCA-HSIC, KCCA, grad-KCCA, multiview-ICA, NCCA, DCCA, and DCCAE.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's ability to find meaningful representations for seismic event classification.
experiments.Method: The dataset consists of seismic events recorded on two directional channels (East and North), represented as sonograms. l0-DCCA will be used to embed the data into 3 dimensions. The performance will be compared against the same set of baselines as the Noisy MNIST experiment.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if l0-DCCA can find correlated representations from multi-modal biomedical data to improve cancer sub-type classification.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses multi-modal data from the METABRIC dataset, specifically RNA gene expression and Copy Number Alteration (CNA) data, which have very high dimensions. l0-DCCA will be used to compute a 10-dimensional embedding. The performance will be compared against the same set of baselines as the Noisy MNIST and Seismic experiments.

risk_factors: The proposed l0-CCA problem albeit not being convex leads to an empirically stable solution across different settings.
Nonetheless, the method has some limitations, specifically, tuning λ requires a cross validation procedure, which could be costly in high dimensional regime.
Another caveat of the existing approach is that it lacks guarantees when trained on small batches.

title: Arbitrary-Order Block SignSGD for Memory-Efficient LLM Fine-Tuning
name: absignsgd-for-llm-finetuning
short_hypothesis: A block-coordinate variant of SignSGD, named ABSignSGD, which enables arbitrary-order block updates, can deliver substantial memory and runtime savings for full-parameter LLM fine-tuning while preserving competitive convergence and downstream performance.
related_work: Existing methods for memory-efficient LLM fine-tuning fall into three categories. 1) Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA train only a small set of parameters but usually yield lower performance than full-parameter training. 2) Low-rank projection methods for full-parameter training (e.g., GaLore) project gradients into a low-rank subspace but can suffer from performance gaps and slow runtime. 3) Block-wise optimization methods like BAdam update only a subset of parameters per iteration, but their reliance on optimizer states (e.g., momentum in Adam) conflicts with block switching, requiring frequent state resets that can degrade convergence. The proposed work aims to combine the efficiency of block-wise and sign-based updates without the performance degradation associated with existing methods.
abstract: We propose ABSignSGD, a block-coordinate variant of sign-based descent with flexible block selection that enables memory- and runtime-efficient full-parameter fine-tuning of large language models. We present a unified convergence analysis under mild conditions, covering both the base method and a majority-vote extension for distributed training. The latter improves communication efficiency by aggregating only gradient signs rather than averaging full gradients.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of ABSignSGD for mathematical reasoning tasks by fine-tuning an LLM and evaluating it on a suite of math benchmarks.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune the Qwen3-8B model on the OpenMathInstruct-2 dataset. The proposed method, ABSignSGD, will be compared against several baseline memory-efficient optimizers: LoRA, GaLore, Apollo, and BAdam.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the math-evaluation-harness, which includes ASDiv, GSM-hard, MATH-OAI, MAWPS, Minerva-Math, Svamp, and Tabmwp benchmarks.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of ABSignSGD for general instruction-following tasks.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune the Qwen3-8B model on the Stanford-Alpaca dataset. The proposed method, ABSignSGD, will be compared against the same baselines: LoRA, GaLore, Apollo, and BAdam.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['MT-Bench score, using GPT-5 as a judge via FastChat.']

experiments.Description: To measure the memory footprint and runtime efficiency of ABSignSGD against baseline optimizers.
experiments.Method: During the fine-tuning of Qwen3-8B on OpenMathInstruct-2, the peak reserved GPU memory and total wall-clock runtime will be recorded for ABSignSGD and the baselines (LoRA, GaLore, BAdam, Apollo).

experiments.Description: To assess the convergence speed of ABSignSGD compared to baselines during training.
experiments.Method: During the fine-tuning of Qwen3-8B on OpenMathInstruct-2, the training loss will be plotted against the number of training tokens and wall-clock time for ABSignSGD and baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training loss vs. training token count', 'Training loss vs. wall-clock time']

experiments.Description: To validate the scalability and generalizability of the proposed method on different model sizes and families.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune the larger Qwen3-32B model on the OpenMathInstruct-2 task. Additionally, fine-tune the Llama3-8B model on both OpenMathInstruct-2 and Stanford-Alpaca tasks.

experiments.Description: To analyze the scaling behavior and robustness of the distributed variant, ABSignSGD-MV.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune Qwen3-8B on OpenMathInstruct-2 using ABSignSGD-MV, varying the number of agents from 1 to 32 while keeping the local batch size fixed.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study justifying the choice of SignSGD as the base optimizer for the block-coordinate framework.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune a smaller Qwen3-1.7B model on OpenMathInstruct-2. Compare the performance of block-coordinate versions of SGD (BlockSGD), SignSGD (ABSignSGD), and Adam (BAdam), alongside their non-block-coordinate base optimizers (SGD, SignSGD, Adam).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training loss vs. training token count', 'Training loss vs. wall-clock time']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the impact of different block selection strategies on convergence and performance.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune a smaller Qwen3-1.7B model on OpenMathInstruct-2 using ABSignSGD with various update rules: depth-biased (DB-c), deep-to-shallow (DS-K), and uniform random selection (UR).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training loss vs. training token count', 'Downstream generalization performance on math benchmarks.']

risk_factors: Convergence of sign-based updates requires the sign-agreement probability to exceed 0.5. Prior work Safaryan & Richtárik (2021) shows divergence on toy problems when this condition fails, so such methods may underperform under certain regimes, e.g. with extremely small batch sizes.
ABSignSGD inherently applies extreme gradient quantization by reducing each gradient coordinate to a single bit, making any additional gradient quantization redundant.
Similarly, offloading of weights or activations is compatible, though optimizer-state offloading is irrelevant due to ABSignSGD's statelessness.

We also hypothesize that this may help mitigate catastrophic forgetting, as shallower layers tend to encode more general features (Howard & Ruder, 2018). We leave a thorough empirical validation of this effect to future work.

title: PREDICTION OF TOURISM FLOW WITH SPARSE DATA INCORPORATING TOURIST GEOLOCATIONS
name: tourism-flow-prediction-with-sparse-data-and-geolocations
short_hypothesis: Extending granular but limited tourism data with exogenous sources like individual tourist geolocation trajectories, weather, and holidays will increase the accuracy of visitor flow predictions. Modern deep learning models, particularly Graph Neural Networks, are capable of incorporating this sparse, heterogeneous data, including mapping geolocation data onto discrete Points of Interest (POI).
related_work: Prior work on tourism demand forecasting has utilized models like Recurrent Neural Networks (LSTMs) and Hidden Markov Models. However, these studies often use a limited set of models and focus on long-term, coarse-grained predictions (e.g., monthly, quarterly, or daily visitor numbers for large regions). There is a research gap in performing granular, hourly predictions for specific Points of Interest (POIs), which is critical for real-time tourism flow control, especially when dealing with sparse datasets.
abstract: A proper and accurate prediction of tourism volume and flow is critical for managing space-limited regions like historical city centers to prevent overcrowding. However, static flow control methods are insufficient. This paper proposes to empirically evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art deep-learning methods (RNNs, GNNs, Transformers) and the classic ARIMA method for this task. The core idea is to extend the granular but limited data from a tourism region with exogenous data, such as geolocation trajectories of individual tourists, weather, and holidays. This approach aims to increase prediction accuracy by incorporating modern input feature handling and mapping sparse geolocation data onto discrete POI data.

experiments.Description: To perform a comprehensive comparison of various deep learning (DL) models against a traditional statistical technique (ARIMA) for hourly, per-POI tourist flow prediction using a real-world dataset with limited historical entries.
experiments.Method: The models will be trained on the "Salzburg Card" dataset from 2017-2019, aggregated hourly.
