This time-series data of visitor counts per POI will be augmented with external features like weather and holiday information. The evaluated DL models include a large set of RNN variations (vanilla-RNN, LSTM, phased-LSTM, GRU-D, CT-RNN, CT-LSTM, Neural-ODE), ANODE, and a Transformer model. The performance will be compared against a non-seasonal ARIMA model as a baseline.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of incorporating sparse geolocation data from mobile phones for tourism flow prediction, specifically testing if a Graph Neural Network (GNN) model can utilize this additional spatial information.
experiments.Method: This experiment uses data from 2019-2021, which includes sparse geolocation data from a third-party service in addition to the "Salzburg Card" visitor counts and other external features. The geolocation data is mapped to an OpenStreetMap (OSM) graph. An Attention Temporal Graph Convolutional Network (A3T-GCN) is applied to utilize this graph-structured data. Its performance is compared against other DL models (various RNNs, Transformer) trained on the same data to assess the utility of the GNN approach for handling sparse spatial inputs.

risk_factors: The question on how to improve awareness of data shared by such apps or services is not answered in this research.
The real-world dataset is considered small because of limited historical entries.

Admittedly, the ARIMA method is most suitable for predicting univariate data which is why our classical methods should be extended by also considering methods for multivariate forecasting such as Vector autoregression (VAR).
The tracking data used for the second set of experiments can not be published due to copyright.
no explicit Hyperparameter tuning was conducted which means that the GNN prediction accuracy could be further approved upon.

title: Towards Policy-Aware World Models
name: esnr_for_policy_aware_world_models
short_hypothesis: The Expected Signal-to-Noise Ratio (ESNR) of policy gradients can serve as a reliable and efficient training-time metric to predict the downstream policy performance of a world model, without needing to fully train a policy.
related_work: Large pretrained world models are increasingly used in robotics, but evaluating their quality for downstream control tasks is a major bottleneck. The standard procedure involves fully training a policy to convergence, which is slow and computationally expensive. Prior work has sought to understand what makes a good world model from a representation learning perspective or has used ESNR in more limited contexts. However, a single, practical metric for predicting downstream policy performance across diverse world model architectures and policy extraction methods has been missing. This work proposes ESNR as a general-purpose diagnostic tool to fill this gap.
abstract: It is unclear what makes a good world model for downstream policy learning. Standard metrics like world model prediction loss are often uncorrelated with final policy performance, forcing practitioners into slow and costly cycles of training and evaluation. This work proposes using the expected signal-to-noise ratio (ESNR) of policy gradients as a reliable, training-time metric for downstream policy performance. ESNR is intended to measure a world model's 'policy awareness'—how well a policy can learn from it. The proposed method allows for understanding when a model is sufficiently trained, how architectural changes affect performance, and which policy learning method is best for a given model. A key advantage is that ESNR can be computed on-the-fly with minimal overhead and without a trained policy.

experiments.Description: Investigate whether ESNR is a better predictor of downstream policy performance compared to standard metrics like prediction loss and LPIPS.
experiments.Method: For prediction loss comparison, TD-MPC2 and a variant (TD-MPC2 basic) will be trained on MetaWorld tasks. For LPIPS comparison, DINO-WM and VJEPA2 will be evaluated on tabletop manipulation tasks. The correlation between each metric (prediction loss, LPIPS, ESNR) and final policy performance will be analyzed.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['ESNR', 'Prediction Loss', 'LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity)', 'Downstream policy performance']

experiments.Description: Determine if ESNR can identify the best policy extraction method for a given world model.
experiments.Method: Using a fixed, pretrained TD-MPC2 world model, three different policy extraction methods will be evaluated: online planning with Cross-Entropy Method (MPC), First-Order Gradients (FoG), and Zeroth-Order Gradients (ZoG). The evaluation will be conducted on tasks from DMControl and Meta-World.

experiments.Description: Assess if ESNR can be used to rank different world model architectures based on their expected downstream performance.
experiments.Method: Three world model architectures (TD-MPC2 basic, TD-MPC2, DreamerV3) will be trained and evaluated on tasks from DMControl and Meta-World. ESNR will be calculated for each architecture and compared against the final policy performance.

experiments.Description: Characterize the behavior of ESNR throughout the world model training process to see if it can signal 'training readiness'.
experiments.Method: World models (TD-MPC2, DreamerV3) will be pretrained on MetaWorld tasks, with checkpoints saved at regular intervals. For each checkpoint, both ESNR and the performance of a policy extracted from it will be measured to observe their relationship over the course of training.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the utility of ESNR as a performance indicator for large, pretrained, vision-based world models.
experiments.Method: Four large pretrained models (ResNet, R3M, DINO-WM, VJEPA2) will be finetuned on an offline dataset for a rope manipulation task (SoftGym) and three manipulation tasks from the ManiSkill benchmark (PickCube-v1, PushCube-v1, PokeCube-v1). Policies will be extracted using online planning (MPC with CEM).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['ESNR', 'Policy performance (inverse Chamfer Distance or inverse accumulated state difference)', 'Wall-clock time for evaluation']

risk_factors: ESNR is not meaningful when the underlying world model is fundamentally inaccurate, as biased gradients can yield deceptively high ESNR values.
This limits its utility in the very early stages of training or when modeling assumptions are severely violated.
Furthermore, we have not yet evaluated ESNR extensively on large-scale world model tasks with billions of parameters.

title: Single-Cell Capsule Attention : an interpretable method of cell type classification for single-cell RNA-sequencing data
name: single-cell-capsule-attention
short_hypothesis: A neural network model, scCA, that uses capsules for feature extraction, an LSTM to generate compound features, and an attention mechanism can achieve high-accuracy cell type classification for single-cell RNA-seq data while also providing interpretability. This interpretability is expected to manifest as distinct, shared patterns in capsule relationships and attention weights among cells of the same type.
related_work: Previous methods for cell type classification are categorized into two groups. The first are methods based on statistical hypotheses of gene expression (e.g., Moana, Garnett, CellAssign, SCINA, scPred), which rely on prior knowledge of marker genes or distribution assumptions that may not be accurate. The second are deep neural network (DNN) based methods (e.g., ACTINN, scCapsNet), which are fast and robust but lack interpretability, making it hard to explain their results. This work aims to create a method that is both accurate and interpretable.
abstract: Single-cell RNA-sequencing can obtain genes' expression level of every cell, which is useful for exploring cellular heterogeneity. Previous methods for cell type classification are either based on statistical hypotheses of gene expression or deep neural networks. However, these hypotheses may not reflect the true expression level, and deep neural networks often lack interpretation. This paper presents an interpretable neural-network based method, single-cell capsule attention (scCA), which aims to assign cells to different cell types based on their different feature patterns. In the proposed model, we first generate capsules to extract different features from the cells. Then, we obtain compound features which combine a set of features' information through an LSTM model. Finally, we propose to train attention weights and apply them to the compound features. The scCA model is designed to provide a strong interpretation for the cell type classification result, with the hypothesis that cells from the same cell type will share similar patterns of capsules' relationships and similar distributions of attention weights.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the classification performance of the proposed scCA model against various standard machine learning methods.
experiments.Method: The scCA model will be trained and tested on nine single-cell RNA-seq datasets: CRC, GSE70580, GSE72056, GSE75688, GSE96993, NSCLC, PBMC, Spleen human, and Spleen mouse. The performance will be compared against several baseline methods: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Decision Tree (DT), and Random Forest (RF).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the classification performance of the proposed scCA model against other neural network-based methods.
experiments.Method: The scCA model will be trained and tested on the same nine single-cell RNA-seq datasets. The performance will be compared against several neural network baselines: scCapsNet, a fully-connected network, and a denoising autoencoder.

experiments.Description: To test the influence of different model hyperparameters on scCA's performance, specifically the number of capsules and the dimension of compound features, using a 128-dimension capsule.
experiments.Method: The scCA model will be evaluated on all nine datasets under different parameter combinations: {8 capsules + 16-dim compound features}, {16 capsules + 16-dim compound features}, {8 capsules + 32-dim compound features}, and {16 capsules + 32-dim compound features}. The capsule dimension is fixed at 128.

experiments.Description: To test the influence of different model hyperparameters on scCA's performance, using a smaller 64-dimension capsule.
experiments.Method: The scCA model will be evaluated on all nine datasets under different parameter combinations: {8 capsules + 16-dim compound features}, {16 capsules + 16-dim compound features}, {8 capsules + 32-dim compound features}, and {16 capsules + 32-dim compound features}. The capsule dimension is fixed at 64.

title: An Efficient Multi-Task Transformer for 3D Face Alignment
name: trans3dhead-multitask-transformer-3d-head-alignment
short_hypothesis: A multi-task transformer framework, named Trans3DHead, with two parallel branches for 2D landmark detection and 3DMM parameter regression, will improve 3D head alignment performance and efficiency. This is achieved by: 1) enhancing information communication via self-attention and cross-attention; 2) using a novel Query-aware Memory (QAMem) module to improve accuracy on low-resolution feature maps by assigning separate memory values to each query; 3) introducing a Multi-layer Additive Residual Regression (MARR) module to improve robustness by regressing residuals from an average face model; and 4) adding an Euler Angles Loss to the loss function to better handle atypical head poses.
related_work: Previous methods for 3D head alignment are mainly based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and can be categorized into direct landmark coordinate regression or 3D Morphable Model (3DMM) parameter regression. These methods face several drawbacks: (1) traditional CNNs limit the discriminability of predictions due to a lack of information communication among different vertices or 3DMM parameters; (2) reliance on high-resolution feature maps makes these algorithms memory-consuming and inefficient; and (3) the rich annotation information available in modern datasets is not fully exploited, which reduces the model's robustness to atypical scenes and head poses.
abstract: In the research of 3D head alignment, few prior works focus on information exchange among different vertices or 3DMM parameters in regression. On the other hand, there is a drawback that using high-resolution feature maps makes algorithms memory-consuming and not efficient. To solve these issues, we first propose a multi-task model equipped with two transformer-based branches which further enhances the information communication among different elements through self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms. To solve the problem of low efficiency of high-resolution feature maps and improve the accuracy of facial landmark detection, a lightweight module named query-aware memory (QAMem) is designed to enhance the discriminative ability of queries on low-resolution feature maps by assigning separate memory values to each query rather than a shared one. To further improve the robustness of the predicted landmarks, we introduce a multi-layer additive residual regression (MARR) module that can provide a more stable and reliable reference based on the average face model. Furthermore, the multi-information loss function with Euler Angles Loss is proposed to supervise the network with more effective information, making the model more robust to handle the case of atypical head poses.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed model on the 3D dense head alignment task.
experiments.Method: The proposed Trans3DHead model will be compared with advanced 3DMM-based methods: RingNet, 3DDFA-V2, and DAD-3DNet. The evaluation will be conducted on the full test dataset of DAD-3DHeads.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['NME (Normalized Mean Error)', 'Z5 Accuracy', 'Chamfer Distance', 'Pose Error', 'FPS (Frames Per Second)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed model on the 3D head pose estimation task.
experiments.Method: The proposed Trans3DHead model will be compared with 10 advanced methods: Dlib, FAN, 3DDFA, RingNet, HopeNet, RetinaFace, 3DDFA-V2, Img2Pose, SynergyNet, and DAD-3DNet. The evaluation will be conducted on the AFLW2000-3D dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['MAE (Mean Absolute Error) of predicted Euler angles (Pitch, Roll, Yaw)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to evaluate the effectiveness of each proposed component: Query-aware Memory (QAMem), Euler Angles Loss, and Multi-layer Additive Residual Regression (MARR).
experiments.Method: Each component will be added one by one to a baseline model (the proposed architecture without these components) and evaluated on the validation set of the DAD-3DHeads dataset.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the number of encoder and decoder layers on model performance.
experiments.Method: The baseline model with QAMem will be evaluated with different numbers of encoder and decoder layers on the validation set of DAD-3DHeads.

risk_factors: It can be seen that both DAD-3DNet and our Trans3DHead are limited to severe occlusions, near horizontal or vertical flips, and the back side of the head with unseen facial features.

title: OGBench: Benchmarking Offline Goal-Conditioned RL
name: ogbench
short_hypothesis: Existing benchmarks are insufficient for systematically evaluating offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) algorithms. A new, comprehensive benchmark, OGBench, with carefully designed environments and datasets, is proposed to directly probe and assess diverse capabilities such as stitching, long-horizon reasoning, and handling stochasticity, which will provide a stronger foundation for building new algorithms.
related_work: Prior works in offline goal-conditioned RL (GCRL) lack a standard benchmark for systematic evaluation. They have typically used either existing datasets from standard offline RL (e.g., D4RL) which are not designed for GCRL and lead to limited evaluation, relatively simple goal-conditioned tasks (e.g., Fetch) that do not test complex behaviors, or bespoke tasks tailored to a specific method's abilities. This fragmented evaluation makes it difficult to compare algorithms comprehensively across different capabilities like goal stitching, long-horizon reasoning, or handling stochasticity.
abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) is a major problem in reinforcement learning (RL) because it provides a simple, unsupervised, and domain-agnostic way to acquire diverse behaviors and representations from unlabeled data without rewards. Despite the importance of this setting, we lack a standard benchmark that can systematically evaluate the capabilities of offline GCRL algorithms. In this work, we propose OGBench, a new, high-quality benchmark for algorithms research in offline goal-conditioned RL. OGBench consists of 8 types of environments, 85 datasets, and reference implementations of 6 representative offline GCRL algorithms. We have designed these challenging and realistic environments and datasets to directly probe different capabilities of algorithms, such as stitching, long-horizon reasoning, and the ability to handle high-dimensional inputs and stochasticity.

experiments.Description: To benchmark a set of representative offline GCRL algorithms on the proposed OGBench suite to systematically assess and compare their performance across a wide range of challenging tasks designed to probe specific capabilities.
experiments.Method: The experiment will evaluate 6 representative offline GCRL algorithms (GCBC, GCIVL, GCIQL, QRL, CRL, and HIQL) on the OGBench benchmark. The benchmark includes 8 environment types: Locomotion (PointMaze, AntMaze, HumanoidMaze, AntSoccer), Manipulation (Cube, Scene, Puzzle), and Drawing (Powderworld). These environments come with 85 distinct datasets designed to test specific challenges, such as 'navigate', 'stitch', and 'explore' datasets for locomotion, and 'play' and 'noisy' datasets for manipulation. Tasks will be evaluated using both state-based and pixel-based observations.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average success rate across five pre-defined state-goal pairs for each task.']

risk_factors: all OGBench tasks assume that the environment dynamics remain the same between the training and evaluation environments.

although several OGBench tasks (e.g., Cube, Puzzle, and Powderworld) require unseen goal generalization to some degree, our tasks do not specifically test visual generalization to entirely new objects.

we have made several trade-offs to reduce computational cost and to focus the benchmark on algorithms research at the expense of sacrificing realism to some degree (e.g., the use of the transparent arm in manipulation environments, the use of synthetic (yet fully controllable) datasets, etc.).

title: Computer Agent Arena: Toward Human-Centric Evaluation and Analysis of Computer-Use Agents
name: computer-agent-arena
short_hypothesis: Current static benchmarks for Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) are narrow, contamination-prone, and diverge from real-world use. A dynamic, open-source platform for head-to-head CUA evaluation that converts human preferences into structured feedback in realistic, cloud-hosted environments will enable a more authentic, fair, and human-centric assessment of agent capabilities.
related_work: Current CUA evaluation relies on static online and offline benchmarks like OSWorld, WebArena, and WebVoyager. These benchmarks feature manually designed tasks and reward functions but are limited in domain, vulnerable to contamination, and lack real-world dynamism and personalization. While human preference evaluation platforms like Chatbot Arena exist for language models, they focus on isolated interactions and are not grounded in users' real, interactive computer environments. There is a gap for a platform that combines the realism of computer-use tasks with scalable, human-centric preference evaluation.
abstract: As Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) proliferate and grow increasingly capable, evaluation has become more challenging: static, manually curated benchmarks are narrow in domain, contamination-prone, and environment-heavy, and they diverge substantially from user-driven, real-world evaluation. We present COMPUTER AGENT ARENA, an open-source platform for head-to-head CUA evaluation and a dynamic methodology that converts human preferences into structured feedback in realistic environments. The system (i) simulates real-world computer use via cloud-hosted, diverse, and dynamic environment initializations and customizations; (ii) ensures authentic, fair comparison by faithfully reproducing open-source CUAs and executing anonymously in matched, controlled environments; and (iii) extends evaluation beyond pairwise preference and correctness to capability- and behavior-oriented signals. We open source the full platform, collected dataset, and code of COMPUTER AGENT ARENA to support future research on the evaluation and development of CUA.

experiments.Description: To evaluate and rank a diverse set of Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) through head-to-head comparisons on authentic, user-defined computer tasks, based on human preference.
experiments.Method: Two anonymized CUAs will be sampled to execute a user-proposed instruction in parallel within identical, cloud-hosted VM environments (Windows/Ubuntu). These environments are configured with diverse initial setups, including pre-opened apps, web pages, and files, and support user-defined customizations. The evaluation will involve 12 CUAs, categorized into: (i) Proprietary agents (e.g., Claude Sonnet, UI-TARS-1.5, Operator), (ii) Open-source CUAs (e.g., OpenCUA-32B, Qwen 2.5 VL 72B, CoAct-1), and (iii) General foundation models (e.g., GPT-5, GPT-4.1, o4-mini, Gemini 2.5 Pro). Human evaluators, sourced from the public and the Prolific platform, will observe the side-by-side execution trajectories and provide feedback.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Pairwise preference votes (A is better, B is better, or Tie)', 'Structured labels for correctness, safety, and efficiency', 'An Elo ranking system derived from the Bradley-Terry model to aggregate votes into a global leaderboard', '95% confidence intervals for Elo scores, computed via bootstrap']

experiments.Description: To analyze the factors influencing user preference and diagnose agent failure modes that are not captured by static benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The collected dataset of human preferences, agent trajectories, and structured labels will be analyzed. The analysis will investigate the correlation between correctness, efficiency (execution length, latency), and user preference. A qualitative case study and quantitative error analysis will be performed to identify common failure modes, such as long-horizon memory failures, information awareness, and fine-grained action failures. The role of agent-human interaction via a `CALL_USER` action will also be examined.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Correlation between win rate and correctness/efficiency metrics', 'Win rate grouped by the number of `CALL_USER` queries', 'Categorization of error types from failed trajectories']

experiments.Description: To compare the agent rankings generated by COMPUTER AGENT ARENA with those from existing static benchmarks and to study the effect of task distribution on performance.
experiments.Method: The leaderboard from COMPUTER AGENT ARENA will be compared against rankings from four public benchmarks: OSWorld, WebArena, WebVoyager, and Online-Mind2Web. Additionally, an ablation study will be performed by sampling user-submitted tasks from the arena, classifying them as in-domain or out-of-domain relative to OSWorld, and recomputing Elo scores for each subset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Relative rank differences of agents across the five leaderboards', 'Comparison of Elo scores on in-domain vs. out-of-domain task subsets']

risk_factors: This suggests that rankings remain stable under moderate subjectivity, though extreme noise can affect closely ranked models.
This suggests that step limits may underrepresent agents with strong long-term planning ability.

title: How Much Space Has Been Explored? Measuring the Chemical Space Covered by Databases and Machine-Generated Molecules
name: measure-chemical-space-coverage
short_hypothesis: We propose a novel evaluation framework for measures of the chemical space based on two analyses: an axiomatic analysis with three intuitive axioms that a good measure should obey, and an empirical analysis on the correlation between a measure and a proxy gold standard. Using this framework, we are able to identify #Circles, a new measure of chemical space coverage, which is superior to existing measures both analytically and empirically.
related_work: Existing metrics for evaluating the quality of molecular databases and generation methods fall into four categories: bioactivities, molecular properties, data likelihood, or chemical space coverage. Common coverage measures include richness, uniqueness, internal diversity, and Fréchet ChemNet Distance (FCD). However, most of these measures are heuristically proposed, often mixing concepts of diversity, coverage, and novelty, and their validity as a measure of exploration is rarely justified. This work is the first to formally investigate the validity of chemical space measures using an axiomatic approach.
abstract: Forming a molecular candidate set that contains a wide range of potentially effective compounds is crucial to the success of drug discovery. While most databases and machine-learning-based generation models aim to optimize particular chemical properties, there is limited literature on how to properly measure the coverage of the chemical space by those candidates included or generated. This problem is challenging due to the lack of formal criteria to select good measures of the chemical space. In this paper, we propose a novel evaluation framework for measures of the chemical space based on two analyses: an axiomatic analysis with three intuitive axioms that a good measure should obey, and an empirical analysis on the correlation between a measure and a proxy gold standard. Using this framework, we are able to identify #Circles, a new measure of chemical space coverage, which is superior to existing measures both analytically and empirically.

experiments.Description: To validate different chemical space measures by testing their correlation with the variety of biological functionalities in a set of molecules.
experiments.Method: Using the BioActivity dataset, molecular subsets will be sampled in two settings: a fixed-size setting and a growing-size setting. The 'gold standard' (GS) is defined as the number of unique bio-activity labels in a subset. Various chemical space measures (including Diversity, SumDiversity, Diameter, Bottleneck, #FG, #RS, #BM, Richness, and the proposed #Circles) will be calculated for these subsets and compared against the gold standard.

experiments.Description: To evaluate and compare how well existing molecular databases cover the chemical space using the proposed and existing measures.
experiments.Method: Five molecular databases (ZINC-250k, MOSES, ChEMBL, GDB-17, and ENAMINE) will be evaluated. The coverage of the full databases and filtered versions (QED ≥ 0.6 and SA < 4) will be measured.

experiments.Description: To evaluate how well machine learning-based molecular generation models explore the chemical space, and to test if incorporating coverage measures into the optimization objective can encourage exploration.
experiments.Method: Four generation models (RationaleRL, DST, JANUS, MARS) will be used to generate molecules optimizing for JNK3 binding affinity, QED, and SA. Three variants of MARS will also be tested, incorporating Diversity, SumBottleneck, and #Circles into the optimization objective. The generated molecules will be filtered based on property thresholds. A virtual screening baseline using a combination of five molecular databases will be used for comparison.

risk_factors: A few limitations of the measurement with #Circles should be noted. For example the running time of calculating #Circles is exponential in theory.
Moreover, the newly suggested objective for molecular generation (Equation 9) inspires further study, since it is computationally challenging as a combinatorial constrained optimization problem.

title: Scale-time Equivalence in Neural Network Training
name: scale-time-equivalence
short_hypothesis: In certain training regimes, increasing a neural network's model size is functionally equivalent to extending its training time. Consequently, a small model trained for a long time can achieve similar performance to a larger model trained for a shorter time.
related_work: Prior work on scaling laws has described how test error decreases with increases in model and data size, often following a power-law. However, the role of training time in these scaling dynamics remains underexplored and poorly understood. Separately, the phenomenon of double descent has been theoretically explained for model and data size, but existing theories do not generally explain double descent with respect to training time. This work proposes a framework to unify the interplay of model scale and training time, and leverage this to provide a new explanation for double descent.
abstract: Neural networks have demonstrated remarkable performance improvements as model size, training time, and data volume have increased, but the relationships among these factors remain poorly understood. We develop a theoretical framework to investigate the interplay between model size and training time, introducing the concept of scale-time equivalence: the idea that in certain training regimes, a small model trained for a long time can achieve similar performance to a larger model trained for a shorter time. To analyze this, we model neural network training as a gradient flow in random subspaces of larger non-linear models. We propose to show theoretically that neural network scale and training time can be traded off with each other. We then plan to investigate the consequences of scale-time equivalence on double descent. In regimes where scale-time equivalence holds, we will attempt to show that double descent with respect to training time and model scale may share a common cause: namely, overfitting to noise early during training. Through this, we aim to provide potential explanations for several previously unexplained empirical phenomena.

experiments.Description: To empirically validate that scale-time equivalence can arise in neural networks trained with gradient descent.
experiments.Method: A 7-layer CNN and a 6-layer MLP are trained with SGD on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and SVHN. Experiments will be run under various network widths (defined by a scale parameter 's') and dataset sizes (by subsampling).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Minimum amount of training time (epochs) required to achieve non-zero generalization (test set MSE below 0.09).', 'Tradeoff curve between model scale and training time.']

experiments.Description: To test if scale-time equivalence can be used to predict the performance of large models by training small models for many epochs, and vice versa.
experiments.Method: Using CNN and MLP models on MNIST, two prediction tasks are planned: 1) Predict performance under varying model scales by training a single small network for a long time (up to 100 epochs). 2) Predict performance over long training times by training larger networks for just 1 epoch. The scale-time equivalence principle (pt = constant) will be used to extrapolate.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Predicted vs. true test and train Mean Squared Error (MSE) curves.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis, derived from scale-time equivalence, that larger models require less data to generalize, in contrast to conventional double descent theory.
experiments.Method: CNN models of different scales (darker colors indicating larger models) will be trained on MNIST with varying amounts of training data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test and train Mean Squared Error (MSE) as a function of data volume for different model scales.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that noise has a global, persistent effect on performance across model scales and training time, as predicted by the scale-time equivalence explanation of double descent, contrasting with the localized effect predicted by conventional theory.
experiments.Method: CNN and MLP models will be trained on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and SVHN. Experiments will be run with and without 20% label noise. Performance will be evaluated as a function of epochs, model scale, and data size.

experiments.Description: To test the prediction that, under certain conditions, increasing model size can worsen performance, which contrasts with conventional double descent theory where performance always improves past the interpolation threshold.
experiments.Method: The error curves from experiments on CIFAR-10 and SVHN with added noise will be analyzed, specifically focusing on the relationship between test error and model scale.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Shape of the test error curve with respect to model scale, looking for a potential U-shape.']

risk_factors: Like a number of scaling studies, our experiments focus on vision benchmarks, and extending the framework to language models is an important next step.
Moreover, we use a heuristic to estimate the effective parameter count of neural networks; this heuristic warrants further investigation.
We emphasize that these results are limited to gradient descent with a fixed learning rate (the setting of our theory).
The equivalence breaks down for small p since here, the randomly chosen subspace of the model may or may not align well with K; as p grows, the amount of alignment becomes less stochastic.

title: Why Solving Multi-agent Path Finding with Large Language Models has not Succeeded Yet
name: llm-mapf-failure-analysis
short_hypothesis: Large Language Models can be prompted to directly solve Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) problems step-by-step without additional training or heuristic guidance, by iteratively generating actions for each agent and using a high-level checker to validate solutions and provide feedback on errors.
related_work: Recent studies have shown that Large Language Models (LLMs) can assist in various reasoning and multi-agent coordination tasks. However, there is very limited work that specifically investigates their application to multi-agent route planning (MAPF). MAPF is a unique problem where coordination is required intrinsically within the planning process, making it challenging to facilitate with external tools. Traditional MAPF methods are either classical (heuristic search, SAT) or learning-based (Reinforcement Learning). This paper explores using pre-trained LLMs directly as an alternative learning-based approach without any task-specific fine-tuning, aiming to understand their capabilities and limitations in this specific domain.
abstract: Multi-agent planning is a challenging domain due to the combined difficulty of multi-agent coordination and planning, making it hard to leverage external tools. This paper focuses on the problem of multi-agent path finding (MAPF) and studies the performance of solving it with Large Language Models (LLMs). The study plans to first investigate single-agent planning and simple multi-agent pathfinding scenarios, followed by an analysis of performance on harder benchmark maps. The paper will present a position on why directly solving MAPF with LLMs has not been successful and will use various experiments to support its hypotheses, aiming to discuss how researchers from different backgrounds could approach this problem.

experiments.Description: To establish a baseline for the LLM's pathfinding capabilities, its performance will be evaluated on single-agent pathfinding tasks across maps of varying difficulty.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the GPT-4-turbo model to solve single-agent pathfinding problems on the ROOM-32-32-4 and MAZE maps from the standard MAPF benchmark. Scenarios will be categorized by the ground-truth shortest path distance. Two generation strategies will be compared: one-shot (OS) and step-by-step (SBS).

experiments.Description: To assess the ability of LLMs to handle multi-agent coordination, their performance will be tested on MAPF problems with an increasing number of agents and on maps with varying complexity.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the GPT-4-turbo model to solve MAPF problems on the EMPTY-8-8, ROOM-32-32-4, and MAZE-32-32-2 benchmark maps. The number of agents will be varied (e.g., 2, 4, 8, 16). The study will compare a one-shot (OS) generation approach using chain-of-thought prompts against a step-by-step (SBS) iterative approach that uses a high-level collision checker for feedback.

experiments.Description: To analyze the causes of failure in solving MAPF, this experiment will investigate the impact of different prompting techniques, model versions, and input representations.
experiments.Method: Using the ROOM-32-32-4 map, this experiment will compare the performance of GPT-4-8K, GPT-4-128K (turbo), and o1-preview models. It will test different prompting strategies, including providing only global obstacle observation (GO) versus providing both global and single-step observation (SSO). It will also compare different map input formats: multimodal image-based (MM), text describing obstacle coordinates (TOO), and text representing the map grid with symbols (TOM). A comparison between a rule-based checker and a human checker will also be conducted.

risk_factors: We would like to note that the methods in this paper are not designed to either outperform the state-of-the-art classic solvers or surpass the performance of learning-based solvers.
Because LLMs are evolving rapidly, it is unclear how long LLMs will still fail.
Although our methods do not combine any external tools like low-level planning.
Success does not indicate anything about the quality of the solution.
The current workflow does not include any heuristics or tool use during the generation but only as a solution checker.
Latency is always a problem for LLMs when used in real-world production.

title: Unlearning or Obfuscating? Jogging the Memory of Unlearned LLMs via Benign Relearning
name: benign-relearning-attack
short_hypothesis: Current finetuning-based approaches for approximate unlearning in LLMs are simply obfuscating the model outputs instead of truly forgetting the information in the forget set, making them susceptible to benign relearning attacks where a small amount of potentially auxiliary data can “jog” the memory of unlearned models so they behave similarly to their pre-unlearning state.
related_work: Prior works have explored relearning to evaluate the robustness of unlearning, but they generally consider relearning directly on the unlearn data. This makes it unclear if relearning occurs by learning the knowledge from scratch or by triggering the model's memory. This research aims to fill this gap by exploring benign relearning attacks, where the relearn set is only loosely related to the evaluation queries and unlearning task, to test if it's possible to "jog" the memory of an unlearned model.
abstract: Machine unlearning is a promising approach to mitigate undesirable memorization of training data in ML models. This work proposes that existing approaches for unlearning in LLMs are susceptible to a simple set of benign relearning attacks. With access to only a small and potentially loosely related set of data, it may be possible to "jog" the memory of unlearned models to reverse the effects of unlearning. For example, relearning on public medical articles could lead an unlearned LLM to output harmful knowledge, and relearning general wiki information could force the model to output verbatim memorized text. This study formalizes the unlearning-relearning pipeline and explores the attack across popular unlearning benchmarks, based on the idea that current approximate unlearning methods simply suppress model outputs rather than robustly forgetting target knowledge.

experiments.Description: Investigate if a relearning attack can recover unlearned keywords using only a limited, disjoint portion of the original unlearn dataset, where the relearn data itself is insufficient to infer the target keywords.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the TOFU and Who's Harry Potter (WHP) benchmarks. For TOFU, a Phi-1.5 model will be unlearned using gradient ascent, and the task is to recover fictitious book titles. For WHP, a Llama-2-7b model will be unlearned by finetuning on alternative labels, and the task is to recover character names. The relearning attack will be performed using LoRA finetuning on a partitioned subset of the unlearn data that does not contain the target keywords.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Attack Success Rate (ASR), a binary prediction on whether the target keywords are contained in the model's generated output."]

experiments.Description: Investigate if relearning on benign, publicly available information can recover unlearned hazardous knowledge from an LLM.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the WMDP benchmark (bio-attack and cyber-attack corpora) with zephyr-7b-beta and Llama-3-8b models. The models will be unlearned using various methods, including gradient ascent (GA), gradient difference (GD), KL minimization (KL), Negative Preference Optimization (NPO), and SCRUB. The relearning set will be constructed from public online articles and GPT-generated paragraphs about general knowledge related to the evaluation queries, ensuring no direct answers are included.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["LLM-as-Judge score (using GPT-4) to evaluate the quality and relevance of the model's answer to a harmful prompt."]

experiments.Description: Investigate if relearning on general public information can cause an unlearned model to regenerate verbatim memorized copyrighted content.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use the Llama-2-7b-chat model, first finetuned to memorize excerpts from 'Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix'. The model will then be unlearned using gradient ascent on the same text. The relearning attack will be performed by finetuning on GPT-generated generic facts about the characters in the book series, which do not contain any verbatim text from the excerpt.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Rouge-L score to measure the similarity between the model's output and the original copyrighted text completion."]

experiments.Description: Study a simplified, synthetic case to provide intuition about when benign relearning attacks are effective, specifically focusing on the correlation between data in the relearn set and the target knowledge.
experiments.Method: A synthetic dataset of concatenated common names will be created, with a specific pair ('Anthony Mark') repeated to establish a strong correlation. A Llama-2-7b model will be finetuned on this data, then unlearned to forget the pair. The relearning phase will use a relearn set containing only the first part of the pair ('Anthony'). The experiment will vary the frequency of the pair in the original dataset to simulate different levels of correlation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) of the target tokens ('Anthony', 'Mark').", "Relearn success rate, measured by how many model generations contain the full 'Anthony Mark' pair."]

risk_factors: In this work we primarily focus on attacking unlearning heuristics within the model parameter space in order to “jog” the memory of the unlearned model.
Thus, the same prompt is used for both the unlearned and relearned model.
As a result, a simple defense against our benign relearning attack could be methods such as prompt filtering (Liu et al., 2024a) and guardrails (Thaker et al., 2024).

title: Scaling In-the-Wild Training for Diffusion-based Illumination Harmonization and Editing by Imposing Consistent Light Transport
name: ic-light
short_hypothesis: Imposing a Consistent Light (IC-Light) transport constraint during the training of diffusion-based image generators, rooted in the physical principle of linear blending of appearances, will enable stable and scalable illumination learning. This will facilitate a physically grounded model behavior that modifies only the illumination of images while keeping other intrinsic properties, such as albedo and fine image details, unchanged, even when trained on large-scale, diverse, in-the-wild datasets.
related_work: Prior work in illumination editing includes learning-based methods using deep neural networks, often with physical priors or light stage data. More recently, diffusion models have been adapted for image editing and appearance manipulation. However, a key challenge remains in scaling the training of these models to large, diverse, 'in-the-wild' datasets without appropriate constraints. This often leads to models that produce random, structure-guided outputs rather than precise illumination changes, failing to preserve intrinsic properties like albedo. Existing methods may not be robust enough for scalable training across varied data sources like real photos, rendered images, and synthetic augmentations.
abstract: Diffusion-based image generators are becoming unique methods for illumination harmonization and editing. The current bottleneck in scaling up the training of diffusion-based illumination editing models is mainly in the difficulty of preserving the underlying image details and maintaining intrinsic properties, such as albedos, unchanged. Without appropriate constraints, directly training the latest large image models with complex, varied, or in-the-wild data is likely to produce a structure-guided random image generator, rather than achieving the intended goal of precise illumination manipulation. We propose Imposing Consistent Light (IC-Light) transport during training, rooted in the physical principle that the linear blending of an object's appearances under different illumination conditions is consistent with its appearance under mixed illumination. This consistency allows for stable and scalable illumination learning, uniform handling of various data sources, and facilitates a physically grounded model behavior that modifies only the illumination of images while keeping other intrinsic properties unchanged. Based on this method, we can scale up the training of diffusion-based illumination editing models to large data quantities (>10 million), across all available data types and using strong backbones (SDXL, Flux, etc.).

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablative study to understand the importance of the proposed light transport consistency and the in-the-wild data augmentation.
experiments.Method: The full model will be compared against two variants: one trained without the in-the-wild image augmentation data, and another trained without the light transport consistency loss. The models are based on Stable Diffusion 1.5 and will be evaluated on their ability to generalize and preserve intrinsic properties for prompts like "toy in room, studio lighting" and "a handsome man, neon city".
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of generalization capability', 'Visual inspection of ability to retain intrinsic properties (albedo/reflectance color)']

experiments.Description: To quantitatively evaluate the proposed method and its ablations against state-of-the-art methods on an unseen test set.
experiments.Method: The proposed method (Ours) and its ablations (w/o LTC, w/o aug. data, w/o 3d data, w/o light stage) will be compared against baselines `SwitchLight` and `DiLightNet`. The evaluation will be performed on a subset of 50,000 unseen 3D rendering data samples from the dataset.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)', 'Structural Similarity Index (SSIM)', 'Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS)']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate additional applications of the trained model, such as background-conditioned harmonization, compatibility with different base diffusion models, and normal map generation.
experiments.Method: The model will be tested for its ability to perform illumination generation conditioned solely on a background image. The framework will be applied to different base models, including `SD1.5`, `SDXL`, and `Flux`. A procedure will be tested to generate normal maps by blending multiple inferences from the model under different lighting conditions.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of generated results for background conditioning', 'Visual inspection of outputs from different base models', 'Visual inspection of generated normal maps']

experiments.Description: To visually compare the relighting and normal map generation capabilities of the proposed method against other state-of-the-art methods.
experiments.Method: The output of the proposed model will be visually compared with `Relightful Harmonization` for portrait background replacement. For normal map generation, the model's output will be compared against `SwitchLight`, `GeoWizard`, and `DSINE`.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual comparison of relighting quality and robustness to shadows', 'Visual comparison of normal map detail and quality']

risk_factors: We also point out that this normal extraction is an empirical method since the neural models are not optimized to approximate light stage ground truths or 3D normal maps.
The extraction of normal maps relies completely on the model's capability to produce consistent appearances in different illumination conditions.

title: Perceiver IO: A General Architecture for Structured Inputs & Outputs
name: perceiver-io
short_hypothesis: A general-purpose architecture, Perceiver IO, can be created by augmenting the Perceiver model with a flexible querying mechanism. This will enable the model to produce outputs of various sizes and semantics (e.g., language, optical flow, audio) directly from its latent space by using output-specific queries. This approach is intended to eliminate the need for task-specific architectural engineering and scale linearly with input and output size.
related_work: Current machine learning architectures are often specialized for specific data domains and tasks, such as using 2D ResNets for vision and Transformers for language. These models typically bake in domain assumptions, scale poorly to large inputs or outputs, and require complex, bespoke systems for multimodal tasks. Even general architectures like the original Perceiver can handle diverse inputs but are limited to simple output spaces like classification. There is a need for a single, general-purpose architecture that can handle arbitrary structured inputs and outputs efficiently.
abstract: A central goal of machine learning is the development of systems that can solve many problems in as many data domains as possible. Current architectures, however, cannot be applied beyond a small set of stereotyped settings, as they bake in domain & task assumptions or scale poorly to large inputs or outputs. In this work, we propose Perceiver IO, a general-purpose architecture that handles data from arbitrary settings while scaling linearly with the size of inputs and outputs. Our model augments the Perceiver with a flexible querying mechanism that enables outputs of various sizes and semantics, doing away with the need for task-specific architecture engineering.

experiments.Description: To evaluate Perceiver IO on language understanding tasks, particularly its ability to operate on raw byte sequences without traditional tokenization and compare its performance against standard Transformer models.
experiments.Method: The model will be pretrained on a Masked Language Modeling (MLM) task using a corpus of English Wikipedia and C4. It will then be finetuned and evaluated on the GLUE benchmark. The primary comparison will be between Perceiver IO operating on UTF-8 bytes and a Transformer-based BERT baseline operating on SentencePiece tokens, matched for FLOPs budget. A multitask finetuning setup on all GLUE tasks simultaneously will also be explored.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average GLUE score', 'Pearson correlation on STS-B', 'Matthews correlation on CoLa', 'Accuracy on remaining tasks']

experiments.Description: To assess Perceiver IO's performance on a dense visual prediction task, optical flow, using a straightforward application of the architecture without specialized components like cost volumes or explicit warping.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on the synthetic AutoFlow dataset. It will take concatenated image frames as input and decode the flow field by querying the latent representation with input-derived features. Evaluation will be conducted on the Sintel and KITTI benchmarks. Performance will be compared against specialist optical flow architectures like PWCNet and RAFT.

experiments.Description: To test the model's ability to handle and jointly reconstruct highly heterogeneous multimodal data (video, audio, and class labels) through a shared latent bottleneck.
experiments.Method: A single Perceiver IO model will be trained for multimodal autoencoding on the Kinetics-700-2020 dataset. The model will process inputs from all three modalities (video, raw audio, class labels) into a single input array, encode them, and then be queried to reconstruct all modalities simultaneously. During training, decoding will be subsampled for efficiency.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['PSNR for video reconstruction', 'PSNR for audio reconstruction', 'Top-1 Accuracy for label classification']

experiments.Description: To evaluate Perceiver IO on the standard large-scale image classification task of ImageNet, comparing it to the original Perceiver and other vision models, and testing its robustness to different levels of architectural priors (e.g., with and without 2D convolutions).
experiments.Method: The model will be trained and evaluated on ImageNet. It will be compared against the original Perceiver architecture to assess the benefit of the new query-based decoder. Different configurations will be tested: using 2D Fourier features, using fully learned positional encodings (no 2D structure), and using a 2D conv+maxpool preprocessing stage. A version will also be pretrained on the JFT dataset. Baselines include ResNet, ViT, DeiT, and NFNet.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate Perceiver IO's applicability to symbolic, set-valued data by using it as a drop-in replacement for a core component in the state-of-the-art StarCraft II agent, AlphaStar.
experiments.Method: Perceiver IO will replace the vanilla Transformer used as the 'entity encoder' within the AlphaStar model. The model will be tasked with encoding a discrete, unordered set of game units. The performance of the resulting agent will be evaluated.

experiments.Description: To confirm that the attention-based decoder is beneficial even for classification tasks, where simpler decoders are applicable, by revisiting the multimodal AudioSet classification domain.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the AudioSet dataset, which involves classifying events from video and audio. Perceiver IO (with a query-based decoder) will be compared directly against the original Perceiver (with an average+project decoder) on this multimodal classification task. Both raw audio and mel-spectrogram audio inputs will be tested.

risk_factors: Although decoding can be done in parallel, allowing us to decode very large output arrays in sequential batches, Perceiver IO requires all points are encoded simultaneously. Addressing this limitation and scaling to even larger inputs is an important direction for future work.
We leave the possibility of making Perceiver IO invariant to input shape to future work.

title: Image Functions In Neural Networks: A Perspective On Generalization
name: dynamic-image-functions-for-generalization
short_hypothesis: The lack of perfect correlation and the changing intersection of activation paths for different images during SGD training on ReLU networks is a key factor in explaining generalization. This dynamic behavior encourages the model to use different features at different times and pass the same image through different functions during training, which may improve generalization by encouraging diverse learning (akin to ensembling) and by improving algorithmic stability.
related_work: Existing research attempts to explain neural network generalization through concepts like capacity control (Rademacher complexity, VC dimension), algorithmic stability of SGD, PAC-Bayes bounds, and spectral bias towards low-frequency functions. Other work has explored how network architectures like ResNets can behave like ensembles or how activation patterns are structured. This paper proposes a new perspective, viewing the processing of each image at each training step as a distinct 'image function' and hypothesizes that the diversity and evolution of these functions, specifically their changing activation paths and intersections, are a key mechanism for generalization.
abstract: In this work, we show that training with SGD on ReLU neural networks gives rise to a natural set of functions for each image that are not perfectly correlated until later in training. Furthermore, we show experimentally that the intersection of paths for different images also changes during the course of training. We hypothesize that this lack of correlation and changing intersection may be a factor in explaining generalization, because it encourages the model to use different features at different times, and pass the same image through different functions during training. This may improve generalization in two ways. 1) By encouraging the model to learn the same image in different ways, and learn different commonalities between images, comparable to model ensembling. 2) By improving algorithmic stability, as for a particular feature, the model is not always reliant on the same set of images, so the removal of an image may not adversely affect the loss.

experiments.Description: To measure the cross-path alignment between different data points during training to test if an image `xi` always has access to the same set of activations from another image `xj`.
experiments.Method: Train AlexNet, ResNet-18, and VGG-19 on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets. The experiment involves measuring the overlap between activation paths for pairs of different images.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Cross path similarity: A normalized measure of the overlap between the intersection of paths for two images at the current epoch versus the previous epoch.', 'A similar normalized metric for the overlap of activation vectors.']

experiments.Description: To assess whether the overlap between path vectors for different images is related to the distance between the original images in the input data space.
experiments.Method: Train AlexNet and ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10. The experiment will compare path overlap to input space distance.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A plot of path overlap (p_i ⋅ p_j) versus the L2 distance in the original image space (||x_i - x_j||_2).']

experiments.Description: To assess how the activation path and representation for a single image change throughout the course of training.
experiments.Method: Train AlexNet on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The experiment will measure the similarity of an image's path to its own path at the previous timestep.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Self-similarity: The normalized dot product of an image's path vector at time `t` and `t-1`.", 'A similar normalized dot product metric for activation vectors.']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether the set of 'most similar' images to a given baseline image changes during training, where similarity is defined by path overlap.
experiments.Method: Using a trained AlexNet on CIFAR-10, for a few selected baseline images, rank other data points based on their path overlap with the baseline image at different training epochs.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['A plot of the rank of nearest neighbors for a baseline image over training epochs, based on path overlap.', 'A similar analysis using activation similarity.']

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that the ratio of early-to-mid training cross-path overlap can be used to predict a model's generalization error.
experiments.Method: For various models (AlexNet, ResNet-110, ResNet-18, VGG-19) and datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet), calculate the ratio of average cross-path overlap before the first learning rate decay to the average overlap during the second stage of training.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["A plot of the 'ratio of early to mid overlap' against the model's generalization error."]

title: Revealing and Mitigating Over-Attention in Knowledge Editing
name: SADR-for-Attention-Drift
short_hypothesis: Knowledge editing methods can cause 'Specificity Failure' where unrelated knowledge is corrupted. This failure is hypothesized to stem from an 'Attention Drift' phenomenon, where the model's attention heads assign excessive scores to edited entities. To mitigate this, we propose Selective Attention Drift Restriction (SADR), a regularization term added during editing to restrict changes in the attention weight distribution for heads that exhibit excessive focus on the edited entity.
related_work: Knowledge editing methods aim to modify model knowledge at low cost and are categorized into parameter-preserving, locate-then-edit, and meta-learning approaches. Despite their promise, these methods can cause ripple effects, under-editing, or over-editing. A key research gap, identified by recent work, is the dramatic performance drop when the edited subject appears in the context, an issue the authors define as 'Specificity Failure' and which motivates this paper's focus.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) still exhibit undesirable errors due to incorrect knowledge. Knowledge editing methods emerged to precisely edit specific model knowledge by modifying a small percentage of parameters. However, those methods can lead to the problem of Specificity Failure, where existing knowledge and capabilities are severely degraded. Our preliminary analysis indicates that Specificity Failure primarily stems from the model's attention heads assigning excessive attention scores to entities related to the edited knowledge, a phenomenon we denote as the Attention Drift. To mitigate this issue, we introduce a simple yet effective method, Selective Attention Drift Restriction (SADR), which introduces an additional regularization term during the knowledge editing process to restrict changes in the attention weight distribution, thereby preventing undue focus on the edited entity.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed SADR method in mitigating Specificity Failure when applied to mainstream locate-then-edit knowledge editing methods across several large language models.
experiments.Method: SADR will be implemented on top of three baseline editing methods: ROME, MEMIT, and PMET. The evaluation will be conducted on GPT-J-6b, Llama3-8b, GPT-NeoX-20b, Llama2-13b, and TinyLlama-1.1b models using a combined dataset from COUNTERFACT and WikiDatacounterfact.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Efficacy Score (ES)', 'Efficacy Magnitude (EM)', 'Paraphrase Score (PS)', 'Paraphrase Magnitude (PM)', 'Neighborhood Score (NS)', 'Neighborhood Magnitude (NM)', 'Relation Score (RS)', 'Relation Magnitude (RM)', 'Distract Neighborhood Score (DNS)', 'Distract Neighborhood Magnitude (DNM)', 'Fluency Score (FL)', 'Reasoning Score (RES)', 'Other Task Score (OS)', 'Distracted Other Task Score (DOS)', 'Perplexity (PPL)', 'Avg. S (harmonic mean)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the cause of Specificity Failure by employing causal tracing and patching experiments to identify which intermediate model components are responsible for the erroneous outputs.
experiments.Method: Causal tracing experiments ('Contaminating Substitution') will be run on the GPT-J-6b model using prompts from the Relation and Distract Neighborhood tasks. The impact of replacing activations from MLP, Attention, and hidden states will be measured. Further analysis will quantify the correlation between attention weight drift and incorrect predictions.

experiments.Description: To verify if the Attention Drift phenomenon and the effectiveness of SADR extend to other categories of knowledge editing methods, specifically parameter-preserving and meta-learning approaches.
experiments.Method: SADR will be applied to the WISE (parameter-preserving) and MEND (meta-learning) editing methods on the GPT-J model. The evaluation will use the combined COUNTERFACT and WikiDatacounterfact dataset.

experiments.Description: To test the robustness and effectiveness of the SADR approach when applied to larger and more diverse datasets, including different data formats like Q&A.
experiments.Method: The ROME baseline with and without SADR will be evaluated on the full COUNTERFACT dataset and a combination of the Zsre and Wikirecent datasets.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies on the SADR method to understand the effect of its components, such as the head selection strategy and the controlling weight hyperparameter (γ).
experiments.Method: Experiments will be run on GPT-J with the ROME baseline. The first ablation will compare selectively restraining heads versus restraining all heads. The second will analyze the trade-off between edit success and specificity by adjusting various hyperparameters. The third will specifically test the sensitivity of the method to the restraining weight γ.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Edit Success (average of PS and ES)', 'Specificity (average of NS, RS, and DNS)']

risk_factors: While our method shows promise, there is still potential for improvement in the Relation task. This may be due to the fact that edits can inadvertently erase or obscure other relevant knowledge about the subject, thereby affecting the model's overall performance in understanding and maintaining relations.

Our study primarily focused on single factual association edits. This scope excluded more complex scenarios such as batch and sequential editing, which involve multiple edits either simultaneously or over a sequence.
We focus on knowledge editing on only transformer-based models, omitting models with new architectures (Gu & Dao, 2023).

title: PULSE: Projection-based Unlearning via Linear Speedy Entropy Maximization
name: pulse-projection-unlearning
short_hypothesis: By introducing a learnable projection matrix between a model's feature extractor and classifier, it is possible to achieve efficient, retain-data-free unlearning. This is done by first jointly training the model and an initial projection matrix. Then, for an unlearning request, the model is frozen and a separate 'forget-specific' projection matrix is trained to maximize prediction confidence on the forget data. Subtracting this forget-specific matrix from the original projection matrix is hypothesized to induce targeted uncertainty on the forget data by inverting its confidence structure, thereby erasing the information without needing the retain set.
related_work: Existing machine unlearning methods are categorized as exact or approximate. Exact methods like retraining are resource-intensive, while more efficient approaches like SISA are still impractical for large models. Approximate methods often face significant limitations, including: 1) dependency on the retain dataset to maintain model performance, which poses privacy risks and increases memory footprint; 2) poor preservation of accuracy on the retain set, especially when unlearning without retain data; 3) lack of scalability for edge or CPU-only deployments; and 4) high computational costs due to full-model backpropagation or parameter importance estimation. Recent methods like BadTeacher and SSD, while effective, remain computationally expensive and require access to retain data, limiting their practicality.
abstract: Machine unlearning enables selective erasure of knowledge associated with specific data points from trained models without retraining from scratch. However, existing unlearning approaches face significant limitations: they typically degrade performance on remaining data, require access to the original retain dataset to maintain the model utility, and incur high computational costs. To address these challenges, we propose PULSE (Projection-based Unlearning via Linear Speedy Entropy Maximization), a novel retain-data-free unlearning method. PULSE jointly learns a projection matrix alongside the model backbone during training. During the unlearning phase, PULSE freezes the model backbone and trains a forget-specific projection matrix that maximizes confidence on the data to be forgotten. By subtracting this forget-specific matrix from the original projection, PULSE transforms confident predictions into targeted uncertainty. Unlike existing methods that modify model outputs to enforce forgetting, PULSE operates directly on representation space.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of PULSE in full-class unlearning scenarios, including both single- and multi-class unlearning requests.
experiments.Method: Experiments will be conducted on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10 datasets using ResNet50, MobileNetV2, and ViT-B/16 models. The proposed method will be compared against BadTeacher (with and without retain data), SSD, and a retrained model as a gold-standard baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the forget set (Df)', 'Accuracy on the retain set (Dr)', 'Similar Class Accuracy', 'Time Taken']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method's ability to forget a specific sub-class without disrupting visually similar sub-classes within the same superclass.
experiments.Method: This scenario will be evaluated on the CIFARSuper20 dataset using ResNet18, MobileNetV2, and ViT-B/16 models. The task will involve forgetting a specific sub-class (e.g., 'baby') from a superclass (e.g., 'people'). Baselines for comparison will be the Retrained model, BadTeacher, and SSD.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on the forget set (Df)', 'Accuracy on the retain set (Dr)', 'Time Taken for Unlearning']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of the method under dynamic conditions where unlearning requests for multiple classes or subsets arrive sequentially over time.
experiments.Method: Incremental unlearning experiments will be carried out on CIFAR-10 using a ResNet-50 model, where classes are removed one by one in sequence. The performance will be compared against a retrained baseline at each step.

experiments.Description: To provide evidence of the selective unlearning mechanism by visualizing the geometric transformations occurring in the representation space.
experiments.Method: Uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP) will be used to project the high-dimensional representations of forget and retain samples into a 2D space. The embeddings will be visualized before and after applying PULSE in the sub-class unlearning scenario on CIFARSuper20.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Visual inspection of the organization, clustering, and separation of embeddings in the UMAP plots.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the computational efficiency and memory requirements of the proposed method.
experiments.Method: The analysis will be performed across MobileNet-V2, ResNet-50, and ViT-B/16 architectures, comparing PULSE against BadTeacher and SSD baselines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of PULSE to membership inference attacks (MIAs).
experiments.Method: Binary classifiers will be trained to distinguish training samples from unseen data on the CIFAR-10 dataset using ResNet50 and ViT-B/16 models. The MIA accuracy of PULSE will be compared to that of the original trained model, a retrained model, BadTeacher, and SSD.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Membership Inference Attack (MIA) Accuracy on the forget set.']

risk_factors: It is worth mentioning that we excluded random unlearning experiments due to their limited practical relevance.

The method's behavior is controlled by a hyperparameter 'α', which creates a trade-off: aggressive forgetting regimes can achieve more complete forgetting but may have a noticeable impact on retain performance.

title: TriGuardFL: Triple-Step Byzantine-Robust Federated Learning against Model Poisoning Attacks
name: triguardfl
short_hypothesis: A novel triple-step defense framework, TriGuardFL, can robustly discriminate malicious actors from benign clients with non-IID data. The proposed method involves: 1) a cosine-similarity-based filter to identify suspicious clients, 2) a fine-grained secondary evaluation using a small, class-stratified dataset to discern if divergence stems from maliciousness or data heterogeneity by analyzing class-wise performance, and 3) a Bayesian reputation model to manage detection uncertainty and enhance long-term robustness.
related_work: Existing Byzantine-robust defenses against model poisoning in Federated Learning are categorized into robust aggregation rules (filtering statistical outliers), server-side validation using a trusted dataset, and client-side defense mechanisms. However, these strategies often become ineffective in non-IID settings, where the natural parameter divergence among benign clients with heterogeneous data can mask malicious updates, making it difficult to distinguish between benign, non-IID updates and malicious ones.
abstract: Federated learning's (FL) distributed architecture is promising, yet it is vulnerable to model poisoning attacks that degrade global model accuracy. Existing defense strategies typically compare the locally updated gradients of clients and exclude or down-weight those exhibiting substantial deviations. However, these strategies may become ineffective when the clients’ datasets are heterogeneous. In this paper, we propose TriGuardFL, a novel triple-step defense framework that robustly discriminates malicious actors from benign and non-IID clients. First, we employ a cosine-similarity-based filter to identify suspicious clients. Second, a fine-grained secondary evaluation assesses their performance using a small class-stratified dataset. By analyzing class-wise performance differences, it can discern whether a divergent update stems from a malicious attack or data heterogeneity. Finally, a Bayesian reputation model is integrated to manage the uncertainty of detection and enhance the long-term robustness.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed TriGuardFL algorithm against state-of-the-art model poisoning attacks in a heterogeneous, non-IID setting, and to compare its performance against established defense mechanisms across different deep learning models.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will be conducted on Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets, with data partitioned among clients using a Dirichlet distribution (α=0.5) to simulate a non-IID environment. For Fashion-MNIST, a LeNet model will be used. For CIFAR-10, AlexNet, VGG11, VGG16, and ResNet18 models will be used. The performance of TriGuardFL will be benchmarked against four baseline defenses: DeFL, FL-WBC, FLTrust, and Multi-Krum. The system will be subjected to three model poisoning attacks: Min-Sum, Min-Max, and LIE, under both 'Full Knowledge' and 'Partial Knowledge' threat models.

experiments.Description: To assess the scalability and robustness of the TriGuardFL method by conducting experiments in more hostile environments with an increased network scale and a proportionally larger number of adversaries.
experiments.Method: This experiment will use the CIFAR-10 dataset with a non-IID distribution and a ResNet18 model. The performance of defense mechanisms will be compared between a baseline configuration (32 total clients, 4 malicious) and a scaled network configuration with a proportionally larger number of attackers (e.g., 64 total clients, 8 malicious). The baselines for comparison are DeFL, FLTrust, FLDetector, and Multi-Krum.

risk_factors: However, TriGuardFL rely on an independent dataset on the server, which restricts the application scenarios.

title: MOLLEO+: Towards Optimized Use of LLMs for Drug Discovery
name: molleo-plus-drug-discovery
short_hypothesis: An LLM-based evolutionary framework for small-molecule generation (MOLLEO) can be optimized for real-world drug discovery by: 1) replacing the standard docking score oracle (AutoDock) with a more accurate biomolecular foundation model (Boltz-2), 2) initializing the starting population with known, target-specific ligands from BindingDB instead of generic molecules from ZINC 250k, and 3) fine-tuning the LLM on a semi-synthetic dataset to improve its ability to perform lead optimization.
related_work: Previous machine learning approaches for small-molecule generation include VAEs, diffusion models, and reinforcement learning, often guided by computationally cheap but inaccurate oracles like AutoDock. A recent state-of-the-art method, MOLLEO, uses an LLM as a mutation and crossover operator in an evolutionary algorithm, but it still relies on an inaccurate docking oracle and a generic starting population (ZINC 250k), limiting its effectiveness for real-world drug discovery. The research gap lies in creating a more practical and effective LLM-based optimization framework that leverages more accurate oracles, incorporates prior knowledge of existing binders, and uses LLMs specifically tuned for lead optimization tasks.
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are a promising tool for small-molecule generation. The state-of-the-art method MOLLEO combines an evolutionary algorithm with an LLM but has aspects not well-suited for real-world drug discovery, such as its reliance on molecular docking scores. We propose MOLLEO+, an optimized LLM workflow for small-molecule generation. The proposed method makes three key changes: first, it replaces the docking oracle with the biomolecular foundation model Boltz-2; second, it incorporates knowledge of existing ligands by using BindingDB instead of ZINC 250k for the starting population; and third, it introduces a fine-tuning strategy to better modify existing ligands towards higher activity.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of using the Boltz-2 model as a fitness oracle within a generative framework, compared to the standard AutoDock oracle, by measuring correlation with gold-standard computational methods.
experiments.Method: The MOLLEO framework will be used to generate ligands for the c-MET protein target. Two versions will be run: one using AutoDock as the oracle and another using Boltz-2. The generated molecules will be benchmarked against those from the MF-LAL baseline. Additionally, a set of 32 known compounds for c-MET will be used to assess the correlation of AutoDock and Boltz-2 scores against ABFE calculations.
