experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Absolute Binding Free Energy (ABFE)', 'Correlation (R^2) between oracle scores and ABFE', 'ROC-AUC for separating binders from non-binders']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the performance of the full MOLLEO+ framework and its individual components (Boltz-2 oracle, BindingDB starting population, and fine-tuned LLM) against baselines.
experiments.Method: The performance of various configurations of the MOLLEO framework will be compared on the c-MET and BRD4 protein targets. The configurations include: the original MOLLEO, MOLLEO with only the BindingDB population, MOLLEO with only the Boltz-2 oracle, the full MOLLEO+ method, and MOLLEO+ with both untuned and fine-tuned Llama models. Baselines for comparison will be MF-LAL and Pocket2Mol.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Binding affinity predicted by Boltz-2 (mean and standard deviation)', 'Number of ligands exceeding a strong-binding threshold', 'Quantitative estimate of drug-likeness (QED)', 'Synthetic accessibility (SA)']

experiments.Description: To analyze the structural novelty of molecules generated by MOLLEO+ compared to the molecules in the starting population.
experiments.Method: Generated ligands from MOLLEO and MOLLEO+ runs will be filtered based on Tanimoto similarity to any ligand in their respective starting populations. The analysis will be conducted using multiple similarity thresholds (0.5, 0.45, 0.4) and also at the Murcko scaffold level.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average maximum similarity to the starting population', 'Filtered mean binding affinity of the top 10 most diverse ligands']

experiments.Description: To test a workaround for the fine-tuning framework when a protein target is not well-studied and has few known ligands in BindingDB.
experiments.Method: A surrogate dataset for fine-tuning will be created for the c-MET target by identifying 20 structurally similar protein targets (via BLASTP) and pooling their known ligands from BindingDB. A Llama model will be fine-tuned on this surrogate dataset and its performance in the MOLLEO+ framework will be compared to a model trained on the original, target-specific c-MET dataset.

risk_factors: While our BindingDB approach to MOLLEO demonstrably improves the performance on the c-MET and BRD4 targets, we recognize that these are both very well-studied targets.
For less studied protein targets, this method may be entirely inapplicable if there are not enough diverse known binders to comprise a starting population.

title: Test-Time RAG: Enhancing Long Context Understanding in LLMs with Retrieval-Augmented Mechanisms
name: test-time-rag
short_hypothesis: A novel integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques with LLMs, called Test-Time RAG (TTRAG), will enhance their ability to effectively manage and utilize large contextual information that is only available at test time. This is achieved through three components: dynamically generating conditional embeddings based on the query, iteratively rewriting the query to refine search intent, and using conditional compute to adapt the processing pipeline to the specific task.
related_work: Classical Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems require pre-computing document embeddings, limiting their application to scenarios where documents cannot be indexed in advance, such as personalized chat interactions or privacy-centric systems. Other methods like recursive summarization can result in lossy compression of context. While architectural modifications for long context exist, they are considered complementary. This work addresses the gap of handling large amounts of data introduced dynamically at test time, where pre-indexing is not feasible, by extending RAG techniques to a test-time setting.
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are becoming increasingly pivotal in applications that depend on extensive personalized context, such as conversational agents and specialized task-oriented systems. In these scenarios, effective long-context handling is essential to support agentic tasks and enhance in-context learning capabilities. To address this challenge, we propose a novel integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques with LLMs, designed to enhance their ability to effectively manage and utilize large contextual information only available at test time. Our methodology, Test-Time RAG (TTRAG), enriches LLMs by dynamically generating novel conditional embeddings coupled with query rewriting and utilizing semantic search to retrieve the most relevant document chunks at test time. This process preserves the context's meaning and enhances the model's responsiveness and accuracy in knowledge-intensive Question Answering (QA) tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the complete Test-Time RAG (TTRAG) system against baselines on knowledge-intensive question-answering tasks.
experiments.Method: The TTRAG system, using GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 and Llama-2-7b-chat-hf models with text-embedding-3-small, will be evaluated on the HotpotQA, QASPER, and Natural Questions datasets, with context lengths extended from 1 million to 9.6 million tokens. Baselines will consist of the same LLMs using a standard retriever to select the top documents and backfilling the context window with random distractor documents.

experiments.Description: To quantify the individual contribution of the Conditional Embeddings component on both general and domain-specific question-answering tasks across various context lengths.
experiments.Method: The Conditional Embeddings component will be evaluated on general QA datasets (HotpotQA, Natural Questions, PubMedQA) and domain-specific datasets (QASPER, QUALITY). Various models will be used, including GPT-4-0613, GPT-3.5-turbo-0125, Claude Haiku, and Llama2-7B Chat. Performance will be compared against baselines that use standard retrieval (BM25 or text-embedding-3-small) without conditional embeddings.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance gains and compute efficiency of the Iterative Query Rewriting component.
experiments.Method: The query rewriting component will be evaluated on the CRAG benchmark using GPT-3.5-0125, Llama-2-7b, and Llama-3-8B models. It will be benchmarked against a formulation using only conditional embeddings for filtering, without the iterative rewriting step.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Exact Match', 'F1 Score', 'Average Number of Rewrites', 'Average Input Tokens Processed']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the Conditional Compute component's ability to minimize compute while maintaining quality on long context tasks outside of traditional question answering.
experiments.Method: The conditional compute feature will be evaluated on the InfiniteBench suite. Specifically, summarization capabilities will be tested on the En.Sum task and key-value retrieval on the Retrieve.KV task. The pipeline will classify queries into categories (summarization, KV retrieval, etc.) and adjust processing accordingly. This will be compared to baseline methods that process the entire context.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Latency', 'Input Tokens Processed', 'Output Tokens Generated', 'Exact Match Score (for KV retrieval)', 'F1 score (for summarization)', 'ROUGE-L (for summarization)']

title: A path toward primitive machine intelligence: LMM not LLM is what you need.
name: lmm-for-chemosensing-theory
short_hypothesis: Using linear mixture models (LMMs), inspired by Piaget's stages of cognitive development, can establish an interpretable engineering theory for chemosensing, allowing for the derivation of analytical expressions for its limits and advancing the statistical mechanics of learning, in contrast to less interpretable deep learning approaches.
related_work: Current approaches for creating artificial noses often apply deep learning to large datasets from hyperspectral hardware, which can achieve high performance but at the cost of interpretability. In contrast, animals exhibit a primitive form of intelligence in their sense of smell that is highly efficient. This work draws inspiration from Jean Piaget's theory of cognitive development and classical chemometrics to formulate a problem in a more meaningful and mathematically tractable way, aiming to fill a gap in the theoretical understanding of chemosensing.
abstract: While large language models (LLMs) and deep reinforcement learning show sparks of super-human intelligence, animals remain supreme in the sense of smell, a primitive form of intelligence. Applying deep learning to hyperspectral data for chemosensing may lead to artificial noses but sacrifices interpretability. This paper proposes a path using linear mixture models (LMMs) to build an engineering theory of cognitive development for chemosensing. The goal is to use creative mathematical models to derive analytical expressions for the limits of chemosensing and advance the statistical mechanics of learning.

experiments.Description: To infer the number of chemicals (M) in a set of mixtures from observed spectral signals, as the first stage of the proposed cognitive theory of chemosensing.
experiments.Method: The primary method is to perform an eigenvalue cutoff on the sample covariance matrix of rescaled observations from a synthetic dataset generated by Algorithm 1. This approach will be evaluated against other dimensionality estimation methods, including the Malinowski indicator function, Akaike information criterion (AIC), minimum description length (MDL), Hyperspectral signal identification by minimum error (HySime), and random matrix theory. The effect of adding noise to the signals will also be considered.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Correct estimation of the number of chemicals (M)', 'Eigenvalue decay plot analysis']

experiments.Description: To learn the pure spectral representations of each inferred chemical, corresponding to the second stage of the cognitive theory.
experiments.Method: The plan is to apply and analyze methods like Nonnegative Matrix Factorization (NMF) and Independent Component Analysis (ICA) on the synthetic dataset (generated by Algorithm 1 with M=8) to recover the pure spectra. These will be compared against Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and a pseudo-inverse method. The goal is to refine the mathematical understanding of ICA and NMF, potentially by reverse-engineering analytic convergence laws, and explore their computational efficiency against alternatives like singular value decomposition, pixel purity index, and maximum distance methods.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Convergence of the algorithm to the pure spectra', 'Comparison of learned spectra to the ideal spectra', 'Computational efficiency']

experiments.Description: To estimate the concentration of each known chemical in a mixture, representing the third stage of the cognitive theory.
experiments.Method: The proposed method involves taking a dot product of the observed spectral signals with an ideal pseudo-inverse spectrum to obtain an estimate of the chemical concentrations. A theoretical analysis will be performed to derive an error scaling law (∆max) for this estimation, considering factors like the number of sensor channels (N), machine precision, signal magnitude (max s), and pseudo-inverse weights (max w). The analysis will also investigate the limits of accurate estimation in the presence of noise.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Maximum absolute error (∆max) in concentration estimation', 'Derivation of the error scaling law']

experiments.Description: To interpret why a mixture is good or bad by reasoning from past examples, which is the fourth and final stage of the cognitive theory.
experiments.Method: This stage will be modeled similarly to the association-response layers of perceptrons, but using the interpretable features (concentrations) learned in the previous stages. The plan is to focus on datasets where the response units are binary, such as using spectral measurements on skin to detect skin diseases. The approach will also be considered for extension to non-binary datasets, such as predicting pH levels of farmland patches using hyperspectral images from the 'geoai-challenge-estimating-soil-parameters-from-hyperspectral-images' competition.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Ability to learn a mapping from chemical concentrations to a response variable (e.g., disease presence, pH level)', 'Generalizability of the learned model']

risk_factors: None of the existing methods are able to learn the pure (ideal) spectra, despite the existence of its pseudo-inverse. Novel update rules for ICA and NMF are needed to solve this problem.
An empirical demo may even require the development of new hardware technology to extend the linear dynamic range of its sensors.
For tasks like predicting pH levels, interpretable learning may require much larger datasets to sample across different chemical species that could result in the same pH.

title: Sample-Imagined Generator: Efficient Virtual Sample Generation Method for Off-policy Reinforcement Learning with Sparse Rewards
name: sample-imagined-generator-for-rl
short_hypothesis: A Sample-Imagined Generator (SIG) module can improve the sample efficiency of off-policy reinforcement learning in sparse reward settings. This is achieved by training a generator to create valuable, close-to-real imagined samples to supplement the experience replay buffer. The SIG consists of two main components: a Self-validating Sample Generator (SSG) with an action-validation closed loop to ensure sample quality, and a Self-adaptive Imagination Inference (SII) module to adaptively adjust the imagined trajectory length, sampling ratio, and the timing for reducing real-world interactions based on the generator's accuracy.
related_work: The problem of low sample efficiency in off-policy RL, especially with sparse rewards, has been addressed through several approaches. One direction is introducing external expert data, such as demonstrations (RLfD) or large-scale offline datasets (Offline RL), to guide the agent. Another direction involves internal algorithmic improvements to enhance exploration or stabilize training, such as entropy maximization (SAC), trust region methods (TRPO), or improving Q-value estimation (TD3). However, these methods still require extensive interaction with the environment. The proposed research aims to fill this gap by designing an independent module that explicitly generates virtual samples to reduce the need for real environmental interactions.
abstract: Off-policy reinforcement learning (RL) requires extensive real interaction with environment to gain experience for policy learning, presenting a challenge of low sample efficiency, especially in the condition of sparse rewards. To address this, we propose a Sample-Imagined Generator (SIG), which automatically trains a sample generator during environment interaction and could adaptively generate valuable imagined samples for policy learning. SIG is designed to be combined with any off-policy RL algorithm to supplement the experience pool with imagined samples, with the goal of reducing interaction with the environment and improving sample efficiency.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the role of the Sample-Imagined Generator (SIG) in enhancing the sample efficiency of various off-policy RL algorithms.
experiments.Method: SIG will be combined with 10 different off-policy RL algorithms (SAC, TD3, GQE, OEFD, CQL, and their MCAC-enhanced versions: SM, TM, GM, OM, CM). These combined methods ('Algo + SIG') will be compared against their respective baseline algorithms ('Algo') on 5 continuous control tasks with sparse rewards: Lift, Door, Extraction, Push, and Navigation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Performance (e.g., average return or success rate) will be plotted against the number of environmental interaction steps to assess sample efficiency and final performance.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the necessity of using SIG to generate samples when reducing environmental interactions.
experiments.Method: The performance of an algorithm combined with SIG (e.g., 'TM + SIG') will be compared to a variant that reduces interaction at a fixed frequency but does not use SIG to generate replacement samples ('TM (Less Interact)'). This will be tested on the 5 continuous control tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Learning curves will be compared to show the effect of removing SIG's sample generation during reduced interaction phases.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the Interaction-adaptive Switch Time component within SIG.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted by comparing the full 'Algo + SIG' model, which uses an adaptive switch time to reduce interactions, against a variant 'Algo + SIG (Fixed Switch)' where the interaction reduction starts at a fixed, predefined timestep. This will be tested on the SM, TM, GM, OM, and CM algorithms across the 5 control tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Learning performance will be compared between the adaptive and fixed switch time versions to determine the benefit of the adaptive mechanism.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the Action Validation Module (AVM) component within SIG.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be conducted by comparing the full 'Algo + SIG' model against a variant 'Algo + SIG (w/o AVM)' where the AVM is removed from the generator. This experiment will be run with the SM, TM, and GM algorithms on the Lift, Door, and Extraction tasks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Learning efficiency and performance will be compared between the full model and the ablated version to isolate the contribution of the AVM.

risk_factors: Before it is accurate enough, the imagined samples are not proper to substitute the real samples for policy learning. They would confuse the learning process, even inducing non-convergence.
In the future, we would continue to focus on the research of high sample efficiency algorithms for RL and further develop SIG to be more stable and applicable in real-world.

title: Large Language Models to Enhance Bayesian Optimization
name: llm-for-bayesian-optimization
short_hypothesis: By framing Bayesian Optimization (BO) components in natural language, Large Language Models (LLMs) can leverage their encoded prior knowledge, few-shot learning proficiency, and contextual understanding to enhance the core BO pipeline. This includes improving zero-shot warmstarting, creating more effective surrogate models, and enabling more efficient candidate sampling, especially in the early stages of search with sparse data.
related_work: Prior work in Bayesian Optimization has focused on improving surrogate models using methods like Gaussian Processes (GPs), neural networks, and transformers. Transfer learning has been explored to leverage knowledge from previous optimizations, but these methods often require dedicated pretraining or structured results from similar tasks. Recent work has started to use LLMs for optimization, such as for prompt optimization or as genetic operators, with one study focusing on using LLMs for the surrogate model in molecular BO. This paper proposes a more comprehensive integration of generalist LLMs into multiple components of the BO pipeline (warmstarting, surrogate modeling, candidate sampling) as a lightweight alternative that does not require dedicated pretraining, and systematically investigates the performance gains from each component.
abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a powerful approach for optimizing complex and expensive-to-evaluate black-box functions. Its importance is underscored in many applications, notably including hyperparameter tuning, but its efficacy depends on efficiently balancing exploration and exploitation. While there has been substantial progress in BO methods, striking this balance remains a delicate process. In this light, we present LLAMBO, a novel approach that integrates the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLM) within BO. At a high level, we frame the BO problem in natural language, enabling LLMs to iteratively propose and evaluate promising solutions conditioned on historical evaluations. More specifically, we explore how combining contextual understanding, few-shot learning proficiency, and domain knowledge of LLMs can improve model-based BO. Our approach is performed in context and does not require LLM finetuning. Additionally, it is modular by design, allowing individual components to be integrated into existing BO frameworks, or function cohesively as an end-to-end method.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the impact of using LLMs for zero-shot warmstarting by providing different levels of contextual information about the optimization problem.
experiments.Method: LLAMBO employs zero-shot prompting with GPT-3.5 to sample 5 initialization points for two BO methods: Gaussian Processes (GP) and Tree Parzen Estimator (TPE). Three settings are explored: 'No context', 'Partial context', and 'Full context'. These are compared against Random, Sobol, and Latin Hypercube (HCube) sampling techniques on tasks from Bayesmark and HPOBench.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Search performance (normalized regret)', 'Diversity of initialization points (generalized variance: det(Σ))']

experiments.Description: To assess the efficacy of a discriminative surrogate model implemented via In-Context Learning (ICL) with an LLM, focusing on prediction performance and uncertainty calibration.
experiments.Method: The LLAMBO discriminative surrogate model is compared against Gaussian Process (GP) and SMAC surrogate models. The evaluation is conducted on tasks with varying numbers of observed points (n ∈ [5, 10, 20, 30]), with predictions tested against 20 unseen points. An ablation study, LLAMBO (UnInf), which omits problem description, is also planned.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Prediction performance (NRMSE, R²)', 'Uncertainty calibration (log predictive density (LPD), empirical coverage, sharpness)', 'Normalized regret of the point acquired using expected improvement (EI)']

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of a generative surrogate model, inspired by TPE, which uses an LLM to predict the probability of a configuration belonging to the 'good' points.
experiments.Method: The proposed LLAMBO generative surrogate model is compared against two variants of the Tree Parzen Estimator: TPE (Ind) and TPE (Multi). The evaluation is conducted on tasks with varying numbers of observed points (n ∈ [5, 10, 20, 30]), with predictions tested against 20 unseen points.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Scoring performance (correlation between estimated and ground-truth scores)', 'Regret']

experiments.Description: To evaluate a novel candidate point sampling mechanism that uses an LLM to conditionally generate candidates based on a desired objective value.
experiments.Method: The proposed LLAMBO sampler is compared against TPE (Ind), TPE (Multi), and random sampling. An ablation study, LLAMBO (UnInf), which omits problem description, is also included. The effect of an exploration hyperparameter 'α' is also investigated.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Candidate point quality (average regret, best regret)', 'Candidate point diversity (generalized variance, log-likelihood)']

experiments.Description: To holistically evaluate the end-to-end performance of the LLAMBO algorithm on a continuous hyperparameter tuning benchmark, including on tasks with proprietary and synthetic datasets to check for memorization.
experiments.Method: LLAMBO (using the discriminative surrogate model) is evaluated on 25 tasks from Bayesmark and an additional 3 proprietary and 2 synthetic datasets. Baselines include GP-DKL, SKOpt (GP), Optuna (TPE), and SMAC3 (RF). All methods are initialized with 5 randomly sampled points and run for 25 trials.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the end-to-end performance of LLAMBO on the HPOBench benchmark.
experiments.Method: LLAMBO is evaluated on 24 tasks from HPOBench. Baselines include GP-DKL, SKOpt (GP), Optuna (TPE), and SMAC3.

risk_factors: While LLAMBO does not perform any finetuning, performing inference through LLMs incurs a much larger computational footprint than traditional BO algorithms.
Additionally, while we have demonstrated the potential for integrating LLM in BO with GPT-3.5, it is important to recognize the choice of LLMs can significantly influence optimization results.
Our study has primarily focused on HPT tasks, which are relatively low-dimensional.

However, a notable area for future research is the expansion LLAMBO’s application to higher-dimensional BO tasks with more complex search spaces, such as neural architecture search and robotic control [39, 69].

title: The Normalized Float Trick: Numerical Stability for Probabilistic Circuits without the LogSumExp Trick
name: normalized-float-trick
short_hypothesis: An alternative numerical stability scheme for probabilistic circuits, the 'normalized float trick' (NoFlo trick), can improve their density estimation capabilities compared to the standard LogSumExp trick, simply by changing the computation scheme.
related_work: Probabilistic circuits (PCs) are a class of tractable deep probabilistic models, but their evaluation is numerically unstable due to repeated multiplication of floating-point numbers in the [0, 1] interval, which can lead to numerical underflow. The standard approach to mitigate this is to perform computations in log-space, using the LogSumExp trick to handle summations. This paper proposes that this standard LogSumExp trick may not be the optimal method for ensuring numerical stability and that an alternative approach could lead to better performance.
abstract: Probabilistic circuits (PCs) are a class of tractable deep probabilistic models that compute event probabilities by recursively nesting sum and product computations. Unfortunately, this is numerically unstable. To mitigate this numerical stability issues, PCs are usually evaluated in log-space via the LogSumExp trick. In this paper we present an alternative to the ubiquitous LogSumExp trick, which we dub "normalized float trick".

experiments.Description: To compare the density estimation capabilities of Probabilistic Circuits (PCs) when using the proposed NoFlo trick versus the traditional LogSumExp trick.
experiments.Method: PCs will be trained by maximizing the log-likelihood on a density estimation task. Two different circuit structures, 'neighbor' and 'cross', will be evaluated. The models will be tested with varying numbers of components per partition (64, 128, 256). The baseline for comparison is the same PC architecture and training setup but using the LogSumExp trick for numerical stability. The experiments will be run on the MNIST, FashionMNIST, and EMNIST datasets.

experiments.Description: To measure and compare the computational costs (wall clock time and memory usage) of the NoFlo trick and the LogSumExp trick during training.
experiments.Method: The wall clock time and peak memory usage for one training epoch will be measured on the MNIST dataset. The experiment will use a PC with the 'cross' structure and will be repeated for different numbers of components per partition. The baseline is the LogSumExp trick implementation.

experiments.Description: To investigate the root cause of performance differences by evaluating models trained with one numerical stability trick using the evaluation scheme of the other trick.
experiments.Method: An ablation study will be performed on the MNIST dataset. First, a PC will be trained using the NoFlo trick, and then evaluated on the test set using both the NoFlo trick and the LogSumExp trick. Second, a PC will be trained using the LogSumExp trick and also evaluated using both tricks. This process will be repeated for varying numbers of components per partition and also with 64-bit precision to test for numerical precision issues.

risk_factors: However, it is also unclear where exactly this increase in performance exactly originates from. We leave the resolution of this open question to future work.

title: The Adversarial Regulation of the Temporal Difference Loss Costs More Than Expected
name: adversarial-rl-q-value-inaccuracy
short_hypothesis: Adversarial training techniques, which add a worst-case regularization term to the Q-learning update, induce significant changes in the learned Q-function. Specifically, this approach will cause deep neural policies to learn overestimated state-action values for optimal actions and will reduce the accuracy of Q-values for non-optimal actions, potentially to the point where their relative ranking is lost.
related_work: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) policies are known to be vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, similar to vulnerabilities in image classification models. To address this, prior work has proposed methods to increase robustness, often by explicitly regulating the temporal difference (TD) loss for worst-case sensitivity (e.g., using a state-adversarial MDP formulation). However, the effects and potential trade-offs of these adversarial training techniques on the learned state-action value function itself are not well understood.
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning research has enabled reaching significant performance levels for sequential decision making in MDPs with highly complex observations and state dynamics with the aid of deep neural networks. However, this aid came with a cost that is inherent to deep neural networks which have increased sensitivities towards indistinguishable peculiarly crafted non-robust directions. To alleviate these sensitivities several studies suggested techniques to cope with this problem via explicitly regulating the temporal difference loss for the worst-case sensitivity. This study aims to show that these worst-case regularization techniques come with a cost that intriguingly causes inconsistencies and overestimations in the state-action value functions. The goal is to reveal foundational intrinsic properties of the adversarial training techniques and demonstrate the need to rethink the approach to robustness in deep reinforcement learning.

experiments.Description: To systematically analyze and compare the state-action value functions learned by vanilla trained deep neural policies and state-of-the-art adversarially trained deep neural policies. The investigation is designed to assess the accuracy of Q-values for non-optimal actions, the degree of Q-value overestimation, and the consistency of action rankings.
experiments.Method: The experiments are to be conducted in high-dimensional state representation MDPs from the Arcade Learning Environment (ALE), specifically on the BankHeist, RoadRunner, and Freeway games. A 'vanilla trained deep neural policy' (Double Deep Q-Network, DDQN, with prioritized experience replay) will serve as the baseline. This will be compared against a 'state-of-the-art adversarially trained deep neural policy' (State-Adversarial Double Deep Q-Network, SA-DDQN, with prioritized experience replay).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: [{'Name': 'Accuracy of Non-Optimal Actions via Performance Drop', 'Description': "A 'performance drop' curve, P_i(p), will be generated by forcing the agent to take the i-th best action (according to its Q-values) in a randomly sampled p-fraction of states and measuring the resulting drop in performance. The curves for vanilla and adversarial agents will be compared, particularly for the second-best (a_2) and worst (a_w) actions. The area under the curve (AUC) of these performance drops will also be compared."}, {'Name': 'Q-Value Overestimation', 'Description': 'The Q-values for the optimal action, Q(s, a*), will be plotted and averaged over states for both agent types to compare their magnitudes.'}, {'Name': 'Action Ranking Consistency', 'Description': 'The relative ordering of performance drop curves (e.g., comparing P_2(p) and P_w(p)) will be checked to determine if the Q-value ranking of suboptimal actions is meaningful and consistent with actual performance degradation.'}, {'Name': 'Action Gap Analysis', 'Description': 'The action gap, defined as the difference between the state-action value of the optimal action and the second-ranked action, will be measured and analyzed in relation to Q-value overestimation to test the hypothesis that a larger action gap reduces overestimation.'}]

title: LLM Microscope: What Model Internals Reveal About Answer Correctness and Context Utilization
name: internals-for-correctness-and-context
short_hypothesis: The internal activations of a large language model contain discernible signals that can be used to predict the correctness of its generated answers and to evaluate the efficacy of provided external context, without relying on fragile prompting-based self-evaluation.
related_work: Current methods for estimating LLM confidence and context efficacy are challenging. Confidence estimation is difficult as LLMs are often poorly calibrated. Existing approaches either rely on fragile self-evaluation via prompting or require complex setups for narrow tasks. Similarly, methods for evaluating retrieved context, such as SelfRAG, depend on fine-tuning or prompting external models. A lightweight method to directly assess context utility from a model's internal states is currently missing.
abstract: Although large language models (LLMs) have tremendous utility, trustworthiness is still a chief concern: models often generate incorrect information with high confidence. While contextual information can help guide generation, identifying when a query would benefit from retrieved context and assessing the effectiveness of that context remains challenging. In this work, we operationalize interpretability methods to ascertain whether we can predict the correctness of model outputs from the model's activations alone. We also explore whether model internals contain signals about the efficacy of external context. We consider correct, incorrect, and irrelevant context and introduce metrics to distinguish amongst them.

experiments.Description: To determine if the correctness of an LLM's generated answer can be estimated from its internal model states alone (RQ1). This is framed as a binary classification task to predict whether a generated answer is correct.
experiments.Method: A random forest classifier will be trained using features extracted from the internal activations of several LLMs (LLaMA-3-8B, LLaMA-2-7B/13B, Qwen-2.5-3B/7B, Gemma-2-9B) on the TriviaQA and MMLU datasets. The features to be tested include raw hidden states, and features derived from Logit Lens, Tuned Lens, and Parametric Knowledge Scores (PKS). This approach will be compared against prompting-based baselines ('Prompting with/without Answer') and a majority-class baseline.

experiments.Description: To determine if the efficacy of a given external context can be estimated directly from the model's internal states (RQ2).
experiments.Method: On the TriviaQA dataset, the model's response to questions with different types of context (correct, incorrect, irrelevant) will be analyzed. The proposed internals-based metrics, External Context Score (ECS) and Parametric Knowledge Score (PKS), will be combined into a proxy for contextual log-likelihood gain (Ψ(Q, C)). This will be used to distinguish between the context types and compared against prompting-based confidence estimation baselines.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Contextual log-likelihood gain', 'Contextual relative utility', 'Proportion of examples where the method correctly differentiates between context types']

risk_factors: Several of our analyses are performed either in aggregate across token positions or only at the first output token.
Although the TriviaQA and MMLU datasets cover diverse domains, we restrict our analysis to short-answer factual question-answering for computational feasibility.
It is valuable to study the generalizability of our results to different tasks and domains; we leave this for future work.
Second, we only experiment with instruction-tuned models.
In addition, experiments with larger models (> 32B) may yield more generalizable results.

title: Bi-Level Dynamic Parameter Sharing among Individuals and Teams for Promoting Collaborations in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
name: bi-level-dynamic-parameter-sharing
short_hypothesis: A bi-level dynamic parameter sharing mechanism (BDPS) can promote effective collaborations in multi-agent systems. At the individual level, virtual dynamic roles are defined based on agents' long-term cumulative advantages, and parameters are shared among agents in the same role. At the team level, agents of different virtual roles are combined into groups, and parameters are shared within the same group. This bi-level approach is hypothesized to create a dynamic balance between agent individuality and commonality, enabling them to learn more complex and complementary collaborative relationships.
related_work: Current parameter sharing mechanisms in multi-agent reinforcement learning are often static and indiscriminate, ignoring the dynamic environments and different roles of agents. This limits the diversity of agent behaviors. While single-level selective parameter sharing can promote diversity, it often fragments the relationships between agents that do not share parameters, making it difficult to establish complementary and cooperative strategies. The research gap lies in developing a mechanism that can dynamically balance agent individuality with team-level cooperation to foster more complex collaborations.
abstract: Parameter sharing has greatly contributed to the success of multi-agent reinforcement learning in recent years. However, most existing parameter sharing mechanisms are static, and parameters are indiscriminately shared among individuals, ignoring the dynamic environments and different roles of multiple agents. In addition, although a single-level selective parameter sharing mechanism can promote the diversity of strategies, it is hard to establish complementary and cooperative relationships between agents. To address these issues, we propose a bi-level dynamic parameter sharing mechanism among individuals and teams for promoting effective collaborations (BDPS). Specifically, at the individual level, we define virtual dynamic roles based on the long-term cumulative advantages of agents and share parameters among agents in the same role. At the team level, we combine agents of different virtual roles and share parameters of agents in the same group. Through the joint efforts of these two levels, we aim to achieve a dynamic balance between the individuality and commonality of agents, enabling agents to learn more complex and complementary collaborative relationships.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed BDPS method against baseline algorithms on challenging micromanagement tasks in the StarCraft II environment.
experiments.Method: The proposed method (BDPS) will be compared with QMIX (with and without full parameter sharing), CDS, EOI, and RODE on a set of hard and super hard maps from the StarCraft II Multi-agent Challenge (SMAC) benchmark, including 'bane_vs_bane', 'MMM2', '5m_vs_6m', '3s_vs_5z', '2c_vs_64zg', '27m_vs_30m', '6h_vs_8z', 'corridor', and '3s5z_vs_3s6z'.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed BDPS method in a partially observable, multi-agent football environment.
experiments.Method: The proposed method (BDPS) will be compared with QMIX (with and without full parameter sharing), CDS, EOI, and RODE in the Google Research Football (GRF) environment. The specific scenarios selected for comparison are 'academy_3_vs_1_with_keeper' and 'academy_pass_and_shoot_with_keeper' in a custom Dec-POMDP setting.

experiments.Description: To verify the effectiveness of the dynamic parameter sharing mechanism for forming complementary relationships in a sparse-reward, high-collaboration-demand environment.
experiments.Method: The performance of different parameter sharing mechanisms will be compared in the Level-Based Foraging (LBF) environment on the '15x15-4p-5f' task. The methods to be compared are bi-layer dynamic parameter sharing (ours), single-layer dynamic parameter sharing (ours with only individual level), full static parameter sharing (classic QMIX), and no parameter sharing (QMIX with non-sharing).

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the utility of each level (individual and team) in the bi-level dynamic parameter sharing mechanism and to analyze the impact of the number of virtual roles.
experiments.Method: Ablation studies will be performed on the SMAC maps '5m_vs_6m' and 'MMM2'. The full BDPS method will be compared against a variant with only the individual-level sharing mechanism, as well as full and no parameter sharing baselines. The effect of varying the number of virtual roles (k) will also be investigated.

risk_factors: Although we defined that agents have the opportunity to be in any role or grouping, they cannot explicitly utilize past knowledge when they were in other roles or groups.
Therefore, how to make explicit use of the role knowledge they have learned and how to make use of other similar knowledge are issues that we need to explore further.

title: A Dense Subset Index for Collective Query Coverage
name: disco-collective-query-coverage
short_hypothesis: By formulating collective retrieval as a submodular coverage maximization problem, it is possible to design an efficient, sublinear-time dense index (DISCO) that retrieves a near-optimal set of covering items. This is achieved by iteratively editing query vectors to account for current coverage and using random projections of a novel, lifted dense vector space to make the marginal gain objective amenable to indexing.
related_work: Traditional dense retrieval methods (e.g., ColBERT) rank items independently based on a relevance score like MaxSim, treating them as competitors. This is suboptimal for complex queries (e.g., multi-hop QA) where information from multiple items must be combined. While maximizing collective coverage can be framed as a submodular optimization problem solvable by a greedy algorithm, this approach is prohibitively expensive for large corpora as it requires linear scans at each step. Other approaches like diversity-based reranking are post-processing steps vulnerable to recall loss in the first stage. This work proposes a new formulation and a scalable algorithm for collective first-stage retrieval that is efficient enough for large corpora.
abstract: In many recent retrieval scenarios such as multi-hop question answering (QA), table QA, or text-to-SQL, items are not self-complete and must collaborate to respond to a query. In dense retrieval, this translates to finding a small collection of corpus items whose contextual word vectors collectively cover the query's contextual word vectors. The central challenge is to retrieve a near-optimal collection of covering items in sublinear time. By establishing coverage as a submodular objective, we propose a method that enables successive dense index probes to quickly assemble an item collection. Successive query vectors are iteratively 'edited' to account for current coverage, and the dense index is built using random projections of a novel, lifted dense vector space, making it amenable to approximate nearest neighbor search.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the trade-off between the average coverage achieved and the query processing efficiency compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
experiments.Method: The proposed method, DISCO, will be compared against two classes of baselines: (1) Submodular optimization solvers: Exact Greedy, Lazy Greedy, Stochastic Greedy, Lazier-than-lazy Greedy; and (2) IR-domain retrieval methods: PLAID, MUVERA, WARP. Experiments will be run on subsets of the BEIR (MS-Marco, HotpotQA, Fever) and LOTTE (Pooled, Science, Technology, Writing) benchmarks.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average coverage objective F(SK, Q)', 'Efficiency (defined as the ratio of per-query time of Exact Greedy to the per-query time of the method)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the suitability of the proposed coverage objective F(S,Q) by measuring its alignment with annotated ground-truth relevant item sets (S_gold) on a multi-hop QA dataset.
experiments.Method: On the HotpotQA dataset, the set of items retrieved by DISCO and baselines (Exact Greedy, Lazy Greedy, Stochastic Greedy, Lazier-than-lazy Greedy, PLAID, MUVERA, WARP) will be compared against the ground-truth gold sets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Error(F) (average deviation between the coverage of the gold set and the retrieved set)', 'Mean Average Precision (MAP)', 'Histogram of the rank of the last relevant item retrieved']

experiments.Description: To assess the quality of the proposed approximate marginal gain function (Gw1:R) by comparing the coverage obtained using it against the exact marginal gain.
experiments.Method: The coverage trajectory of the greedy algorithm using the proposed approximation (Algorithm 2) will be compared to the trajectory of the Exact Greedy algorithm (Algorithm 1) on the NFCorpus and Writing datasets. The number of random hyperplanes (R) will be varied.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average coverage F(SK, Q) vs. number of items K']

experiments.Description: An ablation study to justify the architectural choice of early pooling in DISCO by comparing its performance against a simpler late pooling alternative.
experiments.Method: The standard DISCO model (with early pooling) will be compared against a variant that processes each of the R projected queries independently and merges the results at the end (late pooling). The comparison will be performed on the MS-Marco, Pooled, Fever, HotpotQA, Technology, and Writing datasets.

experiments.Description: To assess the performance of DISCO on the downstream task of question-answering, where retrieved documents are used as context for a large language model.
experiments.Method: A two-stage pipeline will be used. First, DISCO will retrieve the top-K corpus items for a given query. Second, a Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct LLM will be prompted to answer the question using only the retrieved items as context. The number of retrieved items K will be varied (3, 5, 10). The evaluation will be conducted on the 2WikiMultihopQA and Musique datasets.

risk_factors: Our proposed coverage objective does not account for notions of fairness, e.g. group fairness or individual fairness (Zehlike et al., 2022).
As of now, we have focused only on coverage maximization. We do not incorporate diversity explicitly in the model
DISCO is not currently designed to handle evolving corpora. In all datasets, the corpus is fixed, and indexing is done once, with no further updates.

title: Delta-XAI: A Unified Framework for Explaining Prediction Changes in Online Time Series Monitoring
name: swing-for-temporal-explanations
short_hypothesis: By extending Integrated Gradients (IG) to incorporate past observations into the integration path, it will be possible to systematically capture temporal dependencies and mitigate out-of-distribution (OOD) effects. This new method, Shifted Window Integrated Gradients (SWING), is proposed to yield faithful and interpretable attributions for prediction changes in online time series monitoring, while satisfying key theoretical properties like completeness and implementation invariance.
related_work: Existing explainable AI (XAI) methods for time series mostly analyze each time step independently, overlooking temporal dependencies. This makes it difficult to explain prediction changes over time, as these methods fail to leverage online dynamics and overlook contextual information. Furthermore, common evaluation metrics are often unsuitable for evolving prediction settings, as they can exhibit limited correlation with attribution quality.
abstract: Explaining online time series monitoring models is crucial across sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance, where temporal and contextual prediction dynamics underpin critical decisions. While recent XAI methods have improved the explainability of time series models, they mostly analyze each time step independently, overlooking temporal dependencies. This results in further challenges: explaining prediction changes is non-trivial, methods fail to leverage online dynamics, and evaluation remains difficult. To address these challenges, we propose Delta-XAI, which adapts 14 existing XAI methods through a wrapper function and introduces a principled evaluation suite for the online setting. Building on the observation that classical gradient-based methods can outperform recent approaches when adapted for temporal analysis, we propose Shifted Window Integrated Gradients (SWING), which incorporates past observations in the integration path to systematically capture temporal dependencies and mitigate out-of-distribution effects.

experiments.Description: To quantitatively evaluate the faithfulness and sufficiency of SWING's explanations for prediction changes against existing time series XAI baselines on large-scale clinical datasets.
experiments.Method:
Datasets/Tasks: MIMIC-III for decompensation prediction and PhysioNet 2019 for early sepsis detection.
Model Architectures: Primarily LSTM, with additional evaluations on CNN and Transformer backbones to show versatility.
Baselines: LIME, GradSHAP, IG, DeepLIFT, FO, AFO, FIT, WinIT, Dynamask, ExtrMask, ContraLSP, TimeX, TimeX++, TIMING.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: A proposed suite of metrics including Cumulative Prediction Difference (CPD), Cumulative Prediction Preservation (CPP), Area Under Prediction Difference/Preservation (AUPD, AUPP), Macro Prediction Difference/Preservation (MPD, MPP), Area Under Macro Prediction Difference/Preservation (AUMPD, AUMPP), and correlation (Corr.).

experiments.Description: To assess the generalizability of SWING on a real-world human activity dataset and synthetic benchmarks with controlled temporal dynamics.
experiments.Method:
Datasets/Tasks: Activity dataset for human activity recognition, and two synthetic benchmarks: Delayed Spike and Switch-Feature for binary classification.
Model Architecture: LSTM.
Baselines: The same set of 14 baselines used in the clinical data experiments.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The same suite of faithfulness and sufficiency metrics (CPD, AUPD, MPD, AUMPD, CPP, AUPP, MPP, AUMPP, Corr.).

experiments.Description: To examine the contribution of each component of SWING (retrospective baseline selection, piecewise-linear historical integration, dual-path integration) to its overall performance through an ablation study.
experiments.Method:
Dataset/Task: MIMIC-III decompensation prediction benchmark.
Model Architecture: LSTM.
Method Variants: SWING with and without its core components (RBS, PHI, DPI) and with varying baseline offset distances.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The same suite of faithfulness and sufficiency metrics (CPD, AUPD, MPD, AUMPD, CPP, AUPP, MPP, AUMPP, Corr.).

experiments.Description: To qualitatively assess whether SWING produces temporally coherent and clinically plausible explanations through case studies.
experiments.Method:
Dataset/Task: MIMIC-III decompensation risk cases.
Model Architecture: LSTM.
Analysis: Visual inspection of attribution heatmaps generated by SWING and baseline methods, and coherence analysis to check if attributions align with established clinical knowledge.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Qualitative assessment of attribution sharpness, localization, and alignment with domain knowledge (clinical plausibility).

experiments.Description: To analyze SWING's performance under diverse settings, including different model architectures, longer time intervals between explanations, and its computational characteristics.
experiments.Method:
Dataset/Task: MIMIC-III decompensation prediction benchmark.
Settings: 1) Evaluate using CNN and Transformer backbones. 2) Evaluate with larger time gaps between predictions (T2 - T1 = 6 and 24). 3) Measure runtime and memory usage.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Faithfulness and sufficiency metrics for architecture and time gap analysis. Runtime (sec/sample) and peak memory (MB/sample) for efficiency analysis.

risk_factors: First, while SWING extends IG by incorporating historical points with shifted window paths, it introduces additional computational overhead compared to simpler methods like standard IG.

Second, while our prediction wrapper function can seamlessly incorporate most existing single-time XAI algorithms, a few require minor adjustments—particularly those that rely on probability outputs or internal model representations.

title: Wirbelsäule-Plot for Multivariate Timeseries customized with AIP Agent
name: aip-agent-controlled-timeseries-visualization
short_hypothesis: A new approach can be developed to visualize multivariate time series with qualitative explanatory features by treating both task-specific instructions (prompts) and raw time series data as multimodal inputs to an AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) Agent, which in turn interactively controls the visualization.
related_work: Time series analysis and forecasting typically present outputs as numerical data or sentence summaries. For specific domains like athlete evaluation, traditional methods involve Factor Analysis based on checklists of warning flags, where specialists make decisions once a threshold of negative signs is passed. This approach is contrasted with the proposed solution, which aims to provide a more comprehensive, interactive, and visual tool for analysis.
abstract: Growing interest in time series analytics and forecasting leads to demand in visualization including qualitative explanatory features at point in time. Enablement of prompt-specific instructions as inputs creates new horizons in implementation of interactive charts controlled by AIP Agents. In this paper we outline a new approach to visualize the multi-variate time-series with explanatory features at point in time. Palantir Foundry is a platform for this task, equipped with AIP-powered capabilities. The proposed solution combines AIP Agents and a customizable vega chart, the Wirbelsäule-Plot, to create a valuable resource for conducting comprehensive analysis and interactive visualization for decision making systems.

experiments.Description: To develop and evaluate an interactive visualization system, the 'Wirbelsäule-Plot', for multivariate time series data of a study subject (e.g., an athlete), controlled by an AIP Agent via natural language prompts.
experiments.Method: The proposed system will be developed through several tasks. First, data sources will be decomposed into a timeline ontology structure with attributes like Event ID, Type, and Label, converting life events into a continuous sequence of tokens using Modality Encoding. Second, a system for controlled styling will be developed to dynamically generate colors, shapes, and labels for different event types and subjects to ensure they are distinctively recognizable. Third, a GPT-4.1 LLM will be used to pre-train content, generating human-readable tooltips from input notes for each point in time. A supervised model will also be trained to tokenize events of interest. Finally, an AIP Agent will be engineered with prompts to connect to the ontology objects and control the Vega Chart visualization, allowing users to query and visualize data interactively.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Qualitative assessment of the system's ability to provide comprehensive and interactive visualization", 'Effectiveness in supporting decision-making and answering user queries']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the clustering of different subjects based on their time series data.
experiments.Method: The Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) algorithm will be employed to assess the valuation of clusters, which are operated by a Similarity Score.

risk_factors: Implementation has number of limitations. First, datasets reliance human reporting and quality of notes collected by industry professionals.
Second, AIP Agent relies on large data source which can be costly, thus breakdown into multi-Agent approach is required.

title: Towards Noise‐Robust Multi‐Agent Imitation Learning via Global Credit Sequence Decoding
name: global-credit-sequence-decoding
short_hypothesis: A sequential autoregressive architecture that models global dependencies among agents within a generative adversarial imitation learning framework will facilitate more adaptive credit assignment and robust policy optimization, especially in the presence of noisy demonstrations, by enhancing the variance of joint advantages and rewards.
related_work: Classical multi-agent imitation learning (MAIL) methods, such as MAGAIL, extend single-agent approaches by matching distributions from expert demonstrations. However, they often assume agent contributions are independent and linearly additive, which neglects complex dependencies. This leads to issues like poor credit assignment, insufficient reward variance, and vanishing policy gradients, especially when expert demonstrations contain noisy or suboptimal actions.
abstract: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) is a promising approach for complex decision-making problems. To avoid designing complex reward functions, researchers often use imitation learning. Classical methods extend single-agent imitation learning to multi-agent settings by matching distributions from expert demonstrations. However, noisy or low-quality trajectories can mislead joint policy optimization and degrade performance. This study introduces a sequential autoregressive architecture that models global dependencies among agents to facilitate adaptive credit assignment and policy optimization. The architecture is intended to enhance the variance of joint advantages and rewards, addressing issues like vanishing gradients and mode collapse.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed MILD² method on discrete action space, multi-agent cooperative tasks in the StarCraftII environment.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the StarCraftII Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) benchmark on tasks including '3m', '3s5z', '6h vs 8z', and 'MMM2'. Performance will be compared against baselines: MAGAIL, CQL-MA, ICQ-MA, TD3-BC, and OMAR. Expert data is to be collected from a pre-trained HAPPO policy.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed MILD² method on discrete action space, multi-agent cooperative tasks in the Football environment.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Google Research Football benchmark on tasks including '3 vs 1', 'counterattack', and 'pass and shoot'. Performance will be compared against baselines: MAGAIL, CQL-MA, ICQ-MA, TD3-BC, and OMAR. Expert data is to be collected from a pre-trained HAPPO policy.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed MILD² method on continuous action space, bimanual dexterous manipulation tasks.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Bimanual Dexterous Hands Manipulation (Bi-DexHands) benchmark on tasks including 'CatchOver2Underarm', 'DoorOpenInward', and 'DoorCloseOutward'. Performance will be compared against baselines: MAGAIL, CQL-MA, ICQ-MA, TD3-BC, and OMAR. Expert data is to be collected from a pre-trained HAPPO policy.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed MILD² method on continuous action space, multi-agent locomotion tasks.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the Multi-Agent MuJoCo benchmark on the 'HalfCheetah 6x1' task. Performance will be compared against baselines: MAGAIL, CQL-MA, ICQ-MA, TD3-BC, and OMAR. Expert data is to be collected from a pre-trained HAPPO policy.

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the effectiveness of capturing global dependencies by comparing the full model to a variant that lacks this mechanism.
experiments.Method: The proposed MILD² model will be compared against an ablation variant, MILD²-GD, which uses independent individual distribution matching. The comparison will be performed on the '6h_vs_8z' (discrete actions) and 'DoorOpenInward' (continuous actions) tasks.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance and resilience in noisy environments.
experiments.Method: The model's performance will be evaluated using a mixed dataset combining expert and medium-score policy data at varying noise ratios. Performance will be compared against MAGAIL and ICQ-MA on the '6h vs. 8z' scenario.

risk_factors: More other explicit agent dependency structures could explored in future work.

title: Interval-based Offline Policy Evaluation without Sufficient Exploration or Realizability
subfield: Probabilistic Methods and Bayesian Deep Learning
name: minimax_bias_opi
short_hypothesis: In offline policy evaluation settings that lack sufficient exploration or realizability, where point-based estimators suffer from irreducible bias, we propose an alternative formulation called minimax-bias offline policy interval estimation (minimax-bias OPI). The objective is to estimate the shortest possible interval containing the true policy value by characterizing the minimax bias and developing a new interval-based marginal importance sampling (MIS) estimator that asymptotically achieves it.
related_work: Existing offline policy interval estimation (OPI) methods fall into two categories. Non-asymptotic methods often require strong, impractical assumptions like realizability with known complexity. Asymptotic methods typically assume both sufficient exploration and realizability, allowing them to focus on estimating variance while assuming bias is negligible. This makes them inapplicable to settings where an irreducible, asymptotic bias of order O(1) is dominant due to the violation of these assumptions. This paper aims to develop a theoretically-sound value estimator without assuming sufficient exploration or realizability.
abstract: We study the problem of offline policy evaluation (OPE), where the goal is to estimate the value of a given decision-making policy without interacting with the actual environment. In particular, we consider interval-based OPE, where the output is an interval rather than a point, indicating the uncertainty of the evaluation. This is especially important in OPE since, when data coverage is insufficient, any OPE method can be biased even with infinite samples. We characterize this irreducible bias in terms of the discrepancy between the target policy and the data-sampling distribution. We then show that the marginal-importance-sampling (MIS) estimator can achieve the minimax bias with an appropriate importance-weight function. Motivated by this, we propose a new interval-based MIS estimator designed to asymptotically achieve this minimax bias.

experiments.Description: To theoretically validate the proposed minimax optimal interval estimation (MOI) method by proving its key properties under settings without sufficient exploration or realizability. The goal is to show that the proposed estimator is well-posed and can provide a valid and optimally tight interval estimate.
experiments.Method: The validation is planned as a series of theoretical proofs. First, the information-theoretic worst-case bias (minimax bias) of any point-based OPE estimator will be characterized. This characterization will be linked to the distributional Bellman residual (DBR) of a marginal importance sampling (MIS) estimator. Based on this, a new problem, minimax-bias OPI, will be formulated to estimate an optimal interval defined by the optimal point estimator and its minimax bias. Finally, an algorithm, MOI, will be proposed to solve this problem by finding the best importance weight function via minimizing an approximation of the DBR's total variation (TV) norm.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Asymptotic validity: The estimated interval must be proven to contain the true policy value in the large sample limit.', 'Approximate optimality: The estimated interval must be proven to converge to the theoretically optimal (shortest) interval, with the convergence rate dependent on a realizability error term.']

risk_factors: One of the major limitations of the proposed method is its model agnosticity, lying at the opposite end to the model-based approach, e.g., Yu et al. (2020), that depends on the full correctness of the model.
It is left for future work to extend and combine these methods to be applicable to partially correct models.

title: Multi-objective Optimization by Learning Space Partition
name: LaMOO
short_hypothesis: A multi-objective optimizer can be made more sample-efficient by learning to partition the search space into promising and non-promising regions based on the dominance number of observed samples. This partitioning can guide an underlying solver to focus on regions likely to contain the Pareto frontier, while using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to balance exploration and exploitation.
related_work: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) is commonly addressed by Bayesian Optimization (BO) methods like qEHVI and Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs) like NSGA-II and CMA-ES. While effective, these methods can be sample-inefficient or get stuck in local optima. Recent work in single-objective optimization (SOO) has shown success in learning to partition the search space to guide optimization. However, this data-driven space partitioning approach has not been extended to the multi-objective case. This work aims to fill that gap by proposing a meta-algorithm that learns space partitions for MOO.
abstract: In contrast to single-objective optimization (SOO), multi-objective optimization (MOO) requires an optimizer to find the Pareto frontier, a subset of feasible solutions that are not dominated by other feasible solutions. In this paper, we propose LaMOO, a novel multi-objective optimizer that learns a model from observed samples to partition the search space and then focus on promising regions that are likely to contain a subset of the Pareto frontier. The partitioning is based on the dominance number, which measures “how close" a data point is to the Pareto frontier among existing samples. To account for possible partition errors due to limited samples and model mismatch, we leverage Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to exploit promising regions while exploring suboptimal regions that may turn out to contain good solutions later. Theoretically, we plan to prove the efficacy of learning space partitioning via LaMOO under certain assumptions.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the performance of LaMOO on a diverse set of small-scale synthetic and real-world MOO problems, including synthetic functions, vehicle safety design, and neural architecture search.
experiments.Method:
LaMOO will be tested as a meta-algorithm wrapping two inner solvers: qEHVI and CMA-ES. Its performance will be compared against several baselines.

Tasks:
- Synthetic Functions: Branin-Currin (2D input, 2 objectives) and DTLZ2 (18D input, 2 objectives; and 12D input, 10 objectives).
- Vehicle Safety: A real-world problem with 5D input and 3 objectives.
- Neural Architecture Search: Nasbench201, optimizing for FLOPs and accuracy (2 objectives).

Baselines:
- Evolutionary Algorithms: CMA-ES, MOEA/D, NSGA-II, NSGA-III.
- Bayesian Optimization Methods: qEHVI, qParego.

experiments.Description: Evaluate LaMOO on the high-dimensional, practical problem of multi-objective molecular generation, where molecules must satisfy multiple property constraints.
experiments.Method:
LaMOO will be used to propose samples from a pre-trained 32-dimensional latent space to optimize for various combinations of molecular properties.

Tasks:
1. Two objectives: Activity against GSK3β and JNK3.
2. Three objectives: QED, SA, and activity against SARS.
3. Four objectives: Activity against GSK3β and JNK3, plus QED and SA.

Baselines: qEHVI, qParego, NSGA-II, NSGA-III, CMA-ES, MOEAD.

experiments.Description: Conduct ablation studies on the VehicleSafety benchmark to understand the impact of LaMOO's key design choices.
experiments.Method:
The experiment will systematically vary components of the LaMOO algorithm.

Ablations:
1. Sampling Methods: Compare the performance of LaMOO when using qEHVI, CMA-ES, or random sampling as the inner sampler.
2. Exploration Factor (Cp): Evaluate the effect of different Cp values, including a greedy search (Cp=0), to assess the importance of MCTS-based exploration.
3. SVM Kernels: Test the performance of different SVM kernels (linear, polynomial, RBF) for the space partitioning classifier.

risk_factors: Here we use discrete case for simplicity and leave continuous case (i.e., partitioning a region Ωο instead of a discrete set So) to future work.
In the general case, characterizing Ωp is analytically hard and requires domain knowledge about the objectives (Li et al., 2014).

title: Learning Task-Invariant Features in VLMs via Dynamic Bayesian IRM
name: dynamic-birm-for-vlms
short_hypothesis: Adapting Bayesian Invariant Risk Minimization (BIRM) for generative VLMs and introducing a dynamic regularization coefficient that adjusts the invariance penalty throughout training will mitigate regularization decay, maintain an optimal balance between empirical and invariant risk, and lead to the learning of more robust, task-invariant features.
related_work: Visual Language Models (VLMs) trained with standard Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) often rely on spurious correlations and fail on out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks. Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) aims to learn invariant predictors but suffers from practical limitations like regularization decay in deep networks. Bayesian IRM (BIRM) mitigates some of these issues but was designed for discriminative models, has not been adapted for generative multimodal architectures, and still relies on a fixed regularization coefficient, which can lead to suboptimal invariance learning as the invariance penalty becomes ineffective during training.
abstract: While Visual Language Models (VLMs) excel on multimodal tasks, they suffer from performance degradation under distribution shift, particularly when facing out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks not seen during training. Traditional Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) often fails to learn task-invariant features, relying instead on spurious correlations. Although Invariant Risk Minimization (IRM) offers a solution, its application to generative multimodal settings remains unexplored, and it suffers from regularization decay in deep networks. We bridge this gap by adapting Bayesian IRM (BIRM) for generative VLMs. We formalize "environments" in multimodal data through task types (e.g., VQA, Captioning, OCR), treating distribution shift as a shift between tasks. To address regularization decay, we propose Dynamic BIRM — an algorithm that adaptively adjusts the invariance penalty strength throughout training, maintaining an optimal balance between empirical and invariant risk.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the proposed Dynamic BIRM method improves robustness to task-based distribution shifts in VLMs compared to standard training and static BIRM, and to analyze the impact of the dynamic regularization coefficient on training stability and performance.
experiments.Method: The SmolVLM-Base-2B model will be instruction-tuned on the LLaVA-OneVision dataset. Environments are defined by task type. The in-domain training and testing tasks include General (scene description), Math/Reasoning, and Doc/Chart/Screen. The out-of-domain (OOD) test task is OCR (text recognition). The proposed Dynamic BIRM method will be compared against two baselines: standard Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) and static Bayesian IRM (BIRM) with a fixed regularization coefficient.

risk_factors: Computational Overhead: Dynamic BIRM introduces an increase in training time and memory usage due to the additional invariance penalty computation.
Manual Environment Specification: The method requires predefining environments based on task type, which may be non-trivial for complex multimodal datasets and necessitates domain knowledge.

title: Contact-VLA: Zero-Shot Planning and Control for Contact-Rich Manipulation
name: coral-llm-adaptive-control
short_hypothesis: A modular framework, CORAL, that integrates foundation models (a VLM for perception and an LLM for reasoning) with a reactive motion controller (MPPI) can achieve zero-shot planning and adaptive execution for complex, contact-rich manipulation tasks. This is achieved by explicitly separating the roles of the vision and language models, where the LLM generates initial contact strategies and formulates the controller's cost function, enabling adaptation without extensive tele-operated datasets.
related_work: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are often monolithic, data-dependent, and struggle with novel contact-rich scenarios. While some approaches decouple reasoning from control, they often still rely on learned policies or use foundation models in a limited perceptual guidance role. Other methods tackle contact-rich tasks by augmenting policies with physical sensors like force/tactile data, but this requires collecting large-scale, specialized demonstration datasets. The proposed research aims to bridge this gap by using an LLM to directly formulate a controller's cost function and high-level strategy, grounding abstract reasoning in a formal control problem and enabling zero-shot adaptation to physical interactions without prior demonstration data.
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) systems often lack adaptability and explainability due to their black-box structure and dependency on fixed action sets from extensive tele-operated datasets, limiting their effectiveness in complex, dynamic manipulation scenarios. To address this issue, we propose Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based Control (CoRAL), a novel modular framework capable of effectively managing complex, dynamic, and contact-rich manipulation tasks. By integrating foundational vision and language models with motion planning and reactive controllers, our system achieves zero-shot planning and adaptive manipulation without relying on extensive tele-operated action datasets. Unlike conventional VLAs, we explicitly separate the roles of vision models and Large Language Models (LLM): the vision module handles environmental parameter initialization and object pose tracking, while the LLM generates initial contact strategies and cost function estimations. This collaboration establishes a physical understanding of the scene, instantiated as a dynamic planning world model for our planner. Additionally, this modular approach is intended to significantly enhance both the explainability and performance of the overall framework. Furthermore, we introduce a memory unit to leverage past manipulation experiences, enabling the generalization and efficient reuse of learned contact strategies and parameter adjustments across diverse manipulation scenarios.

experiments.Description: To evaluate how CORAL performs on complex, contact-rich manipulation tasks in a zero-shot setting compared to state-of-the-art baselines (RQ1).
experiments.Method: Tasks: Six challenging, contact-rich manipulation tasks (T1: Push and Pick Cutting Board, T2: Pick Box, T3: Pick and Place in Clutter, T4: Push with Constant Force, T5: Flip Box, T6: Flip with Wall) and two benchmark tasks from the LIBERO suite. Model: CORAL. Baselines: State-of-the-art end-to-end VLA models OpenVLA-OFT and πο.5, using officially released checkpoints.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Success Rate (binary measure across 10 trials)', 'Average Completion Time in seconds for successful trials']

experiments.Description: To evaluate how critical each core component of the neuro-symbolic architecture is to the overall success (RQ2).
experiments.Method: Tasks: The same six custom manipulation tasks and two LIBERO tasks. The full CORAL model is compared against several ablation baselines: CORAL (w/o Pose Tracking), CORAL (w/o Memory), CORAL (w/o Refinement), and CORAL (Unified VLM). The experiment also includes two Human Expert-Designed Cost baselines (a single-stage variant and a finite-state machine variant) to provide an upper bound on what carefully engineered, task-specific objectives can achieve.

experiments.Description: To determine if the system can demonstrate robustness and adaptability by reasoning about and recovering from failures (RQ3).
experiments.Method: This involves two targeted analyses. First, an ablation on the 'Flip with Wall' task (T6) to isolate the contribution of the LLM's initial contact strategy (Co) by comparing the full framework against a variant where the MPPI planner relies on uninformed, random sampling. Second, an analysis of the 'Online Adaptation' module by intentionally initializing the 'Push and Pick Cutting Board' task with severely overestimated mass and friction to test the system's ability to correct its internal world model online.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['For contact strategy analysis: planning cost over time, end-effector path length, and execution steps.', 'For online adaptation analysis: convergence of the estimated mass and friction parameters towards their ground truth values over adaptation cycles.']

risk_factors: Fidelity of the Internal World Model: The entire framework is predicated on the quality of the simulated planning world constructed by the vision module.

Currently, the system is vulnerable to inaccuracies in object pose estimation, and VLM “hallucinations” or inaccuracies-misjudging an object's material could lead to a grossly incorrect estimate for mass or friction.
Computational Latency: Vision perception, LLM reasoning, and MPPI planning are the sources of computational latency.

Reliance on Generalist LLMs for Strategy Formulation: A core component of our system is the LLM's ability to generate a viable cost function for the MPPI planner. ... The quality and coherence of the generated cost function are not guaranteed for entirely novel or abstract tasks that fall outside the LLM's vast but general pre-training data.

title: A Discrete Actor and Critic for Reinforcement Learning on Continuous Tasks
name: discrete-actor-critic-continuous-tasks
short_hypothesis: Inspired by discrete signals in control systems (e.g., PWM), a reinforcement learning model with a discrete action space can achieve comparable performance to continuous models on continuous tasks. We propose an RL model with a multi-dimensional discrete action space, a discrete actor that outputs action distributions, and twin discrete critics for value distribution estimation, which can effectively solve continuous control problems.
related_work: Reinforcement Learning for continuous tasks typically uses models with continuous action spaces (e.g., DDPG, TD3, SAC) to avoid the curse of dimensionality faced by discrete models. Distributional RL methods (e.g., C51) model the full distribution of returns, offering stability. While some prior work has explored discretizing continuous action spaces, these approaches often face challenges like oversimplification, increased computational demands, or are limited to specific settings like on-policy or offline learning.
