This work aims to create a novel off-policy, actor-critic model that effectively combines a discrete action space with distributional value estimation for general continuous control.
abstract: Solving continuous reinforcement learning (RL) tasks typically requires models with continuous action spaces, as discrete models face challenges such as the curse of dimensionality. Inspired by discrete controlling signals in control systems, such as pulse-width modulation, we investigated RL models with discrete action spaces with performance comparable to continuous models on continuous tasks. In this paper, we propose an RL model with a discrete action space, designed a discrete actor that outputs action distributions and twin discrete critics for value distribution estimation. We also developed both the training method and exploration strategy for this model.

experiments.Description: To design a toy task to illustrate a failure mode of continuous models that average good actions into a bad one, and to hypothesize that the proposed discrete model does not have this problem.
experiments.Method: Task: A custom 'Trap or Cheese' problem where moving straight is a trap (-1.0 reward) and moving left or right leads to a cheese with a probabilistic reward (1.0 or 0.0). The proposed model will be tested against SAC.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed model on a challenging continuous control task characterized by partial observability, complex terrains, and large penalties for failure.
experiments.Method: Task: BipedalWalkerHardcore-v3. Baselines: SAC, TD3, and TQC.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Reward returns during training', 'Mean score over evaluation trials']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the necessity of the proposed model's key components: the twin critic architecture and the discrete actor.
experiments.Method: Task: BipedalWalkerHardcore-v3. The experiment will involve disabling the twin critic network (running a 'Single Critic') and substituting the discrete actor with a conventional continuous action actor ('Normal Actor').

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on a suite of standard, fully-observable continuous control tasks to assess its general applicability and learning efficiency.
experiments.Method: Tasks: MuJoCo suite, including Ant, HalfCheetah, Hopper, Humanoid, and Walker2D. Baselines: SAC, TD3, TQC.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training curves', 'Average scores in the testing environment']

risk_factors: As a preliminary trial to apply discrete actions to RL models for continuous tasks, our model may not achieve the best performance.
A possible improvement of this work is interpolation between discrete actions.

Although discretizing a continuous action space can decrease control precision and increase the difficulty of problem-solving... its learning efficiency may decrease, requiring more data and training steps.

title: RAPID: An Efficient Reinforcement Learning Algorithm for Small Language Models
name: rapid-rl-for-slms
short_hypothesis: An RL algorithm, RAPID, that alternates between performing inference in large batches and performing off-policy policy gradient updates in smaller mini-batches will substantially reduce the training runtime for finetuning small language models. This efficiency is achieved by separately optimizing batch sizes for inference and backpropagation. The off-policy bias will be corrected using an importance weighted estimator combined with group advantage estimation.
related_work: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a promising alternative to distillation for finetuning small language models (SLMs), but on-policy RL algorithms are computationally expensive as they require re-sampling after each model update. Existing efficient RL frameworks often target large-scale settings and are complementary. Off-policy RL algorithms can improve efficiency, but standard methods like PPO, which use KL-divergence regularization, may limit performance when taking many off-policy steps. This work proposes to instead build on the original policy gradient algorithm, using importance weighting for off-policy corrections, which is hypothesized to be more effective. The proposed method will also incorporate group advantage estimation, a technique shown to be effective for training language models.
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising strategy for finetuning small language models (SLMs) to solve targeted tasks such as math and coding. However, RL algorithms tend to be resource-intensive, taking a significant amount of time to train. We propose RAPID, a novel RL algorithm that can substantially reduce the running time of RL. Our key insight is that RL tends to be costly due to the need to perform both inference and backpropagation during training. To maximize use of computational resources, our algorithm performs inference in large batches, and then performs off-policy policy gradient updates in mini-batches. For off-policy updates, we incorporate group advantage estimation into the policy gradient algorithm, and derive an importance weighted estimator to correct for the bias arising from off-policy learning.

experiments.Description: To empirically evaluate RAPID's performance on accuracy and runtime against several RL baselines across different datasets, model sizes, and model families.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on three datasets: MBPP+ for coding, MATH for mathematics, and MiniF2F for formal theorem-proving. The models to be evaluated are small language models (SLMs): Qwen-2.5 (0.5B and 1.5B), Llama-3.2 1B, and Gemma-3 1B. RAPID will be compared against SFT, GRPO, Policy Gradient (PG), and DAPO baselines.

experiments.Description: To analyze the influence of off-policy sampling on sample staleness, runtime, and accuracy by varying the batch size ratio H.
experiments.Method: Run RAPID with different values for the batch size ratio H (H = Ninference/Nstep), specifically H in {2, 4, 8, 16}. The experiment will be run on the Qwen2.5-0.5B model across the MBPP+, MATH, and MiniF2F datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy (Pass@1, Pass@8)', 'Runtime', 'Sample staleness (measured by averaging the clipped importance weight in log scale)']

risk_factors: We assume that this reward is given once the output is fully generated (e.g., a binary indicator of whether the final answer is correct); while this precludes process rewards...
While this strategy [importance weight clipping] can introduce bias into the gradient estimate, it significantly reduces variance in the gradient estimate due to large importance weights.
Overall, these results call for a careful tuning of H as increasing it does not necessarily reduce training time.
While our study focuses on SLMs in resource constrained setting, our algorithm may have benefits for training larger models as well.

title: Q-SFT: Q-Learning for Language Models via Supervised Fine-Tuning
name: q-sft
short_hypothesis: By casting Q-learning as a modified supervised fine-tuning (SFT) problem, where token probabilities directly translate to Q-values, it is possible to create an offline RL algorithm that smoothly transitions from pretraining to finetuning. This approach, implemented via a weighted cross-entropy loss, can learn a near-optimal Q-function without reinitializing weights or adding new prediction heads, thus fully leveraging the knowledge in pretrained language models.
related_work: Current methods for finetuning large language models (LLMs) for multi-turn tasks have significant drawbacks. Policy gradient methods, while popular for single-turn tasks, are not sample-efficient for sequential problems. Traditional value-based offline RL methods like Q-learning are theoretically suitable but scale poorly to large models due to instability from regressing to non-stationary targets and a fundamental mismatch with the maximum likelihood pretraining objective, which forces them to discard the pretrained output layer and add a new, randomly initialized value head. Other approaches like return-conditioned supervised learning (RCSL) are more stable but are theoretically less powerful and struggle to learn from suboptimal data. The proposed research aims to bridge this gap by creating a value-based method that is as stable and compatible with pretraining as SFT.
abstract: Value-based reinforcement learning (RL) can in principle learn effective policies for a wide range of multi-turn problems, from games to dialogue to robotic control, including via offline RL from static previously collected datasets. However, despite the widespread use of policy gradient methods to train large language models for single turn tasks (e.g., question answering), value-based methods for multi-turn RL in an off-policy or offline setting have proven particularly challenging to scale to the setting of large language models. This setting requires effectively leveraging pretraining, scaling to large architectures with billions of parameters, and training on large datasets, all of which represent major challenges for current value-based RL methods. In this work, we propose a novel offline RL algorithm that addresses these drawbacks, casting Q-learning as a modified supervised fine-tuning (SFT) problem where the probabilities of tokens directly translate to Q-values. In this way we obtain an algorithm that smoothly transitions from maximizing the likelihood of the data during pretraining to learning a near-optimal Q-function during finetuning. Our algorithm has strong theoretical foundations, enjoying performance bounds similar to state-of-the-art Q-learning methods, while in practice utilizing an objective that closely resembles SFT. Because of this, our approach can enjoy the full benefits of the pretraining of language models, without the need to reinitialize any weights before RL finetuning, and without the need to initialize new heads for predicting values or advantages.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the proposed Q-SFT method improves over SFT and traditional value-based RL methods on multi-step language generation and embodied decision-making tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the LMRL benchmark for language games (Chess, Wordle, Twenty Questions) and the ALFWorld benchmark for vision-based navigation. For language games, a GPT2-medium model is used. For ALFWorld, a LLaVA-1.6 model is used. The proposed Q-SFT method will be compared against baselines including ReAct (prompting GPT-3.5/GPT-4V), Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and Implicit Language Q-Learning (ILQL).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Average score (for language games)', 'Success rates (for ALFWorld tasks)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method on interactive web-based tasks that require using tools like search.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the WebShop benchmark. A GPT2-medium model is finetuned on a dataset generated by prompting GPT-3.5. The proposed Q-SFT method will be compared against ReAct (prompting GPT-3.5), Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), and an offline variant of ArCHer.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the underlying RL algorithm on robotics tasks without leveraging pretrained language models.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a large-scale robotic manipulation control task (pick and place). A randomly initialized Transformer architecture (modeled after RT-1) is used. The proposed Q-SFT method will be compared against Behavior Cloning (BC), Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), and Q-transformer (QT).

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the method is better able to benefit from the pretraining of larger models compared to previously proposed multi-turn RL methods.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses the Twenty Questions (20Q) task, training on 10% of the original dataset. Both Q-SFT and the ILQL baseline will be trained using different sized pretrained models: GPT2-medium, GPT2-large, and GPT2-xl.

risk_factors: Note that our probabilities are conservative only over actions that have non-negligible Q-values.
For any policy π, we have ∑t=1 γt−1rt ≤ 1.

Another more interesting direction for futher investigation, is whether our method can be adapted to also work online, as many recent works have considered online Rl optimization of language agents (Zhou et al., 2024).

title: Membership Inference Attacks Against Fine-tuned Diffusion Language Models
name: SAMA-DLM-MIA
short_hypothesis: Diffusion Language Models' (DLMs) use of multiple, bidirectional masking configurations creates unique privacy vulnerabilities not present in autoregressive models. These vulnerabilities can be exploited by a new attack, SAMA (Subset-Aggregated Membership Attack), which probes the model with many diverse mask configurations and uses robust, sign-based aggregation of the resulting sparse signals to reliably detect membership.
related_work: Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) have been extensively studied for autoregressive models (ARMs), focusing on signals from their unidirectional, next-token prediction mechanism. For diffusion models, MIA research has predominantly focused on image generation, analyzing continuous state spaces and noise-based corruption. However, the privacy vulnerabilities of the burgeoning class of Diffusion Language Models (DLMs), which operate on distinct principles of iterative, bidirectional mask prediction in a discrete space, remain critically underexplored, leaving a significant gap in understanding their privacy risks.
abstract: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) represent a promising alternative to autoregressive language models, using bidirectional masked token prediction. Yet their susceptibility to privacy leakage via Membership Inference Attacks (MIA) remains critically underexplored. This paper presents the first systematic investigation of MIA vulnerabilities in DLMs. Unlike the autoregressive models' single fixed prediction pattern, DLMs' multiple maskable configurations exponentially increase attack opportunities. This ability to probe many independent masks is hypothesized to dramatically improve detection chances. To exploit this, we introduce SAMA (Subset-Aggregated Membership Attack), which addresses the sparse signal challenge through robust aggregation. SAMA samples masked subsets across progressive densities and applies sign-based statistics that are intended to remain effective despite heavy-tailed noise. Through inverse-weighted aggregation prioritizing sparse masks' cleaner signals, SAMA aims to transform sparse memorization detection into a robust voting mechanism.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed SAMA attack against a wide range of existing MIA baselines on fine-tuned Diffusion Language Models (DLMs).
experiments.Method: The SAMA attack will be evaluated on two fine-tuned DLMs, LLaDA-8B-Base and Dream-v0-7B-Base, using their pre-trained versions as reference models. Fine-tuning will be performed on datasets from six domains of the MIMIR benchmark (ArXiv, GitHub, HackerNews, PubMed Central, Wikipedia (en), Pile CC) and three standard NLP benchmarks (WikiText-103, AG News, XSum). The attack will be compared against twelve baselines, including autoregressive MIA methods (Loss, ZLIB, Lowercase, Neighbor, Min-K%, Min-K%++, ReCall, CON-ReCall, BoWs, Ratio) and diffusion-specific methods (SecMI, PIA).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)', 'True Positive Rate (TPR) at 10%, 1%, and 0.1% False Positive Rates (FPR)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to analyze the contribution of each of SAMA's core components.
experiments.Method: Starting from a baseline loss attack, the experiment will progressively add each component of SAMA: (1) reference model calibration, (2) progressive masking, (3) robust subset aggregation, and (4) adaptive weighting. The impact of each addition will be measured on the MIMIR datasets.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods and other strategies as potential defenses against the SAMA attack.
experiments.Method: The SAMA attack will be tested against models fine-tuned on the ArXiv dataset using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with varying ranks, and Differentially Private LoRA (DP-LoRA) with varying privacy budgets (€). The experiment will also assess the impact of inference-time defenses like Temperature Scaling and fine-tuning defenses like Selective Data Obfuscation (SOFT).

experiments.Description: To investigate the robustness of SAMA when the ideal reference model is unavailable, requiring the use of alternative, potentially misaligned reference models.
experiments.Method: Using a fine-tuned LLaDA-8B-Base model as the target, SAMA's performance will be evaluated with three distinct reference models: the ideal LLaDA-8B-Base, the slightly misaligned LLaDA-8B-Instruct, and the severely misaligned Dream-v0-7B-Base.

risk_factors: SAMA targets mask-predict diffusion models; its effectiveness on other diffusion paradigms remains unexplored.
The attack requires reference models with compatible tokenizers and masking schemes (see Section D.6).

title: Routing-Deconstructed LoRA in Federated Fine-Tuning
name: RD-LoRA
short_hypothesis: A federated fine-tuning framework, Routing-Deconstructed LoRA (RD-LoRA), can simultaneously mitigate aggregation noise, knowledge contamination, and aggregation distortion in heterogeneous settings. This is achieved by building on an alternating freezing strategy and introducing two novel components: a Server-Client Routing Deconstructor (SCRD) to separate shared semantics from local biases, and a Poly-Consensus Aggregation (PCA) mechanism to adaptively align global LoRA parameters with diverse client distributions.
related_work: Federated fine-tuning of Large Language Models using Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA is promising but faces challenges due to data and resource heterogeneity. Directly applying LoRA in federated learning (FL) introduces aggregation noise, where separate averaging of LoRA matrices A and B deviates from the ideal global model. Existing solutions like merging matrices or alternating their training have limitations, such as increased communication costs or conflicting assumptions about client resources. Furthermore, in heterogeneous FL settings, simple averaging leads to aggregation distortion (deviating from client optima) and knowledge contamination (mixing local biases with shared representations). Current methods for heterogeneous LoRA still suffer from issues like high communication/computation overhead or performance degradation. There is a need for a framework that simultaneously addresses all three challenges—aggregation noise, knowledge contamination, and aggregation distortion—in a communication-efficient manner.
abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) with Federated Learning (FL) offers a promising approach to privacy-preserving Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT). However, resource and data heterogeneity in FL cause differences in local knowledge distribution across clients. As a representative PEFT approach, LoRA still faces three key challenges in such settings: aggregation noise, knowledge contamination, and aggregation distortion. To address these issues, we propose Routing-Deconstructed LoRA (RD-LoRA). Building on an alternating freezing strategy to mitigate aggregation noise and concurrently reduces communication cost, RD-LORA further introduces two novel components. For knowledge contamination, we design a Server-Client Routing Deconstructor (SCRD) that separates shared semantics from local biases, retaining fine-grained knowledge with semantic consistency. To address aggregation distortion, we propose a Poly-Consensus Aggregation (PCA) mechanism that uses adaptive weighted averaging to align global LoRA parameters with heterogeneous client distributions, thus correcting the global update direction.

experiments.Description: To benchmark the performance of RD-LORA against state-of-the-art methods for federated LoRA fine-tuning on general instruction-following and knowledge-based tasks, under both homogeneous and heterogeneous rank settings.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use Llama2-7B, TinyLlama-1.1B, Qwen3-8B, and Llama4-17B as base models. Training will be conducted on the Alpaca-GPT4, Databricks-dolly-15k, and Wizard datasets, with data partitioned using a Dirichlet distribution (α = 0.5). RD-LORA will be compared against FFA-LORA, Zero-padding (FedIT), FLoRA, FlexLoRA, LoRA-A2, and a Local training baseline. Evaluations will be performed in both homogeneous rank (all clients rank 16) and heterogeneous rank settings.

experiments.Description: To assess the mathematical reasoning capabilities of models fine-tuned with RD-LORA compared to baselines under heterogeneous rank settings.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use Qwen3-8B and Llama4-17B as base models, fine-tuned on the Big-Math dataset under a heterogeneous LoRA rank setting. RD-LORA will be compared against Zero-padding and FlexLoRA.

experiments.Description: To verify the effectiveness of the individual components of RD-LORA, specifically the Server-Client Routing Deconstructor (SCRD) and the Poly-Consensus Aggregation (PCA) mechanism.
experiments.Method: Ablation experiments will be performed using the Llama2-7B base model in a heterogeneous setting. Variants of the model will be tested: 'w/o SCRD' (without the deconstructor), 'w/o PCA' (using FedAvg instead of PCA), 'w/o OSIM' (lacking per-position attention), and 'w/o Hist. Align.' (without historical steady alignment). These will be compared to the full RD-LORA pipeline.

experiments.Description: To compare the communication overhead of RD-LORA with other federated LoRA fine-tuning methods.
experiments.Method: A theoretical analysis of the single-round upload and download communication costs will be conducted. The comparison will include RD-LORA, FFA-LORA, Zero-padding, FLORA, LORA-A2, and FlexLoRA.

risk_factors: Cross-rank alignment currently uses zero padding and truncation, causing a small drop when moving to heterogeneous ranks; we will explore adaptive rank matching to mitigate this.
The evaluation is limited to English instruction data and two benchmarks, and generalization to larger LLMs and additional LoRA variants remains to be verified.
Future work includes adaptive alignment, broader PEFT forms and tasks, and multilingual and alignment evaluations.

title: ConforFormer: Representation for Molecules Through Understanding of Conformers
name: ConforFormer
short_hypothesis: A method that explicitly accounts for the diversity of 3D conformations of a molecule can derive a task-agnostic and conformation-agnostic vector representation, which can be generated once and directly applied to downstream tasks without extensive fine-tuning.
related_work: Existing methods combine 2D molecular graphs with 3D atomic coordinates but require retraining the entire model for each prediction task, which is impractical for small datasets. Additionally, a single molecular graph corresponds to multiple 3D geometries (conformers), introducing ambiguity, and typical solutions relying on molecular graphs are not easily generalizable beyond organic molecules.
abstract: Current machine learning approaches for chemical tasks require retraining large models for each prediction task, which limits practical deployment on small real-world datasets. Furthermore, the ambiguity of a single molecular graph corresponding to multiple 3D geometries, called conformers, poses a challenge. We present ConforFormer, a method that explicitly accounts for the diversity of 3D conformations to derive a task-agnostic and conformation-agnostic vector representation. This model is intended to serve as a foundational framework, producing embeddings that can be generated once and directly applied to downstream tasks, including property prediction and structural similarity, without extensive fine-tuning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generated embeddings on downstream property prediction tasks without model retraining.
experiments.Method: The generated ConforFormer embeddings will be used as input for property prediction models. The performance will be compared against methods that require full model retraining.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Not specified in the provided text.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generated embeddings on structural similarity tasks.
experiments.Method: The ConforFormer embeddings will be used to measure structural similarity between molecules.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Not specified in the provided text.

title: Interpolating Video-LLMs:  Toward Longer-sequence LMMs in a Training-free Manner
name: training-free-video-llm-interpolation
short_hypothesis: It is possible to enable pre-trained Video-LLMs to process longer video sequences in a completely training-free manner. This can be achieved by introducing a video token rearrangement technique to bypass fixed encoder limitations and a context window extension method for the LLM backbone to handle the increased number of visual tokens.
related_work: Existing Video-LLMs integrate video encoders with LLMs but are typically pre-trained on short videos (e.g., 8 frames), limiting their ability to understand longer content. Fine-tuning or retraining these models for longer sequences is computationally and data-prohibitively expensive. While methods exist to extend LLM context windows or model video, they often require some form of training. The research gap lies in finding a training-free approach to extend the video processing capabilities of existing, pre-trained Video-LLMs.
abstract: Advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have inspired various strategies for integrating video modalities. A key approach is Video-LLMs, which incorporate an optimizable interface linking sophisticated video encoders to LLMs. However, due to computation and data limitations, existing Video-LLMs are typically pre-trained to process only short videos, limiting their broader application for understanding longer video content. Additionally, fine-tuning Video-LLMs to handle longer videos is cost-prohibitive. Consequently, it is essential to explore the interpolation of Video-LLMs under a completely training-free setting. In this paper, we first identify the primary challenges in interpolating Video-LLMs: the video encoder and modality alignment projector are fixed, preventing the integration of additional frames into Video-LLMs, and the LLM backbone is limited in its content length capabilities. To address these challenges, we propose an INTerPolation method for Video-LLMs (INTP-Video-LLMs). We introduce a video token rearrangement technique that circumvents limitations imposed by the fixed video encoder and alignment projector. Furthermore, we introduce a training-free LLM context window extension method to enable Video-LLMs to understand a correspondingly increased number of visual tokens.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed INTP method on zero-shot open-ended video question-answering benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The INTP method will be applied as a plug-and-play enhancement to the Video-LLaVA framework, which uses a Vicuna-7B v1.5 backbone. This enhanced model (Video-LLaVA+INTP) will be compared against several baselines: FrozenBiLM, VideoChat, LLaMA-Adapter, Video-LLaMA, Video-ChatGPT, and the original Video-LLaVA. The evaluation will be conducted on the MSVD-QA, MSRVTT-QA, and ActivityNet-QA datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['GPT-assisted assessment of response accuracy and correctness (Accuracy, Score)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on multiple-choice video question-answering benchmarks.
experiments.Method: The INTP-enhanced Video-LLaVA model will be evaluated on the NExT-QA and EgoSchema datasets. Performance will be compared against baselines including FrozenBiLM, InternVideo, Sevilla, Video-ChatGPT, and the original Video-LLaVA.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy (correctly selecting the answer from provided options)', 'Average accuracy across NExT-QA sub-categories (Causal, Temporal, Descriptive)']

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study on the impact of varying the number of frames processed by the INTP method.
experiments.Method: The performance of INTP-Video-LLaVA will be assessed while varying the number of input frames (e.g., 8, 16, 32, 64). The performance will be compared to the baseline Video-LLaVA which processes 8 frames. The evaluation will be performed on the MSVD-QA, MSRVTT-QA, and ActivityNet-QA benchmarks.

risk_factors: A performance plateau is observed when the number of frames is scaled to 64. This suggests a limitation in the NTK-based LLM backbone extension method, which appears to struggle with significantly expanding the LLM content window capacity beyond a certain point.
INTP does not address potential security concerns related to the misuse of Video-LLMs.

title: Learning More by Seeing Less: Structure-First Learning for Efficient, Transferable, and Human-Aligned Vision
name: Structure-First-Learning-with-Line-Drawings
short_hypothesis: By training vision models to first understand the world through minimal structural cues like line drawings, we aim to induce representations that are more compact, efficient, generalizable, and human-aligned.
related_work: Prior work in visual representation learning, including self-supervised methods like MAE, DINO, and SwAV, has predominantly relied on dense, texture-rich color photographs as training input. While other methods have sought to instill a shape bias through stylized training data, data augmentation, or architectural constraints, they often do not scale well or are not as explicit as using minimal representations. Research in line drawing synthesis has provided tools to generate sparse sketches from photographs, but these have not been leveraged as a foundational training modality. This work proposes to bridge this gap by using semantically coherent line drawings as the initial phase of a learning curriculum, hypothesizing that this structure-first approach is a powerful, complementary strategy to enhance existing pre-training frameworks by instilling a robust structural bias from the outset.
abstract: Despite remarkable progress in computer vision, modern recognition systems remain fundamentally limited by their dependence on rich, redundant visual inputs. In contrast, humans can effortlessly understand sparse, minimal representations like line drawings, suggesting that structure, rather than appearance, underlies efficient visual understanding. In this work, we propose a novel structure-first learning paradigm that uses line drawings as an initial training modality to induce more compact and generalizable visual representations. We will test if models trained with this approach develop a stronger shape bias, more focused attention, and greater data efficiency. We also plan to investigate if these models exhibit lower intrinsic dimensionality and produce more compressible representations for knowledge distillation.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the core 'structure-first' curriculum against a standard training baseline and other stylistic augmentation controls on an image classification task.
experiments.Method: A two-stage supervised learning curriculum will be used. Stage 1: Train a ResNet-18 model on a source domain (line drawings). Stage 2: Continue training on the target domain (natural color images). This 'Line-Color' approach will be tested on the STL-10 dataset. Baselines will include a 'Color Only' model trained directly on natural images. Control experiments will use other artistic styles (Matisse, Mondrian, Picasso) and Canny edges as the source domain to isolate the effect of line drawings from general stylistic augmentation.

experiments.Description: Assess the data efficiency of the structure-first learning paradigm compared to baseline training.
experiments.Method: The 'Line-Color' curriculum and the 'Color Only' baseline (using ResNet-18 and ViT-Tiny) will be trained on stratified subsets of the STL-10 training corpus, ranging from 5% to 100% of the data. Their performance on the full test set will be compared at each data percentage level.

experiments.Description: Validate the general applicability of the structure-first approach across different model architectures and a larger-scale dataset.
experiments.Method: The 'Line-Color' curriculum will be applied to a Vision Transformer (ViT-Tiny) on the STL-10 dataset and a YOLOv8 backbone on the ImageNet-1K dataset. Performance will be compared against 'Color Only' baselines for each respective architecture and dataset.

experiments.Description: Investigate whether the structure-first approach can complement and enhance existing self-supervised pre-training methods.
experiments.Method: The structure-first phase (pre-training on line drawings) will be integrated into the DINO and SwAV frameworks. A ResNet-18 model will be pre-trained on ImageNet-100 using a mixed-modality curriculum (e.g., 150 epochs on line drawings, then 250 epochs on color images) and compared against baseline DINO and SwAV models pre-trained for 400 epochs on color images only. A reduced-compute scenario will also be tested.

experiments.Description: Analyze the effect of the structure-first curriculum on model attention patterns.
experiments.Method: GradCAM visualizations will be generated for models trained with the 'Line-Color' curriculum and the 'Color Only' baseline. The attention heat maps will be qualitatively and quantitatively compared to assess whether the structure-first model exhibits more focused attention on primary subjects.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative visualization of heat maps', 'Attention scatter metric (number of distinct high-activation regions)']

experiments.Description: Evaluate the impact of the curriculum on the sparsity and efficiency of learned neural representations by analyzing neuron activations.
experiments.Method: The activation tuning curve for the last layer of the trained ResNet-18 models ('Line-Color' vs. 'Color Only') will be analyzed. The average firing activations for each channel will be computed over test images, sorted, and plotted to form a tuning curve, indicating the distribution of response strengths across neurons.

experiments.Description: Assess whether the structure-first curriculum induces a stronger shape bias in model decision-making, aligning it more closely with human perception.
experiments.Method: Models trained with the 'Line-Color' curriculum and the 'Color Only' baseline will be evaluated on the 'model-vs-human' benchmark, which uses cue-conflict images with mismatched shape and texture. The models' classification choices (shape-based vs. texture-based) will be recorded and compared against human decision patterns.

experiments.Description: Determine if the representations learned via the structure-first curriculum generalize effectively to the downstream task of semantic segmentation.
experiments.Method: ResNet-18 models pre-trained on STL-10 with the 'Line-Color' curriculum and the 'Color Only' baseline will be used as encoders in a semantic segmentation framework (with a PPM decoder). These models will then be fine-tuned and evaluated on the ADE20K benchmark.

experiments.Description: Evaluate the data efficiency benefits of the structure-first curriculum on the downstream task of object detection.
experiments.Method: A YOLO-v8-n model will be trained on the PASCAL VOC dataset using the 'Line-Color' curriculum and compared against a 'Color Only' baseline. The experiment will be run across various training data percentages to assess performance in low-data regimes.

experiments.Description: Analyze the intrinsic dimensionality of the learned representations to test the hypothesis that structure-first learning leads to more compact representations.
experiments.Method: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) will be performed on the penultimate layer activations of the ResNet-18 models ('Line-Color' vs. 'Color Only') extracted from the STL-10 test set. The number of principal components required to explain a certain percentage (e.g., 90%) of the variance will be compared.

experiments.Description: Test whether the compact representations from the structure-first curriculum are more effectively transferred to smaller models via knowledge distillation.
experiments.Method: ResNet-18 models trained with the 'Line-Color' curriculum and 'Color Only' supervision will be used as teacher models. Smaller student models (MobileNetV1, ResNet-8, VGG-8) will be distilled from these teachers. To ensure a fair comparison, a teacher model from the 'Line-Color' curriculum will be trained on a subset of data to match the performance of the 'Color Only' teacher trained on full data.

title: MedAgent-Pro: Towards Evidence-based Multi-modal Medical Diagnosis via Reasoning Agentic Workflow
name: medagent-pro-agentic-workflow
short_hypothesis: An agentic reasoning paradigm, MedAgent-Pro, that mirrors modern clinical diagnosis principles through a hierarchical workflow will enable more accurate and reliable multi-modal medical diagnosis compared to standard Vision-Language Models (VLMs). This workflow involves disease-level standardized plan generation using a retrieval-augmented agent and patient-level personalized reasoning using professional tools and an evidence-based reflection mechanism.
related_work: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and agent-based methods are reshaping medical diagnosis. However, they often output direct, empirical-driven conclusions without the support of clinical evidence or quantitative analysis, which compromises their reliability and clinical usability. Existing medical agentic systems tend to integrate tools statically in a fixed pipeline, rather than dynamically organizing them based on the specific disease. This leads to suboptimal handling of complex diagnoses. The research gap lies in creating a system that performs systematic, evidence-based diagnosis through structured reasoning and alignment with clinical guidelines, similar to how human clinicians work.
abstract: Modern clinical diagnosis relies on the comprehensive analysis of multi-modal patient data, drawing on medical expertise to ensure systematic and rigorous reasoning. Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and agent-based methods are reshaping medical diagnosis by effectively integrating multi-modal information. However, they often output direct answers and empirical-driven conclusions without clinical evidence supported by quantitative analysis, which compromises their reliability and hinders clinical usability. Here we propose MedAgent-Pro, an agentic reasoning paradigm that mirrors modern diagnosis principles via a hierarchical diagnostic workflow, consisting of disease-level standardized plan generation and patient-level personalized step-by-step reasoning. To support disease-level planning, a retrieval-augmented generation agent is designed to access medical guidelines for alignment with clinical standards. For patient-level reasoning, MedAgent-Pro leverages professional tools such as visual models to take various actions to analyze multi-modal input, and performs evidence-based reflection to iteratively adjust memory, enforcing rigorous reasoning throughout the process.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of MedAgent-Pro on complex, in-depth diagnostic reasoning tasks against general VLMs and other medical agentic systems.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the REFUGE2 dataset for glaucoma diagnosis and the MITEA dataset for heart disease diagnosis. MedAgent-Pro will be compared against a set of general VLMs (GPT-4o, Janus-Pro-7B, LLaVA-Med, BioMedClip, Qwen2.5-7B-VL, InternVL2.5-8B) and medical agentic systems (MedAgents, MMedAgent, MDAgent).

experiments.Description: To assess the model's capability for multi-disease diagnosis in individual patients using chest X-ray images.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a sampled subset of the MIMIC dataset, involving the identification of up to 12 potential thoracic abnormalities. MedAgent-Pro will be compared against general VLMs.

experiments.Description: To assess the model's performance across a diverse set of real-world diagnostic cases encompassing multiple anatomical regions, imaging modalities, and diseases.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a compiled dataset of 992 cases from the New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM) database. MedAgent-Pro will be compared against general VLMs and other agentic systems.

experiments.Description: To compare MedAgent-Pro with state-of-the-art, task-specific models for glaucoma and chest X-ray diagnosis.
experiments.Method: For glaucoma diagnosis, MedAgent-Pro will be compared to the winners of the REFUGE2 challenge and other ophthalmology VLMs (RetiZero, VisionUnite). For chest X-ray analysis, it will be compared against specialized models like Maira-2 and CheXagent.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to evaluate the effectiveness of the key components of MedAgent-Pro: planning, tool-based action, and evidence-based reflection.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be performed on the glaucoma (REFUGE2) and heart disease (MITEA) datasets. The performance will be evaluated by progressively adding each module (planning, action, reflection) to the baseline VLM.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the diagnostic quality of the generated content through assessment by clinical experts.
experiments.Method: Clinical experts will rate the diagnostic outputs from MedAgent-Pro and baseline VLMs on glaucoma and chest X-ray diagnosis tasks. The rating will be based on five dimensions: relevance, comprehensiveness, clinical reliability, reasoning coherence, and language clarity.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['1-5 Likert scale for relevance, comprehensiveness, clinical reliability, reasoning coherence, and language clarity']

risk_factors: However, the proposed framework depends on the availability of visual tools, which remain limited in certain medical domains.
In addition, qualitative analysis still relies on VLMs, which are prone to VLMs’ inherent hallucination.

title: SeedFT: Structure-Preserving Fusion for Multi-Seed LLM Fine-Tuning
name: seedft-structure-preserving-fusion
short_hypothesis: A training-free fusion method, SeedFT, can preserve matrix geometry and consolidate complementary capabilities from multiple seed-specific fine-tuned models. This is achieved by first aligning seed-specific updates in a shared SVD-derived subspace, and then fusing these aligned representations via orthogonality-constrained optimization with a closed-form solution.
related_work: Existing model merging techniques often rely on element-wise operations (averaging, masking, magnitude-based selection) which treat weight matrices as flat vectors, destroying their inherent geometric structure and leading to suboptimal fusion. Furthermore, most research has focused on multi-task merging (combining models trained on different datasets), leaving the single-task setting (merging models fine-tuned on the same data with different random seeds) underexplored, despite it offering better initial alignment for effective fusion.
abstract: Fine-tuning large language models exhibits high variance across random seeds, often requiring multiple runs to find the best checkpoint. While ensemble methods can leverage this diversity, they incur prohibitive computational costs during inference, and existing model merging techniques rely on element-wise operations that treat weight matrices as vectors, destroying the geometric structure essential for effective knowledge consolidation. We address this limitation through SeedFT, a training-free fusion method that preserves matrix geometry while consolidating complementary capabilities from multiple seed-specific fine-tuned models. Our approach builds on two key observations: layer-wise fine-tuning updates contain substantial redundancy, and different random seeds learn complementary sub-skills within the same task domain. SeedFT operates through structure-preserving aggregation in two stages: first aligning seed-specific updates in a shared SVD-derived subspace, then fusing these aligned representations via orthogonality-constrained optimization with a closed-form solution.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SeedFT on mathematical reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B, and Gemma-2-9B models on the MetaMathQA dataset. The resulting models will be evaluated on the GSM8K and MATH benchmarks. The proposed SeedFT method will be compared against several baselines: Model Soup (uniform averaging), TIES (interference resolution), DARE (parameter dropping with rescaling), and the performance of the best individual seed model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of SeedFT on commonsense reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: Fine-tune LLaMA-2-7B and LLaMA-3-8B models on the Commonsense-170k dataset. The merged models will be evaluated on a suite of eight benchmarks: BoolQ, PIQA, SIQA, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, ARC-C/E, and OBQA. The proposed SeedFT method will be compared against Model Soup, TIES, DARE, and the performance of the best individual seed model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of SeedFT on structured code generation tasks.
experiments.Method: Train LLaMA-2-7B and LLaMA-3-8B models on the Code-Feedback dataset. The merged models will be evaluated on HumanEval and MBPP from EvalPlus. The proposed SeedFT method will be compared against Model Soup, TIES, DARE, and the performance of the best individual seed model.

experiments.Description: To analyze the training efficiency of SeedFT compared to extended single-seed training.
experiments.Method: Compare the performance of SeedFT (using 3 seeds, 3 epochs each) against a single-seed model trained for an equivalent total number of epochs (6 and 9 epochs) on LLaMA2-7B using the MetaMathQA dataset.

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to understand the effect of the number of seeds and the contribution of different layer types.
experiments.Method: 1) Evaluate SeedFT with a varying number of seed models (from 2 to 6) on mathematical reasoning using LLaMA2-7B. 2) Analyze the effect of applying fusion to different layer types individually (Q, K, V, O, Gate, Up, Down) on LLaMA2-7B for mathematical reasoning.

risk_factors: A natural concern with SeedFT is the computational cost of training multiple seed models.

title: RG: OUT-OF-DISTRIBUTION DETECTION WITH REACTIVATE GRADNORM
name: reactivate-gradnorm-ood
short_hypothesis: A simple and effective method for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, named Reactivate Gradnorm (RG), can be designed by utilizing the inputs and outputs of the last layer of a neural network. Specifically, the OOD scoring function can be the product of the l1-norm of the clipped input of the last layer and the logarithm of the exponential sum of the outputs (free energy).
related_work: Previous works on out-of-distribution (OOD) detection have primarily focused on deriving uncertainty measurements from either the model's output space (e.g., softmax probabilities, energy scores) or its feature space (e.g., Mahalanobis distance). More recently, methods using gradient information have been proposed, which combine information from both feature and output spaces. However, these existing approaches, including gradient-based ones, still suffer from overconfidence on OOD inputs, indicating a need for improved methods that can better utilize both feature and output space information.
abstract: Detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) data is critical to building reliable machine learning systems in the open world. Previous works mainly perform OOD detection in feature space or output space. Recently, researchers have achieved promising results using gradient information, which combines the information in both feature and output space for OOD detection. However, existing works still suffer from the problem of overconfidence. To address this problem, we propose a novel method called “Reactivate Gradnorm (RG)”, which exploits the norm of the clipped feature vector and the energy in the output space for OOD detection. RG is easy to implement and does not require additional OOD data or fine-tuning process. We can realize OOD detection in only one forward pass of any pretrained model.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed RG method on a large-scale OOD detection benchmark against existing state-of-the-art approaches.
experiments.Method: The experiment uses a ResNetv2-101 (BiT-S) model pretrained on ImageNet-1k as the in-distribution (ID) dataset. The model is then evaluated for its ability to distinguish ID data from OOD data from four test datasets: iNaturalist, SUN, Places, and Textures. The performance of RG is to be compared against several baselines, including MSP, KL-matching, Energy, ReAct, Mahalanobis, MOS, ODIN, Conor Igoe, and Gradnorm.

experiments.Description: To conduct an ablation study to understand the individual and combined contributions of the output space component (U, energy score) and the feature space component (V, clipped feature norm).
experiments.Method: Using the same ImageNet benchmark setup, different combinations of U and V components from the proposed method (RG) and the GradNorm baseline will be evaluated. This includes using the U or V component from one method with the other component from the other method, as well as evaluating the performance of each component in isolation.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the clipping threshold hyperparameter 'k' on the performance of the RG method.
experiments.Method: The RG method will be evaluated on the ImageNet benchmark while varying the clipping threshold 'k' from 0.1 to 5, as well as an unclipped version (k=infinity). The temperature parameter 'T' is kept at its default value.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the temperature hyperparameter 'T' on the performance of the RG method.
experiments.Method: The RG method will be evaluated on the ImageNet benchmark while varying the temperature 'T' used in the energy score calculation. The clipping threshold 'k' is kept at its default value.

experiments.Description: To explore an alternative formulation of the OOD score based on an additive combination of the feature and output space components, instead of a multiplicative one.
experiments.Method: An alternative OOD score, defined as the sum of the energy score and a scaled version of the clipped feature norm (log(sum(exp(f_i(x)))) + alpha * sum(min(v_i, k))), will be evaluated. The experiment will test the effect of different values for the balance coefficient 'alpha'.

experiments.Description: To verify the effectiveness of the RG method on an alternative neural network architecture.
experiments.Method: The RG method will be evaluated using a DenseNet-121 model pretrained on ImageNet-1k. Its performance will be compared against baselines including MSP, Energy, ReAct, Mahalanobis, ODIN, Gradnorm, and Conor Igoe on the same set of OOD datasets (iNaturalist, SUN, Places, Textures).

experiments.Description: To compare the performance of the proposed RG method with a non-parametric, feature-based OOD detection method (KNN).
experiments.Method: Using a ResNet-50 model trained on ImageNet, the performance of RG will be compared against a KNN-based OOD detection method on the standard OOD benchmark datasets.

risk_factors: However, the choice of g(·) in Section 3.3 is only heuristic, a strict g(·) should also satisfy normalization equation: ∫∑(i=1 to C) e^(g(v)+w_i^t v+b_i) dv = 1.
In the future, we might make some assumptions about g(·) and then learn the parameters of g(·) from the training data.

title: MV-RAG: Retrieval Augmented Multiview Diffusion
name: retrieval-augmented-multiview-diffusion
short_hypothesis: Conditioning a multiview diffusion model on relevant 2D images retrieved from a large database will enable the faithful generation of consistent and accurate multiview outputs for out-of-domain (OOD) and rare concepts. This can be achieved through a novel hybrid training strategy that combines a reconstruction objective on structured 3D data with a held-out view prediction objective on unstructured 2D image collections, along with a prior-guided fusion mechanism to balance retrieval signals with the model's prior.
related_work: Current text-to-3D generation methods, including those using Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) or feed-forward multiview diffusion models, often struggle with out-of-domain (OOD) or rare concepts. This leads to geometric inconsistencies, reduced photorealism, or failure to adhere to the text prompt. While retrieval-augmentation has been explored using 3D assets as geometric priors, this approach is limited by the scale and diversity of available 3D databases. Personalization techniques can adapt models to a specific subject using a few images, but they require inference-time fine-tuning and still face geometric inconsistencies, and are not designed to compose features from multiple different instances of a category.
abstract: Text-to-3D generation approaches have advanced significantly, producing high-quality and 3D-consistent outputs. However, they often fail to produce out-of-domain (OOD) or rare concepts, yielding inconsistent or inaccurate results. To this end, we propose MV-RAG, a novel text-to-3D pipeline that first retrieves relevant 2D images from a large in-the-wild 2D database and then conditions a multiview diffusion model on these images to synthesize consistent and accurate multiview outputs. Training such a retrieval-conditioned model is achieved via a novel hybrid strategy bridging structured multiview data and diverse 2D image collections. This involves training on multiview data using augmented conditioning views that simulate retrieval variance for view-specific reconstruction, alongside training on sets of retrieved real-world 2D images using a distinctive held-out view prediction objective: the model predicts the held-out view from the other views to infer 3D consistency from 2D data. We also introduce a prior-guided fusion mechanism that dynamically balances retrieval signals with the model's prior. To facilitate a rigorous OOD evaluation, we introduce a new collection of challenging OOD prompts.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to generate multiview images for out-of-distribution (OOD) and rare concepts.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on a newly curated 'OOD-Eval' benchmark, consisting of 196 challenging prompts with retrieved images from Wikipedia Commons. The retrieval corpus is LAION-400M. The approach will be compared against three categories of baselines: (1) Text-to-multiview (MVDream, MV-Adapter (TX), SPAD, TRELLIS (TX)), (2) Image-to-multiview (ImageDream, MV-Adapter (IM), Era3D, TRELLIS (IM)), and (3) 3D personalization (MVDreamBooth).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['CLIP similarity', 'DINOv2 similarity', 'Instance Retrieval (IR)', 'FID', 'Inception Score (IS)', '3D consistency assessed by training a 3D reconstruction model (LGM) on generated views and evaluating re-rendered novel views', 'User study rating Realism (Q1), Text Alignment (Q2), and 3D Consistency (Q3)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on standard in-domain concepts to ensure that improvements on OOD concepts do not degrade in-domain performance.
experiments.Method: The model will be evaluated on the 'IND-Eval' benchmark, a curated set of 50 in-domain objects from Objaverse-XL with corresponding ground truth multiview images. Baselines are the same as in the OOD evaluation.

risk_factors: It relies heavily on both the quality of the retrieved image corpus and the capabilities of the underlying generative model, MVDream. When the base model lacks prior knowledge of the object and retrieval fails to provide informative or diverse references, the generated multiviews can be inaccurate or implausible.
As shown in Fig. 15, errors may arise when the retrieved images are visually biased-e.g., all showing similar white flowers, leading to reduced diversity and visual artifacts.
Furthermore, our training objective promotes texture variation, which can make it difficult to reproduce fine-grained or specific patterns, such as the hoverfly's dorsal markings.

Our model also employs an adaptive mechanism that balances attention between the base model and the retrieval adapters, based on a similarity score between the generated initial views and retrieved images. When the base model demonstrates high similarity to the target object but exhibits 3D structural errors (such as a floating dog tail), these artifacts may be inherited.

Further, while our current implementation generates four views, this is not a fundamental limitation of our retrieval-augmented framework, which could be applied to backbones that produce a larger set of views (e.g., Zero123++ Shi et al. (2023a)).
Lastly, our method introduces a retrieval phase prior to generation. Although this adds computational cost relative to standard text-to-image pipelines, the overhead is minimal.

title: Ice Cream Doesn’t Cause Drowning: Benchmarking LLMs Against Statistical Pitfalls in Causal Inference
name: causal-pitfalls-benchmark
short_hypothesis: Current benchmarks for evaluating causal reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) are too simplistic, focusing on semantic relationships rather than rigorous statistical inference. We propose that LLMs are susceptible to common statistical pitfalls like Simpson's paradox and selection bias. To test this, we introduce CausalPitfalls, a comprehensive benchmark with structured challenges and synthetic datasets, designed to rigorously evaluate LLM reliability. We will assess models using two protocols: direct prompting to test intrinsic reasoning, and code-assisted prompting to test their ability to leverage statistical analysis.
related_work: Prior work has evaluated the causal reasoning abilities of LLMs, but these evaluations often focus on commonsense causality from variable names, accuracy in effect estimation, or recovering DAG structures. These benchmarks frequently neglect crucial aspects like robustness, interpretability, and susceptibility to common statistical pitfalls that are prevalent in real-world data analysis. This oversight means that LLMs can produce seemingly convincing yet misleading causal conclusions, limiting their applicability. The proposed research aims to fill this gap by creating a benchmark specifically designed to test LLM robustness against these well-known statistical and causal inference challenges.
abstract: Reliable causal inference is essential for making decisions in high-stakes areas like medicine, economics, and public policy. However, it remains unclear whether large language models (LLMs) can handle rigorous and trustworthy statistical causal inference. Current benchmarks usually involve simplified tasks. For example, these tasks might only ask LLMs to identify semantic causal relationships or draw conclusions directly from raw data. As a result, models may overlook important statistical pitfalls, such as Simpson’s paradox or selection bias. This oversight limits the applicability of LLMs in the real world. To address these limitations, we propose CausalPitfalls, a comprehensive benchmark designed to rigorously evaluate the capability of LLMs in overcoming common causal inference pitfalls. Our benchmark features structured challenges across multiple difficulty levels, each paired with grading rubrics. This approach allows us to quantitatively measure both causal reasoning capabilities and the reliability of LLMs' responses. We evaluate models using two protocols: (1) direct prompting, which assesses intrinsic causal reasoning, and (2) code-assisted prompting, where models generate executable code for statistical analysis. Additionally, we validate the effectiveness of this judge by comparing its scoring with assessments from human experts.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the reliability of LLMs in performing statistical causal inference and their susceptibility to common pitfalls using the proposed CausalPitfalls benchmark.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will be conducted on the CausalPitfalls benchmark, which consists of 6 major categories of pitfalls, broken down into 15 distinct challenges (e.g., Simpson's paradox, selection bias, mediator-outcome confounding, cause-effect direction inference, population shift). For each challenge, 5 distinct datasets are generated from structural causal models, each with over 500 samples. Each challenge includes 5 questions of varying difficulty ('very easy' to 'very hard'). A range of LLMs will be evaluated. The evaluation will follow two protocols: (1) Direct Prompting, where the LLM reasons directly from raw data, and (2) Code-Assisted Prompting, where the LLM first generates executable code for statistical analysis and then provides an answer based on the code's output.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Normalized Score: A score computed for each challenge based on detailed, pre-defined grading rubrics.', 'Causal Reliability: The average normalized score across all benchmark challenges.', 'Code Execution Failure Rate: The percentage of code-generation attempts that result in errors or invalid outputs in the code-assisted protocol.', 'Gap Metric: A metric to measure the consistency between automated grading (using a GPT-4o model) and manual grading by human experts on a subset of responses.']

risk_factors: Future directions include expanding the benchmark to cover more nuanced forms of causal reasoning, such as instrumental variable analysis, latent confounding, and policy evaluation.

title: Multimodal Large Language Models for Inverse Molecular Design with Retrosynthetic Planning
name: Llamole
short_hypothesis: A multimodal LLM, named Llamole, can be developed to enable interleaved text and graph generation for molecular inverse design with retrosynthetic planning. This is achieved by integrating a base LLM with specialized, pre-trained graph models (a Graph Diffusion Transformer for generation and a GNN for reaction inference) and using an A* search with LLM-based cost functions. This architecture is hypothesized to allow the LLM to flexibly control the generation process across text and graph modalities, leading to improved performance in controllable molecular design and planning compared to standard LLMs.
related_work: Existing Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with the unique graph structures of molecular data, limiting their generation quality and planning capabilities in the chemical domain. While multimodal LLMs have been developed for molecular tasks, they either do not treat molecules as graphs (relying on SMILES strings) or do not focus on the complex tasks of inverse molecular design and multi-step retrosynthetic planning. This creates a research gap for a model that can cohesively handle interleaved text and graph generation for controllable and synthesizable molecular design.
abstract: While large language models (LLMs) have integrated images, adapting them to graphs remains challenging, limiting their applications in materials and drug design. This difficulty stems from the need for coherent autoregressive generation across texts and graphs. To address this, we introduce Llamole, the first multimodal LLM capable of interleaved text and graph generation, enabling molecular inverse design with retrosynthetic planning. Llamole integrates a base LLM with the Graph Diffusion Transformer and Graph Neural Networks for multi-conditional molecular generation and reaction inference within texts, while the LLM, with enhanced molecular understanding, flexibly controls activation among the different graph modules. Additionally, Llamole integrates A* search with LLM-based cost functions for efficient retrosynthetic planning.

experiments.Description: To systematically evaluate Llamole's performance in controllable and synthesizable molecular design against various LLM-based and domain-specific methods.
experiments.Method: The evaluation will be conducted on the newly created MolQA benchmark dataset, which contains question-answer pairs for drug and material design. The proposed Llamole model, using various base LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B, Qwen2-7B), will be compared against 14 baseline LLMs (including Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Granite, Flan-T5) using both in-context learning (ICL) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Domain-specific methods like GraphGA, DiGress, and BioNavi will also be included for comparison.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Chemical validity', 'Similarity to reference (Morgan fingerprints)', 'BLEU-4', 'ROUGE-L', 'Balanced Accuracy (BA) for categorical drug properties', 'Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for continuous material properties', 'Retrosynthesis success rate against the Gavail database']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to investigate the synergy between the base LLM and the Graph DiT module in Llamole for controllable molecule generation.
experiments.Method: The experiment will involve two ablations on the Llamole model: 1) removing the text conditions (ctext) from the input, and 2) removing both text and property conditions. The impact of these ablations on generation quality will be assessed.

experiments.Description: To analyze the performance of LLMs in retrosynthetic planning, focusing on error analysis in one-step reaction generation and the effectiveness of multi-step planning.
experiments.Method: For one-step reaction generation, an error analysis will be conducted by categorizing the failure modes of ICL and SFT-based LLMs. For multi-step planning, the performance of Llamole's A* search will be analyzed by comparing its LLM-based heuristic cost function (Jheuristic) against a domain-specific model and by evaluating the impact of removing the planning time constraint.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Error analysis/categorization for one-step generation', 'Success rate for multi-step planning', 'Planning time']

risk_factors: While there is a trade-off between efficiency and effectiveness, it is often acceptable to extend response time by a few minutes to enhance success rates for finding synthesis paths.
Some functional groups, like hydroxyl, may not be precisely represented in the structure. This indicates a need for enhanced text instruction adherence in Graph DiT.

title: GRADSIMCORE: GRADIENT SIMILARITY BASED REPRESENTATIVE INSTANCES AS CORESET
name: gradsimcore-gradient-similarity-coreset
short_hypothesis: A simple and novel framework based on the similarity of loss gradients can identify representative training instances for a coreset. The core intuition is that samples of a particular class produce gradients in similar directions, especially in the early stages of training. Therefore, samples with a strong gradient similarity to many other samples within the same class are the most representative and should be selected for the coreset.
related_work: Prior work on coreset selection includes gradient matching-based methods like CRAIG and GRAD-MATCH, which convert the problem into a submodular function maximization or use orthogonal matching pursuit, relying on sophisticated modules. Other approaches include K-Center Greedy approximation, uncertainty-based methods, and error-based methods like GraNd and EL2N. The proposed method, GradSimCore, aims to provide a simpler, more intuitive alternative that does not require complex optimization or per-element step sizes during training.
abstract: The rise in size and complexity of modern datasets and deep learning models have resulted in the usage of extensive computational resources and a surge in training time and effort. It also has increased the carbon footprint of training and fine-tuning models. One way to reduce the computational requirement is to extract the most representative subset (referred to as coreset) that can substitute for the larger dataset. Coresets can thus replace massive datasets to train models and tune hyperparameters, especially in the early stages of training. This will result in a significant reduction of computational resource requirement and reduce carbon footprint. We propose a simple and novel framework based on the similarity of loss gradients for identifying the representative training instances as a coreset. Our method is dubbed as GradSimCore.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the generalization performance of classifiers trained on coresets selected by the proposed GradSimCore method on several benchmark object recognition datasets, and compare it against state-of-the-art coreset selection algorithms.
experiments.Method: Coresets of various fractions (e.g., 0.1%, 0.5%, 1%, 5%, 10%, 20%) will be selected from CIFAR10, CIFAR100, and ImageNet-1K datasets. A ResNet-18 classifier will be trained from scratch on these resulting coresets. The performance will be compared against the best mean accuracy reported by the DeepCore library for various baseline algorithms like GraphCut, CAL, GraNd, and Forgetting.

experiments.Description: To study the robustness of the coreset selection method by carrying out a cross-architecture generalization performance comparison.
experiments.Method: Coresets will be selected from the CIFAR10 dataset at 1% and 10% fractions using a source architecture (ResNet18, VGG16, or Inception-V3). These same coresets will then be used to train different target architectures (ResNet18, VGG16, and Inception-V3). The performance will be compared against results reported by DeepCore for the same cross-architecture evaluation setup.

risk_factors: Particularly with respect to the approximation error between the gradients computed by the coreset and full dataset.
Inducing diversity in the selected samples along with individual importance weights (or per-sample step size) would further improve the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: A Minimalist Dataset for Systematic Generalization of Perception, Syntax, and Semantics
name: hint-dataset-systematic-generalization
short_hypothesis: A new dataset, Handwritten arithmetic with INTegers (HINT), which requires joint learning of perception, syntax, and semantics from handwritten arithmetic expressions, will reveal the limitations of current sequence-to-sequence models in systematic generalization. We hypothesize that these models will struggle to extrapolate to longer-range syntactic dependencies and more complex semantics, which are core features of the HINT benchmark.
related_work: Prior benchmarks for systematic generalization, such as SCAN and CLEVR, are often based on artificial domains with synthetic data. They typically cover only a subset of concept learning (e.g., syntax and semantics, but not perception from real data) and frequently conflate the evaluation of syntax and semantics. Furthermore, their semantics are often simple mappings, creating a bias towards syntactic generalization. There is a need for a benchmark that requires joint learning across perception (from real handwriting), complex syntax (with long-range dependencies), and complex semantics (arithmetic reasoning) to provide a more comprehensive and rigorous test of generalization.
abstract: Inspired by humans' exceptional ability to master arithmetic and generalize to new problems, we present a new dataset, Handwritten arithmetic with INTegers (HINT), to examine machines' capability of learning generalizable concepts at three levels: perception, syntax, and semantics. In HINT, machines are tasked with learning how concepts are perceived from raw signals such as images (i.e., perception), how multiple concepts are structurally combined to form a valid expression (i.e., syntax), and how concepts are realized to afford various reasoning tasks (i.e., semantics), all in a weakly supervised manner. Focusing on systematic generalization, we carefully design a five-fold test set to evaluate both the interpolation and the extrapolation of learned concepts w.r.t. the three levels. Further, we design a few-shot learning split to determine whether or not models can rapidly learn new concepts and generalize them to more complex scenarios. To comprehend existing models' limitations, we undertake extensive experiments with various sequence-to-sequence models, including RNNs, Transformers, and GPT-3 (with the chain of thought prompting).

experiments.Description: To benchmark various sequence-to-sequence models on the HINT dataset to evaluate their systematic generalization capabilities across perception, syntax, and semantics. The evaluation is structured around five test subsets designed to probe specific generalization patterns like interpolation and extrapolation.
experiments.Method: The task is to predict the final integer result from an input image of a handwritten arithmetic expression. The input image is tokenized using a sliding window and encoded with a ResNet-18. This sequence is then fed into various encoder-decoder architectures. The baselines to be evaluated include Recurrent Neural Networks (LSTM, GRU, with and without attention), Transformers (vanilla, with relative positional encoding, and Universal Transformer), and GPT-3 (in zero-shot and zero-shot with Chain of Thought prompting settings). The evaluation will be performed on the five test subsets of HINT: I (perception generalization), SS (syntactic/semantic interpolation), LS (syntactic extrapolation), SL (semantic extrapolation), and LL (syntactic/semantic extrapolation).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy of the final results, where a prediction is correct only if it exactly matches the ground-truth answer.']

experiments.Description: To determine if models can rapidly learn new concepts from a few examples and generalize them to more complex compositions. This experiment uses a dedicated few-shot learning split with six new concepts (two numbers and four operators).
experiments.Method: Models are first pre-trained on the main HINT training set. Then, they are fine-tuned on a small set of examples for each new concept individually. The ability to generalize the newly learned concept will be tested on a corresponding test set. The top-performing models from the main benchmark experiment (LSTM w/ attn and Transformer rel. uni.) are intended to be used in this setup.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy of the final results on the few-shot test splits.']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether merely increasing the dataset size and the model size can solve the extrapolation challenges posed by the HINT dataset, akin to scaling laws observed in some NLP tasks.
experiments.Method: The experiment will involve training various-sized Transformer models by altering hidden dimensions, embedding dimensions, and the number of attention heads. Similarly, various-sized training sets will be generated by randomly sampling the original training set. The performance of these models will then be evaluated on the most challenging test subset (LL) to observe the scaling trend.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Test accuracy on the LL subset as a function of model size and data size.']

risk_factors: Despite a large visual variance, the handwritten expressions are rather basic in terms of spatial locations and visual complexity.
Although syntax and semantics in HINT are already more complex than those of prior datasets, they remain context-free.

title: The Trade-off between Universality and Label Efficiency of Representations from Contrastive Learning
name: contrastive-learning-universality-efficiency-tradeoff
short_hypothesis: In contrastive learning, a fundamental trade-off exists between universality (usefulness across a wide range of tasks) and label efficiency (learning with a small amount of labeled data). Pre-training on more diverse data improves universality but harms label efficiency by de-emphasizing task-specific features. A proposed contrastive regularization method, added during fine-tuning, can improve this trade-off.
related_work: Prior analyses of self-supervised pre-training typically assume the same data distribution for both pre-training and downstream prediction. This is a critical simplification, as the use of different distributions is what gives rise to the trade-off between universality and label efficiency. While some recent studies have noted this issue, they have not provided a systematic study or a formal analysis of the trade-off, which is the gap this paper aims to fill.
abstract: Pre-training representations has become a prevalent paradigm with two key desiderata: label efficiency (the ability to learn an accurate classifier with a small amount of labeled data) and universality (usefulness across a wide range of downstream tasks). This paper focuses on contrastive learning with linear probing and hypothesizes that a trade-off exists between these two desiderata, making it difficult to achieve both simultaneously. The work provides a theoretical analysis using a data model to show that while more diverse pre-training data leads to more diverse features (improving universality), it puts less emphasis on task-specific features, which could lead to larger sample complexity for downstream tasks. Guided by this analysis, a contrastive regularization method is proposed to improve the trade-off.

experiments.Description: To empirically verify the existence of the trade-off between universality and label efficiency using various contrastive learning methods and datasets.
experiments.Method: A ResNet18 backbone will be pre-trained using different contrastive learning methods (MoCo v2, NNCLR, SimSiam). The pre-training data will be incrementally made more diverse by adding datasets sequentially (e.g., for target task CIFAR-10, pre-training starts with CINIC-10, then adds SVHN, GTSRB, and ImageNet32). After pre-training, a linear classifier (Linear Probing) will be trained on the target task (CIFAR-10, MNIST, or Fer2013) using a small amount of labeled data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: The experiment will measure the test accuracy on the specific target task (as a proxy for label efficiency) and the average test accuracy across all datasets used in pre-training (as a proxy for universality).

experiments.Description: To inspect the similarity of learned features from different pre-training datasets to understand the mechanism of the trade-off.
experiments.Method: MoCo v2 models will be pre-trained on different single datasets (e.g., FaceScrub, CIFAR-10, SVHN, ImageNet32) and on their union. Features for a target task (Fer2013) will be extracted from each pre-trained model. The similarity between these different sets of features will be computed.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Linear Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) will be used to measure the similarity between feature representations.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the proposed contrastive regularization method can improve the trade-off when fine-tuning a pre-trained model.
experiments.Method: A ResNet18 model will be pre-trained with MoCo v2.
