Then, a predictor for the CIFAR-10 task will be learned using three different evaluation methods: Linear Probing (LP), standard Finetuning (FT), and the proposed Finetune with Contrastive Regularization (Ours). This will be compared across different pre-training data compositions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed contrastive regularization method on large-scale foundation models.
experiments.Method: The proposed method will be applied to several popular foundation models, including CLIP (ViT-L backbone), MoCo v3 (ViT-B backbone), and SimCSE (BERT backbone). These models will be evaluated on a spectrum of downstream tasks (ImageNet, SVHN, GTSRB for vision; IMDB, AGNews for text). The performance will be compared against standard baselines like Linear Probing (LP) and Finetuning (FT) with an adapter.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Test accuracy on the respective downstream tasks.

risk_factors: Our analysis thus requires restrictions on the dependence of the label on ZR (in particular, we will assume linear dependence).

To illustrate that inductive biases are still needed in our data model, consider the following simple example... on the representation φ, the classification is an XOR-problem... for which there is no non-trivial error bound for linear predictors. This contradicts the success of linear probing in practice.

title: Expressive Losses for Verified Robustness via Convex Combinations
name: expressive-losses-via-convex-combinations
short_hypothesis: The expressivity of a loss function, defined as its ability to interpolate between lower and upper bounds of the worst-case loss via a single parameter, is a key property for achieving state-of-the-art trade-offs between standard accuracy and verified robustness. Simple yet effective expressive losses can be constructed through convex combinations of adversarial attack-based losses and over-approximation-based losses (like IBP).
related_work: Current methods for verified training either directly optimize an over-approximated worst-case loss, which leads to poor standard accuracy, or enhance adversarial training with verifiability-inducing techniques, which can be brittle. Recent work like SABR combines these approaches by computing losses on over-approximations around adversarial attacks, but a principled understanding of why this combination is effective is needed. This work proposes 'expressivity' as the key underlying principle.
abstract: In order to train networks for verified adversarial robustness, it is common to over-approximate the worst-case loss over perturbation regions, resulting in networks that attain verifiability at the expense of standard performance. As shown in recent work, better trade-offs between accuracy and robustness can be obtained by carefully coupling adversarial training with over-approximations. We hypothesize that the expressivity of a loss function, which we formalize as the ability to span a range of trade-offs between lower and upper bounds to the worst-case loss through a single parameter (the over-approximation coefficient), is key to attaining state-of-the-art performance. To support our hypothesis, we show that trivial expressive losses, obtained via convex combinations between adversarial attacks and IBP bounds, can be designed to yield state-of-the-art results.

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether minimalistic expressive loss functions (CC-IBP, MTL-IBP, Exp-IBP) can attain state-of-the-art performance on various verified robustness benchmarks when compared to existing methods.
experiments.Method: The proposed methods will be trained on CIFAR-10, TinyImageNet, and downscaled ImageNet (64x64) against l-infinity norm perturbations. A 7-layer CNN architecture will be used. Performance will be compared against a suite of baselines including STAPS, SABR, SORTNET, IBP-R, CROWN-IBP, and COLT.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the over-approximation coefficient `α` on the accuracy-robustness trade-offs for the proposed expressive losses.
experiments.Method: The CC-IBP, MTL-IBP, and Exp-IBP models will be trained on CIFAR-10 with varying values of the coefficient `α`.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Standard accuracy', 'Adversarial accuracy', 'Verified robust accuracy under different verifiers (BaB, CROWN/IBP, IBP)']

experiments.Description: To investigate the relationship between the value of the expressive loss, the true worst-case loss (approximated by a branch-and-bound loss), and the resulting model performance, testing the common assumption that better approximations of the worst-case loss lead to better models.
experiments.Method: CC-IBP, MTL-IBP, and SABR models will be trained on a CIFAR-10 validation set while varying the coefficient `α`.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Standard loss', 'Branch-and-bound loss', 'Approximation error between the training loss and the branch-and-bound loss (BaB Error, BaB RMSE)']

risk_factors: while we demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on a suite of commonly-employed vision datasets, this does not guarantee the effectiveness of our methods beyond the tested benchmarks and, in particular, on different data domains.

title: Active Probabilistic Drug Discovery
name: active-probabilistic-drug-discovery
short_hypothesis: Based on the assumption that active molecules binding to a specific target are a small proportion of a library and exhibit cluster-distributed characteristics, a novel method, Active Probabilistic Drug Discovery (APDD), can iteratively update the binding probabilities of molecules to progressively enhance drug discovery performance. This is achieved through three consecutive steps: probabilistic clustering, selective docking of representative molecules, and active selection for wet-experiments to provide feedback.
related_work: Prior work has focused on integrating intelligent computing and automated experimentation. Methods for reducing the search space include unsupervised clustering (e.g., UMAP), separating molecules by chemical properties, or using fragment-based docking. However, these approaches often cannot effectively separate active molecules from decoys. The proposed work aims to address this by formulating drug discovery as an active probabilistic learning problem and using probabilistic clustering algorithms to more effectively identify desirable clusters of molecules.
abstract: Early drug discovery is challenged by the vastness of molecule libraries, high attrition rates, and the intricate nature of molecular interactions. To overcome these challenges, a paradigm shift towards integrating intelligence and automation is proposed. This innovative approach can be viewed as an active probabilistic learning problem, assuming that active molecules binding to a specific target are typically a small proportion and exhibit cluster-distributed characteristics. Based on this formulation, a novel active probabilistic drug discovery (APDD) method is proposed, which iteratively updates the binding probabilities of molecules to progressively enhance drug discovery performance with three consecutive steps: probabilistic clustering, selective docking, and active wet-experiment.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the APDD method in reducing computational and experimental costs compared to a baseline method on well-known screening datasets.
experiments.Method: The performance of APDD will be evaluated on the DUD-E (79 targets) and LIT-PCBA (11 targets) benchmark datasets. The baseline for comparison is Vina Enumeration (VE), which involves docking all candidate molecules against the target protein using VinaGPU+ and sequentially selecting molecules with the best affinity scores for simulated wet lab experiments. The APDD implementation will use Fast Probabilistic Clustering (FPC) with k=50 nearest neighbors and select two representative molecules from each cluster for initial docking.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The reduction of Vina-GPU+ docking', 'The reduction of wet lab experiments for discovering active molecules']

experiments.Description: To validate the underlying assumption that active molecules exhibit a cluster-grouping behavior.
experiments.Method: The distribution of active molecules will be analyzed using statistical results from the probabilistic clustering step on four proteins from DUD-E and four proteins from LIT-PCBA.

experiments.Description: To investigate the efficacy of APDD on large-scale datasets exceeding one million molecules.
experiments.Method: Five proteins will be randomly selected from the DUD-E dataset, and their inactive molecule set will be augmented to 1.4 million molecules. The APDD method will be tested on these augmented datasets and compared against the Vina Enumeration (VE) baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The reduction of Vina-GPU+ docking', 'The reduction of wet lab experiments']

risk_factors: It is worth noting that machine learning models cannot be retrained or fine-tuned due to the limited number of wet experiments. As a result, these methods are not included in the baseline comparison.

title: SRL: Scaling Distributed Reinforcement Learning to Over Ten Thousand Cores
name: srl-scalable-distributed-rl
short_hypothesis: A novel abstraction for RL dataflows, which decouples the major computational components (environment simulation, policy inference, training) into independent 'workers', will enable more efficient resource utilization, massive parallelism, and higher scalability for distributed RL training compared to existing tightly-coupled architectures.
related_work: Existing open-source distributed RL libraries (e.g., RLlib, ACME, SeedRL) are based on architectures like IMPALA-style or SEED-style. These systems suffer from several limitations: 1) Low resource efficiency due to tightly coupling multiple computational components onto the same node, leading to resource waste in heterogeneous clusters. 2) Simplistic implementations with inadequate optimizations for multi-node training, resulting in poor throughput in large-scale scenarios. 3) Tightly coupled algorithm and system interfaces, which makes it difficult to customize algorithms or extend the system for complex workflows. This leaves a gap for a system that is scalable, efficient, and extensible enough for large-scale applications.
abstract: The ever-growing complexity of reinforcement learning (RL) tasks demands a distributed system to efficiently generate and process a massive amount of data. However, existing open-source libraries suffer from various limitations, which impede their practical use in challenging scenarios where large-scale training is necessary. In this paper, we present a novel abstraction on the dataflows of RL training, which unifies diverse RL training applications into a general framework. Following this abstraction, we develop a scalable, efficient, and extensible distributed RL system called ReaLly Scalable RL (SRL), which allows efficient and massively parallelized training and easy development of customized algorithms. The system is designed to perform and scale well on common RL benchmarks and in challenging environments, with the goal of enabling experiments at a large scale with over 15k CPU cores.

experiments.Description: To compare the end-to-end training throughput of the proposed SRL system against existing open-source libraries in both single-machine and distributed settings.
experiments.Method: The experiment will run on Atari (Pong), Google Research Football (11_vs_11), StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC, 27m_vs_30m), and DMLab (watermaze) environments using the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm. SRL's performance will be compared against Sample Factory, rlpyt, and SeedRL in a single-machine setting, and against RLlib in a distributed setting.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training throughput, measured in Frames Per Second (FPS).']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the scalability of SRL's novel decoupled architecture against re-implementations of existing architectures as computing resources are progressively increased.
experiments.Method: Within the SRL framework, three architectures will be implemented and compared: the novel decoupled SRL architecture, an IMPALA-style architecture, and a SEED-style architecture. The evaluation will be conducted on a large-scale cluster across Atari, gFootball, SMAC, and DMLab environments, progressively increasing the allocation of computing resources.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Training throughput (FPS) as the number of trainer workers and allocated computing resources increases.']

experiments.Description: To validate the capability of SRL to support and unify different RL workflows and to analyze the scaling trends of representative RL algorithms.
experiments.Method: Four different RL algorithms (PPO, MAPPO, Apex-DQN, VDN) will be implemented in SRL and evaluated on the Atari benchmark suite and the gFootball environment. Agents will be trained for a fixed amount of time (8 hours) at various scales of batch size and number of workers.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Learning performance, measured by Interquartile Mean (IQM) of human-normalized score in Atari.', 'Learning performance, measured by IQM win rate in gFootball.']

experiments.Description: To demonstrate the applicability of SRL for addressing complex, large-scale problems by attempting to reproduce emergent behaviors in the challenging Hide-and-Seek (HnS) environment and to evaluate its performance.
experiments.Method: Agents will be trained in the HnS environment using PPO self-play with a very large batch size. The experiment will be conducted in a distributed setting using both inline CPU inference and remote GPU inference. A more challenging variant of HnS with a double-sized playground will also be used to explore the performance limit of SRL. The performance will be compared to the reported results of OpenAI's proprietary Rapid system.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ["Wall-clock time and data volume required to achieve the 'ramp lock' stage of emergent behavior.", 'Learning progress (number of locked ramps over time) in the harder HnS variant.']

risk_factors: It is important to note that the outcome in the DMLab environment exhibits instability as the number of actor workers increases. This is primarily due to the lengthy and highly variable reset time of the environment, which is inherent to the original environment implementation and lies beyond the scope of our system optimizations.

title: Causal Structure Learning in Hawkes Processes with Complex Latent Confounder Networks
name: Causal Discovery in Hawkes Processes with Latent Confounders
short_hypothesis: Continuous-time multivariate Hawkes process event sequences can be represented by a discrete-time linear causal model as the time interval shrinks. This representation allows for the derivation of necessary and sufficient conditions, based on second-order statistics (cross-covariance rank tests), to identify both the causal structure and the existence of latent confounder subprocesses without prior knowledge. A two-phase iterative algorithm is proposed to implement this, alternating between inferring causal relationships and discovering new latent subprocesses.
related_work: Existing methods for learning Multivariate Hawkes processes, whether based on likelihood estimation from continuous-time data or binned event counts, largely assume causal sufficiency (i.e., all relevant subprocesses are observed). They do not account for latent components. While causal discovery methods for latent variables exist, they are typically designed for i.i.d. data or time-series settings with restrictive assumptions (e.g., weak autocorrelation, exogenous latents, specific cardinality constraints) that are violated by the dense, endogenous dynamics of Hawkes processes. This leaves a gap for a principled framework that can identify latent subprocesses and recover causal structure in continuous-time event sequences without prior knowledge of the latents' existence or number.
abstract: Multivariate Hawkes process provides a powerful framework for modeling temporal dependencies and event-driven interactions in complex systems. While existing methods primarily focus on uncovering causal structures among observed subprocesses, real-world systems are often only partially observed, with latent subprocesses posing significant challenges. In this paper, we show that continuous-time event sequences can be represented by a discrete-time causal model as the time interval shrinks, and we leverage this insight to establish necessary and sufficient conditions for identifying latent subprocesses and the causal influences. Accordingly, we propose a two-phase iterative algorithm that alternates between inferring causal relationships among discovered subprocesses and uncovering new latent subprocesses, guided by path-based conditions that guarantee identifiability.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed method's ability to recover causal structures on synthetic data, comparing its performance against several baselines across various graph structures, including fully observed cases and cases with different types of latent confounders.
experiments.Method: The method will be tested on two types of synthetic data: event sequences generated by the Hawkes process (Eq. 1) and discrete-time data generated directly from the discrete-time model (Eq. 2). Six synthetic graph families will be used: one fully observed graph (Fig. 1b) and five structures with latent subprocesses (Figs. 2a and 3a-3d). The proposed algorithm will be compared against six baselines: SHP, THP, NPHC, Hier. Rank, RLCD, and LPCMCI.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the method on a real-world public cellular network dataset with an expert-validated ground truth, where one of the subprocesses is manually excluded to simulate a latent confounder.
experiments.Method: The experiment will use a public cellular network dataset (Qiao et al., 2023). A specific subgraph from device_id=8 will be analyzed, consisting of five alarm types (Alarm_ids=0-3 and 7), where Alarm_id=7 is treated as a latent subprocess. The proposed method's performance will be compared against representative baselines: SHP, THP, NPHC, Hier. Rank, RLCD, and LPCMCI.

risk_factors: One future research direction is to relax the excitation function, for example, by introducing node-specific decay rates, to broaden its applicability.
Another direction is to develop discovery algorithms with lower complexity.

If every latent confounder subprocess, along with all its observed surrogates (≥ 2), satisfies Definition 4.4, then the causal graph over observed and latent confounder subprocesses can be identified.

We consider that the excitation function takes the form φij(s) = aijw(s), ∀i, j ∈ {1, . . . , l}, where aij is a constant capturing the peer influence between event types i and j, and w(s) is a common decay function depending only on the time lag s.

title: TurboReBeL: 250$\times$ Accelerated Belief Learning for large Imperfect-Information Extensive-Form Games
name: TurboReBeL-Accelerated-IIEFG-Learning
short_hypothesis: By decoupling strategy evolution from value estimation using 'Single-Sample Multi-Iteration Generation' to generate data for all T CFR iterations in a single pass, and applying 'Isomorphic Data Augmentation' to leverage game symmetries, it is possible to dramatically accelerate the training of Recursive Belief-based Learning (ReBeL) for large Imperfect-Information Extensive-Form Games (IIEFGs) while preserving its performance and convergence guarantees.
related_work: Recursive Belief-based Learning (ReBeL) is a general self-play reinforcement learning framework for solving large Imperfect-Information Extensive-Form Games (IIEFGs) from scratch. However, its practical application is hindered by prohibitive training costs arising from three bottlenecks: high per-sample cost (requiring T full CFR iterations per sample), low sampling efficiency (generating only one data point per T iterations), and massive data requirements for the value network to converge. Prior methods like blueprint-based approaches depend on high-quality pre-trained strategies and extensive real-time computation, while data-driven methods are limited by fixed human data. The proposed work aims to address the fundamental efficiency barrier in the depth-limited solving paradigm, as exemplified by ReBeL, without sacrificing its benefits like from-scratch learning and theoretical guarantees.
abstract: Recursive Belief-based Learning (ReBeL) provides a general framework for large-scale Imperfect-Information Extensive-Form Games (IIEFGs) but suffers from prohibitive computational costs during training. Each sample requires T iterations of Counterfactual Regret Minimization (CFR), and convergence necessitates billions of samples. To address this, we propose TurboReBeL, which aims to accelerate training through two key innovations. The first is Single-Sample Multi-Iteration Generation, which fixes subgame strategies to CFR-averaged policies to generate training data for all T iterations in a single sampling pass, yielding a theoretical O(T) speedup. The second is Isomorphic Data Augmentation, which enhances sample diversity by applying game-theoretic invariants like suit and chip isomorphism with minimal overhead.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the training efficiency and exploitability of TurboReBeL in a simplified but strategically complex environment.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Turn Endgame Hold'em (TEH) game. The proposed TurboReBeL model, using Discounted CFR with T=250 iterations and K=2 isomorphic data augmentations, will be compared against implementations of ReBeL and DeepStack. For a fair comparison, the baseline models will also be enhanced with isomorphic data augmentation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the heads-up performance of TurboReBeL in the full-scale game of Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em (HUNL) against a strong benchmark agent.
experiments.Method: The experiment will be conducted on the Heads-Up No-Limit Texas Hold'em (HUNL) game. The TurboReBeL agent will be trained and then evaluated against the Slumbot agent, the champion of the Annual Computer Poker Competition. Its performance will be compared to the reported performance of prior methods like ReBeL and Student of Games (SoG) against the same opponent.

risk_factors: It is important to note that these limitations are inherent to the depth-limited solving paradigm, not unique to ReBeL. Any previous depth-limited solving method will face similar scalability challenges, highlighting the critical need for fundamental improvements in sampling efficiency and underscoring the significance of our work in overcoming these barriers.

title: Statistically Meaningful Approximation: a Theoretical Analysis for Approximating Turing Machines with Transformers
name: statistically-meaningful-approximation
short_hypothesis: A new formal definition of 'statistically meaningful (SM) approximation', which requires an approximating neural network to not only exist but also exhibit good statistical learnability, can provide more meaningful theoretical guarantees than classical approximation theory. This can be demonstrated by showing that feedforward networks and Transformers can SM-approximate boolean circuits and Turing machines, respectively, with significantly tighter sample complexity bounds than those derived from typical VC-dimension or norm-based analyses.
related_work: Classical universal approximation theory often shows that neural networks can approximate functions, but the constructions may be unrealistic, relying on infinite-precision weights, activations, or an exponential number of parameters. This issue also arises in work demonstrating that RNNs and Transformers can simulate computational models like Turing machines, where arbitrary precision is often required. These constructions are not plausibly learnable from finite data. The research gap is the lack of a theoretical framework that jointly considers a model's expressivity and its statistical learnability.
abstract: A common lens to theoretically study neural net architectures is to analyze the functions they can approximate. However, constructions from approximation theory may be unrealistic and therefore less meaningful, for example by encoding target function values using infinite precision. To address these issues, this work proposes a formal definition of statistically meaningful (SM) approximation which requires the approximating network to exhibit good statistical learnability. We study SM approximation for two function classes: boolean circuits and Turing machines. We propose to show that overparameterized feedforward neural nets can SM approximate boolean circuits and that transformers can SM approximate Turing machines, both with favorable sample complexities.

experiments.Description: To theoretically analyze whether overparameterized feedforward neural nets can 'statistically meaningfully' (SM) approximate boolean circuits, with a sample complexity that depends polynomially on the intrinsic circuit size rather than the (potentially much larger) network size.
experiments.Method: The task is to approximate the function class of layered boolean circuits (G_q,r,s) with a family of feedforward ReLU networks (F_w,d,a). The theoretical plan is to first construct a neural net that directly simulates a given boolean circuit. Then, 'correction functions' are inserted between layers to ensure the construction has a large 'all-layer margin', a measure of stability. Finally, this large margin is used to prove a tight sample complexity bound for the approximation.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The derived sample complexity bound for achieving ε-SM approximation with respect to the 0-1 loss.']

experiments.Description: To theoretically analyze whether Transformers can 'statistically meaningfully' (SM) approximate Turing machines, with a sample complexity that is polynomial in log(T) (where T is the computation time), representing an exponential improvement over what prior constructions would imply.
experiments.Method: The task is to approximate the function class of Turing machines with bounded computation time T (G_k,T) using an encoder-decoder Transformer architecture (F_w,d,a,T). The theoretical plan involves constructing a Transformer that simulates the Turing machine's computation step-by-step. This construction uses binary positional encodings (instead of high-precision ones) and implements a binary search over past timesteps using decoder self-attention. The 'all-layer margin' surrogate loss is then used to derive the sample complexity bound.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['The derived sample complexity bound for achieving ε-SM approximation with respect to the 0-1 loss.']

risk_factors: SM approximation essentially eliminates all statistical concerns for learnability (optimization-related concerns can remain).
Unfortunately, a rigorous analysis of optimization is unresolved even for simple two-layer nets (Mei et al., 2018).

title: Brain Signal Rendering: Unifying EEG Video Representations for Subject-level Few-shot Learning
name: brain-signal-rendering
short_hypothesis: Transforming EEG spectrograms into spatialized 'EEG videos' via a new paradigm called Brain Signal Rendering (BSR), and training with a unified multi-task approach called EEG Consolidation, will make representations invariant to electrode layouts, improve data efficiency, and enable subject-level few-shot learning.
related_work: EEG modeling faces two core challenges: the nonlinear, non-stationary dynamics of the signals and the severe channel mismatch that occurs across different datasets, which arises from varying electrode layouts and sampling protocols.
abstract: EEG modeling faces two core challenges: nonlinear, non-stationary dynamics and severe channel mismatch across datasets. We introduce Brain Signal Rendering (BSR), a new paradigm that reframes EEG representation learning as a rendering problem. BSR transforms EEG spectrograms into spatialized dynamic 'EEG videos', making representations invariant to electrode layouts and sampling protocols while preserving neural topology. Building on this, we propose EEG Consolidation — a unified multi-task training paradigm that integrates heterogeneous EEG-video data to adapt models to EEG-specific dynamics, improve data efficiency, reduce overfitting, and boost cross-task generalization. Crucially, BSR with EEG Consolidation is intended to enable subject-level few-shot learning, where each subject is treated as a distinct task requiring adaptation from minimal data.

experiments.Description: To validate the proposed framework in a subject-level few-shot learning setting, treating it as a realistic benchmark for brain signal models.
experiments.Method: The proposed BSR with EEG Consolidation method will be evaluated on heterogeneous EEG datasets. The experimental setup will treat each subject as a distinct few-shot learning task, requiring adaptation from minimal data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance gains will be measured to demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework.']

title: Reprogramming Large Pretrained Language Models for Antibody Sequence Infilling
name: reprogramming-llm-for-antibody-infilling
short_hypothesis: A large pretrained natural language model (BERT) can be repurposed for the generative task of protein sequence infilling, specifically for antibody CDR design, by freezing the original model and only training lightweight input and output projection layers. This model reprogramming approach is hypothesized to be data-efficient and capable of generating diverse and structurally sound antibody sequences in a low-resource setting without requiring structural information.
related_work: Computational design of antibodies, particularly the highly variable Complementarity-Determining Regions (CDRs), is a key challenge in biomedicine. Recent deep learning approaches, including language models and graph neural networks (GNNs), have shown success but face challenges. Training large models from scratch is difficult due to the limited availability of antibody structural data, which can lead to poor performance and low diversity in generated samples. GNN-based models often require structural information, which can be computationally expensive. The method of Model Reprogramming (MR) has been effective for repurposing pretrained models for classification tasks in data-scarce domains, but its application has not been extended to complex generative tasks like protein sequence infilling.
abstract: Antibodies comprise the most versatile class of binding molecules, with numerous applications in biomedicine. Therapeutic antibody development requires designing novel and diverse sequences with improved properties. Computational design of antibodies is challenging due to the multiple long, variable, and unstructured loops at the complementarity-determining region (CDR). Recently, deep language models and graph neural networks have shown success in antibody sequence generation, but training on limited data can lead to degraded performance and lack of diversity. To address such issues, we leverage the method of Model Reprogramming (MR), which focuses on repurposing pretrained models for target domain tasks with scarce data. We propose to extend the capabilities of reprogramming beyond classification towards the complex problem of antibody sequence generation. Specifically, we introduce Reprogramming for Protein Sequence Infilling, a framework to repurpose pretrained natural language models for protein sequence infilling. For variable CDR sequence design, we formulate the task as text infilling that uses the constant region of an antibody as the sequence template.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the proposed models on template-constrained design of individual heavy chain CDRs (CDR-H1, CDR-H2, and the more challenging CDR-H3).
experiments.Method: The models will be evaluated on the Structural Antibody Database (SabDab). The task is to infill masked CDR-H1, CDR-H2, and CDR-H3 regions separately. The proposed model, ReprogBert, will be compared against sequence-only baselines (LSTM), sequence-and-structure-based baselines (AR-GNN, RefineGNN), and two other proposed BERT-based infilling models: ProtBert (pretrained on proteins) and EnglishBert (BERT with retrained embeddings).
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Amino acid recovery (AAR)', 'Diversity (DIV)', 'Perplexity (PPL-ProGen)', 'Template Modeling (TM) score', 'Root Mean Squared Deviation (RMSD)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the ability of the proposed BERT-based models to infill multiple CDR loops (H1, H2, and H3) simultaneously, a more challenging task.
experiments.Method: Using the SabDab dataset, the task is to generate all three heavy chain CDR loops at once. The proposed models (ReprogBert, ProtBert, EnglishBert) will be evaluated, as they are not architecturally limited to a single region generation, unlike some baselines.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the models on the task of antigen-specific antibody design, where the goal is to design a CDR that binds a given antigen.
experiments.Method: The models will be evaluated on the Rosetta Antibody Design (RabD) dataset for the task of CDR-H3 design. The proposed models (ReprogBert, ProtBert, EnglishBert) will be compared against a physics-based baseline (RabD) and other deep learning models (LSTM, AR-GNN, RefineGNN).

experiments.Description: To evaluate the models on designing CDR-H3 for antibodies that bind to coronaviruses, a task for which ground truth structural information is unavailable.
experiments.Method: The models will be trained and evaluated on the Coronavirus Antibody Database (CoV-AbDab). Two training scenarios will be tested: training only on CoV-AbDab, and training on a combination of CoV-AbDab and SabDab. The proposed models (ReprogBert, ProtBert, EnglishBert) will be compared.

experiments.Description: To measure the ability of the generated antibodies to neutralize the SARS-CoV-2 virus by fine-tuning the infilling models.
experiments.Method: The infilling models will be additionally fine-tuned to generate CDRs that result in better neutralizing antibodies. The generated sequences will be evaluated using a pre-trained neutralization classifier. The proposed models (ReprogBert, ProtBert, EnglishBert) will be compared against baselines (LSTM, AR-GNN, Refine-GNN) under two training scenarios (with and without SabDab pretraining).

risk_factors: Since our BERT-based infill models do not estimate protein structure, we use AlphaFold and IgFold to estimate 3D structure from the generated sequence and compute TM and RMSD scores with respect to groundtruth native structure. However, these values are consistently higher when compared to RMSD for "natively" predicted structure (AR-GNN and Refine-GNN), which is likely due to the estimation errors introduced by the AlphaFold or IgFold algorithm.

title: Prospect Pruning: Finding Trainable Weights at Initialization using Meta-Gradients
name: Prospect Pruning with Meta-Gradients
short_hypothesis: By using meta-gradients to backpropagate through the first few steps of optimization, we can compute a saliency score that accounts for a weight's trainability, rather than just its immediate impact on the loss at initialization. This will allow us to identify more trainable subnetworks when pruning at initialization.
related_work: Current pruning-at-initialization methods (e.g., SNIP) use saliency criteria, often based on first-order gradients, to estimate the importance of weights. However, these criteria are myopic as they only consider the effect of pruning on the loss at a single point in time, before training begins. They fail to account for the fact that the network will be trained after pruning. While iterative pruning methods exist, they are computationally expensive and cumbersome. There is a need for a single-shot pruning-at-initialization method that explicitly considers the subsequent training process in its saliency criterion.
abstract: Pruning neural networks at initialization could enable finding sparse models that retain accuracy while consuming fewer computational resources. However, current methods are insufficient and lead to large performance degradation. This is because their saliency criteria look at a single step at the start of training without accounting for the network's trainability. To overcome this short-sightedness, we propose Prospect Pruning (ProsPr), a method which uses meta-gradients through the first few steps of optimization to determine which weights to prune. ProsPr combines an estimate of the higher-order effects of pruning on the loss with the optimization trajectory to identify a trainable sub-network.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of Prospect Pruning (ProsPr) against existing pruning-at-initialization and pruning-after-convergence methods on standard vision classification benchmarks.
experiments.Method: ProsPr will be evaluated on ResNet-20 and VGG-16 for the CIFAR-10 dataset, and ResNet-18 for the Tiny-ImageNet dataset. Performance will be compared against Unpruned Baseline, Weight Rewinding (LTR), Magnitude (after training), Magnitude (at initialization), SNIP, GraSP, SynFlow, and Random pruning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of a first-order approximation of ProsPr on the large-scale ImageNet dataset, where computing full meta-gradients is computationally intensive.
experiments.Method: The first-order approximation of ProsPr will be evaluated on ResNet-50 and VGG-19 architectures using the ImageNet dataset. It will be compared against state-of-the-art methods including FORCE, Iter-SNIP, and multi-batch versions of SNIP and GraSP (SNIP-MB, GraSP-MB).

experiments.Description: To evaluate ProsPr in a structured pruning setting, where entire channels or columns of linear layers are removed instead of individual weights.
experiments.Method: ProsPr will be adapted for structured pruning on a VGG-19 model, evaluated on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. It will be compared against the 3SP method (an extension of SNIP for structured pruning) and its variants (3SP + reinit, 3SP + rescale), as well as a Random baseline.

experiments.Description: To analyze the effect of the number of meta-gradient steps (M) on the performance of structured pruning with ProsPr.
experiments.Method: The structured pruning experiment on VGG-19 with CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 will be repeated, varying the number of inner training steps (M) from 0 to 3.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the flexibility and applicability of ProsPr on a segmentation task.
experiments.Method: ProsPr will be used to prune a U-Net architecture in a single shot at initialization. The task will be image segmentation on the PhC-C2DH-U373 and DIC-C2DH-HeLa datasets. The method will be compared to a baseline that uses a multi-step approach of gradual magnitude-pruning interleaved with fine-tuning.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the robustness of ProsPr when using a non-random, self-supervised initialization instead of a random one.
experiments.Method: A ResNet-18 model will be initialized with weights learned via BYOL. ProsPr will then be applied to this initialized model for pruning. The task will be classification on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. The performance will be compared to using ProsPr with a standard random initialization.

risk_factors: In practice increasing the number of steps beyond 3 poses a lot of gradient stability issues (and is computationally expensive).

Taking the meta-gradient through many model updates can be memory intensive: in the forward pass, all gradients of the individual update steps need to be retained in memory to then be able to backpropagate all the way to the initial mask.

We start to see gradient stability issues when computing gradients over deep loops. Gradient degradation problems, i.e., vanishing and exploding gradients, have also been observed in other fields that use meta-gradients such as Meta-Learning.

title: MathFimer: Enhancing Mathematical Reasoning by Expanding Reasoning Steps through Fill-in-the-Middle Task
name: math-fim-step-expansion
short_hypothesis: Adapting the 'Fill-in-the-middle' (FIM) paradigm from code generation to mathematical problem-solving can be an efficient and reliable method for expanding reasoning steps. By training a specialized model to reconstruct missing intermediate steps between prefix-suffix pairs of a solution chain, we can then use this model to enrich existing reasoning datasets with more detailed steps, which should in turn improve the mathematical reasoning capabilities of LLMs fine-tuned on this expanded data.
related_work: Recent work has shown that the granularity and completeness of reasoning steps in training data, such as in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, significantly impact an LLM's reasoning performance. Existing approaches to expand these reasoning steps often rely on more powerful external models, which is not scalable, or use computationally expensive search algorithms like MCTS. Furthermore, these methods often generate entirely new reasoning chains, which can introduce errors and reduce solution reliability. This creates a need for a more efficient and reliable method for expanding reasoning steps that preserves the validity of the original human-generated solutions.
abstract: Mathematical reasoning represents a critical frontier in advancing large language models (LLMs). While step-by-step approaches have emerged as the dominant paradigm for mathematical problem-solving in LLMs, the quality of reasoning steps in training data fundamentally constrains the performance of the models. Recent studies have demonstrated that more detailed intermediate steps can enhance model performance, yet existing methods for step expansion either require more powerful external models or incur substantial computational costs. In this paper, we introduce MathFimer, a novel framework for mathematical reasoning step expansion inspired by the “Fill-in-the-middle” task from code reasoning. By decomposing solution chains into prefix-suffix pairs and training models to reconstruct missing intermediate steps, we develop a specialized model, MathFimer-7B, on our carefully curated NuminaMath-FIM dataset. We then apply these models to enhance existing mathematical reasoning datasets by inserting detailed intermediate steps into their solution chains, creating MathFimer-expanded versions.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the effectiveness of the MathFimer framework by comparing the performance of LLMs fine-tuned on original mathematical reasoning datasets versus datasets expanded by MathFimer.
experiments.Method: First, a specialized model, MathFimer-7B, is trained on a newly constructed NuminaMath-FIM dataset, which is derived from NuminaMath-CoT using a fill-in-the-middle objective. This MathFimer-7B model is then used to expand the reasoning steps in several datasets, including a mixture of GSM8K and MATH, MathInstruct-CoT, and MetaMathQA. Multiple base LLMs (Meta-Llama-3.1-8B/70B, Qwen2.5-Math-7B/72B) are then fine-tuned on both the original and the MathFimer-expanded versions of these datasets for comparison.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on GSM8K, MATH, Math Odyssey, and OlympiadBench-EN benchmarks.']

experiments.Description: To investigate the scalability and effect of iterative step expansion using MathFimer.
experiments.Method: The MathFimer model is applied for up to three successive iterations to expand the reasoning chains in the MathInstruct-CoT dataset. A base model is then fine-tuned on the datasets resulting from each iteration.

experiments.Description: To investigate the relationship between the model capacity of the MathFimer model and its step expansion capability.
experiments.Method: Three MathFimer models of different sizes (1.5B, 7B, 72B) are trained on identical data. Each of these models is then used to expand the MathInstruct-CoT dataset. A base model is fine-tuned on each of the resulting expanded datasets.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on GSM8K, MATH, Odyssey, and OB-EN benchmarks.']

experiments.Description: To compare the proposed training-based FIM approach with a prompt-based, zero-shot approach for step expansion.
experiments.Method: A general-purpose instruction-tuned model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) is prompted in a zero-shot manner to expand intermediate steps in the GSM8K+MATH and MathInstruct-CoT datasets. A base model is then fine-tuned on this prompt-expanded data. This will be compared against models fine-tuned on data expanded by the trained MathFimer models.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Accuracy on GSM8K, MATH, Odyssey, and OB-EN benchmarks.']

experiments.Description: To evaluate whether the enhanced reasoning behaviors from training on MathFimer-expanded math data generalize to out-of-distribution (OOD) non-mathematical reasoning tasks.
experiments.Method: Base models (Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B) are fine-tuned on MathFimer-expanded MathInstruct-CoT. These models are then evaluated on OOD benchmarks for general reasoning (BBH), scientific reasoning (GPQA_diamond, MMLU_redux), and logical reasoning (LogicBenchBQA, HellaSwag).

experiments.Description: To verify if the FIM method can be effectively trained on data from other domains to perform step expansion and improve downstream task performance.
experiments.Method: A ReasoningFimer-1.5B model is trained on FIM data constructed from natural reasoning data. This model is then used to expand steps in a logical-reasoning dataset, LogiCoT. A final model is trained on the expanded LogiCoT data.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Performance on general (BBH), scientific (GPQA_D, MMLU_R), and logic (LogicBench, hellaswag) reasoning benchmarks.']

risk_factors: While our approach demonstrates effectiveness in mathematical reasoning, its applicability to other reasoning domains remains uncertain.
Our step expansion process inherently relies on model generation, introducing potential risks of error propagation.
The framework's effectiveness inherently depends on the quality of initial training data and may inherit biases from base models.

Additionally, the current approach primarily focuses on expanding existing solution patterns rather than generating novel solution approaches, potentially limiting its applicability to extremely complex or unconventional problems.

However, MathFimer may not be suitable for certain categories of data. First, for datasets whose CoT traces are already highly refined, such as R1-like reasoning trajectories, MathFimer may offer limited benefits, as the existing reasoning steps are sufficiently detailed.
Moreover, MathFimer may also struggle with non-linear reasoning processes.

title: Augmenting Transformers with Recursively Composed Multi-grained Representations
name: ReCAT-Recursive-Composition-Transformer
short_hypothesis: Proposing novel Contextual Inside-Outside (CIO) layers as an augmentation to the Transformer architecture will allow the model to explicitly emulate the hierarchical composition process of language. Inserting these stacked CIO layers between the embedding and self-attention layers will enable both deep intra-span and inter-span interactions, generating multi-grained, contextualized span representations that can be jointly pre-trained with Transformers to improve performance and interpretability without relying on gold parse trees.
related_work: Existing methods for incorporating syntactic structures into neural models either rely on gold parse trees (e.g., RvNNs, Transformer Grammars), which are expensive and limit scalability, or induce structures in an unsupervised manner. However, unsupervised methods like DIORA and R2D2 are constrained by an information access protocol that prevents communication across span boundaries, making it infeasible to jointly pre-train them with Transformers. The proposed work aims to overcome these limitations by enabling scalable, unsupervised structure induction with cross-span communication, allowing for joint pre-training with a Transformer architecture.
abstract: Existing research on explicitly modeling hierarchical text structures often lacks inter-span communication. To overcome this, we propose ReCAT, a recursive composition augmented Transformer. ReCAT introduces novel contextual inside-outside (CIO) layers, which perform iterative bottom-up and top-down passes to learn contextualized span representations. The bottom-up pass composes low-level spans into high-level ones, while the top-down pass combines information from inside and outside a span. By stacking these CIO layers between the embedding and attention layers of a Transformer, the model is designed to perform deep intra-span and inter-span interactions, generating fully contextualized, multi-grained representations without needing gold trees for training or inference.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the contextualized span representations produced by the ReCAT model on span-level classification tasks.
experiments.Method: The model is pre-trained on WikiText103 and then fine-tuned on four tasks from the OntoNotes 5.0 corpus: Named entity labeling (NEL), Semantic role labeling (SRL), Constituent labeling (CTL), and Coreference resolution (COREF). The proposed ReCAT model is compared against Transformer and Fast-R2D2 baselines. BERT is included for reference.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of the ReCAT model on sentence-level language understanding tasks.
experiments.Method: The model is pre-trained on WikiText103 and then evaluated on the GLUE benchmark. Baselines include vanilla Transformers with varying layer counts (3, 6, 9), Fast-R2D2, a DIORA variant, and other RvNN variants such as Gumbel-Tree, CRvNN, and Ordered Memory for reference.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform unsupervised grammar induction and analyze the accuracy of the induced syntactic trees.
experiments.Method: The model is pre-trained on Wiki-103 and then continue-trained on the PTB dataset for unsupervised parsing. The primary baseline for comparison is Fast-R2D2. Other specialized grammar induction models (C-PCFG, NBL-PCFG, TN-PCFG) and structure-inducing encoders (ON-LSTM, S-DIORA, StructFormer) are included for reference.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['F1 score of unsupervised parsing', 'Recall of constituents (NNP, VP, NP, ADJP)']

risk_factors: The application of ReCAT could be limited by its computational load during training.

title: Guiding Transformers to Process in Steps
name: guiding-transformers-with-intermediate-steps
short_hypothesis: For algorithmic 'System 2' tasks, which are difficult to learn via a direct input-output mapping, providing additional guidance in the form of intermediate computational steps in the training data is necessary for neural networks to learn the task.
related_work: Prior work on enabling 'System 2' reasoning in neural networks has focused on modifying model architectures, such as adding external memory (Neural Turing Machines) or stacks (Stack-augmented RNNs). Another line of work provides extra supervision on specific architectural components (e.g., execution traces for Neural Programmer-Interpreters). This paper diverges by proposing to modify the training data itself, rather than the model architecture. The proposed method formulates tasks as sequence-to-sequence problems where the target sequence includes not just the final output but also the intermediate steps a human might write down, making the approach architecture-agnostic.
abstract: Neural networks have not been as successful when applied to “System 2” tasks that involve conscious multi-step reasoning. In this work, we argue that the kind of training that works for System 1 tasks is not sufficient for System 2 tasks, and propose an alternative. Specifically, while learning a direct mapping from inputs to outputs is feasible for System 1 tasks, we argue that algorithmic System 2 tasks can only be solved by learning a mapping from inputs to outputs through a series of intermediate steps. We propose to show that a 1-layer 1-head Transformer can in principle compute any finite function by using enough intermediate steps. We then plan to show empirically that a Transformer may fail to learn to compute the sum of binary numbers directly, but can succeed when trained to first generate a series of intermediate results. This would demonstrate how a fixed-size neural network can lack the expressivity to encode the direct input-output mapping for an algorithmic task and yet be fully capable of computing the outputs through intermediate steps. We also propose to test this on a Frozen Pretrained Transformer for binary addition.

experiments.Description: To test the hypothesis that a shallow model, which may lack the expressivity to learn a direct input-output mapping, can learn the same task if it is decomposed into intermediate steps. The experiment uses the task of binary addition.
experiments.Method:
A 1-layer 1-head decoder-only Transformer (minGPT) will be trained on binary addition of n-bit numbers (n from 8 to 48), framed as a sequence completion task. The model will be trained in three different regimes:
1. Weak supervision: The model is trained to predict the sum immediately after the inputs, with loss only on the sum.
2. Medium supervision: The model is trained to predict the sum after a sequence of intermediate values (from logic gate decomposition), but the loss is computed only on the final sum.
3. Strong supervision: The model is trained to predict both the intermediate values and the final sum, with the loss computed on the entire generated sequence.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Exact-match accuracy against the target output bits representing the sum.']

experiments.Description: To investigate whether a Frozen Pretrained Transformer (FPT) can solve a multi-step algorithmic task by composing simpler circuits from its pretrained weights, and whether providing intermediate steps is necessary for it to learn the task.
experiments.Method: A Frozen Pretrained Transformer (a pretrained GPT-2 model with most weights frozen) will be fine-tuned on the task of binary addition. The model will be trained in three regimes analogous to the first experiment (weak, medium, and strong supervision), but using only the carry bits as the intermediate steps for the medium and strong supervision settings.

risk_factors: While manually increasing the amount of supervision goes against the tradition of minimizing the reliance on expert knowledge that arguably lead to most of the recent achievements in the System 1 domain, System 2 tasks represent a structurally different class of problems for which a different set of constraints may apply.

title: Universal Multi-Domain Translation via Diffusion Routers
name: diffusion-router-umdt
short_hypothesis: A single, unified diffusion-based framework, termed Diffusion Router (DR), conditioned on source and target domain labels, can learn to translate between any pair of K domains when trained on only K-1 paired datasets involving a central domain. This approach enables both indirect translations (routing through the central domain) and efficient direct translations between non-central domains via a novel variational-bound objective and a Tweedie refinement procedure.
related_work: Existing Multi-Domain Translation (MDT) approaches are limited. They either require fully aligned data tuples across all domains, which is often impractical to collect, or they are trained on multiple paired datasets with a central domain but can only support translations between the central and non-central domains, failing to address translations between two non-central domains. Recent diffusion-based MDT methods often rely on contrastive pretraining, require synthetic fully-aligned data, or have model sizes that scale linearly with the number of domains.
abstract: Multi-domain translation (MDT) aims to learn translations between multiple domains, yet existing approaches either require fully aligned tuples or can only handle domain pairs seen in training, limiting their practicality and excluding many cross-domain mappings. We introduce universal MDT (UMDT), a generalization of MDT that seeks to translate between any pair of K domains using only K-1 paired datasets with a central domain. To tackle this problem, we propose Diffusion Router (DR), a unified diffusion-based framework that models all central<->non-central translations with a single noise predictor conditioned on the source and target domain labels. DR enables indirect non-central translations by routing through the central domain. We further introduce a novel scalable learning strategy with a variational-bound objective and an efficient Tweedie refinement procedure to support direct non-central mappings.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the proposed Diffusion Router (DR) on a paired image-to-image translation task under the newly proposed Universal Multi-Domain Translation (UMDT) setting, comparing its ability to perform translations between a central 'shoe' domain and non-central 'edge' and 'grayscale' domains, as well as directly between the non-central domains.
experiments.Method: Dataset: Shoes-UMDT, created from the Edges2Shoes dataset. The 'shoe' domain is central, paired with 'edge' and 'grayscale' non-central domains. Models: Two versions of the proposed method, iDR (for indirect translation) and dDR (for direct translation), will be evaluated. Baselines: StarGAN, UniDiffuser, and Rectified Flow.

experiments.Description: To evaluate DR on a more complex face translation task under the UMDT setting, involving translations between a central 'face' domain and non-central 'segment' and 'sketch' domains, where the data for non-central domains comes from different underlying distributions.
experiments.Method: Dataset: Faces-UMDT (evaluated in both pixel space and latent space versions), created by combining CelebA-Mask-HQ and FFHQ datasets. The 'face' domain is central, paired with 'segment' and 'sketch' non-central domains. Models: iDR and dDR. Baselines: StarGAN, UniDiffuser, and Rectified Flow.

experiments.Description: To evaluate DR's scalability and performance on a large, diverse dataset with multiple domains, testing its capabilities in both star-shaped and chain-shaped data topologies.
experiments.Method: Datasets: COCO-UMDT-Star and COCO-UMDT-Chain, created from the COCO-Stuff dataset. Domains include color, segment, sketch, and depth. Models: iDR and dDR. Baselines: StarGAN, UniDiffuser, and Rectified Flow.

risk_factors: In future work, we aim to extend DR to large-scale multimodal generation across image, text, and audio, particularly addressing scenarios where paired datasets (e.g., image↔audio) are scarce in practice.

title: ResTran: A GNN Alternative To Learn Graph With Features
name: ResTran-Resistance-Transformation
short_hypothesis: A simple feature transformation, called Resistance Transformation (ResTran), can create a vector representation that incorporates both node features and graph topology. Applying standard vector-based machine learning methods to this new representation will be more robust to the homophilous bias inherent in established Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures, especially on heterophilous graphs.
related_work: The modern approach for vertex classification on graphs with features is Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). However, GNN architectures are known to have a bias towards learning homophilous information, where connected nodes are assumed to be similar. This bias is exacerbated by stacking layers, a phenomenon known as over-smoothing. While some recent, complex GNN models aim to mitigate this bias, this paper proposes a simpler alternative. Instead of modifying the GNN architecture, the proposed approach is to create a new vector representation that captures both feature and graph structure, and then apply standard, simpler vector-based learning methods.
abstract: This paper considers a vertex classification task where we are given a graph and associated vector features. The modern approach to this task is graph neural networks (GNNs). However, due to the nature of GNN architectures, GNNs are known to be biased to primarily learn homophilous information. To overcome this bias in GNN architectures, we take a simpler alternative approach to GNNs. Our approach is to obtain a vector representation capturing both features and the graph topology. We then apply standard vector-based learning methods to this vector representation. For this approach, we propose a simple transformation of features, which we call Resistance Transformation (abbreviated as ResTran).

experiments.Description: To evaluate if the ResTran representation improves upon using only graph information or only feature information in an unsupervised learning context.
experiments.Method: The experiment compares three settings on an unsupervised clustering task using spectral clustering: 1) 'Graph-Only' using the graph Laplacian, 2) 'Feature-Only' using a Gram matrix from features with a Gaussian kernel, and 3) 'Graph + Feature (Ours)' using a Gram matrix constructed from the proposed ResTran (XG) representation. A standard k-means is then applied to the resulting eigenvectors. The evaluation is planned for datasets including Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, Texas, Cornell, and Wisconsin.

experiments.Description: To numerically evaluate if ResTran improves upon existing GNN methods in a semi-supervised learning (SSL) setting on both homophilous and heterophilous graphs.
experiments.Method: ResTran's performance is evaluated by applying various vector-based models to its output representation (XG). These models include non-neural network methods like Label Propagation (LP) and Support Vector Machines (SVM), and neural network methods like Variational Adversarial Training (VAT) and Auxiliary Deep Generative Models (AVAE). These are compared against established GNN baselines: GCN, GAT, and SGC. The evaluation is planned for homophilous datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed, Amazon Photo, Amazon Computer) and heterophilous datasets (Texas, Cornell, Wisconsin, chameleon, squirrel, actor) using a 5% label rate for training.

risk_factors: Limitation and future work are that we are unsure how much ResTran has an expressive power, as done in (Xu et al., 2019).
We conjecture that the expressive power of ResTran is less than the 2-WL test.
Thus, we speculate that we need a different setup for triangle counting problems.

title: ToonComposer: Streamlining Cartoon Production with Generative Post-Keyframing
name: tooncomposer-post-keyframing
short_hypothesis: A generative model, ToonComposer, can unify the inbetweening and colorization stages of cartoon production into a single 'post-keyframing' stage. This can be achieved by adapting a DiT-based video foundation model using a novel Spatial Low-Rank Adapter (SLRA) to learn cartoon styles while preserving temporal priors, and a sparse sketch injection mechanism to enable precise control from minimal artist input like a single reference frame and sparse keyframe sketches.
related_work: Traditional cartoon production involves labor-intensive, separate stages of keyframing, inbetweening, and colorization. Recent AI methods automate inbetweening and colorization but still treat them as separate processes. This sequential approach leads to error accumulation. Inbetweening models struggle with large motions from sparse sketches, while colorization models often require dense per-frame inputs, increasing artist workload. Furthermore, adapting modern video foundation models with coupled spatio-temporal attention (like DiTs) to the cartoon domain is challenging, as previous spatial-only tuning methods designed for UNet architectures are inapplicable and risk disrupting the model's temporal priors.
abstract: Traditional cartoon and anime production involves keyframing, inbetweening, and colorization stages, which require intensive manual effort. Despite recent advances in AI, existing methods often handle these stages separately, leading to error accumulation and artifacts. For instance, inbetweening approaches struggle with large motions, while colorization methods require dense per-frame sketches. To address this, we introduce ToonComposer, a generative model that unifies inbetweening and colorization into a single post-keyframing stage. ToonComposer employs a sparse sketch injection mechanism to provide precise control using keyframe sketches. Additionally, we propose a novel cartoon adaptation method with the spatial low-rank adapter to effectively tailor a modern video foundation model to the cartoon domain while keeping its temporal prior intact. Requiring as few as a single sketch and a colored reference frame, ToonComposer excels with sparse inputs, while also supporting multiple sketches at any temporal location for more precise motion control. This dual capability is intended to reduce manual workload and improve flexibility, empowering artists in real-world scenarios. To evaluate our model, we created PKBench, a benchmark featuring human-drawn sketches that simulate real-world use cases.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance on a synthetic benchmark where ground-truth video is available for reference-based metric calculation.
experiments.Method: The ToonComposer model will be compared against baseline methods on a synthetic benchmark derived from cartoon movies. Baselines (AniDoc, LVCD, ToonCrafter) will be evaluated in a two-stage process: first generating dense sketches via interpolation with ToonCrafter, then colorizing them. ToonComposer will perform this in a single stage. Sketches for the evaluation are generated using a consistent sketch model.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['LPIPS', 'DISTS', 'CLIP image similarity', 'Subject Consistency (S. C.)', 'Motion Consistency (M. C.)', 'Background Consistency (B. C.)', 'Aesthetic Quality (A. Q.)']

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's performance in a more realistic scenario using a benchmark with human-drawn sketches, where no ground-truth video is available.
experiments.Method: The ToonComposer model will be compared against baselines (AniDoc, LVCD, ToonCrafter) on the newly created PKBench. This benchmark contains 30 samples, each with a colored reference frame, a text prompt, and two real sketches drawn by professional artists.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Subject Consistency (S. C.)', 'Motion Consistency (M. C.)', 'Background Consistency (B. C.)', 'Aesthetic Quality (A. Q.)', 'User study on human perceptual quality (Aesthetic Quality and Motion Quality)']

experiments.Description: To conduct ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of the core components of ToonComposer: the Spatial Low-Rank Adapter (SLRA) and the sparse sketch injection mechanism.
experiments.Method: Variants of ToonComposer will be trained and evaluated on the synthetic benchmark. For the SLRA ablation, it will be compared against alternative adaptation strategies including temporal-only (TO), spatial-temporal (ST), a simplified linear adaptation (LA), and standard LoRA. For the sparse sketch injection ablation, the proposed mechanism will be replaced with a conventional channel-wise concatenation approach. Further ablations will assess the impact of removing the positional-encoding mapping and the position-aware residual.

risk_factors: Although employing data augmentation to enhance robustness, ToonComposer may experience performance degradation when handling drastic sketch style variations, such as thick ink strokes, which can lead to reduced alignment with keyframe details in highly unconventional cases.

Moreover, the computational cost of the underlying video diffusion model remains substantial, requiring significant resources for training and inference, which may limit accessibility in resource-constrained settings.
Optimizing the model's inference speed presents a valuable direction for future research.

title: Code Translation with Compiler Representations
name: code-translation-with-compiler-ir
short_hypothesis: Augmenting training data for neural code translation with the corresponding compiler Intermediate Representation (IR) can benefit a Neural Transpiler in two ways: it helps align embeddings for different languages and improves the semantic understanding of the code, thereby improving the semantic quality of neural translations.
related_work: Traditional transpilers use handcrafted rules, which produce unidiomatic code. Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models produce more natural-looking code but often fail to preserve semantics because they treat code as a sequence of text tokens, not differentiating between similar pieces of code with different semantics. This leads to low-quality translations and reduces the practicality of NMT for code.
abstract: We leverage low-level compiler intermediate representations (IR) to improve code translation. Traditional transpilers rely on syntactic information and handcrafted rules, which limits their applicability and produces unnatural-looking code. Applying neural machine translation (NMT) approaches to code has successfully broadened the set of programs on which one can get a natural-looking translation. However, they treat the code as sequences of text tokens, and still do not differentiate well enough between similar pieces of code which have different semantics in different languages. The consequence is low quality translation, reducing the practicality of NMT, and stressing the need for an approach significantly increasing its accuracy. Here we propose to augment code translation with IRs, specifically LLVM IR, with results on the C++, Java, Rust, and Go languages. We also propose to train models with high performance on the problem of IR decompilation and study using IRs as an intermediary pivot for translation.

experiments.Description: To evaluate if augmenting unsupervised code translation with compiler IR improves translation quality. This is done by introducing three new training objectives that leverage parallel source code and IR data.
experiments.Method: A Transformer model (TransCoder architecture) is trained for unsupervised translation between C++, Java, Rust, and Go. In addition to the standard MLM, DAE, and Back-Translation objectives, the model is trained with three new objectives: Translation Language Modeling (TLM), Translation Auto-Encoding (TAE), and IR Generation (MT), which use parallel data of source code and its corresponding LLVM IR. The performance will be compared against a baseline TransCoder model (trained without IR) and an 'IR pivot' method.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the performance of a neural model for decompilation, i.e., generating source code from a given IR.
experiments.Method: A Transformer model is trained to reverse the compilation process by predicting source code (C++, Java, Rust, Go) from its corresponding LLVM IR. This is a supervised task. The model's performance will be compared against RetDec, a rule-based decompiler.

experiments.Description: To evaluate an alternative translation method that uses IR as an intermediary pivot.
experiments.Method: This method, called 'IR pivot', consists of two steps: first, compiling a source program into LLVM IR, and second, decompiling the IR into the target language using a neural decompiler. This approach requires the IR at inference time.

risk_factors: Using IR as pivot requires that the source and destination language have front-ends that use the same IR. This rules out some widely-used languages (e.g. Python).
We only worked with the LLVM IR, but our approach is broadly applicable to any pair of languages that share a common Intermediate Representation.
The functions that fail to compile at this point (e.g. because of missing dependencies) are not included in the parallel dataset.
Another limitation is the scale of our current source and target sequences.
Our models work at function level: this reduces compilation failures over missing dependencies, while keeping sequence lengths short.

title: $\texttt{NAISR}$: A 3D Neural Additive Model for Interpretable Shape Representation
name: neural_additive_model_for_interpretable_shape_representation
short_hypothesis: A 3D Neural Additive Model for Interpretable Shape Representation (NAISR) can model a population of shapes by deforming a common shape atlas, where the deformation is an additive combination of individual functions of each covariate. This construction is proposed to disentangle the effects of each covariate on the shape, enabling interpretability, shape evolution, and shape transfer.
related_work: Deep implicit functions (DIFs) are powerful for representing 3D shapes, and Neural Deformable Models (NDMs) can establish point correspondences. However, for a set of 3D shapes with associated covariates (e.g., age, sex, weight), there is no existing method that can precisely represent the shapes while also capturing and disentangling the individual dependencies on each covariate. While some methods condition on covariates, they do not explore covariate-specific deformations in an additive, interpretable manner. This paper proposes to extend the concept of Neural Additive Models (NAMs), which are used for interpretable tabular data, to the domain of 3D shapes to fill this gap.
abstract: Given a set of 3D shapes with associated covariates, there is at present no shape representation method which allows to precisely represent the shapes while capturing the individual dependencies on each covariate. Such a method would be of high utility to researchers to discover knowledge hidden in a population of shapes. For scientific shape discovery, we propose a 3D Neural Additive Model for Interpretable Shape Representation (NAISR) which describes individual shapes by deforming a shape atlas in accordance to the effect of disentangled covariates. Our approach is intended to capture shape population trends and allow for patient-specific predictions through shape transfer. NAISR is the first approach to combine the benefits of deep implicit shape representations with an atlas deforming according to specified covariates.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to provide competitive shape reconstruction performance for unseen shapes while maintaining interpretability.
experiments.Method: The model will be trained on three datasets: 1) Starman, a simulated 2D shape dataset; 2) the ADNI hippocampus 3D shape dataset; and 3) a pediatric airway 3D shape dataset. Performance will be compared against several baseline methods: DeepSDF, A-SDF, DIT, and NDF. The evaluation will be conducted in two settings: one where covariates are inferred during testing, and another where ground-truth covariates are provided as input.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the model's ability to perform shape transfer, which involves predicting a shape's appearance under new covariate values (e.g., predicting future anatomy).
experiments.Method: Using the Starman, ADNI hippocampus, and pediatric airway datasets, the task is to infer a latent code and covariates from an initial shape and then generate a new shape corresponding to a different set of covariates. Performance will be compared against A-SDF, as other baselines cannot model covariates.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Volume Difference (VD) between transferred and gold standard shapes', 'Chamfer distance (CD)', "Earth mover's distance (EMD)", 'Hausdorff distance (HD)']

experiments.Description: To qualitatively evaluate the model's ability to achieve shape disentanglement and shape evolution by visualizing shape extrapolations in the covariate space.
experiments.Method: Using the Starman, ADNI hippocampus, and pediatric airway datasets, the model will generate shapes by varying individual covariates to demonstrate disentanglement, and by varying multiple covariates to show shape evolution. The generated shapes will be visually compared to those produced by the A-SDF baseline.
experiments.Evaluation Metrics: ['Qualitative visualization of generated shapes', 'Visual inspection of shape changes and volumes when varying covariates']

risk_factors: Invertible transforms are often desirable for shape correspondences but not guaranteed in NAISR.
Invertibility could be guaranteed by representing deformations via velocity fields, but such parameterizations are costly because of the required numerical integration.
So far we only indirectly assess our model by shape reconstruction and transfer performance.
Introducing group sparsity (Yin et al., 2012; Chen et al., 2017) to NAISR for high-dimensional covariates is also promising future work.

title: RLIE: Rule Generation with Logistic Regression, Iterative Refinement, and Evaluation for Large Language Models
name: rlie-probabilistic-rule-learning-with-llms
short_hypothesis: A unified framework, RLIE, that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) for natural language rule generation with probabilistic modeling (logistic regression) for rule weighting and iterative refinement based on prediction errors, will produce a flexible, robust, and reusable set of probabilistic rules for reasoning tasks.
related_work: Traditional rule learning methods are constrained by a predefined predicate space, limiting their applicability to unstructured data. While Large Language Models (LLMs) can generate rules in natural language, existing methods often overlook the combination effects of rules or fail to explore robust probabilistic inference. They might focus on optimizing a single rule or use simple aggregation, but the potential of coupling LLMs with probabilistic rule learning to ensure robust, collaborative inference is not fully explored. This paper aims to bridge this gap.
abstract: Nowadays, Large Lange Models (LLMs) are able to propose rules in natural language, overcoming constrains of a predefined predicate space inherent in traditional rule learning. However, existing methods using LLMs often overlook the combination effects of rules, and the potential of coupling LLMs with probabilistic rule learning to ensure robust inference is not fully explored. To address this gap, we introduce RLIE, a unified framework that integrates LLMs with probabilistic modeling to learn a set of probabilistic rules. The RLIE framework comprises four stages: (1) Rule generation, where a LLM proposes and filters candidate rules; (2) Logistic regression, which learns the probabilistic weights of the rules for global selection and calibration; (3) Iterative refinement, which continuously optimizes the rule set based on prediction errors; and (4) Evaluation, which compares the performance of the weighted rule set as a direct classifier against various methods of injecting the rules into an LLM.

experiments.Description: To evaluate the overall performance and robustness of the RLIE framework against several representative and competitive methods on real-world classification tasks.
experiments.Method: The RLIE method will be evaluated on six binary classification tasks from the HypoBench benchmark: Deception Detection (Review), Mental Stress Detection (Dreddit), News Headline Engagement (Headlines), Paper Citations (Citations), AI-generated Content Detection (LLM Detect), and Retweets (Retweets).
